topics.title: RC Integrating and Differentiating Circuits (Transient Analysis with Different Inputs and Frequency Response)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL407 - ANALOG CIRCUITS AND SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** RC Integrating and Differentiating Circuits (Transient Analysis with Different Inputs and Frequency Response)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **RC Integrating Circuit** is a passive linear time-invariant (LTI) network composed of a resistor and a capacitor arranged such that the **output voltage is taken across the capacitor**, producing a time-domain response that is mathematically proportional to the **time-integral** of the input signal. Conversely, an **RC Differentiating Circuit** is the dual arrangement where the **output is taken across the resistor**, producing a response proportional to the **time-derivative** of the input signal.

These two configurations are the **fundamental analog computing building blocks** of every electronic system, from simple signal shapers in sensor front-ends to the operational amplifier-based integrators used in analog computers, function generators, and active filters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme – Syllabus Definition:**
> In the context of **PCAEL407 (Analog Circuits and Simulation Lab)**, this experiment belongs to the category of *wave-shaping circuits*. The lab demands (i) **transient analysis** with step, square, pulse, triangular and sine inputs, and (ii) the **frequency response (Bode plot)** verification of both integrator and differentiator topologies using simulation tools such as **LTspice, PSpice, Multisim, or equivalent open-source simulators (e.g., ngspice/QUCS)**.

### 1.2 The Two Canonical Topologies

| Topology | Element Receiving Input | Output Taken Across | Mathematical Operation | Waveform Effect |
|----------|------------------------|---------------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| **Integrator (Low-Pass)** | Resistor **R** | Capacitor **C** | $v_o(t) \propto \int v_{in}(t)\,dt$ | Smooths sharp edges, removes high frequencies |
| **Differentiator (High-Pass)** | Capacitor **C** | Resistor **R** | $v_o(t) \propto \frac{dv_{in}(t)}{dt}$ | Sharpens edges, emphasizes high frequencies |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English for First-Time Learners)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Water-Tank Analogy (Integrator):**
> Imagine pouring water into a bucket through a narrow pipe (the **resistor R**) while the bucket itself is the **capacitor C** storing the water. The water level in the bucket (the **output voltage**) cannot jump instantly — it builds up *gradually* and *accumulates* every drop you pour in. This gradual accumulation IS the process of integration. The output "remembers" and "sums up" everything that happened in the past.
>
> **The Bumpy-Road Analogy (Differentiator):**
> Now imagine driving a car over a bumpy road. The car's suspension (the **resistor**) responds sharply to every bump but then quickly returns to neutral. The **output voltage across R** spikes up at every sudden change (edge) of the input but returns to zero during flat, constant regions. The output "reacts only to the *change*" — this is differentiation.

### 1.4 Why This Experiment Matters in Engineering

- **Analog signal processing**: Every audio crossover, every noise filter, every pulse shaper is built on these two primitives.
- **Sensors & Instrumentation**: Wheatstone bridges with RC filters rely on these principles.
- **Control Systems**: The PID controller (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) is the industrial embodiment of these two operations.
- **Communication Systems**: Pulse-shaping networks in line drivers and receivers use RC integrators/differentiators.
- **Digital Electronics**: The **differentiation** of digital clock edges creates the dreaded *ground bounce* and *ringing* on PCB tracks — and the *integrator* is precisely what filters them out.

### 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Fundamental time-constant**: $\tau = RC$, measured in **seconds (s)**
- **Corner frequency (-3 dB point)**: $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$, measured in **Hertz (Hz)**
- **Permittivity of free space**: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}$ (used in deriving C)
- **Standard input amplitude**: $V_{p-p} = 5\text{ V}$ (typical TTL/CMOS-compatible lab signal)
- **Standard frequencies tested**: $100\ \text{Hz},\ 1\ \text{kHz},\ 10\ \text{kHz},\ 100\ \text{kHz}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Fundamental Principle: The Time-Constant $\tau = RC$

The behaviour of both circuits is governed by a single parameter: the **time-constant** $\tau = RC$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Rule of RC Wave-Shaping:**
> The relationship between $\tau$ and the **period $T$** of the input signal determines whether the circuit behaves as an **integrator**, a **differentiator**, or a **linear filter (neither, in the strict mathematical sense)**.

### 2.2 Operating Regions — The Three Behavioural Regimes

| Operating Condition | Relationship | Behaviour | Output Waveform (for Square Input) |
|---------------------|--------------|-----------|-------------------------------------|
| **True Integrator** | $\tau \gg T$ (typically $\tau \geq 10T$) | Output is integral of input | Triangular wave (ramp segments) |
| **True Differentiator** | $\tau \ll T$ (typically $\tau \leq 0.1T$) | Output is derivative of input | Sharp positive & negative spikes |
| **Linear Filter** | $\tau \approx T$ | Partial integration/differentiation | Rounded exponential rise/fall |

### 2.3 The RC Integrator — Detailed Operational Logic

**Circuit Topology:** Input $v_{in}(t) \to$ Resistor $R \to$ Node A $\to$ Capacitor $C \to$ Ground. Output $v_o(t)$ is measured at Node A (across the capacitor).

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

1. **KVL around the loop**: $v_{in}(t) = v_R(t) + v_C(t) = i(t) R + v_o(t)$
2. **Capacitor current-voltage relation**: $i(t) = C\dfrac{dv_o(t)}{dt}$
3. **Substituting**: $v_{in}(t) = RC \dfrac{dv_o(t)}{dt} + v_o(t)$
4. **Solving the first-order ODE** for the assumption $RC \gg T$ (i.e., the $v_o$ term dominates over the $RC\,dv_o/dt$ term):
5. The output voltage **rises/falls exponentially** with time-constant $\tau = RC$, but for a square-wave input, the half-period is so short that only the **initial linear portion** of the exponential is traced.
6. This linear-tracing behaviour is mathematically equivalent to **integration**.

**Steady-state output for a square wave of amplitude $V_m$ and period $T$:**

$$v_o(t) = V_{avg} + \frac{V_m \tau}{T}\left[1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right]\quad \text{at each half-cycle}$$

When $\tau \gg T$, the exponential approximates a **straight line** — the hallmark of integration.

### 2.4 The RC Differentiator — Detailed Operational Logic

**Circuit Topology:** Input $v_{in}(t) \to$ Capacitor $C \to$ Node A $\to$ Resistor $R \to$ Ground. Output $v_o(t)$ is measured at Node A (across the resistor).

1. **KVL**: $v_{in}(t) = v_C(t) + v_o(t)$
2. **Capacitor relation**: $i(t) = C\dfrac{d(v_{in} - v_o)}{dt}$
3. **Resistor relation**: $v_o(t) = i(t) R = RC\left[\dfrac{dv_{in}(t)}{dt} - \dfrac{dv_o(t)}{dt}\right]$
4. **Under the assumption $\tau \ll T$**, the term $RC\,dv_o/dt$ can be dropped, yielding:
5. $v_o(t) \approx RC\dfrac{dv_{in}(t)}{dt}$ — a **true derivative**.

**Output for a square wave of amplitude $V_m$:**
- During the **rising edge**, $dv_{in}/dt \to +\infty$, producing a **positive exponential spike** of peak $\approx V_m$ and decay time $\tau$.
- During the **falling edge**, $dv_{in}/dt \to -\infty$, producing a **negative exponential spike**.
- During the **flat top/bottom**, $dv_{in}/dt = 0$, so $v_o = 0$.

### 2.5 Frequency Response (Bode Analysis) — The Two Sides of One Coin

Both circuits are described by a single first-order transfer function, differing only in the location of the output node.

**Integrator (Low-Pass Filter) — Transfer Function:**

$$H_{INT}(j\omega) = \frac{V_o}{V_{in}} = \frac{1/j\omega C}{R + 1/j\omega C} = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

- **At $\omega \to 0$ (DC)**: $\vert H \vert \to 1$ (0 dB) — pass DC
- **At $\omega = 1/RC$**: $\vert H \vert = 1/\sqrt{2}$ (**-3 dB corner**)
- **At $\omega \to \infty$**: $\vert H \vert \to 1/\omega RC$ — **rolls off at -20 dB/decade**

**Differentiator (High-Pass Filter) — Transfer Function:**

$$H_{DIFF}(j\omega) = \frac{V_o}{V_{in}} = \frac{R}{R + 1/j\omega C} = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

- **At $\omega \to 0$ (DC)**: $\vert H \vert \to 0$ — blocks DC
- **At $\omega = 1/RC$**: $\vert H \vert = 1/\sqrt{2}$ (**-3 dB corner**)
- **At $\omega \to \infty$**: $\vert H \vert \to 1$ (0 dB) — passes high frequencies with rising slope of **+20 dB/decade** below the corner

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize this table — it is the backbone of every KTU exam question on this topic.**

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------------------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | **Time-constant** | $\tau = RC$ | seconds (s) | Universal for both circuits |
| 2 | **Corner frequency** | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Hz | -3 dB point |
| 3 | **Integrator transfer function** | $H_{INT}(j\omega) = \dfrac{1}{1+j\omega RC}$ | dimensionless | First-order low-pass |
| 4 | **Differentiator transfer function** | $H_{DIFF}(j\omega) = \dfrac{j\omega RC}{1+j\omega RC}$ | dimensionless | First-order high-pass |
| 5 | **Integrator magnitude** | $\vert H_{INT} \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1+(\omega RC)^2}}$ | dimensionless | Used for Bode plot |
| 6 | **Differentiator magnitude** | $\vert H_{DIFF} \vert = \dfrac{\omega RC}{\sqrt{1+(\omega RC)^2}}$ | dimensionless | Used for Bode plot |
| 7 | **Integrator phase** | $\angle H_{INT} = -\tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$ | radians or degrees | Always negative (lagging) |
| 8 | **Differentiator phase** | $\angle H_{DIFF} = 90^{\circ} - \tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$ | degrees | Leading for low frequencies |
| 9 | **Capacitor charging equation** | $v_C(t) = V_f + (V_i - V_f)e^{-t/\tau}$ | volts (V) | General transient |
| 10 | **Output slope (integrator)** | $\dfrac{dv_o}{dt} \approx \dfrac{V_{in}}{\tau} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{RC}$ | V/s | Slope of triangular output |
| 11 | **Peak output (differentiator)** | $V_{peak} \approx V_{in}$ | volts (V) | Equal to input amplitude at the edge |
| 12 | **Time for 63.2% change** | $t = \tau$ | seconds | One time-constant |
| 13 | **Time for 95% change** | $t = 3\tau$ | seconds | Practical settling time |
| 14 | **Time for 99.3% change** | $t = 5\tau$ | seconds | Engineering settling time |
| 15 | **Cutoff wavelength analogy** | $\lambda_c = c \cdot 2\pi RC$ | metres | Used in transmission-line analogy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Whenever a question gives you $R$, $C$, and a square-wave frequency $f$, the **first step is always** to compute $\tau$ and $T = 1/f$. Then verify whether the conditions $\tau \gg T$ or $\tau \ll T$ hold. This single step differentiates a 3-mark answer from a 14-mark answer.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Op-amp integrators** in analog computers solve differential equations by integrating sensor inputs.
- **Sawtooth generators** in oscilloscopes use RC integrators to convert square waves to ramp waves for time-base generation.
- **Edge detectors** in digital logic use RC differentiators to generate narrow trigger pulses from logic transitions.
- **Debouncing circuits** in mechanical switches use RC integrators to filter out contact bounce.
- **555-timer and Schmitt trigger input networks** use these topologies to set trigger thresholds.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Transfer Function of the RC Integrator

**Starting Circuit Equation (KVL around the loop):**

$$v_{in}(t) = v_R(t) + v_C(t)$$

**Step 1 — Express the resistor voltage using Ohm's law:**

$$v_R(t) = i(t) \cdot R$$

**Step 2 — Express the capacitor current in terms of output voltage (the output is the capacitor voltage):**

$$i(t) = C \frac{dv_o(t)}{dt} = C \frac{dv_C(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute both into the KVL equation:**

$$v_{in}(t) = R \cdot C \frac{dv_o(t)}{dt} + v_o(t)$$

**Step 4 — Convert to the frequency domain using phasor analysis (steady-state sinusoidal excitation at $\omega$):**

$$v_{in} \to V_{in}\angle 0^{\circ},\quad v_o \to V_o\angle\phi,\quad \frac{d}{dt} \to j\omega$$

$$V_{in} = j\omega RC \cdot V_o + V_o = V_o(1 + j\omega RC)$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the transfer function $H_{INT}(j\omega) = V_o/V_{in}$:**

$$\boxed{H_{INT}(j\omega) = \frac{V_o}{V_{in}} = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}}$$

**Step 6 — Magnitude response:**

$$\vert H_{INT}(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

**Step 7 — Asymptotic analysis:**
- When $\omega RC \ll 1$ (low frequencies, well below the corner): $\vert H \vert \approx 1$ → passes signals unchanged.
- When $\omega RC \gg 1$ (high frequencies, well above the corner): $\vert H \vert \approx \dfrac{1}{\omega RC}$ → rolls off at **-20 dB/decade**.

**Step 8 — Conversion of magnitude to dB:**

$$\vert H \vert_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+(\omega RC)^2}}\right) = -10\log_{10}\bigl(1 + (\omega RC)^2\bigr)$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Transfer Function of the RC Differentiator

**Step 1 — KVL around the loop (output across resistor):**

$$v_{in}(t) = v_C(t) + v_o(t)$$

**Step 2 — The current through the capacitor equals the current through the resistor (series circuit):**

$$i(t) = C\frac{dv_C(t)}{dt} = \frac{v_o(t)}{R}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $v_C(t)$ in terms of $v_o(t)$:**

$$v_C(t) = \frac{1}{C}\int i(t)\,dt = \frac{1}{RC}\int v_o(t)\,dt$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into KVL:**

$$v_{in}(t) = \frac{1}{RC}\int v_o(t)\,dt + v_o(t)$$

**Step 5 — Convert to phasor domain:**

$$V_{in} = \frac{V_o}{j\omega RC} + V_o = V_o\left(\frac{1 + j\omega RC}{j\omega RC}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the transfer function:**

$$\boxed{H_{DIFF}(j\omega) = \frac{V_o}{V_{in}} = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}}$$

**Step 7 — Magnitude response:**

$$\vert H_{DIFF}(j\omega) \vert = \frac{\omega RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

**Step 8 — Asymptotic analysis:**
- When $\omega RC \ll 1$ (low frequencies): $\vert H \vert \approx \omega RC$ → rises at **+20 dB/decade**.
- When $\omega RC \gg 1$ (high frequencies): $\vert H \vert \approx 1$ → passes high frequencies unchanged.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Time-Domain Response of Integrator to a Step Input

Let $v_{in}(t) = V_s \cdot u(t)$ (a step of amplitude $V_s$ at $t=0$) and assume initial capacitor voltage $v_o(0) = 0$.

**Step 1 — Governing ODE:**

$$RC\frac{dv_o}{dt} + v_o = V_s$$

**Step 2 — Standard first-order linear ODE with constant coefficients. The particular solution is the steady-state value $v_o(\infty) = V_s$.**

**Step 3 — The homogeneous solution is of the form $A e^{-t/RC}$:**

$$v_o(t) = V_s + A e^{-t/RC}$$

**Step 4 — Apply initial condition $v_o(0) = 0$:**

$$0 = V_s + A \cdot 1 \Rightarrow A = -V_s$$

**Step 5 — Final closed-form solution:**

$$\boxed{v_o(t) = V_s\left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the slope at $t = 0$ (relevant for square-wave integration):**

$$\left.\frac{dv_o}{dt}\right|_{t=0} = \frac{V_s}{RC} = \frac{V_s}{\tau}$$

This is the **initial linear slope** of the exponential rise. When this exponential is "interrupted" by the next falling edge of a square wave (i.e., $\tau \gg T$), only this initial linear segment is traced, producing a **triangular ramp** — the integration effect.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Time-Domain Response of Differentiator to a Step Input

**Step 1 — Apply the impulse-style analysis. The transfer function $H_{DIFF}(s) = sRC/(1+sRC)$ in the Laplace domain.**

**Step 2 — For a step input $V_s/s$, the output in Laplace domain:**

$$V_o(s) = \frac{sRC}{1+sRC} \cdot \frac{V_s}{s} = \frac{V_s \cdot RC}{1+sRC}$$

**Step 3 — Rewrite for inverse Laplace transform:**

$$V_o(s) = \frac{V_s \cdot RC}{1+sRC} = \frac{V_s}{s + 1/RC}$$

**Step 4 — Apply inverse Laplace transform (table: $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{1/(s+a)\} = e^{-at}$):**

$$\boxed{v_o(t) = V_s \cdot e^{-t/RC} = V_s \cdot e^{-t/\tau}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** The output of the RC differentiator for a step input is an **exponential decay** that begins at $V_s$ (equal to the input step magnitude) and decays to zero with time-constant $\tau$. This is the famous "spike" waveform — the *visual signature* of differentiation.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Behaviour vs. Input Waveform

The following matrix cross-tabulates each input waveform with the corresponding ideal output of the integrator and differentiator. This is a **high-yield visual summary** for KTU exams.

| Input $v_{in}(t)$ | Integrator Output $v_o(t)$ (approx.) | Differentiator Output $v_o(t)$ (approx.) |
|-------------------|---------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|
| **Square wave** | Triangular wave (ramp up & down) | Alternating +ve & -ve exponential spikes |
| **Triangular wave** | Parabolic arcs (curved ramps) | Square-wave-like segments |
| **Sine wave** | -90° phase-shifted cosine (with attenuation) | +90° phase-shifted cosine (with attenuation) |
| **Pulse (narrow)** | Single slow-rise ramp | Single sharp positive spike |
| **Sawtooth wave** | Quadratic curves | Narrow positive + wide negative pulse |
| **DC step** | Single exponential rise to $V_{in}$ | Single exponential decay from $V_{in}$ to 0 |

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Square-Wave Integrator

**Given:** $R = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$, $C = 0.1\ \mu\text{F}$, square wave of amplitude $\pm 5\ \text{V}$, frequency $f = 1\ \text{kHz}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the time-constant:**

$$\tau = RC = (10 \times 10^3)(0.1 \times 10^{-6}) = 10^{-3}\ \text{s} = 1\ \text{ms}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the period:**

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{1000} = 1\ \text{ms}$$

**Step 3 — Check the integration condition $\tau \gg T$:**

$$\tau = T = 1\ \text{ms} \Rightarrow \tau/T = 1$$

This is a **boundary case** — the output will be a mildly rounded exponential, not a true triangle. To get a clean triangle, we need $R$ and/or $C$ to be **at least 10× larger**, e.g., $R = 100\ \text{k}\Omega$ giving $\tau = 10\ \text{ms}$.

**Step 4 — Recompute with $R = 100\ \text{k}\Omega$:**

$$\tau = 10\ \text{ms} = 10T$$

**Step 5 — Peak-to-peak output amplitude (triangular approximation):**

$$V_{o,pp} = V_{in,pp} \cdot \frac{T/2}{\tau} = 10\ \text{V} \cdot \frac{0.5\ \text{ms}}{10\ \text{ms}} = 0.5\ \text{V}$$

**Step 6 — Slope of the ramp:**

$$\frac{dv_o}{dt} = \frac{V_{in}}{\tau} = \frac{5\ \text{V}}{10\ \text{ms}} = 500\ \text{V/s}$$

### 3.7 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Sine-Wave Differentiator

**Given:** $R = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$, $C = 1\ \text{nF}$, sine input of amplitude $5\ \text{V}_{p}$ at $f = 159\ \text{kHz}$.

**Step 1 — Compute $\tau$ and corner frequency:**

$$\tau = RC = 10^{-6}\ \text{s} = 1\ \mu\text{s}$$

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi\tau} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-6}} \approx 159\ \text{kHz}$$

**Step 2 — At $f = f_c$, the magnitude is exactly $-3\ \text{dB}$:**

$$\vert H_{DIFF} \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$$

**Step 3 — Output amplitude:**

$$V_{o,p} = 0.707 \times 5\ \text{V} = 3.535\ \text{V}_p$$

**Step 4 — Phase shift at the corner:**

$$\phi = 90^{\circ} - \tan^{-1}(1) = 90^{\circ} - 45^{\circ} = +45^{\circ}$$

This +45° leading phase is the diagnostic signature of a differentiator operating in its passband.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — RC Integrator

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **signal flow** through the integrator topology, including the transient, frequency, and time-constant decision branches. This is the recommended Mermaid fallback for circuits that are difficult to draw as physical schematics.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    srcA[Vin Pulse Square Step Sine] --> compA[Series Resistor R]
    compA --> nodeA[Output Node A]
    nodeA --> capA[Shunt Capacitor C to GND]
    nodeA --> probeA[Vo Across C]
    probeA --> compB{tau vs T Comparison}
    compB -->|tau greater than T| integA[True Integrator Behaviour]
    compB -->|tau approx T| filtA[Linear Filter Response]
    compB -->|tau smaller than T| diffA[No Integration]
    integA --> wfA[Triangular Ramp Output]
    filtA --> wfB[Rounded Exponential Output]
    diffA --> wfC[Distorted Non Ideal Output]
    probeA --> freqA[Frequency Response H equals 1 over 1 plus j omega RC]
    freqA --> bodeA[Bode Magnitude Plot]
    bodeA --> lpfA[Low Pass Shape Corner at fc 1 over 2 pi RC Slope minus 20 dB per decade]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — RC Differentiator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    srcB[Vin Pulse Square Step Sine] --> capB[Series Capacitor C]
    capB --> nodeB[Output Node B]
    nodeB --> resB[Shunt Resistor R to GND]
    nodeB --> probeB[Vo Across R]
    probeB --> compC{tau vs T Comparison}
    compC -->|tau smaller than T| diffB[True Differentiator Behaviour]
    compC -->|tau approx T| filtB[Linear Filter Response]
    compC -->|tau greater than T| intB[No Differentiation]
    diffB --> wfD[Exponential Spike Train Output]
    filtB --> wfE[Distorted Step Edges]
    intB --> wfF[Integrated Smooth Output]
    probeB --> freqB[Frequency Response H equals j omega RC over 1 plus j omega RC]
    freqB --> bodeB[Bode Magnitude Plot]
    bodeB --> hpfA[High Pass Shape Corner at fc 1 over 2 pi RC Slope plus 20 dB per decade below corner]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Bode-Magnitude Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp[Input Signal Spectrum] --> split{Topology Choice}
    split -->|Integrator| intBlock[Multiply by 1 over sqrt 1 plus omega RC squared]
    split -->|Differentiator| diffBlock[Multiply by omega RC over sqrt 1 plus omega RC squared]
    intBlock --> intOut[Low Pass Response Passband DC Attenuates High Frequencies]
    diffBlock --> diffOut[High Pass Response Rejects DC Passes High Frequencies]
    intOut --> intBode[Bode Plot Flat at 0 dB Falls at minus 20 dB per decade after fc]
    diffOut --> diffBode[Bode Plot Rises at plus 20 dB per decade before fc Plateaus at 0 dB after fc]
```

### 4.4 Waveform Mapping Matrix (Visual Summary)

| Input Type | Integrator Circuit | Differentiator Circuit |
|------------|--------------------|------------------------|
| **Square Wave** | Output → Triangular wave (ramp segments) | Output → Exponential spikes at edges |
| **Triangular Wave** | Output → Parabolic curves | Output → Square-wave-like segments |
| **Sine Wave** | Output → Cosine (lagging 90°) | Output → Cosine (leading 90°) |
| **DC Step** | Output → Exponential rise to steady value | Output → Exponential decay to zero |

### 4.5 Simulation-Workflow Flow (Lab Procedure Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start Lab] --> specA[Select R and C Values]
    specA --> tauA[Compute tau equals RC]
    tauA --> sigA[Choose Input Signal Type and Frequency]
    sigA --> simA[Run Transient Analysis in LTspice]
    simA --> plotA[Plot Vin and Vo Waveforms]
    plotA --> checkA{Check tau versus T}
    checkA -->|tau greater than T| okA[Integrator Triangle Confirmed]
    checkA -->|tau smaller than T| okB[Differentiator Spike Confirmed]
    plotA --> acA[Run AC Analysis for Bode Plot]
    acA --> fcA[Measure minus 3 dB Corner Frequency]
    fcA --> compA[Compare Simulated fc to Theoretical 1 over 2 pi RC]
    compA --> errA{Error within 5 percent}
    errA -->|Yes| doneA[Result Validated]
    errA -->|No| debugA[Debug Component Values and Probe Connections]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part A questions in the lab exam are typically 1-mark definition, 1-mark formula, and 1-mark circuit or waveform identification. Total = 3 marks per question.

---

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain the time-constant condition required for an RC circuit to act as a good integrator. State the relationship between $R$, $C$, and the input period $T$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For an RC circuit (output across capacitor) to function as a true integrator, the **time-constant $\tau = RC$ must be much greater than the period $T$ of the input signal**.

$$\tau = RC \gg T$$

Typically, this requires:

$$RC \geq 10\,T \quad \text{(i.e., } \tau \text{ at least 10 times the period)}$$

Under this condition, the capacitor charges and discharges only along the **initial linear portion** of its exponential curve (since only a small fraction of $5\tau$ is available in each half-period). This linear charging/discharging is mathematically identical to the **integration** of the input signal. The output for a square-wave input becomes a **triangular waveform**. **(3 marks)**

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State the transfer function of an RC differentiator and identify its filter type with respect to frequency.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The transfer function of an RC differentiator (output across the resistor) is:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{V_o}{V_{in}} = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Filter type identification:** This is a **first-order high-pass filter** because:
- At $\omega \to 0$ (DC): $\vert H \vert \to 0$ — blocks DC
- At $\omega \to \infty$: $\vert H \vert \to 1$ — passes high frequencies

The corner (cut-off) frequency is:

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

Below $f_c$, the magnitude rises at **+20 dB/decade**; above $f_c$, the response plateaus at 0 dB. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: Q-A or Q-B)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each 14-mark Part-B question in a lab exam is split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Part (a) usually tests theoretical/design aspects, part (b) tests simulation/calculation/interpretation.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set-A]`

**Design an RC integrator circuit using $R = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ and $C = 1\ \mu\text{F}$.**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Derive the transfer function of the RC integrator and identify its filter type.**

**Solution:**

For the RC integrator, applying KVL:

$$v_{in} = iR + v_o \quad \text{where}\quad i = C\frac{dv_o}{dt}$$

Therefore:

$$v_{in} = RC\frac{dv_o}{dt} + v_o$$

In the phasor (frequency) domain:

$$V_{in} = (1 + j\omega RC)V_o$$

$$\boxed{H(j\omega) = \frac{V_o}{V_{in}} = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}}$$

**Filter type:** First-order **low-pass filter** (passes low frequencies, attenuates high frequencies). At the corner frequency $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$, the gain is $-3$ dB. **(3 marks)**

**(ii) Compute the corner frequency and the time-constant.**

**Solution:**

$$RC = (10 \times 10^3)(1 \times 10^{-6}) = 10^{-2}\ \text{s} = 10\ \text{ms}$$

$$\tau = RC = 10\ \text{ms}$$

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-2}} = 15.915\ \text{Hz} \approx 15.92\ \text{Hz}$$

**(2 marks)**

**(iii) Verify the integration condition for a 1 kHz square-wave input.**

**Solution:**

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{1000} = 1\ \text{ms}$$

$$\frac{\tau}{T} = \frac{10\ \text{ms}}{1\ \text{ms}} = 10$$

Since $\tau \gg T$ (by a factor of 10), the circuit **behaves as a true integrator** for a 1 kHz input. **(2 marks)**

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Sketch the expected input (1 kHz, $\pm 5$ V square) and output waveforms with proper scaling.**

**Solution:**

- **Input:** Square wave, amplitude $\pm 5$ V, period $T = 1$ ms.
- **Output:** Triangular wave, centred at 0 V (assuming AC coupling), with peak-to-peak amplitude:

$$V_{o,pp} = V_{in,pp} \cdot \frac{T/2}{\tau} = 10\ \text{V} \cdot \frac{0.5\ \text{ms}}{10\ \text{ms}} = 0.5\ \text{V}$$

- **Slope of the triangular ramp:**

$$\frac{dv_o}{dt} = \frac{V_{in}}{\tau} = \frac{5\ \text{V}}{10\ \text{ms}} = 500\ \text{V/s}$$

So the triangular wave ramps from $-0.25$ V to $+0.25$ V over each half-period of 0.5 ms. **(3 marks)**

**(ii) Describe the transient response you would observe during the first few cycles from a fully discharged capacitor.**

**Solution:**

For the first half-cycle (positive step of 5 V from $v_C(0) = 0$):

$$v_o(t) = 5\left(1 - e^{-t/0.01}\right)\ \text{V}$$

At $t = T/2 = 0.5\ \text{ms}$:

$$v_o = 5(1 - e^{-0.05}) = 5(1 - 0.9512) = 5 \times 0.0488 = 0.244\ \text{V}$$

Since $\tau = 10\ \text{ms}$ and $T/2 = 0.5\ \text{ms} \ll \tau$, the output is essentially a **straight ramp** from 0 to 0.244 V. After a few cycles, the circuit reaches **steady-state**, and the triangular wave oscillates between $\approx -0.244$ V and $+0.244$ V (centred at 0 V). **(4 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set-B]`

**Design an RC differentiator circuit using $R = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.1\ \mu\text{F}$.**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Derive the transfer function of the RC differentiator and identify its filter type.**

**Solution:**

For the RC differentiator (output across the resistor):

$$v_{in} = v_C + v_o,\quad i = C\frac{dv_C}{dt} = \frac{v_o}{R}$$

In the phasor domain:

$$V_{in} = \frac{V_o}{j\omega RC} + V_o = V_o\left(\frac{1 + j\omega RC}{j\omega RC}\right)$$

$$\boxed{H(j\omega) = \frac{V_o}{V_{in}} = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}}$$

**Filter type:** First-order **high-pass filter** (blocks DC, passes high frequencies). The corner frequency is $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$. Below $f_c$, magnitude rises at +20 dB/decade; above $f_c$, the response is flat at 0 dB. **(3 marks)**

**(ii) Compute the corner frequency and verify the differentiation condition for a 100 kHz square-wave input.**

**Solution:**

$$\tau = RC = (10^3)(10^{-7}) = 10^{-4}\ \text{s} = 0.1\ \text{ms}$$

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-4}} = 1591.55\ \text{Hz} \approx 1.59\ \text{kHz}$$

For 100 kHz input:

$$T = \frac{1}{10^5} = 10\ \mu\text{s}$$

$$\frac{\tau}{T} = \frac{100\ \mu\text{s}}{10\ \mu\text{s}} = 10 \Rightarrow \tau \gg T$$

**Confirmed:** The circuit behaves as a true differentiator. **(4 marks)**

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Sketch the expected input and output waveforms.**

**Solution:**

- **Input:** 100 kHz square wave, amplitude $\pm 5$ V.
- **Output:** Train of exponential spikes.
  - On the **rising edge** ($v_{in}: -5 \to +5$, a step of $+10$ V), the output is a positive exponential spike of peak $\approx +10$ V decaying with $\tau = 0.1$ ms.
  - On the **falling edge** ($v_{in}: +5 \to -5$, a step of $-10$ V), the output is a negative exponential spike of peak $\approx -10$ V.
  - Since $\tau/T = 10$ and $T/2 = 5\ \mu\text{s} = 0.05\tau$, the spikes decay to nearly zero well before the next edge. Hence, well-separated clean spikes are obtained. **(3 marks)**

**(ii) State and explain the time-domain output equation when a 0–5 V step is applied at $t=0$.**

**Solution:**

For a step input of magnitude $V_s = 5$ V applied at $t = 0$:

$$v_o(t) = V_s \cdot e^{-t/\tau} = 5 \cdot e^{-t/10^{-4}} = 5 e^{-10^4 t}\ \text{V}$$

- At $t = 0$: $v_o = 5$ V (output equals input step magnitude).
- At $t = \tau = 0.1$ ms: $v_o = 5/e \approx 1.84$ V (decayed to 36.8% of peak).
- At $t = 5\tau = 0.5$ ms: $v_o \approx 0.034$ V (effectively zero — engineering settling time).
- For $t < 0$: $v_o = 0$ (no response before the step is applied).

This exponential decay is the **time-domain signature of a true differentiator**. **(4 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\tau$ vs. $T$ check** — A student who directly writes the integration formula without verifying $\tau \geq 10T$ will lose 2 marks. **Always state and verify the condition explicitly.**
> 2. **Confusing the output node** — Drawing the integrator circuit with the output across the resistor (which is actually a differentiator) is the most common circuit-drawing error. **Draw the circuit before writing the formula.**
> 3. **Mixing up integrator and differentiator transfer functions** — These are the *dual* of each other. Memorize both and double-check by taking the limit at $\omega = 0$ and $\omega = \infty$.
> 4. **Using the wrong peak-to-peak amplitude** — For the triangular output of the integrator, the peak-to-peak is **$V_{o,pp} = V_{in,pp} \cdot (T/2)/\tau$**, not $V_{in}/\tau$. Many students compute the latter, which has wrong units.
> 5. **Omitting units in the final answer** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per quantity if units are missing. Always include **V, s, Hz, F, $\Omega$**.
> 6. **Forgetting the dB conversion** — When the question asks for the frequency response, express magnitude in **dB** and phase in **degrees**, not as raw ratios and radians.
> 7. **Not mentioning the 5% tolerance in simulation** — When comparing simulated $f_c$ to theoretical $1/(2\pi RC)$, mention that a discrepancy within ±5% is acceptable due to component tolerances.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- **Integrator**: Output across **C**, $\tau \gg T$, transfer function $H = 1/(1 + j\omega RC)$ — **Low-Pass**.
- **Differentiator**: Output across **R**, $\tau \ll T$, transfer function $H = j\omega RC/(1 + j\omega RC)$ — **High-Pass**.
- **Time-constant**: $\tau = RC$, governs the speed of charge/discharge.
- **Corner frequency**: $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$ — the $-3$ dB point.
- **Bode slopes**: Integrator falls at **-20 dB/decade**; Differentiator rises at **+20 dB/decade** below the corner.
- **Square wave → Integrator**: produces a **triangular wave**; the ramp slope is $V_{in}/\tau$.
- **Square wave → Differentiator**: produces **exponential spikes** of peak $\approx V_{in}$ (or $V_{in,pp}$ for edge transitions).
- **Sine wave → Integrator**: output is the **-90° phase-shifted cosine**, attenuated by $|H|$.
- **Sine wave → Differentiator**: output is the **+90° phase-shifted cosine**, attenuated by $|H|$.
- **Step → Integrator**: $v_o(t) = V_s(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ — exponential **rise**.
- **Step → Differentiator**: $v_o(t) = V_s e^{-t/\tau}$ — exponential **decay**.
- **Triangle → Integrator**: output is **parabolic** arcs.
- **Triangle → Differentiator**: output is **square-wave-like** segments.
- **Settling times**: $1\tau = 63.2\%$, $3\tau = 95\%$, $5\tau = 99.3\%$.
- **Lab simulation tip**: In LTspice, set the transient analysis time-step to ≤ $T/100$ for accurate waveform capture; use the `.ac dec 100 1 1Meg` directive for Bode plots.
- **Component selection rule of thumb**: Choose $R$ first (typically 1 k$\Omega$ to 100 k$\Omega$ for signal-level circuits), then compute $C$ from the desired $\tau$.
- **Dual relationship**: $H_{INT}(j\omega) \cdot H_{DIFF}(j\omega) = \dfrac{j\omega RC}{1 + (\omega RC)^2}$ — not 1; they are **complementary, not inverses** at the same node.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line mnemonics for the exam:**
> - *"**C**apacitor for **C**umulative (Integrator) — output across C."*
> - *"**R**esistor for **R**apid (Differentiator) — output across R."*
> - *"Integrator makes **smooth**; Differentiator makes **spiky**."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL407 - ANALOG CIRCUITS AND SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Clipping and Clamping Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE EXPERIMENT 2: CLIPPING AND CLAMPING CIRCUITS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Formal)**
> A **clipper circuit** is a wave-shaping circuit that limits (or clips) the amplitude of an input signal above or below a reference voltage level, without altering the remaining portion of the waveform. A **clamper circuit** is a wave-shaping circuit that shifts the entire input signal waveform either upward (positive clamping) or downward (negative clamping) by adding a DC offset, while preserving the peak-to-peak amplitude of the original signal.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English Explanation)

Think of a **clipper** like a pair of gardening shears. The shears do not cut the entire branch — they only trim the parts that exceed a certain length. Similarly, an electronic clipper "shears off" the portion of an input waveform that goes beyond a preset voltage level (positive or negative) and lets the rest of the waveform pass through untouched.

Now think of a **clamper** like a **shifting elevator** for signals. The shape (height) of every passenger stays the same, but the elevator takes the whole group either to a higher floor (positive clamping) or a lower floor (negative clamping). In an electronic clamper, the entire waveform gets pushed up or down by a DC voltage, but the signal's peak-to-peak swing remains unchanged.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Tested in KTU):**
> - **Clipper** = Changes the **shape** of the signal (amplitude is altered).
> - **Clamper** = Changes the **position** of the signal (DC level is shifted), shape is preserved.

### 1.2 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Threshold / Reference Voltage (V_ref)**: Typically **±0.7 V** for silicon diodes (when forward biased), or **±0.3 V** for germanium diodes. In **ideal diode analysis**, V_ref = 0 V.
- **Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV)** of the diode: This is the maximum reverse-bias voltage the diode in the circuit must withstand without breakdown.
- **Diode Forward Voltage Drop (V_F)** = **0.7 V** for silicon, **0.3 V** for germanium.
- **Time Constant (τ = R·C)**: For proper clamping operation, the discharge time constant must satisfy **τ ≫ T/2**, where **T** is the period of the input signal. A common design rule is **RC ≥ 10 T**.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of a Clipper Circuit

A clipper is constructed using a **diode**, a **resistor**, and a **DC reference voltage source (V_ref)**. The diode acts as an electronically controlled switch:

- When the input signal **V_in** is such that the diode is **forward biased** (ON state), the diode behaves like a closed switch and the output is clamped to the reference voltage level (V_ref + V_F for practical diode).
- When the diode is **reverse biased** (OFF state), it behaves like an open switch and the output follows the input: **V_out = V_in**.

The key steps in analyzing any clipper are:

1. Identify the **anode and cathode** of the diode.
2. Write the **diode conduction condition** by comparing V_in with V_ref.
3. For **V_in > V_ref + V_F** (diode ON) → Output is clamped at **V_ref + V_F**.
4. For **V_in ≤ V_ref + V_F** (diode OFF) → Output equals input: **V_out = V_in**.

### 2.2 Operational Breakdown of a Clamper Circuit

A clamper consists of a **capacitor**, a **diode**, and a **resistor (optional load)**. It operates in two phases based on the input signal cycle:

1. **Capacitor Charging Phase**: During the first quarter cycle (when the diode is forward biased), the diode conducts and the capacitor charges rapidly to the peak value of the input signal minus the diode drop. The charging time constant is **τ_c = R_F·C**, where **R_F** is the small forward resistance of the diode.

2. **Capacitor Holding Phase**: During the rest of the cycle (when the diode is reverse biased), the diode acts as an open switch and the capacitor cannot discharge quickly (because the discharge path has a large resistor R_L, making **τ_d = R_L·C ≫ T**). Hence the voltage across the capacitor remains approximately constant at its peak value, and this DC voltage is added to the input signal, shifting the waveform.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all key formulas** a KTU student needs for clipping and clamping problems. Memorize these for the lab record and viva voce.

| # | Circuit Type | Output During Diode ON | Output During Diode OFF | Net DC Shift |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ideal Positive Clipper** | $V_{out} = 0$ V | $V_{out} = V_{in}$ | 0 V (clips above 0) |
| 2 | **Ideal Negative Clipper** | $V_{out} = 0$ V | $V_{out} = V_{in}$ | 0 V (clips below 0) |
| 3 | **Biased Positive Clipper (V_ref > 0)** | $V_{out} = V_{ref} + V_F$ | $V_{out} = V_{in}$ | Clips above $V_{ref}+V_F$ |
| 4 | **Biased Negative Clipper (V_ref < 0)** | $V_{out} = V_{ref} - V_F$ | $V_{out} = V_{in}$ | Clips below $V_{ref}-V_F$ |
| 5 | **Positive Clamper** | Capacitor charges to $(V_{peak} - V_F)$ | $V_{out} = V_{in} + V_{C}$ | $+V_{C}$ (shifts up) |
| 6 | **Negative Clamper** | Capacitor charges to $(V_{peak} - V_F)$ | $V_{out} = V_{in} - V_{C}$ | $-V_{C}$ (shifts down) |
| 7 | **Transfer Ratio (Clipper)** | $V_{out}/V_{in} = 1$ (in passband) | — | Linear passband |
| 8 | **Clamper Time Constants** | $\tau_c = R_F \cdot C$ (charge) | $\tau_d = R_L \cdot C$ (discharge) | $\tau_d \gg T$ |
| 9 | **Design Rule** | — | — | $R_L \cdot C \geq 10T$ |
| 10 | **PIV of Diode** | $PIV = V_{peak,in} + \vert V_{ref} \vert$ | — | Used for diode selection |

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Output Voltage Expression for a Positive Clipper with Bias V_ref

**Circuit Configuration**: A silicon diode (V_F = 0.7 V) has its cathode connected to a DC reference voltage **V_ref = +5 V**, and its anode connected to the input **V_in**. A resistor R is placed between the output node and ground.

**Step 1: Define the diode conduction condition.**
The diode conducts (ON) when the voltage at its anode exceeds the voltage at its cathode by at least **V_F**.

$$V_{anode} - V_{cathode} \geq V_F$$

$$V_{in} - V_{ref} \geq V_F$$

**Step 2: Solve for the threshold input voltage.**
Rearranging the inequality to find the value of V_in at which the diode just begins to conduct:

$$V_{in} \geq V_{ref} + V_F$$

Substituting the values V_ref = +5 V and V_F = 0.7 V:

$$V_{in} \geq 5 + 0.7 = 5.7 \text{ V}$$

This means the diode turns ON whenever the input tries to exceed **5.7 V**.

**Step 3: Write the output voltage expression for the ON state.**
When the diode is ON, it acts as a closed switch (with a 0.7 V drop). The output node is therefore held at:

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} + V_F = 5 + 0.7 = 5.7 \text{ V}$$

This is the **clipping level**. Any part of the input waveform that tries to rise above 5.7 V is flattened at this value.

**Step 4: Write the output voltage expression for the OFF state.**
When the diode is OFF (V_in < 5.7 V), it acts as an open switch. No current flows through R, so there is no voltage drop across R. Therefore:

$$V_{out} = V_{in}$$

**Step 5: Combine into a single piecewise transfer function.**

$$
V_{out} =
\begin{cases}
5.7 \text{ V}, & V_{in} \geq 5.7 \text{ V (diode ON, clipping region)} \\[4pt]
V_{in}, & V_{in} < 5.7 \text{ V (diode OFF, linear region)}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 6: Verify the Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV).**
The maximum reverse voltage across the diode occurs at the negative peak of the input signal, **V_in(min) = -V_p**:

$$
PIV = V_{ref} - V_{in(min)} = V_{ref} - (-V_p) = V_{ref} + V_p
$$

This is the voltage rating required when selecting the diode for the circuit.

### 3.2 Derivation: DC Shift in a Positive Clamper Circuit

**Circuit Configuration**: A diode D (anode at output, cathode to ground) is in parallel with a load resistor R_L. A capacitor C couples the input V_in to the output node. Assume V_in is a sinusoid with peak **V_p**.

**Step 1: Analyze the positive half-cycle (first quarter period).**
During the first positive half-cycle, when V_in rises toward V_p, the diode becomes forward biased (anode is more positive than cathode). The diode conducts and the capacitor charges through the small forward resistance R_F of the diode.

The capacitor charges to:

$$
V_C = V_{in(peak)} - V_F = V_p - V_F
$$

with the polarity shown (left plate positive, right plate negative for a positive clamper).

**Step 2: Analyze the negative half-cycle.**
When V_in begins to fall and goes negative, the diode becomes reverse biased. It now acts as an open switch. The only path for the capacitor to discharge is through the load resistor R_L.

The discharge time constant is:

$$
\tau_d = R_L \cdot C
$$

If we design the circuit such that $\tau_d \gg T$ (where T is the period of the input), then the capacitor voltage remains approximately constant at:

$$
V_C \approx V_p - V_F
$$

**Step 3: Apply KVL to find the output voltage.**
Using Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the loop (input source → capacitor → output node):

$$
V_{out} = V_{in} + V_C
$$

$$
V_{out} = V_{in} + (V_p - V_F)
$$

**Step 4: Determine the new peak values of the clamped waveform.**
The original input had a peak of +V_p and a trough of -V_p. After clamping:

$$
V_{out(max)} = V_p + (V_p - V_F) = 2V_p - V_F
$$

$$
V_{out(min)} = -V_p + (V_p - V_F) = -V_F
$$

**Step 5: Verify that the peak-to-peak amplitude is preserved.**

$$
V_{out(p-p)} = V_{out(max)} - V_{out(min)} = (2V_p - V_F) - (-V_F) = 2V_p = V_{in(p-p)}
$$

This confirms that the peak-to-peak amplitude is unchanged — only the DC level has shifted by **+V_C = +(V_p - V_F)**.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Clipper vs. Clamper

| Parameter | Clipper | Clamper |
|---|---|---|
| **Active Components** | Diode + Resistor + V_ref | Diode + Capacitor + Resistor |
| **Uses Capacitor?** | No | Yes (energy storage element) |
| **DC Level Shift** | None (only amplitude clipped) | Yes (entire waveform shifts) |
| **Peak-to-Peak Amplitude** | Reduced | Preserved |
| **Time Constant Consideration** | Not applicable | $\tau_d = R_L C \gg T$ |
| **Application** | Signal limiting, protection | DC restoration, TV signal baseline |
| **Power Consumption** | Low | Low |
| **Typical Use Case** | Voltage regulation input stage | AC coupling in oscilloscopes, TV receivers |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Categories of Clipping and Clamping Circuits

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Wave Shaping Circuits]:::root --> B1[Clipping Circuits]:::cat
    A0 --> B2[Clamping Circuits]:::cat

    B1 --> C1[Unbiased Clippers]:::sub
    B1 --> C2[Biased Clippers]:::sub
    B1 --> C3[Combinational Clippers]:::sub

    C1 --> D1[Positive Clipper]:::leaf
    C1 --> D2[Negative Clipper]:::leaf

    C2 --> D3[Positive Biased Clipper]:::leaf
    C2 --> D4[Negative Biased Clipper]:::leaf

    C3 --> D5[Dual Diode Positive-Negative Clipper]:::leaf
    C3 --> D6[Dual Diode Asymmetric Clipper]:::leaf

    B2 --> E1[Unbiased Clampers]:::sub
    B2 --> E2[Biased Clampers]:::sub

    E1 --> F1[Positive Clamper]:::leaf
    E1 --> F2[Negative Clamper]:::leaf

    E2 --> F3[Positive Biased Clamper]:::leaf
    E2 --> F4[Negative Biased Clamper]:::leaf

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2c4a,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef cat fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef sub fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#2e75b6,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef leaf fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#5b9bd5,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Functional Operation Flow of a Positive Clipper

```mermaid
graph LR
    IN1[Input Signal V_in Sinusoidal]:::input --> DEC1{Diode Conduction Check}:::decision
    DEC1 -- V_in GREATER THAN V_ref Plus V_F --> SW1[Diode ON Closed Switch]:::active
    DEC1 -- V_in LESS THAN OR EQUAL TO V_ref Plus V_F --> SW2[Diode OFF Open Switch]:::inactive
    SW1 --> RES1[Output Clamped at V_ref Plus V_F]:::output
    SW2 --> RES2[Output Follows V_in]:::output
    RES1 --> SUM1[Waveform Clipped at Top]:::final
    RES2 --> SUM1

    classDef input fill:#70ad47,stroke:#385723,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef decision fill:#ffc000,stroke:#bf8f00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef active fill:#c00000,stroke:#7f0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef inactive fill:#808080,stroke:#404040,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef output fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#a14f10,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef final fill:#7030a0,stroke:#3f1a5f,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Two-Phase Operation of a Positive Clamper

```mermaid
graph TB
    SIG1[Input Sinusoid V_in]:::input --> PHASE1{First Quarter Cycle V_in Rising}:::decision
    PHASE1 -- Positive Half Cycle --> CHG[Diode Forward Biased Capacitor Charges to V_p Minus V_F]:::active
    PHASE1 -- Rest of Cycle --> HOLD[Diode Reverse Biased Capacitor Holds Voltage Almost Constant]:::inactive
    CHG --> SUM2[Output V_out EQUALS V_in Plus V_C DC Shifted Upward]:::final
    HOLD --> SUM2

    classDef input fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef decision fill:#ffc000,stroke:#bf8f00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef active fill:#70ad47,stroke:#385723,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef inactive fill:#808080,stroke:#404040,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef final fill:#c00000,stroke:#7f0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture: Transfer Characteristics Comparison

```mermaid
graph TB
    AXIS[Input Voltage V_in Horizontal Axis]:::axis --> PLOT1[Clipper Transfer Line Slope Equals 1 with Flat Region]:::plot
    AXIS --> PLOT2[Clamper Transfer Line Slope Equals 1 Shifted Vertically by V_C]:::plot
    PLOT1 --> NOTE1[Amplitude is Reduced Peaks Flattened]:::note
    PLOT2 --> NOTE2[Amplitude Preserved Only DC Level Shifted]:::note

    classDef axis fill:#000000,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef plot fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#2e75b6,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef note fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf8f00,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a clipping circuit. With the help of a transfer characteristic, explain the operation of an ideal positive clipper.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A clipping circuit is a wave-shaping circuit that limits the amplitude of an input signal to a predetermined level without distorting the remaining portion of the waveform **(1 mark)**.

An ideal positive clipper consists of a diode in parallel with the output, with its cathode connected to the output node and anode to ground **(1 mark)**.

**Transfer Characteristic:**
- For V_in > 0 V: The diode is forward biased (ON), so V_out = 0 V (the positive half-cycle is clipped to zero).
- For V_in ≤ 0 V: The diode is reverse biased (OFF), so V_out = V_in (the negative half-cycle passes through unchanged) **(1 mark)**.

The transfer characteristic is a horizontal line at V_out = 0 for V_in > 0, and a straight line of slope 1 (i.e., V_out = V_in) for V_in ≤ 0.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Distinguish between a clipper and a clamper. State one practical application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Clipper | Clamper |
|---|---|---|
| Function | Limits amplitude | Shifts DC level |
| Capacitor used | No | Yes |
| Output waveform shape | Altered | Preserved |

**(2 marks for distinction table)**

**Applications:**
- **Clipper**: Used in TV receivers to remove noise spikes above a threshold voltage; also used in AM signal processing for limiting the signal before detection **(1 mark)**.
- **Clamper**: Used as a DC restorer in TV receivers to restore the DC level of composite video signal that was lost during AC coupling **(1 mark)**.

---

### PART B (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Design a biased positive clipper that clips the input sinusoid (peak ±10 V) at **+5 V** using a silicon diode. Draw the circuit diagram and derive the output waveform expression. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the operation of a positive clamper circuit with a neat circuit diagram. Derive the expression for the DC shift introduced by the circuit, assuming an ideal diode. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Circuit Design:**
- Use a silicon diode (V_F = 0.7 V).
- Place the diode with its **cathode connected to the output node** and its **anode connected to a DC reference voltage of +5 V**.
- A resistor R = 1 kΩ is placed from input to output.

**Threshold Voltage Calculation:**

The diode conducts when:

$$V_{anode} - V_{cathode} \geq V_F$$

$$5 - V_{out} \geq 0.7$$

$$V_{out} \leq 4.3 \text{ V (diode OFF region)}$$

Equivalently, the diode turns ON when V_in exceeds V_ref + V_F:

$$V_{in} \geq 5 + 0.7 = 5.7 \text{ V}$$

**Piecewise Output Expression:**

$$
V_{out} =
\begin{cases}
5.7 \text{ V}, & V_{in} \geq 5.7 \text{ V} \\[4pt]
V_{in}, & V_{in} < 5.7 \text{ V}
\end{cases}
$$

**(3 marks for circuit diagram, 2 marks for threshold calculation, 2 marks for piecewise output)**

**Output Waveform Sketch:**
- The input sinusoid (peak ±10 V) has its top portion (above +5.7 V) flattened.
- The bottom portion (down to -10 V) passes through unchanged.
- This produces a waveform with a clipped top at +5.7 V.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Positive Clamper Circuit:**
A positive clamper consists of a capacitor C in series between the input and output, with a diode D connected in parallel with the output (anode at ground, cathode at output node). A load resistor R_L is connected from the output to ground.

**Operation:**

**Step 1 (Charging Phase):** During the first positive half-cycle, when V_in approaches its peak V_p, the diode is reverse biased (because its cathode is more positive than its anode at ground). Wait — for a **positive clamper**, the diode is oriented with cathode at the output and anode at ground. During the negative half-cycle, V_out goes below 0 V, the diode becomes forward biased and conducts. The capacitor charges rapidly through the diode's small forward resistance to the peak negative value V_p (for ideal diode), with the left plate positive and right plate negative.

So for an ideal positive clamper: $V_C = V_p$

**Step 2 (Holding Phase):** During the positive half-cycle, the diode becomes reverse biased (acts as open). The capacitor cannot discharge through the high-resistance load R_L (since $\tau_d = R_L C \gg T$), so V_C remains approximately constant.

**Step 3 (DC Shift Calculation):** Applying KVL:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} + V_C = V_{in} + V_p$$

**New Output Limits:**

$$V_{out(max)} = V_p + V_p = 2V_p$$

$$V_{out(min)} = -V_p + V_p = 0 \text{ V}$$

**DC Shift Introduced:** $+V_p$ (the entire waveform is shifted up by V_p volts).

**(2 marks for circuit diagram, 3 marks for operation phases, 2 marks for DC shift derivation)**

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram and input-output waveforms, explain the operation of a **negative clipper with bias**. Derive the expression for the clipping level. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sinusoidal signal of peak ±8 V is applied to a positive clamper using an ideal diode and a 1 µF capacitor. The circuit uses a load resistor of 100 kΩ and the input frequency is 1 kHz. Verify whether the circuit acts as a proper clamper. Compute the DC shift introduced. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Negative Biased Clipper Circuit:**
The diode has its **anode connected to a DC reference V_ref** (typically negative, e.g., -5 V) and its **cathode connected to the output node**. The output is taken across a resistor R connected to ground.

**Conduction Condition:**
The diode conducts when:

$$V_{anode} - V_{cathode} \geq V_F$$

$$V_{ref} - V_{out} \geq V_F$$

$$V_{out} \leq V_{ref} - V_F$$

So the diode is ON when $V_{in} \leq V_{ref} - V_F$, clipping the **negative portion** of the input.

**Clipping Level:**

$$V_{clip} = V_{ref} - V_F = -5 - 0.7 = -5.7 \text{ V}$$

**Piecewise Output Expression:**

$$
V_{out} =
\begin{cases}
-5.7 \text{ V}, & V_{in} \leq -5.7 \text{ V} \\[4pt]
V_{in}, & V_{in} > -5.7 \text{ V}
\end{cases}
$$

**Input-Output Waveform:** The bottom portion (below -5.7 V) is flattened at -5.7 V. The top portion (up to +V_p) passes through linearly.

**(2 marks circuit + waveform, 3 marks conduction condition, 2 marks clipping level derivation)**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: Calculate the period of the input signal.**

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{1000} = 1 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the discharge time constant.**

$$\tau_d = R_L \cdot C = 100 \times 10^3 \times 1 \times 10^{-6} = 0.1 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3: Verify the proper clamper condition.**

For a proper clamper, $\tau_d \gg T$, i.e., $\tau_d \geq 10T$:

$$10T = 10 \times 1 \text{ ms} = 10 \text{ ms}$$

$$\tau_d = 0.1 \text{ s} = 100 \text{ ms} \gg 10 \text{ ms}$$

Since $\tau_d$ is much greater than 10T, the capacitor holds its charge during the negative half-cycle, and the circuit **acts as a proper clamper** ✓.

**Step 4: Compute the DC shift.**

For a positive clamper using an ideal diode, the capacitor charges to:

$$V_C = V_p = 8 \text{ V}$$

The DC shift introduced is therefore **+8 V**. The output waveform varies between 0 V and +16 V, with the original peak-to-peak amplitude of 16 V preserved.

**(2 marks time constant, 2 marks verification, 2 marks DC shift calculation, 1 mark conclusion)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks**
> 1. **Confusing clipper polarity**: Many students draw a positive clipper circuit but write the output equation for a negative clipper (or vice versa). **Always double-check the diode orientation before writing the piecewise expression.**
> 2. **Forgetting V_F = 0.7 V in biased clippers**: When the question specifies a silicon diode and a reference voltage V_ref, the actual clipping level is **V_ref + V_F** (or V_ref - V_F), NOT V_ref alone. Examiners specifically deduct 1 mark for this omission.
> 3. **Incorrect DC shift in clampers**: The DC shift for a positive clamper is **+V_C = +(V_p - V_F)**, NOT +V_p alone. The V_F drop must be accounted for unless the diode is specified as ideal.
> 4. **Not verifying the time constant condition**: When designing a clamper, always state the condition $\tau_d = R_L C \geq 10T$ and verify it numerically. Examiners award a full mark for this check.
> 5. **Confusing peak-to-peak amplitude preservation in clampers**: Some students wrongly state that a clamper changes the signal amplitude. The correct statement is: **"Peak-to-peak amplitude is preserved; only the DC level shifts."**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **clipper** uses a diode to limit (clip) the amplitude of a waveform above or below a reference level — **shape is altered, DC level is unchanged**.
- A **clamper** uses a diode + capacitor to shift the DC level of a waveform — **shape is preserved, DC level shifts**.
- For silicon diodes, always use **V_F = 0.7 V** in practical calculations; for germanium, use **V_F = 0.3 V**; for ideal diodes, **V_F = 0 V**.
- **Biased clipper clipping level** = V_ref + V_F (positive biased) or V_ref - V_F (negative biased).
- **Positive clamper DC shift** = +(V_p - V_F), where V_p is the peak of the input.
- **Negative clamper DC shift** = -(V_p - V_F).
- **Proper clamper design rule**: $\tau_d = R_L \cdot C \geq 10 T$, where T is the period of the input signal.
- **PIV of clipper diode** = V_peak(in) + \vert V_ref \vert — always calculate this for diode selection.
- The **transfer characteristic** of a clipper has a flat (horizontal) region, while a clamper has a linear region (slope = 1) shifted vertically.
- **Applications**: Clippers are used in AM receivers for noise limiting and signal conditioning; clampers are used in TV receivers as DC restorers and in oscilloscope input stages.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Astable / Monostable / Bistable Multivibrator Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL407 - ANALOG CIRCUITS AND SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Astable / Monostable / Bistable Multivibrator Circuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Multivibrator** is a two-stage regenerative electronic switching circuit that employs two active devices (transistors or op-amps) coupled in a positive feedback loop to produce square wave, rectangular wave, or pulse outputs. Multivibrators are classified into three fundamental categories based on their stable operating states:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A multivibrator is a sequential logic circuit with two cross-coupled active devices exhibiting regenerative switching action. It is the fundamental building block of pulse and digital timing circuits.

| Type | Stable States | Trigger Required | Output Nature |
|------|---------------|------------------|---------------|
| **Astable** | 0 (No stable state) | No | Free-running square wave |
| **Monostable** | 1 (One stable state) | Yes (External trigger) | Single pulse of fixed width |
| **Bistable** | 2 (Two stable states) | Yes (Set/Reset trigger) | Latched output (0 or 1) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Bucket and Seesaw System**

Imagine three different mechanical seesaws representing the three multivibrator types:

- **Astable Multivibrator (The Auto-Flipping Seesaw):** Picture a seesaw with no locking mechanism. A small weight at one end tips it, the weight slides off due to gravity, and the other end falls. This cycle repeats forever, producing a continuous back-and-forth motion. The seesaw **never rests** — like an astable circuit, it oscillates continuously without any external input.

- **Monostable Multivibrator (The Push-Button Doorbell):** When you press the doorbell, it rings for a fixed duration (say 2 seconds) regardless of how long you hold the button. It naturally returns to its resting (silent) state. The single trigger produces **one pulse of fixed width** — this is monostable operation.

- **Bistable Multivibrator (The Light Switch):** A standard wall light switch has two stable positions — ON and OFF. You must manually flip it to change states. It stays in whatever state you leave it. This latching behavior is bistable operation, the fundamental principle behind a **flip-flop**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Design Parameters for Multivibrator Circuits:**
> - **Supply Voltage (VCC):** Typically **+5V to +12V** for BJT-based designs, **±12V to ±15V** for op-amp designs.
> - **BJT Saturation Voltage (VCE(sat)):** Standard value is **0.2V**.
> - **BJT Base-Emitter Forward Voltage (VBE(sat)):** Standard value is **0.7V**.
> - **Comparator Threshold:** For op-amp based designs, the reference voltage is set by resistive divider.
> - **Time Period Range:** Astable circuits typically produce **1 Hz to 1 MHz** waveforms.
> - **Standard Capacitor Values:** **0.1 μF, 1 μF, 10 μF, 100 μF** for RC timing networks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Astable Multivibrator — Operational Logic

The **Astable Multivibrator** has no stable state. It continuously switches between its two quasi-stable states, producing a **free-running square wave** at the output.

### Operating Cycle (BJT-based, Collector-Coupled):
1. **Initial State Assumption:** Suppose transistor **Q1 is ON (saturated)** and **Q2 is OFF (cut-off)**.
2. **Q1 ON state:** VCE1 = VCE(sat) ≈ 0.2V. The capacitor **C1** (connected between collector of Q1 and base of Q2) discharges through **R1** toward VCC.
3. **Base of Q2 reaches 0.7V:** When the voltage at the base of Q2 exceeds the cut-in voltage, **Q2 turns ON**.
4. **Regenerative Switching:** As Q2 turns ON, its collector voltage drops from VCC to VCE(sat). This sharp negative transition is coupled through **C2** to the base of Q1, driving Q1 into cut-off.
5. **Q2 now ON, Q1 OFF:** The roles reverse. **C2 discharges** through R2, eventually turning Q1 back ON.
6. **Cycle Repeats:** This produces a continuous square wave output at either collector.

### Why It Oscillates (The Regenerative Loop):
The positive feedback path is: **Q1 collector → C1 → Q2 base → Q2 collector → C2 → Q1 base → Q1 collector.** Once a small perturbation occurs (due to thermal noise or power-up transients), the regenerative action amplifies it to produce a full switching transition.

## 2.2 Monostable Multivibrator — Operational Logic

The **Monostable Multivibrator** (also called **One-Shot**) has **one stable state** and **one quasi-stable state**.

### Operating Cycle:
1. **Stable State:** In the resting condition, **Q2 is ON** (saturated) and **Q1 is OFF** (cut-off). The circuit remains in this state indefinitely.
2. **Trigger Pulse:** A negative trigger pulse applied to the base of Q2 (or positive to Q1) forces Q2 OFF.
3. **Quasi-Stable State:** As Q2 turns OFF, its collector voltage rises to VCC. Simultaneously, C1 couples this rise to Q1 base, turning Q1 ON.
4. **Capacitor Discharge:** C1 (charged previously to VCC) now discharges through R1 toward 0V (or to a negative value clamped by Q1's base). The discharge continues until the base voltage of Q2 rises back to 0.7V.
5. **Return to Stable State:** Q2 turns ON again, Q1 turns OFF, and the circuit returns to its stable state.
6. **Pulse Width:** The output pulse width **T = 0.693 × R × C** (for op-amp version) or **T ≈ 0.69 × R_C × R_B × C** (for BJT version, with base resistor R_B).

## 2.3 Bistable Multivibrator — Operational Logic

The **Bistable Multivibrator** (also called **Flip-Flop** or **Schmitt Trigger variant**) has **two stable states**.

### Operating Cycle:
1. **State 1:** Q1 ON, Q2 OFF — stable indefinitely.
2. **State 2:** Q1 OFF, Q2 ON — stable indefinitely.
3. **Transition (Trigger):** A trigger pulse (set pulse to Q1 base or reset pulse to Q2 base) forces the circuit from one stable state to the other.
4. **Memory Property:** The circuit "remembers" the last state even after the trigger is removed — this is the fundamental principle of **digital memory storage**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — Memorize These for KTU Exam**

| Circuit Type | Parameter | Formula | Units |
|--------------|-----------|---------|-------|
| **Astable (BJT)** | Time Period (T) | $T = 0.693 \times (R_{C1} \cdot C_1 + R_{C2} \cdot C_2)$ | seconds |
| **Astable (BJT, Symmetric)** | Time Period (T) | $T = 1.386 \times R_C \cdot C$ | seconds |
| **Astable (BJT)** | Frequency (f) | $f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{1.386 \cdot R_C \cdot C}$ | Hz |
| **Astable (Op-Amp)** | Time Period (T) | $T = 2 \cdot R \cdot C \cdot \ln\left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_2}\right)$ | seconds |
| **Monostable (BJT)** | Pulse Width (T) | $T = 0.693 \cdot R_B \cdot C$ | seconds |
| **Monostable (Op-Amp)** | Pulse Width (T) | $T = R \cdot C \cdot \ln\left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$ | seconds |
| **Bistable (Schmitt)** | Upper Threshold ($V_{UT}$) | $V_{UT} = +V_{sat} \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ | volts |
| **Bistable (Schmitt)** | Lower Threshold ($V_{LT}$) | $V_{LT} = -V_{sat} \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ | volts |
| **Bistable (Schmitt)** | Hysteresis Width ($V_H$) | $V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = \dfrac{2 \cdot V_{sat} \cdot R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ | volts |
| **All Types** | BJT Saturation Voltage | $V_{CE(sat)} \approx \mathbf{0.2V}$ | volts |
| **All Types** | BJT Base-Emitter ON Voltage | $V_{BE(sat)} \approx \mathbf{0.7V}$ | volts |

**Notation Key:**
- $R_C$ = Collector Resistor of BJT
- $R_B$ = Base Resistor (timing resistor in monostable)
- $C$ = Timing Capacitor
- $R_1, R_2$ = Feedback network resistors in op-amp circuits
- $V_{sat}$ = Op-amp saturation voltage (typically $\pm 13.5V$ for $\pm 15V$ supply)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Applications of Multivibrators:**
> - **Astable:** LED flashers, tone generators, clock pulse generators for digital systems, PWM generation, simple AM/FM modulator carrier.
> - **Monostable:** Debouncing circuits for mechanical switches, missing pulse detectors, pulse stretching circuits, digital timing circuits, ramp generator trigger.
> - **Bistable:** Memory cell in SRAM, toggle switch in digital logic, latch circuits, Schmitt trigger for noise immunity in signal conditioning, over-voltage protection latches.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Astable Multivibrator Time Period (BJT, Collector-Coupled)

### Circuit Setup:
- Two cross-coupled NPN transistors **Q1** and **Q2**.
- **R_C1, R_C2**: Collector resistors connected to VCC.
- **R1, R2**: Base resistors connected to VCC.
- **C1**: Coupling capacitor between Q1 collector and Q2 base.
- **C2**: Coupling capacitor between Q2 collector and Q1 base.

### Derivation Assumption:
Assume the circuit is **symmetric**: $R_{C1} = R_{C2} = R_C$, $R_1 = R_2 = R_B$, and $C_1 = C_2 = C$.

**Step 1: Initial state of capacitor voltage.**

Suppose at $t = 0$, Q1 just turned ON (saturated). Then $V_{CE1} = V_{CE(sat)} = 0.2V$. The capacitor C1 has its left plate at $V_{CE1} = 0.2V$ and its right plate connected to Q2 base, which is at $V_{BE2(sat)} = 0.7V$ (Q2 was previously ON, now going OFF). So the initial voltage across C1 is:

$$
V_{C1}(0) = V_{CE1} - V_{BE2} = 0.2 - 0.7 = -0.5V
$$

**Step 2: Q2 turns OFF, C1 begins charging through R1.**

With Q2 OFF, its base is pulled up toward VCC through R1. The capacitor C1 charges from its initial voltage of $-0.5V$ toward the final voltage $V_{CC}$ through resistor R1.

The voltage at Q2 base follows the standard RC charging equation:

$$
V_{B2}(t) = V_{CC} - (V_{CC} - V_{C1}(0)) \cdot e^{-t/(R_1 \cdot C_1)}
$$

Substituting $V_{C1}(0) = V_{CE1} - V_{BE2} = 0.2 - 0.7 = -0.5V$:

$$
V_{B2}(t) = V_{CC} - (V_{CC} - (-0.5)) \cdot e^{-t/(R_1 \cdot C_1)} = V_{CC} - (V_{CC} + 0.5) \cdot e^{-t/(R_1 \cdot C_1)}
$$

**Step 3: Q2 turns ON when $V_{B2}$ reaches $V_{BE(sat)} = 0.7V$.**

At the switching instant $t = T_1$ (half-period):

$$
0.7 = V_{CC} - (V_{CC} + 0.5) \cdot e^{-T_1/(R_1 \cdot C_1)}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
(V_{CC} + 0.5) \cdot e^{-T_1/(R_1 \cdot C_1)} = V_{CC} - 0.7
$$

$$
e^{-T_1/(R_1 \cdot C_1)} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - 0.7}{V_{CC} + 0.5}
$$

**Step 4: Solve for $T_1$.**

Taking natural logarithm on both sides:

$$
-\dfrac{T_1}{R_1 \cdot C_1} = \ln\left(\dfrac{V_{CC} - 0.7}{V_{CC} + 0.5}\right)
$$

$$
T_1 = R_1 \cdot C_1 \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{V_{CC} + 0.5}{V_{CC} - 0.7}\right)
$$

**Step 5: Simplify using the approximation $V_{CC} \gg 0.7$ and $V_{CC} \gg 0.5$.**

For typical $V_{CC} = 10V$ or $12V$, the term $\dfrac{V_{CC} + 0.5}{V_{CC} - 0.7} \approx 1$. We use the standard approximation:

$$
\ln\left(\dfrac{V_{CC} + 0.5}{V_{CC} - 0.7}\right) \approx \ln(2) \approx 0.693
$$

Therefore:

$$
T_1 \approx 0.693 \cdot R_1 \cdot C_1
$$

**Step 6: By symmetry, $T_2 = 0.693 \cdot R_2 \cdot C_2$.**

The total time period is:

$$
T = T_1 + T_2 = 0.693 \cdot (R_1 \cdot C_1 + R_2 \cdot C_2)
$$

For a symmetric design where $R_1 = R_2 = R_C$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$:

$$
T = 0.693 \cdot (R_C \cdot C + R_C \cdot C) = 0.693 \cdot 2 \cdot R_C \cdot C = 1.386 \cdot R_C \cdot C
$$

**Step 7: Frequency of oscillation.**

$$
f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{1.386 \cdot R_C \cdot C} = \dfrac{0.721}{R_C \cdot C}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The factor 0.693 comes from $\ln(2)$, which arises naturally from the RC exponential charging curve crossing a threshold that is approximately halfway between its initial and final values.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Op-Amp Astable Multivibrator Time Period

### Circuit Setup:
- Op-amp configured as an inverting Schmitt trigger with RC timing network.
- **C**: Timing capacitor connected between op-amp output and inverting input.
- **R**: Charging resistor from output to inverting input.
- **R1, R2**: Feedback resistors forming the non-inverting voltage divider.

**Step 1: Determine the threshold voltages.**

The non-inverting input voltage is set by the voltage divider from output:

$$
V_{+} = \dfrac{R_1 \cdot V_{out} + R_2 \cdot V_{+}}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

Wait — this requires careful analysis. The non-inverting input sees a voltage divider formed by R1 (from output to $V_+$) and R2 (from $V_+$ to ground):

$$
V_{+} = V_{out} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

When $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$: $V_{+} = +V_{sat} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = V_{UT}$ (Upper Threshold)

When $V_{out} = -V_{sat}$: $V_{+} = -V_{sat} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = V_{LT}$ (Lower Threshold)

**Step 2: Capacitor charging equation.**

The capacitor C charges through R from $V_{out}$. The voltage at the inverting input follows:

$$
V_{-}(t) = V_{out} - (V_{out} - V_{-}(0)) \cdot e^{-t/(R \cdot C)}
$$

**Step 3: Determine the half-period.**

Assume $V_{out}$ just switched to $+V_{sat}$. The capacitor charges from $V_{LT} = -V_{sat} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}$ toward $+V_{sat}$. Switching occurs when $V_{-}$ crosses $V_{UT}$.

At $t = T/2$:

$$
V_{UT} = V_{out} - (V_{out} - V_{LT}) \cdot e^{-T/(2RC)}
$$

Substituting:

$$
+V_{sat} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} = +V_{sat} - (+V_{sat} - (-V_{sat} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2})) \cdot e^{-T/(2RC)}
$$

Let $\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ for compactness. Then:

$$
+V_{sat} \cdot \beta = +V_{sat} - (V_{sat} + V_{sat} \cdot \beta) \cdot e^{-T/(2RC)}
$$

$$
+V_{sat} \cdot \beta - V_{sat} = -(V_{sat})(1 + \beta) \cdot e^{-T/(2RC)}
$$

$$
V_{sat}(\beta - 1) = -V_{sat}(1 + \beta) \cdot e^{-T/(2RC)}
$$

$$
-(1 - \beta) = -(1 + \beta) \cdot e^{-T/(2RC)}
$$

$$
e^{-T/(2RC)} = \dfrac{1 - \beta}{1 + \beta}
$$

**Step 4: Solve for T/2.**

$$
\dfrac{T}{2} = R \cdot C \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{1 + \beta}{1 - \beta}\right)
$$

**Step 5: Substitute back $\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$.**

$$
1 + \beta = 1 + \dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} = \dfrac{R_1 + 2R_2}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

$$
1 - \beta = 1 - \dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

$$
\dfrac{1 + \beta}{1 - \beta} = \dfrac{R_1 + 2R_2}{R_1}
$$

**Step 6: Final expression for the time period.**

$$
T = 2 \cdot R \cdot C \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{R_1 + 2R_2}{R_1}\right) = 2 \cdot R \cdot C \cdot \ln\left(1 + \dfrac{2R_2}{R_1}\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symmetric Case:** When $R_1 = R_2$, then $T = 2 \cdot R \cdot C \cdot \ln(3) = 2.197 \cdot R \cdot C$ and the duty cycle is exactly 50%.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Astable | Monostable | Bistable |
|-----------|---------|------------|----------|
| **Number of Stable States** | 0 | 1 | 2 |
| **Number of Quasi-Stable States** | 2 | 1 | 0 |
| **External Trigger** | Not required | Required | Required |
| **Output Waveform** | Square wave (continuous) | Single pulse | Latched DC level |
| **Energy Storage Element** | Two capacitors | One capacitor | None (purely resistive) |
| **Typical Use** | Clock generation | Pulse shaping | Memory storage |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | ★★★★★ (Most asked) | ★★★★ | ★★★ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
