# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Define a monostable multivibrator. State its two main applications.**

**Model Answer:**

A **monostable multivibrator** is a two-stage regenerative switching circuit that has **one stable state** and **one quasi-stable state**. In the absence of any external trigger, the circuit remains in its stable state indefinitely. Upon application of an external trigger pulse, the circuit switches to the quasi-stable state for a predetermined duration (determined by the RC time constant) and then automatically returns to its stable state.

The output is a **single pulse of fixed width** regardless of the trigger pulse duration.

**Two main applications:**
1. **Pulse stretching circuit** — used to lengthen narrow pulses to a measurable width.
2. **Missing pulse detector** — used to detect the absence of a regular pulse train in industrial control systems.
3. **Switch debouncing** — eliminates spurious transitions from mechanical switch contacts.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Compare astable and bistable multivibrators based on stable states and trigger requirement.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Astable Multivibrator | Bistable Multivibrator |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **Number of Stable States** | Zero (no stable state) | Two (two stable states) |
| **Trigger Required** | No (self-oscillating) | Yes (Set/Reset trigger) |
| **Output Waveform** | Continuous square wave | Latched DC level (0 or 1) |
| **Energy Storage** | Two capacitors | No capacitors (resistive only) |
| **Time Period** | $T = 0.693 \times (R_1 C_1 + R_2 C_2)$ | No time period (memory element) |
| **Application** | Clock generators, oscillators | Memory cells, flip-flops, Schmitt triggers |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]

**Q: With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of an astable multivibrator using BJT. Derive the expression for the time period of oscillation. (14 Marks)**

#### (a) Circuit Diagram and Working Explanation (7 Marks)

**Circuit Description:**

An astable multivibrator consists of two NPN transistors Q1 and Q2 with the following cross-coupling arrangement:
- The **collector of Q1** is connected to the **base of Q2** through coupling capacitor **C1**.
- The **collector of Q2** is connected to the **base of Q1** through coupling capacitor **C2**.
- **R_C1 and R_C2** are collector resistors connected between VCC and the respective collectors.
- **R1 and R2** are base resistors connected between VCC and the respective bases.
- **VCC** is the DC supply voltage (typically +12V).

**Working Principle:**

**Stage 1 — Q1 ON, Q2 OFF (quasi-stable state):**

Suppose at $t = 0$, Q1 is ON (saturated) and Q2 is OFF (cut-off). Since Q1 is saturated, its collector voltage is:

$$
V_{C1} = V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2V
$$

The capacitor C1, connected between Q1's collector and Q2's base, was previously charged. Its left plate is at $V_{C1} = 0.2V$. The right plate (Q2 base) was at $-V_{CC} + V_{BE} \approx -V_{CC}$ from the previous cycle. 

Now C1 begins to **charge through R1** from $-V_{CC}$ toward $+V_{CC}$ (asymptotically). The voltage at Q2 base rises exponentially:

$$
V_{B2}(t) = V_{CC} - (V_{CC} + V_{CC}) \cdot e^{-t/(R_1 C_1)} = V_{CC}(1 - 2e^{-t/(R_1 C_1)})
$$

When $V_{B2}$ reaches the cut-in voltage of **0.7V**, Q2 turns ON.

**Stage 2 — Regenerative Switching:**

As Q2 turns ON, its collector voltage drops sharply from VCC to $V_{CE(sat)} = 0.2V$. This sharp negative transition is coupled through C2 to Q1's base, driving Q1 from saturation to cut-off.

**Stage 3 — Q2 ON, Q1 OFF (second quasi-stable state):**

Now the roles are reversed. C2 charges through R2, and when $V_{B1}$ reaches 0.7V, Q1 turns ON again, completing one full cycle.

**Output:** A continuous **square wave** is obtained at the collector of either transistor, with the two outputs being **180° out of phase**.

#### (b) Derivation of Time Period (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Assume symmetric design: $R_{C1} = R_{C2} = R_C$, $R_1 = R_2 = R_B$, $C_1 = C_2 = C$.

**Step 2:** The capacitor C1 charges through R1 from initial voltage $(V_{CE(sat)} - V_{CC}) \approx -V_{CC}$ toward final voltage $V_{CC}$.

**Step 3:** The voltage at Q2 base as a function of time:

$$
V_{B2}(t) = V_{CC} - 2V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t/(R_B C)} = V_{CC}(1 - 2e^{-t/(R_B C)})
$$

**Step 4:** Q2 switches ON when $V_{B2} = 0.7V$ (which is negligible compared to $V_{CC}$):

$$
0.7 \approx V_{CC}(1 - 2e^{-T_1/(R_B C)})
$$

Ignoring 0.7V (valid for $V_{CC} \geq 5V$):

$$
1 = 1 - 2e^{-T_1/(R_B C)}
$$

$$
e^{-T_1/(R_B C)} = 0.5
$$

**Step 5:** Taking natural log:

$$
-\dfrac{T_1}{R_B C} = \ln(0.5) = -\ln(2) = -0.693
$$

$$
T_1 = 0.693 \cdot R_B \cdot C
$$

**Step 6:** By symmetry, the other half-period is:

$$
T_2 = 0.693 \cdot R_B \cdot C
$$

**Step 7:** Total time period:

$$
T = T_1 + T_2 = 2 \times 0.693 \cdot R_B \cdot C = 1.386 \cdot R_B \cdot C
$$

**Step 8:** Frequency of oscillation:

$$
f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{1.386 \cdot R_B \cdot C} = \dfrac{0.721}{R_B \cdot C} \text{ Hz}
$$

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]

**Q: Design a monostable multivibrator using 555 timer IC to generate a pulse of width 1 ms. Explain its working with internal block diagram. (14 Marks)**

#### (a) Internal Block Diagram and Working (7 Marks)

**555 Timer Internal Architecture:**

The 555 timer IC consists of:
- A **voltage divider** with three 5 kΩ resistors (hence the name "555") setting reference voltages at $\dfrac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ and $\dfrac{2}{3}V_{CC}$.
- **Two comparators**: Comparator 1 (Threshold) and Comparator 2 (Trigger).
- A **SR Flip-Flop** (the bistable element).
- A **discharge transistor** (Q_dis) connected to pin 7.
- An **output buffer/driver** providing up to 200 mA at pin 3.

**Pin Configuration for Monostable Mode:**
- Pin 1: Ground
- Pin 2: Trigger input (active LOW)
- Pin 3: Output
- Pin 4: Reset (active LOW, tied to VCC)
- Pin 5: Control voltage (decoupled with 0.01 μF capacitor)
- Pin 6: Threshold (connected to external RC network)
- Pin 7: Discharge (connected to external timing capacitor)
- Pin 8: VCC

**Working Principle:**

**Stage 1 — Stable State (Output LOW):**

In the resting condition, the timing capacitor C is **discharged** (voltage across C = 0V, which is less than $\dfrac{1}{3}V_{CC}$). This means the Trigger comparator output is HIGH, setting the internal flip-flop. With the flip-flop RESET, the output is LOW and the discharge transistor is ON, keeping C short-circuited.

**Stage 2 — Trigger Pulse Arrives:**

A **negative-going trigger pulse** (voltage $< \dfrac{1}{3}V_{CC}$) is applied to pin 2. This causes the Trigger comparator to SET the internal flip-flop. The discharge transistor turns OFF, and the output goes **HIGH**.

**Stage 3 — Quasi-Stable State (Output HIGH):**

With the discharge transistor OFF, the timing capacitor C begins to **charge through external resistor R** from 0V toward $V_{CC}$. The voltage across C follows:

$$
V_C(t) = V_{CC} \cdot (1 - e^{-t/(RC)})
$$

**Stage 4 — Return to Stable State:**

When $V_C$ reaches $\dfrac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ (the threshold voltage), the Threshold comparator output goes HIGH, RESETting the internal flip-flop. The output returns to **LOW**, and the discharge transistor turns ON, rapidly discharging C back to 0V. The circuit is now ready for the next trigger.

#### (b) Design Calculations for T = 1 ms (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Required pulse width: $T = 1 \text{ ms} = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ seconds.

**Step 2:** Standard formula for 555 monostable pulse width:

$$
T = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C
$$

**Step 3:** Choose a standard capacitor value. Let $C = 0.1 \mu F = 0.1 \times 10^{-6}$ F.

**Step 4:** Solve for R:

$$
R = \dfrac{T}{1.1 \cdot C} = \dfrac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{1.1 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{1.1 \times 10^{-7}} = \dfrac{10^4}{1.1}
$$

$$
R = 9090.9 \, \Omega \approx 9.1 \, k\Omega \text{ (standard value)}
$$

**Step 5:** Verify:

$$
T = 1.1 \times 9100 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6} = 1.1 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-4} = 1.001 \text{ ms} \approx 1 \text{ ms} \checkmark
$$

**Step 6:** Choose supply voltage $V_{CC} = +5V$ (standard for digital compatibility).

**Step 7:** Trigger pulse requirements:
- Amplitude: Less than $\dfrac{1}{3}V_{CC} = 1.67V$
- Width: Less than the output pulse width T (typically < 100 ns is sufficient)
- Polarity: Negative-going

**Final Component List:**
- R = 9.1 kΩ (timing resistor)
- C = 0.1 μF (timing capacitor, non-polarized ceramic or film type)
- 555 Timer IC
- 0.01 μF decoupling capacitor on pin 5

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Monostable Pulse Width Formula:** Students often write $T = 0.693 \cdot RC$ for the 555 timer, but the **correct formula is $T = 1.1 \cdot RC$** because the charging goes from 0V to $\dfrac{2}{3}V_{CC}$, not 0V to $V_{CC}$.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to Derive, Only Memorizing:** KTU examiners award 2-3 marks specifically for the **derivation steps**. Writing only the final formula will cost you marks even if the answer is correct.
> 
> 3. **Wrong Capacitor Value Selection:** Using polarized electrolytic capacitors in timing-critical positions without considering leakage current can shift the actual frequency by 10-20%. Prefer **ceramic or film capacitors** for the timing capacitor.
> 
> 4. **Confusing Astable 555 Frequency:** For astable 555, the formula is $f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C}$. Do NOT confuse this with the monostable formula.
> 
> 5. **Missing the Duty Cycle Discussion:** In astable 555 circuits, the duty cycle is $\dfrac{R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} \times 100\%$. If the question asks for "design a 50% duty cycle astable," students must set $R_A \ll R_B$ (practically $R_A = R_B$ for approximately 50%).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Multivibrator Circuits**

### 🔑 Astable Multivibrator Key Points:
- ⭐ **No stable state** — oscillates continuously without external trigger.
- ⭐ Time period for BJT version: $T = 0.693 \times (R_1 C_1 + R_2 C_2)$
- ⭐ Time period for symmetric design: $T = 1.386 \cdot R \cdot C$
- ⭐ Time period for op-amp version: $T = 2RC \ln\left(1 + \dfrac{2R_2}{R_1}\right)$
- ⭐ Two outputs are **180° out of phase** with each other.
- ⭐ Output amplitude swings from **VCE(sat) to VCC** (≈ 0.2V to VCC).
- ⭐ **Cannot achieve 50% duty cycle easily** in BJT version without emitter-coupled modifications.

### 🔑 Monostable Multivibrator Key Points:
- ⭐ **One stable state, one quasi-stable state.**
- ⭐ Requires **external trigger** to switch states.
- ⭐ Pulse width for BJT: $T = 0.693 \cdot R_B \cdot C$
- ⭐ Pulse width for 555 timer: $T = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C$ (different factor!)
- ⭐ Pulse width for op-amp: $T = RC \ln\left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$
- ⭐ Output is **active HIGH** (or active LOW depending on configuration) for duration T.
- ⭐ **Recovery time** must be allowed before next trigger (usually 5RC).

### 🔑 Bistable Multivibrator Key Points:
- ⭐ **Two stable states** — acts as a 1-bit memory element.
- ⭐ Requires **set and reset triggers** to change states.
- ⭐ **No timing capacitor** — purely resistive cross-coupling.
- ⭐ Hysteresis voltage: $V_H = \dfrac{2 V_{sat} R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ (for Schmitt trigger version).
- ⭐ Foundation of **SR Flip-Flop, D Flip-Flop, JK Flip-Flop** in digital electronics.
- ⭐ Used in **Schmitt trigger** circuits for noise immunity in signal conditioning.

### 🔑 Universal Design Guidelines:
- ✅ Always use **VCC = +5V or +12V** for BJT circuits, **±15V** for op-amp circuits.
- ✅ For BJT multivibrators, prefer **BC107, 2N2222, 2N3904** (NPN) or equivalent.
- ✅ For op-amp multivibrators, use **μA741, LM358, TL081** or modern low-noise equivalents.
- ✅ Always include a **0.1 μF decoupling capacitor** near the VCC pin of ICs.
- ✅ Verify with **SPICE simulation (LTspice, Multisim, Proteus)** before hardware implementation.
- ✅ Measure output with **oscilloscope** — both channels to verify 180° phase shift.

### 🔑 Common KTU Exam Traps:
- ⚠️ Do NOT confuse the factor **0.693** with **0.721** — the latter is the inverse of 1.386.
- ⚠️ Do NOT use $V_{CC}$ in op-amp threshold formulas — use $V_{sat}$ (typically 1-2V less than supply).
- ⚠️ Do NOT forget that astable **does not need a trigger** — this is the most distinguishing feature.
- ⚠️ Do NOT state that monostable produces a "continuous waveform" — it produces a **single pulse**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RC Coupled CE Amplifier - Frequency Response Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAEL407 - ANALOG CIRCUITS AND SIMULATION LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** RC Coupled CE Amplifier - Frequency Response Characteristics ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) An **RC Coupled Common Emitter (CE) Amplifier** is a multistage voltage amplifier in which the output of one CE stage is coupled to the input of the next stage through a **resistor–capacitor (RC) coupling network**, while the load is connected at the final stage either directly or through another RC network. In the KTU 2024 Scheme lab curriculum, the experiment focuses on the **Frequency Response Characteristics** — i.e., the plot of **voltage gain magnitude $\vert A_v \vert$ (in dB)** versus **signal frequency $f$ (in Hz)** on a **semi-logarithmic scale**, identifying the **lower cut-off frequency $f_L$**, the **upper cut-off frequency $f_H$**, the **mid-band gain $A_{v(\text{mid})}$**, and the **bandwidth $\text{BW}$**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Expected Outcome (CO Mapping):** > After performing this experiment, the student must be able to: > - **CO2:** Analyze the frequency response of an RC coupled CE amplifier and compute the gain (in dB) and bandwidth. > - **CO3:** Simulate the amplifier using SPICE (or equivalent) and validate practical measurements against theoretical design. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Ear & Stereo System" Imagine a **home theatre system** with three sliders: **Bass**, **Mid**, **Treble**. - The **bass slider** controls how well **low-frequency sounds** (drums, deep voice) are amplified. - The **treble slider** controls how well **high-frequency sounds** (cymbals, whistles) are amplified. - The **mid slider** controls the **mid-range** (vocals, guitar) — this is where most of the music lives. An RC coupled CE amplifier behaves **exactly** like this stereo system: - At **very low frequencies**, the coupling and emitter-bypass capacitors behave like **open switches**, blocking the signal — so the bass is weak. - At **very high frequencies**, internal transistor capacitances (like $C_{bc}$, $C_{be}$) behave like **short circuits**, shunting the signal to ground — so the treble is weak. - At **mid frequencies**, the capacitors are "invisible" — neither blocking nor shorting — and the amplifier delivers its **maximum, flat gain**. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** > - **Audio pre-amplifiers and public-address (PA) systems** > - **Intermediate Frequency (IF) stages of AM/FM radio receivers (typically $f_c = 455\,\text{kHz}$ for AM, $10.7\,\text{MHz}$ for FM)** > - **Biomedical signal amplifiers (ECG, EEG front-ends)** > - **Communication transmitters and sensor signal-conditioning units** --- ### 1.3 Standard Frequency-Response Metrics (in **bold**) | Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (BC547 / 2N2222 Class) | |---|---|---| | Mid-band voltage gain | $A_{v(\text{mid})}$ | **40 to 200** (linear) | | Mid-band gain in dB | $A_{v(\text{mid,dB})}$ | **32 dB to 46 dB** | | Lower cut-off frequency | $f_L$ | **20 Hz to 300 Hz** | | Upper cut-off frequency | $f_H$ | **20 kHz to 500 kHz** | | Bandwidth | $\text{BW}$ | **$f_H - f_L$** | > [!NOTE] > **Golden Rule of Frequency Response:** The bandwidth is the range of frequencies for which the amplifier gain is **at least 70.7% ($\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$) of its mid-band value**, i.e., the gain has dropped by **no more than 3 dB** from its mid-band level. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Three Regions of Frequency Response — The "Why" and "How" The single most tested concept in KTU exams on this topic is the **classification of reactive elements across three frequency zones**. Let us dissect each region: #### **Region 1 — Low-Frequency Zone ($f < f_L$)** - **Reactive behaviour:** The **coupling capacitor $C_c$** and **emitter-bypass capacitor $C_E$** present a **significant capacitive reactance** $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$. - **Why gain drops:** These capacitors form **high-pass RC networks** in series with the signal path. As $f \to 0$, $X_C \to \infty$, and the capacitors act as **open circuits**, blocking the signal. - **Effect:** Voltage gain $\vert A_v \vert$ rolls off at **+20 dB/decade** as frequency decreases. - **Dominant pole:** The **input coupling capacitor $C_{c1}$** typically dominates the lower cut-off because it sees the source resistance plus the input resistance of the transistor (which is large). #### **Region 2 — Mid-Band Zone ($f_L \le f \le f_H$)** - **Reactive behaviour:** All coupling and bypass capacitors act as **perfect short circuits** ($X_C \approx 0$). All internal transistor capacitances act as **perfect open circuits**. - **Why gain is flat:** The amplifier becomes a **purely resistive network** — no frequency-dependent elements remain active. - **Effect:** $\vert A_v \vert = A_{v(\text{mid})}$, a **constant, frequency-independent** value. - **Designation:** This is the **useful operating region** of the amplifier. #### **Region 3 — High-Frequency Zone ($f > f_H$)** - **Reactive behaviour:** The **internal parasitic capacitances** — base–emitter diffusion capacitance $C_{be}$, base–collector junction capacitance $C_{bc}$ — and the **Miller effect** come into play. - **Why gain drops:** $C_{bc}$ creates a feedback path from collector to base, and its effective input capacitance becomes $(1 + \vert A_v \vert) \cdot C_{bc}$, which **shunts high-frequency signals to ground**. - **Effect:** Voltage gain rolls off at **–20 dB/decade** as frequency increases. --- ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these 12 equations — they cover ~95% of all KTU numerical and derivations on this topic.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary Condition / Note | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Small-signal CE voltage gain (mid-band) | $A_v = -g_m R_C$ | dimensionless | $g_m = I_C / V_T$; $V_T \approx 26\,\text{mV}$ at $300\,\text{K}$ | | 2 | Mid-band gain using $\beta$ and $r_e$ | $A_v = -\dfrac{\beta \, R_C}{r_\pi} = -\dfrac{R_C}{r_e}$ | dimensionless | $r_e = \dfrac{V_T}{I_E} \approx \dfrac{26\,\text{mV}}{I_E(\text{mA})}$ | | 3 | Input resistance of CE stage | $R_{in} = R_1 \,\Vert\, R_2 \,\Vert\, r_\pi$ | $\Omega$ | $r_\pi = \beta \, r_e$ | | 4 | Output resistance of CE stage | $R_{out} \approx R_C$ | $\Omega$ | Neglecting $r_o$ | | 5 | Lower cut-off frequency (input coupling cap) | $f_{L1} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_S + R_{in}) \, C_{c1}}$ | Hz | $R_S$ = source resistance | | 6 | Lower cut-off frequency (output coupling cap) | $f_{L2} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_C + R_L) \, C_{c2}}$ | Hz | $R_L$ = load resistance | | 7 | Lower cut-off frequency (emitter bypass cap) | $f_{L3} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_E \,\Vert\, r_e) \, C_E}$ | Hz | Typically the **dominant** low-frequency pole | | 8 | Overall lower cut-off frequency | $f_L \approx \sqrt{f_{L1}^2 + f_{L2}^2 + f_{L3}^2}$ | Hz | When poles are non-interacting; sum form also valid: $f_L \approx 1.1\sqrt{f_{L1}^2 + f_{L2}^2 + f_{L3}^2}$ | | 9 | Miller-augmented input capacitance | $C_{M} = C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ | F | **The most important** high-frequency effect | | 10 | Upper cut-off frequency | $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} \, C_{eq}}$ | Hz | $R_{eq} = R_S \,\Vert\, R_1 \,\Vert\, R_2 \,\Vert\, r_\pi$; $C_{eq} = C_{be} + C_{M}$ | | 11 | Bandwidth | $\text{BW} = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ | Hz | Valid when $f_H \gg f_L$ (always true in practice) | | 12 | Gain in dB | $A_{v(\text{dB})} = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$ | dB | Negative sign of $A_v$ (phase inversion) is ignored in dB magnitude | > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Students frequently confuse the **input pole** ($R_S + R_{in}$) with the **output pole** ($R_C + R_L$). Remember: > - The **coupling capacitor $C_{c1}$ at the input** sees the **Thevenin resistance of the source PLUS the input resistance of the amplifier**. > - The **coupling capacitor $C_{c2}$ at the output** sees **$R_C$ in series with the load $R_L$**. --- ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **IF Amplifier Design in Superheterodyne Receivers:** The RC coupled CE amplifier is the **workhorse IF stage** at $455\,\text{kHz}$ in AM radios. Its bandwidth of ~$10\,\text{kHz}$ matches the AM channel spacing. 2. **Biomedical Instrumentation:** ECG and EEG amplifiers use multi-stage RC coupled designs with carefully shaped low-frequency cut-off (typically $0.05\,\text{Hz}$ to $150\,\text{Hz}$) to reject DC electrode offsets. 3. **Audio Power Amplifier Pre-stages:** Before the power transistors, voltage-gain stages using RC coupling provide the necessary **$30$–$50\,\text{dB}$** of voltage swing to drive the output stage. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mid-Band Voltage Gain $A_{v(\text{mid})}$ of a CE Stage **Given:** A CE stage biased in the active region with $R_C$ as the collector load, $R_E$ completely bypassed by $C_E$, and external load $R_L$. **Step 1 — Hybrid-π small-signal model:** In the mid-band, $C_{c1}, C_{c2}, C_E$ are all short circuits. The relevant small-signal parameters are: $$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T}, \qquad r_\pi = \frac{\beta}{g_m}, \qquad r_e = \frac{1}{g_m} = \frac{V_T}{I_E}$$ **Step 2 — Identify the AC load resistance:** Since $R_C$ and $R_L$ appear in parallel at the output (AC-wise): $$R_{L,\text{ac}} = R_C \;\Vert\; R_L = \frac{R_C R_L}{R_C + R_L}$$ **Step 3 — Apply KVL at the output loop:** The collector current $g_m v_{be}$ flows through $R_{L,\text{ac}}$ producing: $$v_{out} = -g_m \, v_{be} \, R_{L,\text{ac}}$$ The negative sign arises because an increase in $v_{be}$ increases $i_c$, which **drops more voltage across $R_C$**, **decreasing** $v_c$. **Step 4 — Voltage gain from base to collector:** $$A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{be}} = -g_m R_{L,\text{ac}} = -\frac{I_C R_{L,\text{ac}}}{V_T}$$ **Step 5 — In terms of $\beta$ and $r_\pi$:** $$A_v = -\frac{\beta \, R_{L,\text{ac}}}{r_\pi} = -\frac{R_{L,\text{ac}}}{r_e}$$ > **Engineering Insight:** A larger collector current **increases** $g_m$, but **decreases** $r_e$, thereby increasing gain. However, it also **shrinks the linear input swing**, creating a classic design trade-off. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Lower Cut-off Frequency $f_{L1}$ (Input Coupling Network) **Given:** Input coupling capacitor $C_{c1}$ in series with source resistance $R_S$ and amplifier input resistance $R_{in}$. **Step 1 — Form the high-pass RC network:** The Thevenin resistance seen by $C_{c1}$ is: $$R_{th} = R_S + R_{in}$$ where $R_{in} = R_1 \,\Vert\, R_2 \,\Vert\, r_\pi$. **Step 2 — Transfer function magnitude:** $$\left\vert \frac{v_{in}}{v_s} \right\vert = \frac{R_{in}}{\sqrt{(R_S + R_{in})^2 + \left(\frac{1}{2\pi f C_{c1}}\right)^2}}$$ **Step 3 — At very high frequencies** ($f \to \infty$), the capacitor reactance vanishes and the ratio becomes $\dfrac{R_{in}}{R_S + R_{in}}$ (the maximum). **Step 4 — Define the cut-off frequency** where the magnitude drops to $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ of the mid-band value: $$\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{R_{in}}{\sqrt{(R_S + R_{in})^2 + X_{c1}^2}}$$ Squaring both sides: $$\frac{1}{2} = \frac{R_{in}^2}{(R_S + R_{in})^2 + X_{c1}^2}$$ Cross-multiplying: $$(R_S + R_{in})^2 + X_{c1}^2 = 2 R_{in}^2$$ This gives: $$X_{c1}^2 = 2 R_{in}^2 - (R_S + R_{in})^2 = R_{in}^2 - 2R_S R_{in} - R_S^2$$ For typical design where $R_{in} \gg R_S$ (which is the usual case for CE with biasing resistors in the k$\Omega$ range), this simplifies to: $$X_{c1} \approx R_S + R_{in}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute $X_{c1} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f_L C_{c1}}$ and solve:** $$\boxed{f_{L1} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S + R_{in}) C_{c1}}}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Miller's Theorem and Upper Cut-off Frequency **Given:** Base–collector capacitance $C_{bc}$ provides a feedback path from output (high-impedance) to input (low-impedance). **Step 1 — Apply Miller's Theorem:** The feedback impedance $Z_{bc}$ between input and output is reflected as two grounded impedances: $$Z_{in(\text{Miller})} = \frac{Z_{bc}}{1 - A_v}$$ $$Z_{out(\text{Miller})} = \frac{Z_{bc} \cdot A_v}{A_v - 1}$$ For a pure capacitance $C_{bc}$, the input Miller capacitance becomes: $$C_{M} = C_{bc}(1 - A_v) \approx C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$$ (since $A_v$ is negative, $1 - A_v = 1 + \vert A_v \vert$). **Step 2 — Total input capacitance:** $$C_{in,\text{total}} = C_{be} + C_{M} = C_{be} + C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$$ **Step 3 — Equivalent input resistance:** Thevenin equivalent resistance at the base node: $$R_{eq} = R_S \;\Vert\; R_1 \;\Vert\; R_2 \;\Vert\; r_\pi$$ **Step 4 — Upper cut-off frequency (low-pass RC):** $$\boxed{f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} \, C_{in,\text{total}}}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why Miller's theorem is critical for high-frequency design:** > If $A_v = 100$ and $C_{bc} = 5\,\text{pF}$, then $C_M = 505\,\text{pF}$ — a **100× amplification** of the parasitic capacitance. This is the single most important reason why CE amplifiers have **limited bandwidth**. --- ### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Typical Problem **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A single-stage RC coupled CE amplifier uses a BJT with $\beta = 100$, $V_{CC} = 12\,\text{V}$, $R_C = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_E = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$ (fully bypassed), $R_1 = 68\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 12\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C_{c1} = C_{c2} = 10\,\mu\text{F}$, $C_E = 100\,\mu\text{F}$, $R_L = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_S = 600\,\Omega$, $C_{bc} = 5\,\text{pF}$, $C_{be} = 30\,\text{pF}$. Calculate (a) the operating point $I_C$, (b) the mid-band voltage gain in dB, and (c) the bandwidth. **Solution:** **(a) DC Bias Point:** $$V_B = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = 12 \cdot \frac{12}{68 + 12} = 12 \cdot \frac{12}{80} = 1.8\,\text{V}$$ $$V_E = V_B - V_{BE} = 1.8 - 0.7 = 1.1\,\text{V}$$ $$I_E = \frac{V_E}{R_E} = \frac{1.1}{1000} = 1.1\,\text{mA} \approx I_C$$ **(b) Mid-band Voltage Gain:** $$r_e = \frac{V_T}{I_E} = \frac{26\,\text{mV}}{1.1\,\text{mA}} \approx 23.6\,\Omega$$ $$R_{L,\text{ac}} = \frac{R_C \cdot R_L}{R_C + R_L} = \frac{4700 \cdot 4700}{9400} = 2350\,\Omega$$ $$A_{v(\text{mid})} = -\frac{R_{L,\text{ac}}}{r_e} = -\frac{2350}{23.6} \approx -99.6$$ $$A_{v(\text{mid,dB})} = 20 \log_{10}(99.6) \approx 39.97\,\text{dB} \approx 40\,\text{dB}$$ **(c) Bandwidth:** Miller capacitance: $$C_M = C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_v \vert) = 5 \times 10^{-12} \times (1 + 99.6) \approx 503\,\text{pF}$$ Input resistance: $$r_\pi = \beta r_e = 100 \times 23.6 = 2360\,\Omega$$ $$R_{in} = R_1 \,\Vert\, R_2 \,\Vert\, r_\pi = 68k \,\Vert\, 12k \,\Vert\, 2.36k = 1992\,\Omega$$ $$R_{eq} = R_S \,\Vert\, R_{in} = 600 \,\Vert\, 1992 = \frac{600 \times 1992}{2592} \approx 461\,\Omega$$ Total input capacitance: $$C_{in,\text{total}} = C_{be} + C_M = 30 + 503 = 533\,\text{pF}$$ Upper cut-off frequency: $$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} C_{in,\text{total}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 461 \times 533 \times 10^{-12}}$$ $$f_H \approx \frac{1}{1.543 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 648\,\text{kHz}$$ Lower cut-off frequency (assume $C_E$ dominates): $$f_{L3} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_E \,\Vert\, r_e) C_E} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times (1000 \,\Vert\, 23.6) \times 100 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$R_E \,\Vert\, r_e = \frac{1000 \times 23.6}{1023.6} \approx 23.05\,\Omega$$ $$f_{L3} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 23.05 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 69\,\text{Hz}$$ $$\boxed{\text{Bandwidth BW} = f_H - f_L \approx 648\,\text{kHz} - 69\,\text{Hz} \approx 648\,\text{kHz}}$$ --- ### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Mid-Band vs. Low-Frequency vs. High-Frequency | Parameter | Low-Frequency Region | Mid-Band Region | High-Frequency Region | |---|---|---|---| | **Dominant reactive element** | $C_{c1}, C_{c2}, C_E$ | None (all invisible) | $C_{be}, C_{bc}$ (via Miller) | | **Capacitor state** | Open / high reactance | Short circuit | Short circuit | | **Equivalent circuit type** | High-pass RC | Resistive | Low-pass RC | | **Gain behaviour** | Rising (+20 dB/dec) | Flat, constant | Falling (–20 dB/dec) | | **Phase shift** | +90° (leading) | 180° (inversion only) | –90° (lagging) | | **Design lever to extend region** | Increase $C_c, C_E$ | Reduce $R_C$ (but lower gain) | Use high-$f_T$ transistor, cascode | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Circuit Schematic of Single-Stage RC Coupled CE Amplifier ```mermaid flowchart LR Vs["Input Signal Vs
