[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper]** **(a)** Define the **bandwidth** of an RC coupled amplifier. How are the **lower cut-off frequency $f_L$** and **upper cut-off frequency $f_H$** identified on the frequency response plot? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **bandwidth (BW)** of an RC coupled amplifier is defined as the range of frequencies over which the voltage gain is at least **70.7% ($\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$) of its maximum mid-band value**, i.e., the gain does not fall by more than **3 dB** below the mid-band gain. $$\text{BW} = f_H - f_L \approx f_H \quad (\text{since } f_H \gg f_L)$$ **Identification of cut-off frequencies on the Bode plot:** - The **lower cut-off frequency $f_L$** is the frequency at which the gain first drops to **3 dB below** the mid-band gain as frequency decreases. Below $f_L$, gain rolls off at **+20 dB/decade** (slopes upward when read left to right with falling $f$). - The **upper cut-off frequency $f_H$** is the frequency at which the gain drops to **3 dB below** the mid-band gain as frequency increases. Above $f_H$, gain rolls off at **–20 dB/decade**. **(b)** Why are **coupling capacitors** used between stages of a multistage amplifier? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Coupling capacitors are used between stages of a multistage amplifier for the following reasons: 1. **DC Isolation:** They block the **DC bias voltage** of one stage from reaching the next stage, preventing a shift in the operating point (Q-point) of the subsequent stage. 2. **AC Coupling:** They allow the **AC signal** to pass from one stage to the next with negligible attenuation in the mid-band region (since $X_C \to 0$ at high frequencies). 3. **Independent Bias Design:** Each stage can be designed with its **own bias network** without interaction from the DC levels of adjacent stages. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Supplemented]** **(a)** With the help of a neat **circuit diagram**, explain the working of a **single-stage RC coupled CE amplifier**. Derive an expression for its **mid-band voltage gain**. (7 Marks) **(b)** Derive the expression for the **lower cut-off frequency** considering the effect of the **emitter bypass capacitor $C_E$**, and explain why this capacitor usually determines the dominant low-frequency pole. (7 Marks) --- #### **Model Solution — Question A** **(a) Part (a) — 7 Marks Solution:** **Circuit Description:** A single-stage RC coupled CE amplifier consists of: - **Voltage-divider biasing:** $R_1$ (upper) and $R_2$ (lower) set the base voltage. - **BJT** in CE configuration with $R_C$ as the collector load and $R_E$ for thermal stability. - **Coupling capacitors** $C_{c1}$ (input) and $C_{c2}$ (output) block DC and pass AC. - **Emitter bypass capacitor $C_E$** shorts $R_E$ for AC signals, preventing AC negative feedback. **Working Principle:** A small AC signal $V_s$ applied at the input is coupled through $C_{c1}$ to the base of the BJT. The transistor amplifies the signal — a small change in $V_{BE}$ produces a much larger change in $I_C$, which develops an amplified voltage across $R_C$. This amplified and **180° phase-inverted** output is coupled through $C_{c2}$ to the load. **Mid-band Gain Derivation:** In mid-band, $C_{c1}, C_{c2}, C_E$ act as short circuits. Applying the hybrid-π model: $$\begin{aligned} v_{out} &= -g_m \, v_{be} \, R_{L,\text{ac}} \\ \text{where } R_{L,\text{ac}} &= R_C \,\Vert\, R_L \\ \therefore A_v &= \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -g_m \, R_{L,\text{ac}} = -\frac{R_{L,\text{ac}}}{r_e} = -\frac{\beta R_{L,\text{ac}}}{r_\pi} \end{aligned}$$ The negative sign indicates **180° phase inversion** between input and output. **(b) Part (b) — 7 Marks Solution:** **Lower Cut-off Frequency due to $C_E$:** The bypass capacitor $C_E$ in parallel with $R_E$ creates a low-frequency pole because at low frequencies, $C_E$ fails to short-circuit $R_E$, introducing **AC negative feedback** that reduces gain. The effective impedance of the emitter network is: $$Z_E = R_E \,\Vert\, \frac{1}{j 2\pi f C_E} = \frac{R_E}{1 + j 2\pi f R_E C_E}$$ For an unbypassed $R_E$, the gain becomes: $$A_v(f) = -\frac{g_m R_{L,\text{ac}}}{1 + g_m Z_E}$$ At mid-band ($f$ large), $Z_E \to 0$, giving $A_v = -g_m R_{L,\text{ac}}$. At low $f$, $Z_E \to R_E$, and the gain reduces by factor $(1 + g_m R_E)$. The frequency at which the gain drops by 3 dB (factor $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$) is found by setting $\vert 1 + g_m Z_E \vert = \sqrt{2}$. $$\begin{aligned} \left\vert 1 + g_m \cdot \frac{R_E}{1 + j 2\pi f R_E C_E} \right\vert^2 &= 2 \\ \left\vert \frac{1 + j 2\pi f R_E C_E + g_m R_E}{1 + j 2\pi f R_E C_E} \right\vert^2 &= 2 \end{aligned}$$ For $g_m R_E \gg 1$ (typical case), this simplifies to: $$2\pi f_{L3} R_E C_E \approx g_m R_E$$ Solving: $$\boxed{f_{L3} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_E C_E} \cdot (1 + g_m R_E) \approx \frac{g_m}{2\pi C_E} = \frac{1}{2\pi r_e C_E}}$$ The exact form with the parallel combination is: $$f_{L3} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_E \,\Vert\, r_e) C_E}$$ **Why $C_E$ dominates the low-frequency pole:** 1. **Low effective resistance:** The Thevenin resistance seen by $C_E$ is $R_E \,\Vert\, r_e$, which is small (since $r_e$ is typically $10$–$50\,\Omega$), making the RC time constant **small** and $f_{L3}$ **high**. 2. **High gain sensitivity:** The unbypassed $R_E$ introduces **strong AC negative feedback** ($1 + g_m R_E$), which becomes the dominant gain-reducing factor. 3. **Large bypass capacitor required:** To make $f_{L3}$ low enough, $C_E$ must be **10–100× larger** than the coupling capacitors ($C_E \sim 100\,\mu\text{F}$ vs $C_c \sim 10\,\mu\text{F}$). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]** **(a)** With the help of a **circuit diagram of the hybrid-π model**, explain the concept of **Miller's theorem**. Derive the expression for the **Miller input capacitance** of a CE amplifier. (7 Marks) **(b)** An RC coupled CE amplifier has the following parameters: $\beta = 120$, $I_C = 2\,\text{mA}$, $R_C = 2.2\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_S = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_1 = 33\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_L = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C_{bc} = 4\,\text{pF}$, $C_{be} = 25\,\text{pF}$, $C_{c1} = C_{c2} = 4.7\,\mu\text{F}$, $C_E = 47\,\mu\text{F}$, $V_{CC} = 15\,\text{V}$. Calculate (i) the mid-band voltage gain in dB, (ii) the lower cut-off frequency $f_L$, and (iii) the upper cut-off frequency $f_H$. (7 Marks) --- #### **Model Solution — Question B** **(a) Part (a) — 7 Marks Solution:** **Miller's Theorem Statement:** If an impedance $Z$ is connected between the input node (voltage $V_1$) and output node (voltage $V_2$) of an amplifier with voltage gain $A_v = V_2 / V_1$, then $Z$ can be replaced by two separate grounded impedances: - $Z_1 = \dfrac{Z}{1 - A_v}$ at the input - $Z_2 = \dfrac{Z \cdot A_v}{A_v - 1}$ at the output **Application to CE Amplifier:** The base–collector capacitance $C_{bc}$ is connected between input (base) and output (collector). For $A_v$ negative: $$C_{in,\text{Miller}} = C_{bc}(1 - A_v) = C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$$ $$C_{out,\text{Miller}} = C_{bc}\left(\frac{A_v - 1}{A_v}\right) \approx C_{bc}$$ **Derivation:** Current through $C_{bc}$ is $I = j \omega C_{bc}(V_1 - V_2)$. From the input side: $$I_1 = j \omega C_{bc} V_1 (1 - A_v)$$ Hence the equivalent input admittance is $Y_1 = j \omega C_{bc}(1 - A_v)$, giving $C_{in,\text{Miller}} = C_{bc}(1 - A_v)$. **(b) Part (b) — 7 Marks Solution:** **Given:** $\beta = 120$, $I_C = 2\,\text{mA}$, $V_T = 26\,\text{mV}$. $$r_e = \frac{26\,\text{mV}}{2\,\text{mA}} = 13\,\Omega$$ $$r_\pi = 120 \times 13 = 1560\,\Omega$$ **(i) Mid-band Voltage Gain:** $$R_{L,\text{ac}} = \frac{2200 \times 4700}{2200 + 4700} = \frac{10{,}340{,}000}{6900} \approx 1498.5\,\Omega$$ $$A_v = -\frac{R_{L,\text{ac}}}{r_e} = -\frac{1498.5}{13} \approx -115.3$$ $$A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(115.3) \approx 41.24\,\text{dB}$$ **(ii) Lower Cut-off Frequency:** $$R_{in} = R_1 \,\Vert\, R_2 \,\Vert\, r_\pi = 33k \,\Vert\, 10k \,\Vert\, 1.56k$$ $$= \frac{1}{\frac{1}{33000} + \frac{1}{10000} + \frac{1}{1560}} = \frac{1}{0.0000303 + 0.0001 + 0.000641}$$ $$= \frac{1}{0.0007713} \approx 1296.5\,\Omega$$ Pole 1 (input coupling): $$f_{L1} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S + R_{in}) C_{c1}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (1000 + 1296.5)(4.7 \times 10^{-6})}$$ $$= \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2296.5 \times 4.7 \times 10^{-6}} \approx \frac{1}{0.0678} \approx 14.75\,\text{Hz}$$ Pole 2 (output coupling): $$f_{L2} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_C + R_L) C_{c2}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (2200 + 4700)(4.7 \times 10^{-6})}$$ $$= \frac{1}{2\pi \times 6900 \times 4.7 \times 10^{-6}} \approx \frac{1}{0.2037} \approx 4.91\,\text{Hz}$$ Pole 3 (emitter bypass — dominant): $$R_E \,\Vert\, r_e = \frac{1000 \times 13}{1013} \approx 12.83\,\Omega$$ $$f_{L3} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 12.83 \times 47 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 6.03 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 264\,\text{Hz}$$ Overall (dominant pole approximation): $$\boxed{f_L \approx f_{L3} \approx 264\,\text{Hz}}$$ **(iii) Upper Cut-off Frequency:** Miller capacitance: $$C_M = C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_v \vert) = 4 \times 10^{-12} \times 116.3 \approx 465.2\,\text{pF}$$ Total input capacitance: $$C_{in,\text{total}} = C_{be} + C_M = 25 + 465.2 = 490.2\,\text{pF}$$ Equivalent input resistance: $$R_{eq} = R_S \,\Vert\, R_{in} = \frac{1000 \times 1296.5}{2296.5} \approx 564.6\,\Omega$$ Upper cut-off frequency: $$\boxed{f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} C_{in,\text{total}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 564.6 \times 490.2 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 575\,\text{kHz}}$$ **Bandwidth:** $\text{BW} = f_H - f_L \approx 575\,\text{kHz} - 264\,\text{Hz} \approx 575\,\text{kHz}$ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Valuation — Read Carefully!** > > 1. **Forgetting the 180° Phase Inversion Sign:** The mid-band gain of a CE amplifier is **negative** ($A_v = -g_m R_C$). Writing $A_v = +g_m R_C$ costs 1 mark. > 2. **Using $R_E$ instead of $R_E \,\Vert\, r_e$ in the $C_E$ pole formula:** A very common error. The Thevenin resistance seen by $C_E$ is the **parallel combination**, not $R_E$ alone. > 3. **Neglecting the Miller Effect in $f_H$ calculations:** Writing $C_{in} = C_{be}$ instead of $C_{be} + C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ is a fatal error that produces a wildly incorrect $f_H$. > 4. **Units Confusion:** Mixing $\mu\text{F}$ with $\text{F}$, or $\text{k}\Omega$ with $\Omega$, in the same equation. Always normalize to base units ($\text{F}$, $\Omega$, $\text{Hz}$). > 5. **Bandwidth vs Gain–Bandwidth Product (GBP):** KTU sometimes asks for GBP $= \vert A_{v(\text{mid})} \vert \cdot \text{BW}$. Do not confuse this with simple bandwidth. > 6. **Source Resistance:** Many students ignore $R_S$ when calculating $f_L$. The source resistance is **in series** with $R_{in}$, so the total input resistance is $(R_S + R_{in})$. > 7. **Plotting on Semi-Log Paper:** KTU practical exams often require drawing the **Bode plot** with frequency on a logarithmic axis. Forgetting to scale the x-axis logarithmically loses 2 marks. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must Memorize Before Exam)** - [x] **Three regions:** Low-freq (capacitors open, gain drops), Mid-band (flat gain), High-freq (Miller effect dominates). - [x] **Mid-band gain formula:** $A_v = -g_m R_{L,\text{ac}} = -R_{L,\text{ac}} / r_e$. - [x] **Lower cut-off:** $f_L$ dominated by **emitter bypass cap** $C_E$; formula uses $(R_E \,\Vert\, r_e)$. - [x] **Upper cut-off:** $f_H$ dominated by **Miller capacitance**; $C_M = C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$. - [x] **Bandwidth:** $\text{BW} = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ (since $f_H \gg f_L$). - [x] **Gain in dB:** $A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$. - [x] **Roll-off rates:** +20 dB/decade in low-freq, –20 dB/decade in high-freq (outside the band). - [x] **3 dB rule:** Cut-off frequencies are the points where gain drops by 3 dB from mid-band. - [x] **Phase:** 0° at low f → 180° at mid → 270° at high f (cumulative phase shifts). - [x] **Design trade-offs:** Larger $C_E$ lowers $f_L$ but increases physical size; lower $R_C$ extends bandwidth but reduces gain; cascode configuration breaks the Miller effect. **🎯 Final KTU Tip:** Always check whether the question asks for gain in **linear scale** or **dB**. The dB form is preferred for frequency response plots (Bode plot) but linear form is used when computing exact $A_v$ for $f_H$ derivation.

topics.title: Cascade Amplifier - Gain and Frequency Response
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL407 - ANALOG CIRCUITS AND SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Cascade Amplifier - Gain and Frequency Response

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Cascade Amplifier** is a multistage amplifier configuration in which two or more amplifier stages (typically Common Emitter, CE configurations using BJTs, or Common Source, CS configurations using FETs) are connected in series (head-to-tail) such that the **output of the first stage is capacitively coupled to the input of the second stage**. The primary objective is to multiply the overall voltage gain (or power gain) and achieve specific gain-bandwidth characteristics that a single-stage amplifier cannot provide.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (aligned with NEP 2020 and Outcome-Based Education), the cascade amplifier is studied as a canonical example of:
- **Multistage Gain Multiplication Principle**
- **Bandwidth Shrinkage Effect** (Gain-Bandwidth Trade-off)
- **Frequency Response Analysis** using mid-band, low-frequency, and high-frequency regions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A two-stage RC-coupled CE–CE cascade amplifier is the **standard experimental topology** for this module. Students must compute mid-band gain $A_M$, lower cutoff $f_L$, upper cutoff $f_H$, and bandwidth $BW = f_H - f_L$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of a **cascade amplifier like a relay race**:
- **Runner 1 (Stage 1)** picks up a small signal (the "baton") and amplifies it to a stronger level.
- He hands it over (via a **coupling capacitor**, which acts like a clean hand-off zone that blocks DC but passes the amplified AC) to **Runner 2 (Stage 2)**.
- Runner 2 amplifies it further and passes it to the output.

A single runner (single-stage amplifier) cannot run fast enough for very long. By passing the baton through multiple runners, you cover **more ground (more gain)**. However, the relay hand-off takes some time, so the overall **speed (bandwidth)** is slightly reduced.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (in Bold)

The following standard metrics govern every cascade amplifier analysis:

- **Mid-Band Voltage Gain ($A_M$):** The flat gain in the central frequency range, typically **40 dB to 60 dB** for a 2-stage CE-CE cascade.
- **Lower Cutoff Frequency ($f_L$):** The −3 dB point at low frequencies, typically **50 Hz to 300 Hz**.
- **Upper Cutoff Frequency ($f_H$):** The −3 dB point at high frequencies, typically **100 kHz to 1 MHz**.
- **Bandwidth ($BW$):** $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ (since $f_H \gg f_L$).
- **Gain-Bandwidth Product ($GBP$):** A constant for a given transistor, **GBP = $A_M \times BW$**.
- **Decibel Gain:** $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(A_v)$.
- **Standard Capacitance:** Coupling capacitor $C_C \approx$ **1 μF to 10 μF**; Emitter bypass capacitor $C_E \approx$ **47 μF to 100 μF**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Coupling Capacitors?** They block the DC bias of one stage from disturbing the DC bias of the next stage, while allowing the AC signal to flow freely. Their reactance $X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C}$ must be **negligibly small** at mid-band frequencies, making the mid-band gain independent of frequency.

---

## 1.4 Block-Level Understanding

A cascade amplifier is composed of three functional blocks per stage:

1. **Input Coupling Network:** $C_{in}$, source resistance $R_S$ — sets the **input pole** $f_{L1}$.
2. **Transistor Stage with Bypass Capacitor:** $C_E$ — sets the **emitter pole** $f_{L2}$ (dominant low-frequency pole).
3. **Output Coupling Network:** $C_{out}$, load $R_L$ — sets the **output pole** $f_{L3}$.

At high frequencies, **internal transistor capacitances** ($C_{be}$, $C_{bc}$) — modified by the **Miller Effect** — create the high-frequency poles $f_{H1}, f_{H2}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Why and How

### Step 1: Why Cascade Two Stages?
A single CE stage provides a voltage gain of approximately:
$$A_{v1} \approx -\frac{R_C \parallel R_L}{r_e}$$
This is typically **10 to 100** (20 dB to 40 dB). For many real-world applications (audio pre-amplifiers, sensor signal conditioning, communication receivers), much higher gains (**60 dB to 100 dB**) are required. A single stage cannot deliver this without sacrificing linearity, bias stability, or bandwidth.

### Step 2: How is Gain Multiplied?
The overall **mid-band voltage gain** of a cascade amplifier is the **product** of individual stage gains:
$$A_M = A_{v1} \times A_{v2} \times A_{v3} \times \ldots \times A_{vn}$$
In decibels:
$$A_{M(dB)} = A_{v1(dB)} + A_{v2(dB)} + \ldots + A_{vn(dB)}$$

### Step 3: Why Does Bandwidth Shrink?
Each stage has its own −3 dB cutoffs. When stages are cascaded, the overall gain falls off **faster** at both ends because each stage contributes a 6 dB/octave roll-off near its cutoff. The cumulative roll-off makes the new cutoffs shift inward.

### Step 4: How are Cutoff Frequencies Computed?
- **Low frequencies:** The poles add in quadrature:
$$f_L^{cascade} = \sqrt{f_{L1}^2 + f_{L2}^2 + f_{L3}^2}$$
- **High frequencies:** The poles combine reciprocally:
$$\frac{1}{(f_H^{cascade})^2} = \frac{1}{f_{H1}^2} + \frac{1}{f_{H2}^2}$$
- For **two identical stages** ($f_{L1}=f_{L2}$ and $f_{H1}=f_{H2}$):
$$f_L^{cascade} = f_{L1} \cdot \sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1} \quad ; \quad f_H^{cascade} = \frac{f_{H1}}{\sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}}$$
where $n$ is the number of identical stages. For $n=2$: $\sqrt{2^{0.5}-1} \approx 0.6436$.

### Step 5: Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Audio Amplifiers (Pre-amp stage):** Boost low-amplitude microphone signals.
- **RF/IF Amplifiers in Radio Receivers:** Provide high gain before demodulation.
- **Instrumentation Amplifiers:** Amplify tiny biomedical signals (ECG, EEG).
- **Operational Amplifier Input Stages:** Differential pairs at the front end of every op-amp.
- **Communication Base-Station Receivers:** Selective narrow-band amplification.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---------------------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Single-stage Voltage Gain (CE) | $A_{v1} = -g_m \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L)$ | Mid-band, no units |
| 2 | Transconductance | $g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{I_C(\text{mA})}{25\,\text{mV}}$ | Siemens (S) |
| 3 | Thermal Voltage | $V_T = 26\,\text{mV}$ at **300 K** | Volts |
| 4 | Input Resistance of CE stage | $R_{in} = R_B \parallel r_\pi$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 5 | $r_\pi$ (Base-emitter resistance) | $r_\pi = \frac{\beta}{g_m} = \frac{\beta V_T}{I_C}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 6 | **Overall Mid-Band Gain** | $A_M = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2}$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | **Gain in Decibels** | $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$ | Decibels (dB) |
| 8 | Lower Cutoff (single stage) | $f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S + R_{in}) C_{in}}$ (input pole) | Hertz (Hz) |
| 9 | Emitter Pole (dominant) | $f_{Le} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_E C_E}$ (approximation) | Hertz (Hz) |
| 10 | Miller Capacitance | $C_M = C_{bc}(1 - A_v) \approx C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ | Farads (F) |
| 11 | Upper Cutoff (single stage) | $f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S \parallel R_{in}) (C_{be} + C_M)}$ | Hertz (Hz) |
| 12 | **Cascade Lower Cutoff** | $f_L^{casc} = \sqrt{f_{L1}^2 + f_{L2}^2 + \ldots + f_{Ln}^2}$ | Hertz (Hz) |
| 13 | **Cascade Upper Cutoff** | $f_H^{casc} = \left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{f_{Hi}^2}\right]^{-1/2}$ | Hertz (Hz) |
| 14 | Bandwidth | $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ | Hertz (Hz) |
| 15 | Gain-Bandwidth Product | $GBP = \vert A_M \vert \cdot BW$ | Constant for a stage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Convention:** In all cascade cutoff formulas, the high-frequency formula uses the **harmonic sum** (sum of reciprocals squared) because high-frequency poles multiply the gain roll-off. The low-frequency poles are **independent** and add in quadrature because each pole independently attenuates the gain at low frequencies.

---

## 2.3 Why the Cascade Gain Formula is Multiplicative

When two non-inverting or inverting stages are connected:
- The voltage at the output of stage 1 is $V_{o1} = A_{v1} \cdot V_{i1}$.
- This becomes the input to stage 2: $V_{i2} = V_{o1}$ (assuming loading is included in $A_{v1}$ already via the effective load $R_C \parallel R_{in2}$).
- Then $V_{o2} = A_{v2} \cdot V_{i2} = A_{v2} \cdot A_{v1} \cdot V_{i1}$.
- Therefore: $\boxed{V_{o2}/V_{i1} = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2}}$.

This is the **single most important principle** of cascade amplifier analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Overall Mid-Band Voltage Gain of a Two-Stage CE-CE Cascade

### Step 1 — Define the Single-Stage CE Gain
For a single Common Emitter stage with emitter resistor **fully bypassed** by $C_E$ (mid-band condition):
$$A_{v1} = -g_{m1} \cdot R_{L1}^{eff}$$
where $R_{L1}^{eff} = R_{C1} \parallel R_{in2}$.

### Step 2 — Recognize the Inter-Stage Loading
The effective load of stage 1 is **not** just $R_{C1}$ — it must include the input resistance of stage 2 in parallel:
$$R_{L1}^{eff} = R_{C1} \parallel R_{B2} \parallel r_{\pi 2}$$
This is the **KTU examiners' favourite loading concept**.

### Step 3 — Write the Stage 2 Gain
$$A_{v2} = -g_{m2} \cdot (R_{C2} \parallel R_L)$$

### Step 4 — Multiply the Gains
The **overall mid-band voltage gain** is:
$$\boxed{A_M = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2} = \left[ g_{m1} \cdot (R_{C1} \parallel R_{B2} \parallel r_{\pi 2}) \right] \cdot \left[ g_{m2} \cdot (R_{C2} \parallel R_L) \right]}$$
The negative signs cancel because two inversions = one non-inversion.

### Step 5 — Convert to Decibels
$$A_{M(dB)} = 20 \log_{10} A_{v1} + 20 \log_{10} A_{v2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Two CE stages each with a gain of **30** give a cascade gain of **900** (≈ **59.08 dB**). One stage alone would give only **30 (≈ 29.54 dB)**. This ~30 dB jump is what makes cascading indispensable.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cascade Lower Cutoff Frequency (Quadrature Addition)

### Step 1 — Recall the Single-Stage Transfer Function
Each stage contributes one dominant low-frequency pole, $f_{Li}$. The magnitude of the gain for stage $i$ at low frequencies is:
$$\vert A_i(f) \vert = \frac{A_{Mi}}{\sqrt{1 + (f_{Li}/f)^2}}$$

### Step 2 — Write the Cascade Magnitude Response
$$\vert A(f) \vert = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{A_{Mi}}{\sqrt{1 + (f_{Li}/f)^2}}$$
$$\vert A(f) \vert^2 = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{A_{Mi}^2}{1 + (f_{Li}/f)^2}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the −3 dB Condition
At the cascade lower cutoff $f_L^{casc}$, the magnitude-squared drops by 1/2:
$$\prod_{i=1}^{n} \left[ 1 + \left(\frac{f_{Li}}{f_L^{casc}}\right)^2 \right] = 2$$

### Step 4 — Simplify Using Geometric Mean (for widely-spaced poles)
When $f_{L1}, f_{L2}, \ldots$ are not equal, the −3 dB condition yields the **quadrature sum** approximation:
$$\boxed{\left(f_L^{casc}\right)^2 \approx \left(f_{L1}\right)^2 + \left(f_{L2}\right)^2 + \ldots + \left(f_{Ln}\right)^2}$$

### Step 5 — Special Case: Two Identical Stages
When $f_{L1} = f_{L2} = f_L$:
$$2 \left[ 1 + \left(\frac{f_L}{f_L^{casc}}\right)^2 \right] = 2$$
$$\left(\frac{f_L}{f_L^{casc}}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{2^{1/n} - 1}$$
$$\boxed{f_L^{casc} = \frac{f_L}{\sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}}}$$
For $n=2$: $f_L^{casc} = \frac{f_L}{\sqrt{2^{0.5}-1}} = \frac{f_L}{0.6436} \approx 1.554\, f_L$ (the lower cutoff **increases** → bandwidth shrinks at low end).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Cascade Upper Cutoff Frequency (Harmonic Sum)

### Step 1 — Recall the High-Frequency Response of a Single Stage
$$\vert A_i(f) \vert = \frac{A_{Mi}}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_{Hi})^2}}$$

### Step 2 — Cascade Magnitude-Squared Response
$$\vert A(f) \vert^2 = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{A_{Mi}^2}{1 + (f/f_{Hi})^2}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the −3 dB Condition
$$\prod_{i=1}^{n} \left[ 1 + \left(\frac{f_H^{casc}}{f_{Hi}}\right)^2 \right] = 2$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the Cascade Upper Cutoff (Widely-Spaced Approximation)
$$\boxed{\frac{1}{(f_H^{casc})^2} \approx \frac{1}{f_{H1}^2} + \frac{1}{f_{H2}^2} + \ldots + \frac{1}{f_{Hn}^2}}$$

### Step 5 — Special Case: Two Identical Stages
$$2 \left[ 1 + \left(\frac{f_H^{casc}}{f_H}\right)^2 \right] = 2$$
$$\frac{f_H^{casc}}{f_H} = \sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}$$
$$\boxed{f_H^{casc} = f_H \cdot \sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}}$$
For $n=2$: $f_H^{casc} = f_H \cdot 0.6436 \approx 0.644\, f_H$ (the upper cutoff **decreases** → bandwidth shrinks at high end).

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Standard KTU Lab Problem)

**Given:** Two-stage CE-CE cascade amplifier with the following measured/specified values:
- $g_{m1} = g_{m2} = 40\,\text{mA/V}$
- $R_{C1} = R_{C2} = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$
- $R_{B1} = R_{B2} = 100\,\text{k}\Omega$
- $r_{\pi 1} = r_{\pi 2} = 2.5\,\text{k}\Omega$
- $R_L = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$
- $f_{L1} = f_{L2} = 100\,\text{Hz}$
- $f_{H1} = f_{H2} = 200\,\text{kHz}$

**Find:** Overall mid-band gain (in V/V and dB), cascade cutoffs, and bandwidth.

### Solution:

**Step A — Effective load of stage 1:**
$$R_{L1}^{eff} = R_{C1} \parallel R_{B2} \parallel r_{\pi 2} = 4.7\,\text{k} \parallel 100\,\text{k} \parallel 2.5\,\text{k}$$
$$= \frac{1}{1/4.7 + 1/100 + 1/2.5}\,\text{k}\Omega \approx 1.62\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step B — Stage 1 gain:**
$$A_{v1} = -g_{m1} \cdot R_{L1}^{eff} = -0.040 \cdot 1620 = -64.8$$

**Step C — Effective load of stage 2:**
$$R_{L2}^{eff} = R_{C2} \parallel R_L = 4.7\,\text{k} \parallel 4.7\,\text{k} = 2.35\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step D — Stage 2 gain:**
$$A_{v2} = -g_{m2} \cdot R_{L2}^{eff} = -0.040 \cdot 2350 = -94.0$$

**Step E — Overall gain:**
$$A_M = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2} = (-64.8) \cdot (-94.0) = 6091$$

**Step F — Gain in dB:**
$$A_{M(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(6091) \approx 75.7\,\text{dB}$$

**Step G — Cascade Lower Cutoff (two identical poles):**
$$f_L^{casc} = \frac{f_L}{\sqrt{2^{0.5}-1}} = \frac{100}{0.6436} \approx 155.4\,\text{Hz}$$

**Step H — Cascade Upper Cutoff:**
$$f_H^{casc} = f_H \cdot \sqrt{2^{0.5}-1} = 200{,}000 \cdot 0.6436 \approx 128.7\,\text{kHz}$$

**Step I — Bandwidth:**
$$BW = f_H^{casc} - f_L^{casc} \approx 128.7\,\text{kHz} - 155\,\text{Hz} \approx 128.55\,\text{kHz}$$

**Step J — Single-stage bandwidth for comparison:**
$$BW_{single} = 200\,\text{kHz} - 100\,\text{Hz} \approx 199.9\,\text{kHz}$$

**Result:** Bandwidth **shrinks by ≈ 35.6%** when two identical stages are cascaded — confirming the **Gain-Bandwidth Trade-off**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Two-Stage Cascade Amplifier

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **functional architecture and signal flow** through a two-stage CE-CE cascade amplifier — from input source to final load, including all poles and coupling nodes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Vin Signal Source with Rs]:::inputNode
    cinCouple[Coupling Capacitor Cin]:::coupleNode
    stage1In[Stage1 Input Node and Bias Network R1 R2]:::biasNode
    transistor1[Transistor Q1 CE Configuration]:::activeNode
    bypassCap[Emitter Bypass Capacitor CE1]:::coupleNode
    interCouple[Interstage Coupling Capacitor CC]:::coupleNode
    stage2In[Stage2 Input Node and Bias Network R3 R4]:::biasNode
    transistor2[Transistor Q2 CE Configuration]:::activeNode
    bypassCap2[Emitter Bypass Capacitor CE2]:::coupleNode
    coutCouple[Output Coupling Capacitor Cout]:::coupleNode
    loadNode[Load Resistor RL]:::loadNode
    gnd[Common Ground Reference]:::groundNode

    src --> cinCouple --> stage1In --> transistor1
    transistor1 --> interCouple --> stage2In --> transistor2
    transistor2 --> coutCouple --> loadNode
    bypassCap -.-> transistor1
    bypassCap2 -.-> transistor2
    stage1In --> gnd
    stage2In --> gnd
    bypassCap --> gnd
    bypassCap2 --> gnd
    src --> gnd
    loadNode --> gnd

    classDef inputNode fill:#FFD580,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef coupleNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00688B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef biasNode fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#800080,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef activeNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef loadNode fill:#F08080,stroke:#CD5C5C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef groundNode fill:#A9A9A9,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Legend (Color-Coded):**
- 🟧 **Orange Node:** Input signal source
- 🟦 **Sky-Blue Node:** Coupling / bypass capacitor (signal hand-off)
- 🟪 **Purple Node:** DC bias network (resistor divider)
- 🟩 **Green Node:** Active transistor stage (amplification)
- 🟥 **Red Node:** Load resistor (output)
- ⬜ **Gray Node:** Ground reference

---

## 4.2 Frequency Response Curve of a Two-Stage Cascade Amplifier

The following Mermaid plot is a **conceptual x-y style block diagram** illustrating the **Bode magnitude plot** of a cascade amplifier, showing how the slope steepens beyond the cutoffs.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LowFreq["LOW FREQUENCY REGION"]
        LF1[Slope 12 dB per octave roll-off]:::lfNode
        LF2[Cascade fL shifts upward to 155 Hz]:::lfNode
        LF3[Caused by Cin CE CC poles]:::lfNode
    end

    subgraph MidBand["MID BAND REGION"]
        MB1[Flat Gain Plateau at 75.7 dB]:::mbNode
        MB2[Coupling caps act as short circuits]:::mbNode
        MB3[Transistor internal caps negligible]:::mbNode
    end

    subgraph HighFreq["HIGH FREQUENCY REGION"]
        HF1[Slope 12 dB per octave roll-off]:::hfNode
        HF2[Cascade fH shifts downward to 128 kHz]:::hfNode
        HF3[Caused by Miller Cbc effect]:::hfNode
    end

    LowFreq -->|Increases| MidBand
    MidBand -->|Decreases| HighFreq

    classDef lfNode fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mbNode fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef hfNode fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#483D8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each stage contributes **6 dB/octave** (= **20 dB/decade**) of roll-off near its cutoff. A two-stage cascade therefore shows **12 dB/octave** (= **40 dB/decade**) of roll-off on either side of the mid-band plateau. This is why the response curve is **steeper** than a single-stage Bode plot.

---

## 4.3 Signal Flow and Pole/Zero Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Pole 1 at 100 Hz from Cin]:::pole --> A2[Pole 2 at 100 Hz from CE]:::pole
    A2 --> A3[Mid Band Flat Region 1 kHz to 100 kHz]:::flat
    A3 --> A4[Pole 3 at 200 kHz from Cbe and Miller Cbc of Q1]:::pole
    A4 --> A5[Pole 4 at 200 kHz from Cbe and Miller Cbc of Q2]:::pole
    A5 --> A6[Cascade Response Plateau ends and rolls off]:::flat

    classDef pole fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef flat fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Engineering Reading:**
- **Two low-frequency poles** (input and emitter bypass) at ~100 Hz each → cascade $f_L$ moves to ~155 Hz.
- **Two high-frequency poles** (Miller-multiplied $C_{bc}$) at ~200 kHz each → cascade $f_H$ moves to ~128 kHz.
- **Cascade bandwidth ≈ 128 kHz**, while single-stage bandwidth ≈ 200 kHz — a **36% reduction**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define cascade amplifier. Why are coupling capacitors used between stages?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
A **cascade amplifier** is a multistage amplifier in which two or more amplifier stages are connected in series, with the output of one stage driving the input of the next, so that the overall voltage gain equals the product of individual stage gains.

**Coupling capacitors** are used between stages for the following reasons:
1. To **block the DC bias voltage** of one stage from reaching the next stage (preventing bias-point disturbance).
2. To **allow the AC signal** to pass from one stage to the next with negligible attenuation at mid-band frequencies.
3. To provide **DC isolation** between stages, enabling each stage to be biased independently.

At mid-band, the capacitor reactance $X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C}$ becomes negligibly small, allowing the signal to pass without loss.

---

### **Question 2: State the Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP) and explain how it is affected in a cascade amplifier.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
The **Gain-Bandwidth Product** is defined as:
$$GBP = \vert A_M \vert \cdot BW$$
For a single transistor stage, the GBP is approximately **constant**.

In a cascade amplifier, when $n$ identical stages are connected:
- The overall gain $A_M$ increases by a factor of $A_v^n$.
- The overall bandwidth $BW$ decreases.
- The **product** $A_M \cdot BW$ scales as $A_v^{n-1} \cdot (GBP_{single})$.

Thus, the GBP of the cascade is **not** constant — it increases with the number of identical stages for the same transistor type, but only if the bandwidth degradation is acceptable.

---

## 📝 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Computing the cascade lower cutoff using the **harmonic sum** ($1/f_L = \sum 1/f_{Li}$) — this is **WRONG**. Low-frequency poles are independent and must be added in **quadrature**: $f_L^{casc} = \sqrt{\sum f_{Li}^2}$.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to **include the input resistance of stage 2** when computing the effective load of stage 1. This results in a gain that is **too large** — and full marks are lost.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing the **Miller capacitance** formula. The correct form is $C_M = C_{bc}(1 - A_v) \approx C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ for an inverting CE stage. Sign error → wrong $f_H$ → lose 4–5 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Reporting the **voltage gain** in V/V instead of **dB** when the question asks for both. KTU examiners deduct 2 marks for unit inconsistency.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Two-Stage CE-CE Cascade Amplifier — Full Design Analysis** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

A two-stage RC-coupled CE-CE cascade amplifier has the following specifications:
- $g_{m1} = g_{m2} = 50\,\text{mA/V}$
- $R_{C1} = R_{C2} = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$
- $R_{B1} = R_{B2} = 110\,\text{k}\Omega$
- $r_{\pi 1} = r_{\pi 2} = 2.5\,\text{k}\Omega$
- $R_L = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$
- Lower cutoff of each stage = $80\,\text{Hz}$
- Upper cutoff of each stage = $250\,\text{kHz}$

#### **(a) Compute the overall mid-band voltage gain in V/V and in dB. Also calculate the effective load seen by each stage.** **(7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Effective load of Stage 1:**
$$R_{L1}^{eff} = R_{C1} \parallel R_{B2} \parallel r_{\pi 2}$$
$$= \frac{1}{1/5 + 1/110 + 1/2.5}\,\text{k}\Omega = \frac{1}{0.2 + 0.00909 + 0.4}\,\text{k}\Omega$$
$$= \frac{1}{0.60909}\,\text{k}\Omega \approx 1.642\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 2 — Stage 1 gain:**
$$A_{v1} = -g_{m1} \cdot R_{L1}^{eff} = -0.050 \cdot 1642 = -82.1$$

**Step 3 — Effective load of Stage 2:**
$$R_{L2}^{eff} = R_{C2} \parallel R_L = \frac{5 \cdot 5}{5 + 5} = 2.5\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Stage 2 gain:**
$$A_{v2} = -g_{m2} \cdot R_{L2}^{eff} = -0.050 \cdot 2500 = -125$$

**Step 5 — Overall mid-band gain:**
$$A_M = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2} = (-82.1) \cdot (-125) = 10{,}262.5$$

**Step 6 — Gain in dB:**
$$A_{M(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(10{,}262.5) = 20 \cdot 4.0112 = 80.22\,\text{dB}$$

✅ **Answer:** $\boxed{A_M = 10{,}262.5\,\text{V/V} \approx 80.22\,\text{dB}}$

#### **(b) Compute the cascade lower cutoff, upper cutoff, and bandwidth. Comment on the gain-bandwidth trade-off.** **(7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Cascade Lower Cutoff (two identical LF poles):**
$$f_L^{casc} = \frac{f_L}{\sqrt{2^{1/2}-1}} = \frac{80}{0.6436} \approx 124.3\,\text{Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Cascade Upper Cutoff (two identical HF poles):**
$$f_H^{casc} = f_H \cdot \sqrt{2^{1/2}-1} = 250{,}000 \cdot 0.6436 \approx 160.9\,\text{kHz}$$

**Step 3 — Cascade Bandwidth:**
$$BW^{casc} = f_H^{casc} - f_L^{casc} = 160{,}900 - 124.3 \approx 160.78\,\text{kHz}$$

**Step 4 — Single-stage bandwidth:**
$$BW_{single} = 250{,}000 - 80 \approx 249.92\,\text{kHz}$$

**Step 5 — Bandwidth shrinkage:**
$$\%\text{shrinkage} = \frac{249.92 - 160.78}{249.92} \cdot 100 \approx 35.66\%$$

**Step 6 — Gain-Bandwidth Trade-off comment:**
The mid-band gain has increased from $\approx 82.1$ (single stage) to $\approx 10{,}262$ (cascade) — a **125× increase**. However, the bandwidth has decreased from ≈ 250 kHz to ≈ 161 kHz — a **35.7% reduction**. This clearly demonstrates the **fundamental gain-bandwidth trade-off**: you cannot simultaneously increase gain and bandwidth indefinitely when cascading amplifier stages.

✅ **Answer:** $\boxed{f_L^{casc} \approx 124.3\,\text{Hz},\quad f_H^{casc} \approx 160.9\,\text{kHz},\quad BW^{casc} \approx 160.78\,\text{kHz}}$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Alternative — Frequency Response and Miller Effect Analysis** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

A two-stage RC-coupled cascade amplifier has the following parameters:
- Each stage: $g_m = 40\,\text{mA/V}$, $C_{bc} = 5\,\text{pF}$, $C_{be} = 20\,\text{pF}$
- Source resistance $R_S = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_{in} = 2\,\text{k}\Omega$, mid-band voltage gain per stage $\vert A_{v1} \vert = \vert A_{v2} \vert = 50$
- Coupling capacitor $C_C = 1\,\mu\text{F}$, emitter capacitor $C_E = 100\,\mu\text{F}$, $R_E = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$

#### **(a) Compute the Miller capacitance and the upper cutoff frequency of a single stage. Also compute the upper cutoff of the cascade.** **(7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Miller Multiplied Capacitance:**
For an inverting CE stage ($A_v$ is negative):
$$C_M = C_{bc}(1 - A_v) = C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_v \vert) = 5 \cdot (1 + 50) = 5 \cdot 51 = 255\,\text{pF}$$

**Step 2 — Total input capacitance:**
$$C_{in}^{total} = C_{be} + C_M = 20 + 255 = 275\,\text{pF}$$

**Step 3 — Source resistance seen at input:**
$$R_{eq} = R_S \parallel R_{in} = \frac{1 \cdot 2}{1 + 2} = 0.667\,\text{k}\Omega = 667\,\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Single-stage upper cutoff:**
$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} C_{in}^{total}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 667 \cdot 275 \cdot 10^{-12}}$$
$$f_H = \frac{1}{1.152 \cdot 10^{-6}} \approx 868\,\text{kHz}$$

**Step 5 — Cascade upper cutoff (two identical stages):**
$$f_H^{casc} = f_H \cdot \sqrt{2^{1/2}-1} = 868{,}000 \cdot 0.6436 \approx 558.6\,\text{kHz}$$

✅ **Answer:** $\boxed{C_M = 255\,\text{pF},\quad f_H \approx 868\,\text{kHz},\quad f_H^{casc} \approx 558.6\,\text{kHz}}$

#### **(b) Compute the lower cutoff frequency of a single stage due to the emitter bypass capacitor, and the cascade lower cutoff. Comment on the overall frequency response.** **(7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Emitter pole of a single stage (approximation):**
$$f_{Le} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_E C_E} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 1000 \cdot 100 \cdot 10^{-6}}$$
$$f_{Le} = \frac{1}{0.0628} \approx 15.92\,\text{Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Input pole of a single stage:**
$$f_{Lin} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S + R_{in}) C_C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 3000 \cdot 1 \cdot 10^{-6}}$$
$$f_{Lin} = \frac{1}{0.01885} \approx 53.05\,\text{Hz}$$

**Step 3 — Dominant single-stage lower cutoff (input pole, since $f_{Lin} > f_{Le}$):**
$$f_{L1} \approx 53\,\text{Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Assuming both stages have similar LF poles ($f_{L1} = f_{L2} \approx 53\,\text{Hz}$):**
$$f_L^{casc} = \frac{f_L}{\sqrt{2^{1/2}-1}} = \frac{53}{0.6436} \approx 82.3\,\text{Hz}$$

**Step 5 — Overall frequency response comment:**
- **Mid-band gain** = $50 \times 50 = 2500$ V/V ≈ **67.96 dB**
- **Cascade lower cutoff** ≈ 82.3 Hz (a bit higher than single-stage)
- **Cascade upper cutoff** ≈ 558.6 kHz (much lower than single-stage 868 kHz)
- **Cascade bandwidth** ≈ 558.5 kHz (35.6% narrower than single-stage)

The cascade amplifier provides significantly more gain (≈ 2500×) at the cost of a moderately narrower bandwidth, validating the **gain-bandwidth trade-off** in multistage amplifier design.

✅ **Answer:** $\boxed{f_{Le} \approx 15.92\,\text{Hz},\quad f_{Lin} \approx 53.05\,\text{Hz},\quad f_L^{casc} \approx 82.3\,\text{Hz}}$

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rapid-Revision Checklist — Cascade Amplifier: Gain and Frequency Response**

### ✅ Key Concepts
- A **cascade amplifier** multiplies gains of individual stages: $A_M = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2} \cdot \ldots \cdot A_{vn}$.
- Two CE stages cascaded = **non-inverting** (two inversions cancel out).
- **Coupling capacitor** blocks DC, passes AC; essential for inter-stage DC isolation.
- **Emitter bypass capacitor** $C_E$ shorts $R_E$ at mid-band to maximize gain.

### ✅ Critical Formulas (Must Memorize)
- $A_M = \prod A_{vi}$, $A_{M(dB)} = \sum A_{vi(dB)}$
- $C_M = C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ — **Miller Multiplication**
- $f_L^{casc} = \sqrt{\sum f_{Li}^2}$ — **Quadrature sum**
- $f_H^{casc} = \left[\sum 1/f_{Hi}^2\right]^{-1/2}$ — **Harmonic sum**
- For **two identical stages**: $f_L$ shifts up by factor **1.554**, $f_H$ shifts down by factor **0.644**.

### ✅ Standard Parameter Values
- $V_T = 26\,\text{mV}$ at 300 K
- $g_m = I_C / 26\,\text{mV}$ (when $I_C$ is in mA)
- Typical $f_L \approx 50$–$300\,\text{Hz}$, $f_H \approx 100\,\text{kHz}$–$1\,\text{MHz}$

### ✅ KTU Examiner's Hot-Spots (Lose Marks Here!)
1. ❌ Forgetting **inter-stage loading** ($R_{in2}$ in parallel with $R_{C1}$).
2. ❌ Using harmonic sum for low-frequency poles (use **quadrature sum**).
3. ❌ Wrong sign in Miller formula (use $1 + \vert A_v \vert$ for CE).
4. ❌ Mixing up V/V and dB units.

### ✅ Engineering Real-World Applications
- 🎙️ Audio pre-amplifiers and public-address systems.
- 📡 RF and IF amplifier stages in superheterodyne radio receivers.
- 🏥 Biomedical signal amplifiers (ECG, EEG, EMG).
- 📶 Communication base-station front-ends.
- 🔬 Instrumentation amplifier front-stages in measurement systems.

> **Final Takeaway:** Cascading amplifier stages is the most fundamental technique to achieve high gain — but always remember the **Gain-Bandwidth Trade-off**: more gain ⇒ narrower bandwidth, always!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MOSFET Amplifier (CS - Frequency Response Characteristics)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAEL407 - ANALOG CIRCUITS AND SIMULATION LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** MOSFET Amplifier (CS - Frequency Response Characteristics) # MOSFET Amplifier (CS – Frequency Response Characteristics) ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Common Source (CS) MOSFET Amplifier** is a small-signal analog amplifier configuration in which the **input signal is applied between the Gate and Source terminals**, and the **amplified output is taken between the Drain and Source terminals** of a Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor (MOSFET). The source terminal is therefore *common* to both the input port and the output port, hence the name. **Frequency Response Characteristics** refer to the quantitative behaviour of the amplifier's voltage gain ($A_v$), input impedance ($Z_{in}$), and output impedance ($Z_{out}$) as a function of the input signal frequency, typically plotted as a **Bode magnitude plot** (gain in dB vs. frequency on a logarithmic scale) and a **Bode phase plot** (phase angle vs. frequency). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment is concerned with the *complete three-region response* — the **low-frequency region (LF)**, the **mid-band region (MB)**, and the **high-frequency region (HF)**. The student is expected to identify the three cutoff frequencies $f_{L1}, f_{L2}, \dots, f_{L(overall)}$ and $f_H$, and compute the **bandwidth (BW)** as $BW = f_H - f_{L(overall)}$. ## 1.2 Symbolic Conventions Used in This Module - $g_m$ — Transconductance of the MOSFET (Siemens, S) - $r_o$ — Output resistance of the MOSFET (Ohms, $\Omega$) - $R_D, R_S$ — Drain and Source bias resistors - $R_G$ — Gate bias resistor - $R_{sig}$ — Source (signal generator) resistance - $C_{in}, C_S, C_{out}$ — Coupling and bypass capacitors - $C_{gs}, C_{gd}$ — Intrinsic MOSFET capacitances - $f_L$ — Lower cutoff frequency (Hz) - $f_H$ — Upper cutoff frequency (Hz) - $BW$ — Bandwidth of the amplifier ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Window Amplifier" Intuition Imagine a **radio tuning dial** that lets only a specific *band* of sound frequencies pass through clearly: - **Below the dial's lower limit** → bass is muffled (low-frequency attenuation by coupling/bypass capacitors). - **Above the dial's upper limit** → treble becomes fuzzy (high-frequency roll-off by internal MOSFET capacitances). - **Between the two limits** → the "sweet spot" where the music sounds perfect (the **mid-band**). The CS amplifier behaves exactly like this radio. The **mid-band** is the region where every reactive component is "transparent" — coupling capacitors act as **short circuits** and internal MOSFET capacitances act as **open circuits**. The gain is flat, predictable, and equal to $-g_m R_D$ (the negative sign indicates a 180° phase inversion). > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The Common Source amplifier is the *transistor-level equivalent of the inverting operational amplifier topology* — it inverts the signal and provides high voltage gain, but has moderate input impedance and relatively high output impedance when compared to a source follower. ## 1.4 Practical Engineering Utility CS amplifiers are the **workhorse of analogue integrated circuit design**. They form the basic gain stage inside virtually every op-amp, audio pre-amplifier, RF front-end LNA, mixer, and switched-capacitor filter. Characterising the **frequency response** is critical because the amplifier must operate over a defined bandwidth (e.g., audio = 20 Hz – 20 kHz, video = DC – 4.5 MHz) without distortion. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Frequency Regions — A Structural Overview The small-signal gain of the CS amplifier is a **complex, frequency-dependent function**. It can be divided into three regions, each dominated by a different physical mechanism: | Region | Frequency Range | Dominant Reactive Element | Behaviour of Gain | |---|---|---|---| | **Low-Frequency (LF)** | $f < f_L$ | External coupling & bypass capacitors $C_{in}, C_S, C_{out}$ | Gain rolls off at **+20 dB/decade** per pole | | **Mid-Band (MB)** | $f_L \le f \le f_H$ | All capacitors are "ideal" — coupling C's → short, internal C's → open | Gain is **flat** at maximum value $A_{v(MB)}$ | | **High-Frequency (HF)** | $f > f_H$ | Internal MOSFET capacitances $C_{gs}, C_{gd}$ (Miller effect) | Gain rolls off at **−20 dB/decade** per pole | ## 2.2 The Common Source Amplifier — Circuit Topology The standard CS amplifier with biasing, coupling, and bypass capacitors is shown schematically in Section 4. The AC small-signal equivalent circuit replaces the MOSFET with its hybrid-π model: - A voltage-controlled current source $g_m v_{gs}$ between Drain and Source - An output resistance $r_o$ in parallel - The capacitances $C_{gs}$ (Gate–Source) and $C_{gd}$ (Gate–Drain) appear as physical device parasitics ## 2.3 Mid-Band Analysis (Why it is the "Ideal" Region) In the **mid-band**: - The reactance of all coupling/bypass capacitors is negligible → they behave as **short circuits**. - The reactance of all internal MOSFET capacitances is extremely high → they behave as **open circuits**. Therefore, the small-signal model collapses to its simplest form, and the **mid-band voltage gain** becomes: $$A_{v(MB)} = -g_m (R_D \Vert r_o)$$ The negative sign indicates the **180° phase inversion** between input and output — a defining feature of the CS topology. ## 2.4 Low-Frequency Analysis (The Effect of Coupling Capacitors) At low frequencies, the **reactance** of $C_{in}$, $C_S$, and $C_{out}$ becomes significant. Each capacitor, together with the Thévenin resistance it "sees", forms a **high-pass RC filter** that attenuates low frequencies. For a simple RC high-pass network, the lower cutoff (corner) frequency is: $$f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} C}$$ Each coupling/bypass capacitor contributes one pole (one cutoff frequency). The **overall lower cutoff** is *not* the sum of the individual cutoffs; instead, it must be calculated by adding the **individual decibel losses** at any reference frequency and finding the −3 dB point, or by using the approximation: $$f_{L(overall)} \approx \sqrt{f_{L1}^2 + f_{L2}^2 + f_{L3}^2 + \dots}$$ For two dominant poles, a widely used short-cut is $f_L \approx 0.707 \sqrt{f_{L1}^2 + f_{L2}^2}$, but the **−3 dB summation method** is the academically correct one. ## 2.5 High-Frequency Analysis (Miller's Theorem and Internal Capacitances) At high frequencies, the internal capacitances $C_{gs}$ and $C_{gd}$ become low-impedance paths. The most significant effect is the **Miller multiplication** of $C_{gd}$. By **Miller's Theorem**, a capacitance $C_{gd}$ connected between Gate and Drain appears at the input side as: $$C_{M1} = C_{gd}(1 - A_v)$$ Since $A_v$ is large and negative for the CS stage, $(1 - A_v) \approx \vert A_v \vert + 1$, so the input sees a **multiplied capacitance** that is orders of magnitude larger than the physical $C_{gd}$. The total input capacitance becomes: $$C_{in(total)} = C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 - A_v)$$ Applying the short-circuit time-constant technique, the **upper cutoff frequency** is: $$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R'_{sig} C_{in(total)}}$$ where $R'_{sig}$ is the Thévenin resistance seen by the input capacitance network. ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **The following table is the authoritative "cheat sheet" for KTU 2024 valuation of this experiment. Memorise every entry.** | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | DC Drain Current (saturation) | $I_{DQ} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$ | A | Operating point current | | 2 | Transconductance | $g_m = \frac{2 I_{DQ}}{V_{GS} - V_{th}} = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} I_{DQ}}$ | S (A/V) | Voltage-to-current gain of MOSFET | | 3 | Output resistance | $r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_{DQ}}$ | $\Omega$ | Channel-length modulation effect | | 4 | Mid-band voltage gain | $A_{v(MB)} = -g_m (R_D \Vert r_o)$ | dimensionless | Flat-region gain | | 5 | Mid-band gain in dB | $A_{v(dB)} = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_{v(MB)} \vert$ | dB | Bode plot Y-axis value | | 6 | Lower cutoff (single pole) | $f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} C}$ | Hz | Corner of high-pass filter | | 7 | Lower cutoff for $C_{in}$ | $f_{L(C_{in})} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_{sig} + R_G) C_{in}}$ | Hz | Input coupling pole | | 8 | Lower cutoff for $C_S$ | $f_{L(C_S)} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S \Vert \frac{1}{g_m}) C_S}$ | Hz | Source bypass pole | | 9 | Lower cutoff for $C_{out}$ | $f_{L(C_{out})} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_D \Vert r_o + R_L) C_{out}}$ | Hz | Output coupling pole | | 10 | Miller input capacitance | $C_{M} = C_{gd} (1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ | F | Miller-multiplied $C_{gd}$ | | 11 | Total input capacitance (HF) | $C_{in,total} = C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ | F | Total HF input pole | | 12 | Upper cutoff frequency | $f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{sig,eq} C_{in,total}}$ | Hz | Beginning of HF roll-off | | 13 | Bandwidth | $BW = f_H - f_{L(overall)}$ | Hz | Useful frequency range | | 14 | Gain-Bandwidth Product | $GBP = \vert A_{v(MB)} \vert \cdot BW$ | Hz | Figure of merit | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL: Do not use the pipe character `|` inside table cells** — it breaks the markdown. The notation `\vert` and `\Vert` is used above in the source LaTeX to keep the table safe. When transcribing these formulas in your answer script, use $\mid$ or $\vert$ explicitly. ## 2.7 The "Why" Behind Each Step — Engineering Logic - **Why does $f_L$ exist?** Because real capacitors cannot pass DC and low-frequency AC efficiently; they form a series impedance that divides the signal voltage. - **Why does $f_H$ exist?** Because real MOSFETs have physical gate-oxide and junction capacitances that shunt high-frequency signals to ground. - **Why is the mid-band "flat"?** Because the frequency is high enough that the coupling capacitors are "transparent" (low impedance), but low enough that the internal capacitances are also "transparent" (high impedance). - **Why is Miller's effect so important?** Because $C_{gd}$ sits across a high-gain inverting stage, so it gets reflected to the input as a *much larger* effective capacitance, drastically reducing $f_H$. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mid-Band Voltage Gain of the CS Amplifier **Step 1:** Start with the small-signal equivalent circuit of the CS amplifier in the mid-band region. Replace all capacitors by their mid-band idealisations: - $C_{in}, C_S, C_{out}$ → **short circuits** - $C_{gs}, C_{gd}$ → **open circuits** **Step 2:** The simplified circuit consists of: - A signal source $v_{sig}$ in series with $R_{sig}$ - A gate resistor $R_G$ to ground - A controlled current source $g_m v_{gs}$ flowing from Drain to Source - $R_D$ in parallel with $r_o$ at the Drain node - The output is taken at the Drain node **Step 3:** Since the gate draws **no DC current** (ideal MOSFET), no current flows through $R_G$ in the mid-band AC analysis. Hence, the entire signal source voltage appears across the Gate–Source junction: $$v_{gs} = v_{sig}$$ **Step 4:** Apply KCL at the Drain node. The output voltage is the current times the equivalent load resistance: $$v_{out} = -(g_m v_{gs})(R_D \Vert r_o)$$ **Step 5:** Substitute $v_{gs} = v_{sig}$: $$v_{out} = -g_m (R_D \Vert r_o) \, v_{sig}$$ **Step 6:** The voltage gain is defined as $A_v = v_{out}/v_{in}$. Since the input to the amplifier itself is $v_{gs}$ (not $v_{sig}$), the **source-to-output gain** is: $$\frac{v_{out}}{v_{sig}} = -g_m (R_D \Vert r_o)$$ And the **overall gain from the signal source** (including the voltage divider formed by $R_{sig}$ and $R_G$) is: $$A_{v(sig)} = -g_m (R_D \Vert r_o) \cdot \frac{R_G}{R_{sig} + R_G}$$ **Numerical Example:** Suppose $g_m = 5$ mS, $R_D = 4.7$ k$\Omega$, $r_o = 50$ k$\Omega$, $R_G = 1$ M$\Omega$, $R_{sig} = 50$ $\Omega$. $$R_D \Vert r_o = \frac{4700 \times 50000}{4700 + 50000} = \frac{235,000,000}{54,700} \approx 4296 \, \Omega$$ $$A_{v(MB)} = -(0.005)(4296) = -21.48 \approx -21.5 \, \text{V/V}$$ In decibels: $$A_{v(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(21.5) \approx 26.6 \, \text{dB}$$ The negative sign simply denotes the 180° phase shift. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Lower Cutoff Frequency for the Input Coupling Capacitor **Step 1:** Identify the Thévenin resistance seen by $C_{in}$. Looking into the terminals of $C_{in}$: - On the source side → $R_{sig}$ - On the amplifier side → $R_G$ (since the gate is essentially open-circuit at low frequencies) **Step 2:** Therefore: $$R_{eq(C_{in})} = R_{sig} + R_G$$ **Step 3:** The capacitor $C_{in}$ together with $R_{eq(C_{in})}$ forms a first-order high-pass filter. Its cutoff is: $$f_{L(C_{in})} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_{sig} + R_G) C_{in}}$$ **Step 4:** Numerical example — let $R_{sig} = 50 \, \Omega$, $R_G = 1 \, \text{M}\Omega$, $C_{in} = 1 \, \mu\text{F}$: $$f_{L(C_{in})} = \frac{1}{2\pi (50 + 1,000,000)(1 \times 10^{-6})}$$ $$= \frac{1}{2\pi (1,000,050)(10^{-6})} = \frac{1}{6.2832 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$f_{L(C_{in})} \approx 159.15 \, \text{Hz}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Lower Cutoff Frequency for the Source Bypass Capacitor **Step 1:** Looking into the Source node, the resistance "seen" by $C_S$ is the **parallel combination** of $R_S$ and the small-signal source resistance of the MOSFET, which equals $1/g_m$ (this is the resistance looking into the Source of a common-source amplifier with the gate AC-grounded). $$R_{eq(C_S)} = R_S \Vert \frac{1}{g_m}$$ **Step 2:** The cutoff frequency contributed by $C_S$ is: $$f_{L(C_S)} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S \Vert \frac{1}{g_m}) C_S}$$ **Step 3:** Why $1/g_m$? In the small-signal model, if we set $v_{gs} = -v_s$ (because the gate is grounded for AC), the current through the source is $i_s = -g_m v_{gs} = g_m v_s$, so the resistance is $v_s / i_s = 1/g_m$. **Step 4:** Numerical example — let $R_S = 1 \, \text{k}\Omega$, $g_m = 5$ mS, $C_S = 100 \, \mu\text{F}$: $$\frac{1}{g_m} = \frac{1}{0.005} = 200 \, \Omega$$ $$R_S \Vert \frac{1}{g_m} = \frac{1000 \times 200}{1000 + 200} = \frac{200,000}{1200} \approx 166.67 \, \Omega$$ $$f_{L(C_S)} = \frac{1}{2\pi (166.67)(100 \times 10^{-6})} = \frac{1}{2\pi (0.01667)} = \frac{1}{0.1047} \approx 9.55 \, \text{Hz}$$ ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Overall Lower Cutoff Frequency (The "−3 dB Method") **The −3 dB Summation Method** is the academically correct way to combine multiple low-frequency poles: **Step 1:** Identify the individual lower cutoff frequencies $f_{L1}, f_{L2}, f_{L3}, \dots$ **Step 2:** For each pole, calculate the gain loss in dB at any chosen reference frequency $f_0$ (usually taken as the dominant pole — the highest one). **Step 3:** Use the formula: $$\text{Loss}_{dB}(f) = -10 \log_{10}\left[1 + \left(\frac{f}{f_L}\right)^2\right]$$ For each pole, and add them up. **Step 4:** The **overall lower cutoff** $f_{L(overall)}$ is the frequency at which the total loss equals −3 dB. **Approximate Formula (for well-separated poles):** $$f_{L(overall)} \approx 1.1 \sqrt{f_{L1}^2 + f_{L2}^2 + f_{L3}^2}$$ The factor 1.1 is a correction for non-ideal pole interaction. **Numerical Example — Two Dominant Poles:** $f_{L1} = 159$ Hz (from $C_{in}$), $f_{L2} = 9.55$ Hz (from $C_S$). $$f_{L(overall)} \approx 1.1 \sqrt{159^2 + 9.55^2} = 1.1 \sqrt{25281 + 91.2} = 1.1 \sqrt{25372.2}$$ $$= 1.1 \times 159.29 = 175.2 \, \text{Hz}$$ The dominant pole is the input coupling capacitor — it dictates the overall lower cutoff. ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Upper Cutoff Frequency using Miller's Theorem **Step 1:** Identify the intrinsic MOSFET capacitances. From the datasheet, a typical small-signal MOSFET has $C_{gs} = 5$ pF, $C_{gd} = 1$ pF. **Step 2:** Apply Miller's Theorem to $C_{gd}$. The Miller-multiplied input-side capacitance is: $$C_{M,in} = C_{gd} (1 - A_v) = C_{gd} (1 + \vert A_v \vert)$$ (We use $1 + \vert A_v \vert$ because $A_v$ is negative for the CS amplifier.) **Step 3:** With $A_v = -21.5$: $$C_{M,in} = (1 \text{ pF}) (1 + 21.5) = 22.5 \, \text{pF}$$ **Step 4:** Total input capacitance: $$C_{in,total} = C_{gs} + C_{M,in} = 5 + 22.5 = 27.5 \, \text{pF}$$ **Step 5:** The Thévenin resistance seen at the input is: $$R_{sig,eq} = R_{sig} \Vert R_G = \frac{50 \times 1,000,000}{50 + 1,000,000} \approx 50 \, \Omega$$ **Step 6:** Upper cutoff frequency: $$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{sig,eq} C_{in,total}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (50)(27.5 \times 10^{-12})}$$ $$= \frac{1}{2\pi (1.375 \times 10^{-9})} = \frac{1}{8.64 \times 10^{-9}}$$ $$f_H \approx 115.7 \, \text{MHz}$$ ## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Bandwidth and Gain-Bandwidth Product **Step 1:** Bandwidth is the difference between upper and lower cutoffs. Since $f_H \gg f_{L(overall)}$: $$BW = f_H - f_{L(overall)} \approx 115.7 \times 10^6 - 175.2 \approx 115.7 \, \text{MHz}$$ **Step 2:** Gain-Bandwidth Product: $$GBP = \vert A_{v(MB)} \vert \cdot BW = 21.5 \times 115.7 \times 10^6 \approx 2.49 \times 10^9 \, \text{Hz} = 2.49 \, \text{GHz}$$ ## 3.7 Comprehensive Numerical Worked Example (Complete) > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the canonical KTU 2024 lab calculation format — practise writing it in this exact sequence in your record.** **Given:** CS amplifier with $R_{sig} = 50 \, \Omega$, $R_G = 1 \, \text{M}\Omega$, $R_D = 4.7 \, \text{k}\Omega$, $R_S = 1 \, \text{k}\Omega$, $C_{in} = 1 \, \mu\text{F}$, $C_S = 100 \, \mu\text{F}$, $C_{out} = 1 \, \mu\text{F}$, $C_L = \infty$ (direct load), $V_{DD} = 12$ V, $I_{DQ} = 2.5$ mA, $V_{th} = 2$ V, $\lambda = 0.02$ V$^{-1}$, MOSFET has $C_{gs} = 5$ pF, $C_{gd} = 1$ pF. **Step A — Operating Point Parameters:** $$g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} I_{DQ}} = \frac{2 I_{DQ}}{V_{GS} - V_{th}} = \frac{2 (2.5 \times 10^{-3})}{V_{GS} - 2}$$ For $V_{GS} = 4$ V: $$g_m = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 2.5 \, \text{mS}$$ $$r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_{DQ}} = \frac{1}{0.02 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 20 \, \text{k}\Omega$$ **Step B — Mid-Band Gain:** $$R_D \Vert r_o = \frac{4700 \times 20000}{4700 + 20000} = \frac{94,000,000}{24,700} \approx 3805.7 \, \Omega$$ $$A_{v(MB)} = -(0.0025)(3805.7) = -9.51 \, \text{V/V}$$ $$A_{v(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(9.51) = 19.57 \, \text{dB}$$ **Step C — Lower Cutoffs:** $$f_{L(C_{in})} = \frac{1}{2\pi (50 + 10^6)(10^{-6})} \approx 159.2 \, \text{Hz}$$ $$f_{L(C_S)} = \frac{1}{2\pi (1000 \Vert 400)(100 \times 10^{-6})} = \frac{1}{2\pi (285.7)(10^{-4})} = \frac{1}{0.1795} \approx 5.57 \, \text{Hz}$$ $$f_{L(C_{out})} = \frac{1}{2\pi (3805.7 + 0)(10^{-6})} = \frac{1}{2\pi (3805.7)(10^{-6})} = \frac{1}{0.0239} \approx 41.8 \, \text{Hz}$$ **Step D — Overall Lower Cutoff (dominant pole = 159.2 Hz):** $$f_{L(overall)} \approx 159.2 \, \text{Hz} \cdot 1.1 \approx 175.1 \, \text{Hz}$$ **Step E — Upper Cutoff:** $$C_{in,total} = 5 + 1(1 + 9.51) = 5 + 10.51 = 15.51 \, \text{pF}$$ $$R_{sig,eq} = 50 \Vert 10^6 \approx 50 \, \Omega$$ $$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi (50)(15.51 \times 10^{-12})} = \frac{1}{4.872 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 205.3 \, \text{MHz}$$ **Step F — Bandwidth and GBP:** $$BW \approx 205.3 \, \text{MHz}$$ $$GBP = 9.51 \times 205.3 \times 10^6 \approx 1.95 \, \text{GHz}$$ # 4. Each block has its own dominant mechanism, and the **mid-band block** is sandwiched between the low-frequency high-pass filter and the high-frequency low-pass filter. ```mermaid flowchart LR Vin["Input
Signal"]:::src HPF["HIGH-PASS
FILTER
fl"]:::filter MBA["MID-BAND
AMPLIFIER
Av MB"]:::amp LPF["LOW-PASS
FILTER
fH"]:::filter Vout["Vout"]:::src Vin --> HPF --> MBA --> LPF --> Vout classDef src fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px classDef filter fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px classDef amp fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Bode Plot — Typical CS Amplifier Frequency Response The Bode magnitude plot of a CS amplifier is a **band-pass** characteristic. Below is the textual description of the curve, with key features annotated. ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Gain in dB"]:::axis B["20 log Av MB flat region"]:::label C["+20 dB/decade slope low-frequency region"]:::label D["-20 dB/decade slope high-frequency region"]:::label E["fL overall lower cutoff"]:::label F["fH upper cutoff"]:::label G["Bandwidth BW = fH - fL"]:::label H["Frequency in Hz log scale"]:::axis I["-3 dB drop at fL"]:::label J["-3 dB drop at fH"]:::label classDef axis fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px classDef label fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` **Reading the Bode Plot:** - **Far left (very low $f$):** Gain rises at +20 dB/decade (one pole) until it reaches the mid-band plateau. - **Centre (mid-band):** Gain is **flat** at $20 \log_{10}(\vert A_{v(MB)} \vert)$. - **Far right (high $f$):** Gain falls at −20 dB/decade from $f_H$ onwards. - **−3 dB points:** $f_{L(overall)}$ on the left, $f_H$ on the right. ## 4.4 Small-Signal Hybrid-π Model (For High-Frequency Analysis) ```mermaid flowchart TB G1["Gate

KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define the term "frequency response" of an amplifier and explain the three regions in which it is divided.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The frequency response of an amplifier is the variation of its voltage gain (magnitude and phase) as a function of the input signal frequency, typically represented by a Bode plot. It is divided into three regions: 1. **Low-frequency region** ($f < f_{L(overall)}$): Coupling and bypass capacitors cause the gain to roll off at +20 dB/decade per pole. 2. **Mid-band region** ($f_{L(overall)} \le f \le f_H$): All coupling capacitors act as short circuits and internal MOSFET capacitances act as open circuits, giving a **flat maximum gain** of $A_{v(MB)} = -g_m (R_D \Vert r_o)$. 3. **High-frequency region** ($f > f_H$): Internal MOSFET capacitances $C_{gs}$ and $C_{gd}$ (Miller-multiplied) cause the gain to roll off at −20 dB/decade. > [!NOTE] > **Valuation Tip:** Mentioning the *exact slope per decade* (+20 dB/decade) and the *Miller effect* in the HF region earns the third mark. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **What is the Miller effect and how does it limit the high-frequency response of a CS amplifier?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Miller effect is the phenomenon by which an impedance connected between the input and output of an inverting amplifier appears "multiplied" when viewed from either side. For a CS amplifier with voltage gain $A_v$ (negative), a capacitance $C_{gd}$ between Gate and Drain appears at the input as: $$C_{M,in} = C_{gd}(1 - A_v) = C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$$ Since $\vert A_v \vert$ is large, the input sees a **much larger effective capacitance** than the physical $C_{gd}$. This large capacitance, in conjunction with the source resistance $R_{sig}$, forms a low-pass filter that significantly **lowers the upper cutoff frequency** $f_H$, thereby limiting the high-frequency response. > [!NOTE] > **Valuation Tip:** Drawing the equivalent Miller circuit with $C_{M,in}$ and $C_{M,out}$ side-by-side and giving the upper-cutoff formula fetches full marks. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Rule:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as 7 + 7 (or 5 + 9 in some cases). The student attempts **one full question** from the internal choice pair. ### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **Common Source MOSFET amplifier** using voltage-divider biasing. Derive the expression for its mid-band voltage gain. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For the CS amplifier, derive the expressions for the **lower cutoff frequencies** contributed by the input coupling capacitor and the source bypass capacitor. Explain the procedure to find the overall lower cutoff frequency. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Operation and Mid-band Gain Derivation (7 marks):** A CS amplifier uses a MOSFET biased in the saturation region. The drain current is set by the gate-source voltage $V_{GS}$ (controlled by the voltage divider $R_1, R_2$), and the drain resistor $R_D$ converts the current variation into an output voltage variation. - **Input:** Applied at the Gate through coupling capacitor $C_{in}$. - **Output:** Taken at the Drain through coupling capacitor $C_{out}$. - **Source:** AC-grounded by bypass capacitor $C_S$. When $v_{gs}$ increases, $i_d = g_m v_{gs}$ increases, causing the voltage drop across $R_D$ to increase, which makes $v_{out}$ **decrease**. This is the 180° phase inversion. **Mid-band gain derivation** (already covered in Section 3.1): $$A_{v(MB)} = -g_m (R_D \Vert r_o)$$ Including the source-to-gate voltage divider: $$A_{v(sig)} = -g_m (R_D \Vert r_o) \cdot \frac{R_G}{R_{sig} + R_G}$$ **(b) Lower Cutoff Frequency Derivations (7 marks):** **For $C_{in}$:** The Thévenin resistance seen by $C_{in}$ is $R_{sig} + R_G$ (since the gate is essentially open-circuit at low frequencies): $$f_{L(C_{in})} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_{sig} + R_G) C_{in}}$$ **For $C_S$:** The Thévenin resistance seen by $C_S$ is $R_S \Vert (1/g_m)$: $$f_{L(C_S)} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S \Vert \frac{1}{g_m}) C_S}$$ **Overall lower cutoff:** When multiple poles are present, the overall −3 dB frequency is **not** the simple sum. It is found using the **−3 dB summation method**: 1. Calculate each individual $f_{L,i}$. 2. Compute the total loss in dB at the dominant pole frequency: $\sum (-10 \log_{10}[1 + (f_{L,i}/f_{L,i})^2])$. 3. Find the frequency at which the total loss equals −3 dB. A widely used approximation: $$f_{L(overall)} \approx 1.1 \sqrt{\sum_i f_{L,i}^2}$$ --- ### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the **high-frequency small-signal model** of the MOSFET. Using Miller's theorem, derive the expression for the **upper cutoff frequency** $f_H$ of a CS amplifier. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A CS amplifier has the following parameters: $R_{sig} = 50 \, \Omega$, $R_G = 1 \, \text{M}\Omega$, $g_m = 3 \, \text{mS}$, $R_D = 3.3 \, \text{k}\Omega$, $r_o = 40 \, \text{k}\Omega$, $C_{gs} = 4 \, \text{pF}$, $C_{gd} = 1.5 \, \text{pF}$. Calculate: (i) the mid-band voltage gain in dB, (ii) the Miller input capacitance, (iii) the upper cutoff frequency $f_H$, and (iv) the gain-bandwidth product. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) High-Frequency Model and $f_H$ Derivation (7 marks):** At high frequencies, the MOSFET is represented by its **hybrid-π model**, which includes: - A voltage-controlled current source $g_m v_{gs}$ between Drain and Source. - An output resistance $r_o$ in parallel. - A gate-source capacitance $C_{gs}$ (oxide + overlap). - A gate-drain capacitance $C_{gd}$ (Miller capacitance). **Miller's Theorem** states that a capacitor $C_{gd}$ connected between input (Gate) and output (Drain) can be replaced by two grounded capacitors: - Input side: $C_{M,in} = C_{gd}(1 - A_v)$ - Output side: $C_{M,out} = C_{gd}\left(1 - \frac{1}{A_v}\right)$ For the CS amplifier, $A_v$ is negative and large in magnitude, so $(1 - A_v) \approx 1 + \vert A_v \vert$. The total input capacitance becomes: $$C_{in,total} = C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$$ The Thévenin resistance at the input is $R_{sig} \Vert R_G \approx R_{sig}$ (since $R_G \gg R_{sig}$). The upper cutoff is: $$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{sig} C_{in,total}}$$ **(b) Numerical Problem Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Mid-band gain:** $$R_D \Vert r_o = \frac{3300 \times 40,000}{3300 + 40,000} = \frac{132,000,000}{43,300} \approx 3048.5 \, \Omega$$ $$A_{v(MB)} = -(0.003)(3048.5) = -9.146 \, \text{V/V}$$ $$A_{v(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(9.146) \approx 19.23 \, \text{dB}$$ **Step 2 — Miller input capacitance:** $$C_{M,in} = 1.5 \text{ pF} \times (1 + 9.146) = 1.5 \times 10.146 = 15.22 \, \text{pF}$$ **Step 3 — Total input capacitance:** $$C_{in,total} = 4 + 15.22 = 19.22 \, \text{pF}$$ **Step 4 — Upper cutoff frequency:** $$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi (50)(19.22 \times 10^{-12})} = \frac{1}{6.038 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 165.6 \, \text{MHz}$$ **Step 5 — Gain-Bandwidth Product:** $$GBP = 9.146 \times 165.6 \times 10^6 \approx 1.515 \times 10^9 \, \text{Hz} = 1.52 \, \text{GHz}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Most Common Mistakes on This Question:** > 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in $A_v$** — Miller's theorem uses $A_v$ directly; using $\vert A_v \vert$ requires the $(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ form. Mixing them gives wrong signs. > 2. **Ignoring $R_{sig}$ in the Thévenin resistance** — $R_{sig} \Vert R_G \ne R_G$ when computing $f_H$. Many students write $f_H = 1/(2\pi R_G C_{in,total})$ and lose 2 marks. > 3. **Adding lower cutoffs directly** — The overall $f_L$ is **not** $f_{L1} + f_{L2}$. Use the −3 dB summation method. > 4. **Forgetting to convert $g_m$ to Siemens** — $g_m = 3$ mS = 0.003 S. A common error is to write $g_m = 3$ in the formula. > 5. **Wrong dB conversion** — $A_{v(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(\vert A_v \vert)$, not $10 \log_{10}$. (Power gain would be 10 log; voltage gain is 20 log.) --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read this the night before the exam.** - **CS amplifier** → inverting voltage amplifier; $A_v$ is **negative**. - **Mid-band gain** is the *flat* gain in the middle of the response: $A_{v(MB)} = -g_m (R_D \Vert r_o)$. - **Three regions** of frequency response: **low-frequency**, **mid-band**, **high-frequency**. - **Low-frequency poles** are caused by **external** capacitors $C_{in}, C_S, C_{out}$. Each contributes one pole $f_L = 1/(2\pi R_{eq} C)$. - **High-frequency pole** is caused by **internal** MOSFET capacitances $C_{gs}$ and $C_{gd}$. The dominant effect is **Miller multiplication** of $C_{gd}$. - **Miller's theorem:** $C_{M,in} = C_{gd}(1 - A_v)$; for CS, $C_{M,in} = C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$. - **Upper cutoff:** $f_H = 1/(2\pi R_{sig,eq} C_{in,total})$. - **Bandwidth:** $BW = f_H - f_{L(overall)}$. - **GBP** (Gain-Bandwidth Product) is a **figure of merit** — higher is better. - **The overall lower cutoff** is **not** the simple sum of individual cutoffs — use the −3 dB summation method or the $1.1 \sqrt{\sum f_{L,i}^2}$ approximation. - **Units are critical** — $g_m$ in S (A/V), $C$ in F, $f$ in Hz. The factor $2\pi$ is always there. - **Phase inversion (180°)** is the *signature* of the CS topology; the Bode phase plot passes through −180° in the mid-band. - **Bode plot slopes** are ±20 dB/decade per pole (or ±6 dB/octave per pole). - **The source bypass capacitor $C_S$** is critical — without it, the gain is reduced by a factor of $g_m R_S$. With $C_S$, the source is AC-grounded and the full mid-band gain is achieved. - **Typical lab procedure** (for record): (1) Build circuit on breadboard / simulate in Multisim/LTspice, (2) Measure DC operating point, (3) Apply 10 mV peak sine input, (4) Sweep frequency from 10 Hz to 10 MHz, (5) Record $V_{out}$ at each frequency, (6) Compute $A_v$ in dB, (7) Plot Bode magnitude plot, (8) Identify $f_L$ and $f_H$ at −3 dB points, (9) Compute BW and GBP. - **Golden rule of frequency-response analysis:** "At low frequencies, big external capacitors look like open circuits; at high frequencies, tiny internal capacitances look like short circuits."

topics.title: Feedback Amplifiers (Current Series, Voltage Series) - Gain and Frequency Response
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL407 - ANALOG CIRCUITS AND SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Feedback Amplifiers (Current Series, Voltage Series) - Gain and Frequency Response

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Feedback Amplifier?

A **Feedback Amplifier** is an amplifier circuit in which a portion of the output signal is intentionally routed back to the input and combined (mixed) with the original input signal. This returned signal is called the **Feedback Signal**, and the network that produces it is called the **Feedback Network (β-network)**.

The entire purpose of introducing feedback is to trade off a small amount of gain for huge improvements in **linearity, bandwidth, input/output impedance control, and sensitivity reduction**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCAEL407 — Module 2):**
> The lab demands design, simulation (using SPICE / Multisim / LTspice) and hardware implementation of two specific feedback topologies:
> 1. **Voltage-Series Feedback Amplifier** (acts as a Voltage Amplifier)
> 2. **Current-Series Feedback Amplifier** (acts as a Transconductance Amplifier)

## 1.2 Classification of Feedback Topologies

Feedback is classified based on **two independent decisions** taken by the designer:

| Decision Point | Options | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Output Sampling** | Voltage Sampling OR Current Sampling | How output is sensed: parallel (voltage) or series (current) |
| **Input Mixing** | Series Mixing OR Shunt Mixing | How feedback is injected: in series (voltage) or in parallel (current) with the source |

| Topology | Output Sampling | Input Mixing | Ideal Transfer Function | Ideal Impedance (In, Out) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Voltage-Series** | Voltage | Series | Voltage Gain $A_{vf}$ | High, Low |
| **Current-Series** | Current | Series | Transconductance $G_{mf}$ | High, High |
| Voltage-Shunt | Voltage | Shunt | Transresistance $R_{mf}$ | Low, Low |
| Current-Shunt | Current | Shunt | Current Gain $A_{if}$ | Low, High |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Steering Wheel of an Amplifier"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Driving a Car:**
> Imagine the open-loop amplifier is a car with no steering wheel. It runs at a fixed, jerky speed that is hard to control. Adding **negative feedback** is like adding a steering wheel: the car still moves forward, but now the driver (feedback) constantly corrects small errors.
> - **More feedback (higher $1+A\beta$)** = tighter steering control = lower gain, but smoother, more predictable performance.
> - **Less feedback** = loose steering = higher gain, but unstable and unpredictable.
> - **Voltage-Series feedback** is like cruise control (it regulates the *voltage/speed* at the *output* by sampling it).
> - **Current-Series feedback** is like a torque regulator (it regulates the *current/load pull* at the *output* by sensing it).

## 1.4 Why Use Negative Feedback? — The Four Pillars

1. **Desensitization of Gain:** The closed-loop gain becomes nearly independent of transistor $\beta$, temperature, and supply variations.
2. **Bandwidth Extension:** The upper cutoff frequency $f_H$ is multiplied by the factor $(1+A\beta)$.
3. **Impedance Transformation:** Input and output impedances can be increased or decreased as per requirement.
4. **Reduction of Nonlinear Distortion and Noise:** By a factor of $(1+A\beta)$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Negative feedback only works if the original phase shift around the loop is correct.** If the feedback signal arrives back in phase with the input (positive feedback), the amplifier becomes an **oscillator** — this is the boundary between an amplifier and an oscillator.
> **KTU students must always verify the sign of feedback experimentally** by checking that the output amplitude *reduces* when feedback is applied.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General Block Diagram of a Feedback Amplifier

Every negative feedback amplifier can be reduced to the canonical two-port block structure:

$$
\text{Source} \rightarrow X_s \xrightarrow{\sum} X_i = X_s - X_f \rightarrow \boxed{A} \rightarrow X_o \rightarrow \text{Load}
$$
$$
X_o \rightarrow \boxed{\beta} \rightarrow X_f \rightarrow \text{back to } \sum
$$

Where:
- $X_s$ = Source signal (input to feedback system)
- $X_i$ = Error signal (input to basic amplifier)
- $X_f$ = Feedback signal
- $X_o$ = Output signal
- $A = X_o / X_i$ = **Open-Loop Gain** (Basic Amplifier)
- $\beta = X_f / X_o$ = **Feedback Factor** (Feedback Network)
- $A\beta$ = **Loop Gain** (Loop Transmission)
- $1 + A\beta$ = **Desensitivity Factor** (Amount of Feedback)

## 2.2 The Master Equation — Closed-Loop Gain

The error signal is $X_i = X_s - X_f = X_s - \beta X_o$.

Since $X_o = A X_i = A(X_s - \beta X_o)$, solving for $X_o / X_s$:

$$
A_f = \frac{X_o}{X_s} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This single equation is the heart of every feedback amplifier problem in KTU exams.** All four topologies use this same equation — only the *units* of $A$, $\beta$, and $A_f$ change.

## 2.3 Sensitivity, Bandwidth, Distortion and Noise

For a feedback amplifier with loop gain $T = A\beta$:

| Property | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop | Improvement Factor |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gain** | $A$ | $A/(1+A\beta)$ | Reduced by $(1+A\beta)$ |
| **Sensitivity** $\frac{dA_f/A_f}{dA/A}$ | $1$ | $\frac{1}{1+A\beta}$ | Reduced by $(1+A\beta)$ |
| **Upper Cutoff Freq.** | $f_H$ | $f_{Hf} = f_H(1+A\beta)$ | Increased by $(1+A\beta)$ |
