# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define an RC integrator circuit. Derive the condition under which it produces a triangular wave from a square wave input.**

**Model Answer:**
An RC integrator is a passive low-pass network formed by a resistor in series with the input and a capacitor connected from the output node to ground. The transfer function is $H(s) = \dfrac{1}{1+sRC}$.

For a square wave of period $T$, the integrating condition requires that the time constant be much larger than the period, $RC \gg T$. Under this condition, the capacitor charges and discharges linearly (rather than reaching the steady-state value), and the output becomes a triangular wave with peak-to-peak amplitude $V_{out,pp} = \dfrac{V_m T}{RC}$, where $V_m$ is the input amplitude.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Explain how an RC differentiator produces spikes from a square wave input. What is the role of the time constant?**

**Model Answer:**
An RC differentiator is a passive high-pass network with a series capacitor and a shunt resistor. When a square wave with sharp edges is applied, the capacitor passes the high-frequency edge transients to the output. The output consists of positive and negative spikes occurring at the rising and falling edges, respectively. Each spike decays exponentially with time constant $\tau = RC$. The condition for clean, non-overlapping spikes is $RC \ll T$ (typically $RC \leq 0.1T$), which ensures the pulse decays back to zero before the next edge.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks**
For an RC integrator with $R = 10$ k$\Omega$ and $C = 100$ nF, calculate (i) the time constant, (ii) the corner frequency, and (iii) the expected peak-to-peak triangular output amplitude when a 5 V peak, 1 kHz square wave is applied. Is the integrating approximation valid?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Time constant:**
$$\tau = RC = (10 \times 10^3) \times (100 \times 10^{-9}) = 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — Corner frequency:**
$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-3}} = 159.15 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3 — Test signal period:**
$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{1000} = 1 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Validity check:**
The ratio $RC/T = 1$ ms / 1 ms = $1$. This is **not $\gg 1$**, so the ideal integrating condition is **NOT** met. The output will be a distorted exponential, not a clean triangle.

**Step 5 — Re-design for valid integration:** Reduce the test frequency to 100 Hz so that $T = 10$ ms, giving $RC/T = 10$, which satisfies the condition.

**Step 6 — Peak-to-peak amplitude (at 100 Hz, $V_m = 5$ V):**
$$V_{out,pp} = \frac{V_m T}{RC} = \frac{5 \times 10 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-3}} = 50 \text{ V}$$

This exceeds the supply, indicating the test conditions must be readjusted (use lower input amplitude such that $V_m T/RC < V_m$, i.e., $T < RC$, which is a contradiction). Therefore, for an *ideal* integrator in the strict sense, a very small-amplitude square wave or a very high time constant must be used. For practical KTU lab records, we report the result as $V_{out,pp} = V_m T / RC$ and note that the output saturates.

---

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks**
Derive the transfer function of the RC integrator, plot its magnitude (in dB) versus frequency on a log scale, and identify the corner frequency and asymptotic slope.

**Model Solution:**

The governing ODE: $RC \dfrac{dV_{out}}{dt} + V_{out} = V_{in}$. Taking the Laplace transform:
$$RC \cdot sV_{out}(s) + V_{out}(s) = V_{in}(s)$$
$$H(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + sRC}$$

Substituting $s = j\omega$ and computing the magnitude:

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

In decibels:
$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}} = -10\log_{10}\left[1 + (\omega RC)^2\right]$$

For $\omega RC \ll 1$ (low frequency, $f \ll f_c$): $\vert H \vert \approx 1$ → **0 dB** (flat passband).

For $\omega RC \gg 1$ (high frequency, $f \gg f_c$): $\vert H \vert \approx \dfrac{1}{\omega RC}$ → slope **$-20$ dB/decade**.

The corner frequency is $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC} = 159.15$ Hz, where $\vert H \vert = -3$ dB.

```
|H| dB
  0  |------------_________________________
     |            /
 -3  |- - - - - ./
     |          /
-20  |         /  <- -20 dB/decade slope
     |        /
-40  |       /
     |______/______________________________
        f_c=159Hz     10 f_c    100 f_c
                            Frequency (log)
```

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks**
With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an RC differentiator circuit. Derive its transfer function and explain the phase relationship for a sinusoidal input.

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit:** Capacitor $C$ in series with input, resistor $R$ across the output (from output node to ground).

**Derivation of transfer function:** Applying KVL and using $i = C\dfrac{d(V_{in} - V_{out})}{dt}$:
$$V_{out} = iR = RC\frac{d(V_{in} - V_{out})}{dt}$$

Taking the Laplace transform:
$$V_{out}(s)(1 + sRC) = sRC \cdot V_{in}(s)$$
$$H(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \frac{sRC}{1 + sRC}$$

**Sinusoidal input** $V_{in}(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$: Substitute $s = j\omega$:

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{\omega RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$
$$\angle H(j\omega) = 90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$$

For $\omega RC \ll 1$ (well below corner): $\vert H \vert \approx \omega RC$ (small), phase $\approx +90^\circ$. The output **leads** the input by $90^\circ$ — a hallmark of differentiation, since $\dfrac{d}{dt}\sin(\omega t) = \omega \cos(\omega t)$ is a phase advance of $90^\circ$ with amplitude scaling by $\omega$.

**Output expression:**
$$V_{out}(t) \approx \omega RC \cdot V_m \cos(\omega t)$$

---

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks**
For an RC differentiator with $R = 1$ k$\Omega$ and $C = 100$ nF, calculate (i) the corner frequency, (ii) the magnitude ratio at $f = 16$ kHz, and (iii) the phase angle at $f = 16$ kHz.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Corner frequency:**
$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times (10^3 \times 10^{-7})} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-4}} = 1591.55 \text{ Hz} \approx 1.59 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude ratio at $f = 16$ kHz:**
$$\frac{f}{f_c} = \frac{16000}{1591.55} = 10.05 \approx 10$$
$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{\omega RC}{\sqrt{1+(\omega RC)^2}} = \frac{10.05}{\sqrt{1+101}} = \frac{10.05}{\sqrt{102}} \approx \frac{10.05}{10.10} \approx 0.995$$

In dB: $20\log_{10}(0.995) \approx -0.043$ dB ≈ **0 dB** (the high-frequency asymptote).

**Step 3 — Phase angle at $f = 16$ kHz:**
$$\angle H = 90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(\omega RC) = 90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(10.05) = 90^\circ - 84.32^\circ = 5.68^\circ$$

This confirms that at frequencies well above $f_c$, the differentiator behaves as a pure wire (0 dB magnitude, 0° phase) — it cannot differentiate signals whose frequency is much higher than the corner.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout**
> 
> 1. **Confusing $RC/T$ ratios:** Students frequently state "use $RC > T$" for integrator, but the KTU rubric requires $RC \geq 10T$. Writing $RC = T$ loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Sign error in ODE:** In the differentiator derivation, students often write $V_{out} = RC \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$ directly, skipping the intermediate step showing that $V_{out}$ is the voltage across the resistor. Always include $V_{out} = iR$ and the KVL equation.
> 3. **Forgetting units:** $RC$ has units of seconds. In the formula $V_{out,pp} = V_m T/RC$, both $T$ and $RC$ must be in seconds, or the result is meaningless. KTU examiners specifically check for this.
> 4. **Misidentifying outputs:** Students sometimes write "integrator gives spikes" or "differentiator gives triangle." This is the *opposite* of the correct behavior and is a guaranteed 3-mark deduction.
> 5. **Skipping the Bode plot:** Part (b) of the frequency response question requires a labeled plot with axes, corner frequency, and asymptotic slopes. A purely numerical answer without the plot loses 3 of the 7 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

The following high-density checklist is the **last-page summary** every PCAEL407 student should revise before the viva or end-semester lab exam:

- [ ] **Integrator topology:** R in series, output across C — this is a **low-pass filter**.
- [ ] **Differentiator topology:** C in series, output across R — this is a **high-pass filter**.
- [ ] **Corner frequency** for both: $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ (same formula, same value).
- [ ] **Integrator condition:** $RC \geq 10T$ (square wave period).
- [ ] **Differentiator condition:** $RC \leq 0.1T$.
- [ ] **Triangular wave peak-to-peak amplitude:** $V_{out,pp} = \dfrac{V_m T}{RC}$ (integrator).
- [ ] **Differentiator spike peak amplitude:** $V_m$ at the edge, decays as $e^{-t/RC}$.
- [ ] **Bode slope of integrator above $f_c$:** $-20$ dB/decade.
- [ ] **Bode slope of differentiator below $f_c$:** $+20$ dB/decade.
- [ ] **Phase of integrator at $f \gg f_c$:** $-90^\circ$ (output lags input by quarter cycle).
- [ ] **Phase of differentiator at $f \ll f_c$:** $+90^\circ$ (output leads input by quarter cycle).
- [ ] **DC behavior:** Integrator passes DC at 0 dB; differentiator **blocks DC** completely.
- [ ] **Simulation tool:** Multisim or PSpice. Transient analysis for waveform observation, AC analysis for frequency response.
- [ ] **KTU mandated accuracy:** 5% error tolerance between theoretical and simulated values; if exceeded, check component tolerances and simulation step size.
- [ ] **Active vs. passive:** Pure RC is *passive*; op-amp-based integrators/differentiators are *active* and use DC gain to overcome loading — the KTU 2024 syllabus for this experiment covers the **passive** version.
- [ ] **Memory aid:** "**I**ntegrator = **L**ow-pass = **T**riangle" and "**D**ifferentiator = **H**igh-pass = **S**pike" — use the **ILTS / DHS** mnemonic.
- [ ] **Record must contain:** Circuit schematic, simulation procedure, input/output waveforms (screenshot), measured vs. theoretical values table, frequency response Bode plot, and a brief discussion of deviations.
- [ ] **Common viva question:** "What happens if you interchange R and C in an integrator circuit?" — Answer: It becomes a differentiator (and vice versa), because the topology determines the filter class.
- [ ] **Second common viva question:** "Why does the integrator saturate for large $T/RC$ ratios?" — Answer: The triangular output amplitude $V_m T/RC$ exceeds the input amplitude $V_m$, which is impossible in a passive network; the op-amp or capacitor saturates. This is why active integrators include a feedback resistor to limit DC gain.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clipping and Clamping Circuits Simulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAEL407 - ANALOG CIRCUITS AND SIMULATION LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Clipping and Clamping Circuits Simulation** # Module Experiment 2: Clipping and Clamping Circuits Simulation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of Wave-Shaping Circuits **Clipping Circuits (Limiters)** are diode-based wave-shaping networks designed to transmit a portion of an input waveform that lies above (or below) a specified reference voltage level. The portion of the waveform that exceeds this threshold is "clipped" (removed), while the remaining portion is passed to the output with **negligible distortion**. **Clamping Circuits (DC Restorers)** are networks consisting of a diode, a capacitor, and a resistor that shift an entire AC waveform either upward (positive clamping) or downward (negative clamping) by introducing a desired DC level, **without changing the peak-to-peak amplitude** of the original signal. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A *clipper* reshapes the **amplitude** of a signal, whereas a *clamper* shifts the **DC reference** (average level) of a signal. The peak-to-peak value remains unchanged in a clamper, but is reduced in a clipper. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Picture (Plain English):** > - Think of a **clipper** as a pair of garden hedge-shears. You set the shears at a certain height (the reference voltage). Any branch (signal portion) growing above that height is **cut off**, but everything below the height is left untouched. > - Think of a **clamper** as an **elevator platform**. The shape of the building (the AC waveform) does not change, but the entire structure is **lifted up** (positive clamp) or **pushed down** (negative clamp) by adding a fixed DC offset. The height of the building (peak-to-peak amplitude) stays exactly the same. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - Silicon Diode forward voltage drop: **V_D(on) = 0.7 V** - Germanium Diode forward voltage drop: **V_D(on) = 0.3 V** - Standard reference voltages used in biased clippers: **±1.4 V, ±2.1 V, ±3.0 V** (integer multiples of 0.7 V for silicon) - For ideal diode assumption (used in KTU derivations): **V_D(on) = 0 V**, **R_f (forward resistance) = 0 Ω**, **R_r (reverse resistance) = ∞** > [!TIP] > **Practical Clue for Lab Simulation:** In LTspice or Multisim simulations, students must explicitly set the diode model. For a *practical silicon diode* use `1N4148`; for an *ideal diode* use the built-in ideal model (`Diode_Ideal` in Multisim). The output waveform differs by exactly the V_D drop. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Clipping Circuits **A. Based on Configuration:** 1. **Series Clipper** – The diode is connected in *series* with the load. Conduction occurs only when the diode is forward-biased. 2. **Parallel (Shunt) Clipper** – The diode is connected in *parallel* with the load. Conduction occurs when the diode is forward-biased, *shorting* the output. **B. Based on Reference Level:** 1. **Unbiased Clipper** – Clipping level is fixed at **0 V** (ground). 2. **Biased Clipper** – Clipping level is shifted using a DC reference voltage **V_REF**. **C. Based on Clipped Region:** 1. **Positive Clipper** – Removes the **positive half** (or top portion) of the waveform. 2. **Negative Clipper** – Removes the **negative half** (or bottom portion) of the waveform. 3. **Double Clipper (Slope Clipper)** – Removes *both* positive and negative peaks symmetrically or asymmetrically. ### 2.2 Classification of Clamping Circuits | Type of Clamper | Diode Orientation | DC Shift Direction | |---|---|---| | Negative Clamper | Diode points UP (cathode at output node) | Output shifts UP by V_m | | Positive Clamper | Diode points DOWN (anode at output node) | Output shifts DOWN by V_m | | Biased Negative Clamper | With DC source V_B in series with diode | Output shifts by (V_m + V_B) | | Biased Positive Clamper | With DC source V_B in series with diode | Output shifts by -(V_m + V_B) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Reality:** A clamping circuit is essentially a **peak detector with a memory**. The capacitor "remembers" the peak voltage it was charged to, and the diode ensures this memory is set in only **one direction** (charging path), preventing discharge through itself. ### 2.3 Operating Principles – Step-by-Step Logic #### For a Series Negative Clipper (Diode in series, pointing toward output): - **Logic Step 1:** When V_in > 0 (positive half-cycle), the diode is **forward-biased** → diode acts as a closed switch → **V_out = V_in**. - **Logic Step 2:** When V_in < 0 (negative half-cycle), the diode is **reverse-biased** → diode acts as an open switch → **V_out = 0 V** (clipped to ground). - **Why?** The series diode only allows current flow in one direction, so the negative portion never reaches the load. #### For a Parallel Positive Clipper (Diode in parallel, anode at input, cathode at ground): - **Logic Step 1:** When V_in > +0.7 V, the diode is **forward-biased** → diode shorts the output to ground → **V_out = +0.7 V** (clipped at diode drop). - **Logic Step 2:** When V_in < +0.7 V, the diode is **reverse-biased** → no current through diode → **V_out = V_in** (passed unchanged). - **How?** The diode acts as a "voltage ceiling" by clamping anything above 0.7 V to that level. #### For a Negative Clamper (Capacitor in series, Diode from output to ground, cathode up): - **Logic Step 1 (Negative half-cycle, V_in = -V_m):** Diode is forward-biased → capacitor charges rapidly to V_m (with cathode at top, plate at -V_m relative to ground). Diode resistance is small, so charging time constant τ_c = R_d·C is very small. - **Logic Step 2 (Positive half-cycle, V_in = +V_m):** Diode is reverse-biased → capacitor *cannot* discharge through diode. It discharges only through R, with time constant τ_d = R·C. For correct clamping, **τ_d >> T** (period of input). - **Logic Step 3 (Steady state):** Capacitor holds nearly V_m across it. The output voltage becomes V_out = V_in + V_m, shifting the entire waveform upward by V_m. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Memorize the table below. The "DC shift" column is the most frequently asked sub-question in KTU ESE papers for clamping circuits. | Circuit Type | Condition for Diode ON | Output Expression (Ideal Diode) | Output Expression (Practical, V_D = 0.7V) | DC Shift | |---|---|---|---|---| | Series Positive Clipper | V_in < 0 | V_out = 0 | V_out = 0 | 0 V | | Series Negative Clipper | V_in > 0 | V_out = V_in | V_out = V_in - 0.7 | 0 V | | Parallel Positive Clipper | V_in > 0 | V_out = 0 | V_out = 0.7 V | -V_m | | Parallel Negative Clipper | V_in < 0 | V_out = 0 | V_out = -0.7 V | +V_m | | Biased Positive Clipper (Ref = V_R) | V_in > V_R | V_out = V_R | V_out = V_R + 0.7 | -(V_m - V_R) | | Biased Negative Clipper (Ref = V_R) | V_in < V_R | V_out = V_R | V_out = V_R - 0.7 | +(V_m - V_R) | | Negative Clamper | V_in < 0 (first half) | V_out = V_in + V_m | V_out = V_in + V_m - 0.7 | +V_m | | Positive Clamper | V_in > 0 (first half) | V_out = V_in - V_m | V_out = V_in - V_m + 0.7 | -V_m | | Biased Negative Clamper (Bias = V_B) | V_in < 0 | V_out = V_in + (V_m + V_B) | V_out = V_in + V_m + V_B - 0.7 | +(V_m + V_B) | ### 2.5 Time Constant Design Rule for Clampers For a clamper to operate correctly in steady state: $$ \begin{aligned} \tau_{\text{charge}} &= R_d \cdot C \ll \frac{T}{2} \\ \tau_{\text{discharge}} &= R \cdot C \gg T \end{aligned} $$ Where: - $R_d$ = forward resistance of the diode (typically $10\ \Omega$ to $100\ \Omega$) - $R$ = load resistance - $T$ = time period of input signal - $C$ = clamping capacitor > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** Clampers are widely used in **television receivers** (to restore the DC level lost after AC coupling), **radar systems** (for baseline stabilization of pulse trains), and **oscilloscope input stages** (to shift a waveform into the visible screen region). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Output Voltage of a Biased Positive Clipper Consider a parallel biased positive clipper with a reference voltage $V_R = 5\ \text{V}$ and an input $v_i(t) = 10 \sin(\omega t)\ \text{V}$ applied through a series resistor R. Assume an **ideal diode** for simplicity. **Circuit Description:** The diode's anode is at the output node, cathode is connected to $V_R = 5\ \text{V}$. The load resistor R connects output to ground. **Step 1 – Identify diode bias condition:** The diode conducts when: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{\text{anode}} &> V_{\text{cathode}} \\ v_o(t) &> 5\ \text{V} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 – When diode is OFF (Reverse-biased):** If $v_o < 5\ \text{V}$, no current flows through the diode branch. Since the input source has zero internal resistance, no current flows through R, hence: $$ v_o(t) = v_i(t) = 10 \sin(\omega t)\ \text{V} $$ **Step 3 – When diode is ON (Forward-biased):** If $v_i(t)$ tries to exceed $5\ \text{V}$, the diode shorts the output to the reference level: $$ v_o(t) = V_R = 5\ \text{V} $$ **Step 4 – Determine the clipping angle:** Find the time $t_1$ when $v_i(t_1) = 5\ \text{V}$: $$ \begin{aligned} 10 \sin(\omega t_1) &= 5 \\ \sin(\omega t_1) &= 0.5 \\ \omega t_1 &= \frac{\pi}{6}\ \text{radians} = 30^\circ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 – Construct the output waveform equation:** $$ v_o(t) = \begin{cases} 5\ \text{V}, & \text{for } 30^\circ \leq \omega t \leq 150^\circ \\ 10 \sin(\omega t)\ \text{V}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ **Step 6 – Calculate the percentage of waveform clipped:** The waveform is clipped for an angular range of $150^\circ - 30^\circ = 120^\circ$. As a fraction of the full cycle ($360^\circ$): $$ \text{Clipped Fraction} = \frac{120^\circ}{360^\circ} = 33.33\% $$ ### 3.2 Derivation 2: DC Shift of a Negative Clamper with Bias Consider a negative clamper with input $v_i(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$, where $V_m = 10\ \text{V}$, a bias battery $V_B = 3\ \text{V}$ in series with the diode (cathode at output, anode at top of bias battery), and capacitor C. Assume ideal diode. **Step 1 – Analyze the first negative half-cycle (initial condition):** At $t = 0$, the capacitor is uncharged, so $V_C(0) = 0$. As $v_i(t)$ goes negative, the diode becomes forward-biased (because the cathode of diode is being driven below the bias level). The diode conducts. **Step 2 – Determine the steady-state capacitor voltage:** The diode will conduct until the current through it becomes zero, which happens when: $$ \begin{aligned} V_C + V_B + v_i(t) &= 0 \quad \text{(KVL around the loop)} \\ V_C &= -(V_B + v_i(t)) \end{aligned} $$ At the negative peak, $v_i = -V_m = -10\ \text{V}$: $$ \begin{aligned} V_C &= -(3 + (-10)) = -(-7) = 7\ \text{V} \end{aligned} $$ Wait — the sign convention requires care. Let us re-derive using the conventional **KVL approach**: Define the capacitor voltage with **left plate positive** (the plate connected to the diode-cathode node). The loop equation during diode conduction (when $v_i$ is most negative) is: $$ \begin{aligned} v_i(t) + V_B - V_{\text{diode}} - V_C &= 0 \\ \text{At diode ON: } V_{\text{diode}} &= 0 \quad \text{(ideal)} \\ V_C &= v_i(t) + V_B \end{aligned} $$ At the negative peak: $$ V_C = -V_m + V_B = -10 + 3 = -7\ \text{V} $$ **Step 3 – Analyze steady-state after the first cycle:** In steady state, the capacitor reaches a fixed charge and **never discharges through the diode** during the positive half-cycle (diode is reverse-biased). It can only lose charge through the load R, but with $\tau = RC \gg T$, the discharge is negligible. **Step 4 – Write the output voltage expression:** The output voltage at the node between diode and capacitor is given by KVL: $$ v_o(t) = v_i(t) - V_C = v_i(t) - (-V_m + V_B) = v_i(t) + V_m - V_B $$ **Step 5 – Substitute to verify peaks:** At positive peak ($v_i = +V_m$): $$ v_o(+) = +V_m + V_m - V_B = 2V_m - V_B = 2(10) - 3 = 17\ \text{V} $$ At negative peak ($v_i = -V_m$): $$ v_o(-) = -V_m + V_m - V_B = -V_B = -3\ \text{V} $$ **Step 6 – Verify peak-to-peak value:** $$ V_{pp}(\text{output}) = v_o(+) - v_o(-) = 17 - (-3) = 20\ \text{V} = V_{pp}(\text{input}) $$ > [!NOTE] > **Sanity Check:** The peak-to-peak voltage at the output (**20 V**) equals the peak-to-peak voltage at the input (**20 V**). This confirms the **fundamental property of a clamper**: the shape (and thus $V_{pp}$) is preserved; only the DC level changes. **Step 7 – DC Shift value:** The DC shift introduced by this biased negative clamper is: $$ \Delta V_{DC} = +V_m - V_B = 10 - 3 = +7\ \text{V} $$ ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Selection of Clamping Capacitor (Design Equation) Given: Input frequency $f = 1\ \text{kHz}$, $R = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$, diode forward resistance $R_d = 50\ \Omega$. **Step 1 – Compute the time period:** $$ T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{1000} = 1\ \text{ms} $$ **Step 2 – Apply the discharge condition $\tau_d \gg T$:** We choose $\tau_d = 100 \cdot T = 100\ \text{ms}$: $$ \begin{aligned} RC &= 100T \\ C &= \frac{100T}{R} = \frac{100 \times 1\ \text{ms}}{10\ \text{k}\Omega} = \frac{100 \times 10^{-3}}{10^4} \\ C &= 10\ \mu\text{F} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 – Verify the charge condition $\tau_c \ll T/2$:** $$ \tau_c = R_d \cdot C = 50 \times 10 \times 10^{-6} = 500\ \mu\text{s} $$ Required: $\tau_c \ll T/2 = 500\ \mu\text{s}$. The condition is **borderline satisfied**. For a cleaner design, we may halve C to $5\ \mu\text{F}$, giving $\tau_c = 250\ \mu\text{s} \ll 500\ \mu\text{s}$. ✓ ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Effect of Each Component | Component | Effect on Clamper Output | Design Trade-off | |---|---|---| | Increasing C | Better DC restoration, less sag | Slower initial settling time | | Increasing R | Less discharge between cycles, better clamping | Higher output impedance, signal loading | | Decreasing R_d (faster diode) | Faster charging, better peak capture | Cost (Schottky diodes are more expensive) | | Increasing V_B (bias) | Larger DC shift | Clipping may occur if V_B > V_m | | Increasing input frequency | Easier to satisfy $\tau_d \gg T$ | Stray capacitance may dominate at very high f | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 – Block-Level Architecture of a Clamper Circuit ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[AC Input Source
Vi t equals Vm sin omega t] --> nodeB[Series Capacitor
C microfarad] nodeB --> nodeC[Output Node
Vo t] nodeC --> nodeD[Load Resistor
R kilo ohm to ground] nodeC --> nodeE[Diode Branch] nodeE --> nodeF[Bias Source
V_B volts] nodeF --> nodeG[Ground] nodeC --> nodeH[Output Terminal
to CRO or Oscilloscope] classDef inputStyle fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef componentStyle fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef outputStyle fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 class nodeA inputStyle class nodeB,nodeD,nodeE,nodeF componentStyle class nodeC,nodeH outputStyle ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** The series capacitor C is the **memory element** that stores the DC offset. The diode branch (diode + bias) sets the polarity and magnitude of the offset. The load resistor R controls the discharge rate. ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 – Operational Flow of a Negative Clamper ```mermaid flowchart TD stepA[Start: Input Vi t equals Vm sin omega t] --> stepB{Is Vi less than zero
negative half cycle} stepB -- Yes --> stepC[Diode is Forward Biased
Conduction State] stepC --> stepD[Capacitor C charges rapidly
through diode small R_d] stepD --> stepE[At negative peak
Vc reaches Vm minus V_B] stepB -- No --> stepF[Diode is Reverse Biased
Non Conduction State] stepF --> stepG[Capacitor C holds charge
discharges slowly through R] stepG --> stepH[Output Vo equals Vi plus Vc
shifted upward by Vm] stepE --> stepI[Cycle repeats
Steady state reached after few cycles] stepH --> stepI stepI --> stepJ[Final Output
DC shifted AC waveform] classDef decisionStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef actionStyle fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef outputStyle fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 class stepB decisionStyle class stepC,stepD,stepE,stepF,stepG,stepH,stepI actionStyle class stepA,stepJ outputStyle ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 – Simulation Workflow in LTspice / Multisim ```mermaid flowchart LR simA[Open LTspice or Multisim] --> simB[Place components
Diode Resistor Capacitor Vsin source] simB --> simC[Set diode model
1N4148 or Ideal] simC --> simD[Configure input signal
Vm and frequency] simD --> simE[Run Transient Analysis
Time domain simulation] simE --> simF[Probe input and output
using voltage markers] simF --> simG{Output matches
expected waveform} simG -- No --> simH[Check diode polarity
and bias direction] simH --> simD simG -- Yes --> simI[Record screenshots
for lab report] simI --> simJ[Measure DC shift
using cursor tool] simJ --> simK[Compare with
theoretical calculation] simK --> simL[Submit lab record] classDef stepStyle fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef decisionStyle fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef finalStyle fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 class simA,simB,simC,simD,simE,simF,simH,simI,simJ,simK stepStyle class simG decisionStyle class simL finalStyle ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 – Transfer Characteristics of Clipper Types ```mermaid flowchart TB transA[Transfer Characteristic
Vout versus Vin] --> transB{Type of Clipper} transB -- Series Positive --> transC[Vin greater than zero: Vout equals Vin
Vin less than zero: Vout equals zero
Hard clipping at zero axis] transB -- Series Negative --> transD[Vin less than zero: Vout equals Vin
Vin greater than zero: Vout equals zero
Removes positive half] transB -- Parallel Positive --> transE[Vin greater than Vref: Vout equals Vref
Vin less than Vref: Vout equals Vin
Clips above reference] transB -- Parallel Negative --> transF[Vin less than Vref: Vout equals Vref
Vin greater than Vref: Vout equals Vin
Clips below reference] transB -- Double Clipper --> transG[Vin greater than VR2: Vout equals VR2
Vin less than VR1: Vout equals VR1
VR1 less than Vin less than VR2: Vout equals Vin] classDef rootStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef decisionStyle fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef typeStyle fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 class transA rootStyle class transB decisionStyle class transC,transD,transE,transF,transG typeStyle ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *Distinguish between a clipping circuit and a clamping circuit. Mention one application of each.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Parameter | Clipping Circuit | Clamping Circuit | |---|---|---| | Function | Removes portion of waveform exceeding a reference level | Shifts DC level of waveform | | Effect on peak-to-peak amplitude | **Reduces** $V_{pp}$ | **Preserves** $V_{pp}$ | | Effect on DC level | May change | **Shifts by fixed DC value** | | Key components | Diode + Resistor (no capacitor for memory) | Diode + Capacitor + Resistor | | Applications | (1) AM signal generation in transmitters (2) Overvoltage protection in relays | (1) TV receivers for DC restoration (2) Baseline stabilization in radar pulse trains | *(1 mark for distinction table, 1 mark for clipping application, 1 mark for clamping application)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]** *For a clamping circuit, the time constant RC must satisfy two contradictory conditions. State and justify both conditions.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Condition 1 – Fast Charging:** During the half-cycle when the diode conducts, the capacitor must charge up to the peak value rapidly. $$ \tau_c = R_d \cdot C \ll \frac{T}{2} $$ where $R_d$ is the small forward resistance of the diode. This ensures the capacitor reaches the peak voltage before the diode switches off. **(1.5 marks)** **Condition 2 – Slow Discharging:** During the opposite half-cycle (diode OFF), the capacitor must not discharge appreciably through the load R, otherwise the DC shift will "sag" between cycles. $$ \tau_d = R \cdot C \gg T $$ This ensures the capacitor holds its charge throughout the cycle. **(1.5 marks)** The apparent contradiction is resolved by the **asymmetry of the circuit**: the discharge path goes through the **large load R**, while the charge path goes through the **small diode resistance $R_d$**. Thus, the same capacitor C can be charged quickly and discharged slowly. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – KTU ESE Module Internal Choice --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **biased positive clipper** using a silicon diode. Sketch the input and output waveforms for $v_i(t) = 10 \sin(\omega t)$ V and reference voltage $V_R = 4$ V. Also derive the **transfer characteristic** equation. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Design a **negative clamper circuit** to shift a $5$ V peak, $1$ kHz sine wave such that its positive peak is at $+8$ V. Show the capacitor and bias voltage values, and sketch the steady-state output waveform. Assume load resistance $R = 10$ k$\Omega$ and diode forward resistance $R_d = 50$ $\Omega$. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Q-A (a):** **Step 1 – Circuit Description:** A biased positive clipper has a diode connected in **parallel** with the output, but the diode's cathode is tied to a positive reference $V_R = 4$ V (instead of ground). The input is applied through a series resistor R. **Step 2 – Diode Conduction Condition:** The diode conducts when $v_i(t) > V_R + V_D$ (where $V_D = 0.7$ V for silicon). $$ v_i(t) > 4 + 0.7 = 4.7\ \text{V} $$ **Step 3 – Two Operating Regions:** - **Region 1 (Diode OFF):** When $v_i < 4.7$ V, no current flows through the diode. Output follows input: $v_o(t) = v_i(t)$. - **Region 2 (Diode ON):** When $v_i \geq 4.7$ V, the diode clamps the output: $v_o(t) = 4.7$ V. **Step 4 – Find the Clipping Angle:** $$ \begin{aligned} 10 \sin(\omega t_1) &= 4.7 \\ \sin(\omega t_1) &= 0.47 \\ \omega t_1 &= \sin^{-1}(0.47) \approx 28.0^\circ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 – Transfer Characteristic Equation:** $$ v_o(t) = \begin{cases} 4.7\ \text{V}, & \text{for } 28^\circ \leq \omega t \leq 152^\circ \\ 10 \sin(\omega t)\ \text{V}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ **Step 6 – Sketch Waveforms:** (The output follows the sine wave from $-10$ V to $+4.7$ V, then remains flat at $+4.7$ V for the central portion of the positive half-cycle, then resumes the sine shape.) #### **Model Solution for Q-A (b):** **Step 1 – Required DC Shift:** Desired positive peak: $+8$ V. Given positive peak of input: $+5$ V. So required shift: $$ \Delta V = +8 - 5 = +3\ \text{V} \quad \text{(upward shift)} $$ **Step 2 – Choose a Negative Clamper with Bias:** For a negative clamper with bias $V_B$, the shift is: $$ \Delta V = V_m - V_B $$ Setting $\Delta V = 3$ V and $V_m = 5$ V: $$ V_B = V_m - \Delta V = 5 - 3 = 2\ \text{V} $$ **Step 3 – Compute Capacitor Value:** For $f = 1$ kHz, $T = 1$ ms. Choose $\tau_d = 100T = 100$ ms: $$ C = \frac{\tau_d}{R} = \frac{100 \times 10^{-3}}{10 \times 10^3} = 1\ \mu\text{F} $$ **Step 4 – Verify Charge Condition:** $$ \tau_c = R_d \cdot C = 50 \times 1 \times 10^{-6} = 50\ \mu\text{s} \ll \frac{T}{2} = 500\ \mu\text{s} \quad \checkmark $$ **Step 5 – Verify Peak Voltages of Output:** Using $v_o(t) = v_i(t) + (V_m - V_B)$: - Positive peak: $5 + 3 = 8$ V ✓ - Negative peak: $-5 + 3 = -2$ V - Peak-to-peak: $8 - (-2) = 10$ V (preserved from input) ✓ **Step 6 – Final Design:** Capacitor $C = 1\ \mu\text{F}$, Bias battery $V_B = 2$ V (with diode anode connected to bias positive terminal for negative clamping), Load $R = 10$ k$\Omega$. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the operation of a **double clipper (slope clipper)** circuit using two diodes and two reference voltages. Derive the transfer characteristic and sketch the output for a $20\sin(\omega t)$ V input with $V_{R1} = -5$ V and $V_{R2} = +6$ V. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A **positive clamper circuit** has an input $v_i(t) = 8 \sin(2000\pi t)$ V, capacitor $C = 4.7\ \mu\text{F}$, and load resistance $R = 4.7$ k$\Omega$. Using a silicon diode, calculate: (i) the steady-state capacitor voltage, (ii) the peak values of the output waveform, and (iii) the time constant of the discharge path. Comment on whether the circuit operates as a proper clamper. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Q-B (a):** **Step 1 – Circuit Description:** A double clipper uses **two diodes in parallel**, with their cathodes tied to **two different reference voltages** $V_{R1}$ and $V_{R2}$ (where $V_{R1} < V_{R2}$). D1 clips the negative excursion at $V_{R1}$, D2 clips the positive excursion at $V_{R2}$. **Step 2 – Diode Conduction Conditions:** - Diode D1 (cathode at $V_{R1} = -5$ V) conducts when $v_i < -5 + 0.7 = -4.3$ V. - Diode D2 (cathode at $V_{R2} = +6$ V) conducts when $v_i > 6 - 0.7 = 5.3$ V. *(Note: For the diode whose cathode is at $V_{R2}$, the diode points UP; it conducts when $v_i > V_{R2} - V_D$)* **Step 3 – Three Operating Regions:** $$ v_o(t) = \begin{cases} -4.3\ \text{V}, & \text{when } v_i(t) < -4.3\ \text{V (D1 ON)} \\ +5.3\ \text{V}, & \text{when } v_i(t) > +5.3\ \text{V (D2 ON)} \\ v_i(t), & \text{when } -4.3\ \text{V} \leq v_i(t) \leq +5.3\ \text{V} \end{cases} $$ **Step 4 – Clipping Angles (assuming $V_m = 20$ V):** For D1: $20 \sin(\omega t) = -4.3$ → $\sin(\omega t) = -0.215$ → $\omega t \approx 192.4°$ and $347.6°$. For D2: $20 \sin(\omega t) = 5.3$ → $\sin(\omega t) = 0.265$ → $\omega t \approx 15.4°$ and $164.6°$. **Step 5 – Sketch Output:** The waveform rises from $-4.3$ V (clipped bottom) following the sine, transitions through the linear region between $\approx 192.4°$ and $15.4°$ (one full cycle), and is clipped at $+5.3$ V during the central portion of the positive half-cycle. #### **Model Solution for Q-B (b):** **Given:** $V_m = 8$ V, $\omega = 2000\pi$ rad/s (so $f = 1$ kHz, $T = 1$ ms), $C = 4.7\ \mu\text{F}$, $R = 4.7$ k$\Omega$, silicon diode $V_D = 0.7$ V. **(i) Steady-state capacitor voltage:** For a positive clamper, the diode conducts during the **positive half-cycle** and charges the capacitor to the positive peak minus diode drop: $$ V_C = V_m - V_D = 8 - 0.7 = 7.3\ \text{V} $$ **(ii) Peak values of output:** The positive clamper shifts the waveform **downward**. The output expression is: $$ v_o(t) = v_i(t) - V_C = v_i(t) - 7.3 $$ - Positive peak: $8 - 7.3 = +0.7$ V - Negative peak: $-8 - 7.3 = -15.3$ V - Peak-to-peak: $0.7 - (-15.3) = 16$ V (preserved from input) ✓ **(iii) Discharge time constant:** $$ \tau_d = R \cdot C = 4.7 \times 10^3 \times 4.7 \times 10^{-6} = 22.09\ \text{ms} $$ **Comment on proper operation:** Required condition: $\tau_d \gg T$. We have $\tau_d = 22.09$ ms and $T = 1$ ms, so: $$ \frac{\tau_d}{T} = 22.09 $$ This is a **good design** (ratio > 10 ensures minimal sag), so the circuit **operates properly as a positive clamper** with negligible distortion of the DC level. ✓ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Sign error in clamper output equation** – Students frequently write $v_o = v_i - V_m$ for *all* clampers, regardless of whether the diode points up or down. The correct rule is: **whichever polarity the diode conducts, the capacitor charges to that peak with the diode's drop subtracted, and the output shifts in the opposite direction of the charging.** > 2. **Forgetting $V_D$ in practical diode analysis** – KTU paper setters love testing with $V_D = 0.7$ V. Marks are specifically awarded for including (or excluding) this term correctly. > 3. **Wrong angle calculation** – When asked for the "angle for which the diode conducts," students often confuse degrees with radians. Always specify the unit. > 4. **Misidentifying the circuit type** – A circuit with a capacitor in **series** is a clamper, while a circuit with only a diode and resistor (no series capacitor) is a clipper. This is the **first classification step** examiners check. > 5. **Sag calculation oversight** – For clampers, always verify $\tau_d \geq 10T$; if not, mention "slight tilt in output waveform" in your answer. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **Clipper** = reshapes amplitude; uses only diode + resistor (no series capacitor). - [x] **Clamper** = shifts DC level; uses diode + capacitor + resistor (capacitor in series). - [x] **Ideal diode**: $V_D = 0$ V when ON, $R = \infty$ when OFF. - [x] **Practical silicon diode**: $V_D = 0.7$ V when ON. - [x] **Series clipper**: Diode in series with load; conducts when forward-biased. - [x] **Parallel (shunt) clipper**: Diode in parallel with load; conducts to clamp output. - [x] **Biased clipper**: Adds a DC reference $V_R$ to shift the clipping threshold. - [x] **Double clipper**: Uses two diodes and two references to clip both halves. - [x] **Negative clamper**: Output shifts **upward** by $V_m$ (or $V_m - V_B$ with bias). - [x] **Positive clamper**: Output shifts **downward** by $V_m$ (or $V_m - V_B$ with bias). - [x] **Clamper design rule 1**: $\tau_c = R_d C \ll T/2$ (fast charging). - [x] **Clamper design rule 2**: $\tau_d = RC \gg T$, typically $\geq 10T$ (slow discharge). - [x] **Clamper peak-to-peak** is **always preserved** (a sanity-check identity). - [x] **Clipper peak-to-peak** is **always reduced** by the amount clipped. - [x] **LTspice / Multisim**: Use `1N4148` for silicon, ideal diode model for theoretical verification. - [x] **Transfer characteristic** is a piecewise-linear function of $v_o$ vs $v_i$. - [x] **Clamper settling time** = typically 5 time constants of the discharge path. - [x] **Applications**: TV DC restoration, radar baseline clamping, signal conditioning, overvoltage protection. - [x] **Common exam trap**: Forgetting to subtract $V_D$ in practical (non-ideal) diode analysis.

topics.title: Astable, Monostable and Bistable Multivibrator Circuits Simulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL407 - ANALOG CIRCUITS AND SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Astable, Monostable and Bistable Multivibrator Circuits Simulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Multivibrators — Formal KTU Definition

A **multivibrator** is a two-stage regenerative electronic switching circuit employing two active devices (BJTs, FETs, or Op-amps) cross-coupled by resistive or RC networks, capable of producing non-sinusoidal waveforms such as square, rectangular, or pulse trains. Based on the number of stable operating states, multivibrators are classified into three families:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor — Definition (CO2, Understand):**
> "A multivibrator is a sequential regenerative switching circuit with two active devices cross-coupled such that the output of one device drives the input of the other, producing quasi-square waveforms. The classification depends on the energy-storage (timing) elements in the coupling path."

| Class | Stable States | Quasi-Stable States | Trigger Required? | Self-Starting? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Astable (Free-Running)** | **0** | 2 | No | **Yes** |
| **Monostable (One-Shot)** | **1** | 1 | Yes | No |
| **Bistable (Flip-Flop)** | **2** | 0 | Yes | No |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Light Switch" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Three Types of Switches:**

- **Astable Multivibrator = Automatic Blinker on an Old Car**: A turn-signal relay that turns itself ON, then automatically turns itself OFF, then back ON — forever, with no human pressing a button. *It is its own boss.* It has **no rest state**.

- **Monostable Multivibrator = Automatic Door Bell**: A doorbell that, when pressed once, rings continuously for a *fixed duration* (say, 2 seconds) and then automatically stops and waits for the next press. *One stable resting state, one temporary action state.*

- **Bistable Multivibrator = A Toggle Light Switch**: A staircase light switch — press UP, light turns ON and stays ON forever until you press DOWN. It "remembers" the last position. *Two stable states, both permanent.*

### 1.3 Simulation in KTU Lab Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU PCAEL407 lab, the experiment uses **SPICE-based simulation** (LTspice / Multisim / PSpice) to:
> 1. Build the schematic on the virtual workbench.
> 2. Run **Transient (.TRAN) Analysis** to observe the output waveform at both collectors.
> 3. Measure **time period (T)**, **frequency (f)**, **pulse width (PW)**, and **duty cycle**.
> 4. Verify theoretical values against simulated values and compute **percentage error**.

Standard simulation command used: `.TRAN 0.1m 5m` (Step = 0.1 ms, Stop = 5 ms) for a 1 kHz astable.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Astable Multivibrator — Circuit Operation

**Topology**: Two identical BJT stages (Q1, Q2) cross-coupled. The *collector* of each transistor is connected to the *base* of the other through a **coupling capacitor** (C1, C2). Base resistors R1, R2 connect bases to **+Vcc** to provide DC bias (reverse-biasing the base-emitter junction during OFF state).

**Operational Cycle (Symmetric Case, R1 = R2 = R, C1 = C2 = C, Rc1 = Rc2 = Rc)**:

- **Phase 1 (Q1 OFF, Q2 ON)**: Q2 is saturated → its collector ≈ **0.2 V (VCEsat)**. The capacitor C1 (charged to ≈ −Vcc) is now being charged through R1 toward +Vcc. Q1's base voltage rises exponentially. Q1 turns ON when VBE1 reaches **+0.7 V**.

- **Phase 2 (Q1 ON, Q2 OFF)**: The roles reverse. C2 charges through R2.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the transistor switch so fast? — Regeneration**: When Q1 begins to conduct, its collector voltage drops. This drop is transmitted via C1 to Q2's base, cutting Q2 OFF. As Q2's collector rises, it raises Q1's base through R2, pushing Q1 further into saturation. This **positive feedback loop** switches states in microseconds, producing near-vertical edges on the output waveform.

**Boundary Condition for Saturation**:
The base current must satisfy:

$$I_B \geq \frac{I_{C(sat)}}{\beta_{dc}} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}}{R_C \cdot \beta_{dc}}$$

For a guaranteed saturation design, choose $\beta_{dc} \cdot R_C \geq 10 R_B$ as a thumb rule.

### 2.2 Monostable Multivibrator — Circuit Operation

**Topology**: Similar to astable, but with one coupling as a **resistor R1 (DC-coupled)** and the other as an **RC timing network (C1 + R2)**. Q2 is normally held in saturation by R1.

- **Stable State (Q1 OFF, Q2 ON)**: No C1 discharge path exists. Q1's base is held near 0 V through resistor R2 (or −VBB bias), keeping it OFF. Q2 saturated via R1.

- **Trigger**: A negative pulse at Q1's base turns Q1 ON. Its collector drops, pulling Q2's base low via C1, cutting Q2 OFF. Q2's collector rises to +Vcc — this is the **output pulse leading edge**.

- **Quasi-Stable State**: C1 now charges through R1 toward +Vcc. When VBE2 reaches +0.7 V, Q2 turns ON again — this produces the **output pulse trailing edge**, and the circuit snaps back to its stable state.

- The output pulse width is determined by the RC charging, not by the trigger.

### 2.3 Bistable Multivibrator — Circuit Operation

**Topology**: Two cross-coupled stages, both couplings are **resistive (no capacitors)**. The bases are held by resistors connected to either +Vcc or −VBB.

- **Two Stable States**:
  - **State A**: Q1 ON, Q2 OFF
  - **State B**: Q1 OFF, Q2 ON

- **Triggering**: A negative pulse applied to the ON transistor's base cuts it OFF, and regenerative action flips the circuit to the other stable state. The circuit **latches** the new state indefinitely.

- The state persists with no timing capacitor and no time limit — it is true digital memory.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Must-Memorize Equations Table** (Use `\vert` for absolute value to avoid table breaks):

| Parameter | Astable | Monostable | Bistable |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Time Period T** | $T = 0.693 \, (R_1 C_1 + R_2 C_2)$ | $T = 0.693 \, R C$ | Not Applicable (memory element) |
| **Symmetric T** | $T = 1.386 \, R C$ | — | — |
| **Frequency f** | $f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{0.721}{R C}$ | — | — |
| **Duty Cycle** | $D = \dfrac{T_1}{T} = \dfrac{R_1 C_1}{R_1 C_1 + R_2 C_2}$ | $D = \dfrac{PW}{T}$ | — |
| **Pulse Width PW** | — | $PW = 0.693 \, R C$ | — |
| **Saturation $I_C$** | $I_{C(sat)} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}}{R_C}$ | Same | Same |
| **Base Drive $I_B$** | $I_B = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}$ | Same | Same |
| **Trigger Polarity** | Not Required | Negative pulse to OFF transistor | Negative pulse to ON transistor |

**Universal constants to know**:
- $\ln(2) = 0.693$
- $\ln(2) + \ln(2) = 1.386$
- $V_{BE(on)} = 0.7 \, V$ (silicon)
- $V_{CE(sat)} = 0.2 \, V$

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Astable**: Used as a **clock generator** in digital systems, **LED flashers**, **tone generators** in alarms, **PWM generation** for motor control, and **time-base generators** in CROs.
> - **Monostable**: Used in **pulse stretching**, **missing-pulse detection**, **debouncing mechanical switches**, **digital timing circuits**, and **one-shot pulse generation** in digital logic (replaced today by the 74121/74123 ICs).
> - **Bistable**: The **fundamental building block of all flip-flops** (SR, D, JK, T) and therefore of **registers, counters, and memory cells** in every computer and microcontroller (SRAM cell).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Astable Time Period T

**Step 1 — Setup at the switching instant.**
Let us assume at $t = 0$, Q1 just turned ON and Q2 just turned OFF. The capacitor C1 (coupling Q1's collector to Q2's base) has its right plate (Q2's base) at $V_{BE1(on)} = +0.7 \, V$ (Q1 is ON), and its left plate (Q1's collector) drops to $V_{CE(sat)} = 0.2 \, V$.

So the voltage across C1 just after switching is:

$$V_{C1}(0^+) = V_{CE(sat)} - V_{BE(on)} = 0.2 - 0.7 = -0.5 \, V$$

**Step 2 — The capacitor C1 charges.**
Q2's base is now being driven by R1 (connected to +Vcc) through the series combination. The voltage at Q2's base rises exponentially as C1 charges:

$$V_{B2}(t) = V_{CC} - (V_{CC} + 0.5) \cdot e^{-t / R_1 C_1}$$

This is the standard form of an RC charging transient from an initial voltage $V_i$ to a final voltage $V_f$:

$$V(t) = V_f - (V_f - V_i) \cdot e^{-t/RC}$$

**Step 3 — Switching condition.**
Q2 turns ON when its base-emitter junction becomes forward-biased, i.e., when $V_{B2} = V_{BE(on)} = +0.7 \, V$.

**Step 4 — Solve for the time interval T1.**
Setting $V_{B2}(T_1) = 0.7 \, V$:

$$0.7 = V_{CC} - (V_{CC} + 0.5) \cdot e^{-T_1 / R_1 C_1}$$

Rearranging:

$$e^{-T_1 / R_1 C_1} = \frac{V_{CC} - 0.7}{V_{CC} + 0.5}$$

Taking the natural logarithm on both sides:

$$-\frac{T_1}{R_1 C_1} = \ln\!\left(\frac{V_{CC} - 0.7}{V_{CC} + 0.5}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Engineering approximation.**
In practice, $V_{CC} \gg 0.7 \, V$ and $V_{CC} \gg 0.5 \, V$, so:

$$\frac{V_{CC} - 0.7}{V_{CC} + 0.5} \approx \frac{V_{CC}}{V_{CC}} \cdot \frac{1 - 0.7/V_{CC}}{1 + 0.5/V_{CC}} \approx 1 - \frac{1.2}{V_{CC}} \approx \frac{1}{2}$$

Therefore:

$$\ln(1/2) = -0.693$$

Substituting back:

$$\frac{T_1}{R_1 C_1} = 0.693 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad T_1 = 0.693 \, R_1 C_1$$

**Step 6 — Total period.**
By perfect symmetry of analysis, $T_2 = 0.693 \, R_2 C_2$. Therefore:

$$T = T_1 + T_2 = 0.693 \, (R_1 C_1 + R_2 C_2)$$

**Step 7 — Symmetric special case.**
If $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$:

$$T = 0.693 \cdot 2RC = 1.386 \, RC$$

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{1.386 \, RC} = \frac{0.721}{RC}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Monostable Pulse Width

**Setup**: In the stable state, Q1 is OFF and Q2 is ON. A trigger pulse turns Q1 ON. C1 (coupling Q1's collector to Q2's base) was previously charged to ≈ $V_{CC}$ (Q1 OFF, so collector was at $+V_{CC}$; Q2 ON, so base was at $+0.7 \, V$). Voltage across C1: $V_{CC} - 0.7 \approx V_{CC}$.

At $t = 0$ (after trigger), Q1's collector drops to $V_{CE(sat)} = 0.2 \, V$, dragging Q2's base to:

$$V_{B2}(0^+) = 0.2 - V_{CC}$$

This large negative value cuts Q2 OFF. C1 now charges through R1 (pull-up to Vcc) toward $+V_{CC}$. The base voltage rises as:

$$V_{B2}(t) = V_{CC} - (V_{CC} + V_{CC} - 0.2) \cdot e^{-t/RC} \approx V_{CC} - (2V_{CC}) \cdot e^{-t/RC}$$

Wait — let us be precise. The Thevenin seen by the base is $V_{CC}$ in series with R, charging C1 from initial voltage $(0.2 - V_{CC})$ toward final voltage $V_{CC}$:

$$V_{B2}(t) = V_{CC} - (V_{CC} - (0.2 - V_{CC})) \cdot e^{-t/RC} = V_{CC} - (2V_{CC} - 0.2) \cdot e^{-t/RC}$$

When $V_{B2} = 0.7 \, V$ (Q2 turns ON, ending the pulse):

$$0.7 = V_{CC} - (2V_{CC} - 0.2) \cdot e^{-T/RC}$$

$$e^{-T/RC} = \frac{V_{CC} - 0.7}{2V_{CC} - 0.2} \approx \frac{V_{CC}}{2V_{CC}} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$\therefore \quad T = 0.693 \, RC \quad \text{(Pulse Width)}$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU-style)**: Design an astable multivibrator using BJTs with $V_{CC} = 12 \, V$ to generate a square wave of frequency $f = 1 \, kHz$ with 50% duty cycle. Use $R_C = 1 \, k\Omega$.

**Solution** (with full step-by-step):

For 50% duty cycle, choose $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$.

Step 1: Required period.

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{1000} = 1 \, \text{ms}$$

Step 2: Symmetric formula.

$$T = 1.386 \, RC \quad \Longrightarrow \quad RC = \frac{T}{1.386} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{1.386} = 0.7215 \times 10^{-3} \, s$$

Step 3: Choose $C = 0.1 \, \mu F = 10^{-7} \, F$.

$$R = \frac{0.7215 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-7}} = 7215 \, \Omega \approx 7.2 \, k\Omega$$

Step 4: Standard value $R = 6.8 \, k\Omega$ (closest E12) or $R = 7.5 \, k\Omega$.

Step 5: Saturation check. Choose $\beta_{dc(min)} = 50$.

$$I_{C(sat)} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}}{R_C} = \frac{12 - 0.2}{1000} = 11.8 \, \text{mA}$$

$$I_{B(min)} = \frac{I_{C(sat)}}{\beta_{dc(min)}} = \frac{11.8 \, \text{mA}}{50} = 0.236 \, \text{mA}$$

$$I_{B(actual)} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R} = \frac{12 - 0.7}{7200} = 1.57 \, \text{mA}$$

Since $I_{B(actual)} = 1.57 \, \text{mA} \gg I_{B(min)} = 0.236 \, \text{mA}$, **saturation is guaranteed** with a safety factor of ~6.6. Design accepted.

Step 6: Simulation command in SPICE.

```
.TRAN 0.01m 3m UIC
.PROBE
.END
```

Expected output: 1 kHz square wave, 50% duty cycle, peak amplitude ≈ 12 V.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Favourite Table)

| Property | Astable | Monostable | Bistable |
|---|---|---|---|
| Coupling 1 | Capacitor $C_1$ | Capacitor $C$ (timing) | Resistor $R$ |
| Coupling 2 | Capacitor $C_2$ | Resistor $R_1$ (DC) | Resistor $R$ |
| Number of Capacitors | **2** | **1** | **0** |
| Stable States | 0 | 1 | 2 |
| Frequency Formula | $f = 0.721/RC$ | — | — |
| PW Formula | — | $PW = 0.693 RC$ | — |
| Use of $\ln 2$ | Yes | Yes | No |
| Trigger Input | None | Yes (negative) | Yes (set/reset) |
| Also Called | Free-running MV | One-shot MV | Flip-flop / Eccles-Jordan |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Three Multivibrator Families

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ASTABLE[Astable Multivibrator Free Running]
        Q1A[Q1 BJT] -- COUPLING_C1 --> Q2A[Q2 BJT]
        Q2A -- COUPLING_C2 --> Q1A
        Q1A -- VCC_PULL_R1 --> Q2A
        TIMING_A[RC TIMING NETWORK] -.-> Q1A
        TIMING_A -.-> Q2A
        OUTA[OUTPUT AT COLLECTORS] --> WAVE_A[CONTINUOUS SQUARE WAVE]
    end

    subgraph MONO[Monostable Multivibrator One Shot]
        Q1M[Q1 BJT] -- COUPLING_CM --> Q2M[Q2 BJT]
        Q2M -- DC_COUPLE_R1 --> Q1M
        TIMING_M[RC TIMING C and R2] -.-> Q2M
        TRIG[EXTERNAL TRIGGER PULSE] --> Q1M
        OUTM[OUTPUT AT Q2 COLLECTOR] --> WAVE_M[SINGLE PULSE OF WIDTH 0.693 RC]
    end

    subgraph BISTABLE[Bistable Multivibrator Flip Flop]
        Q1B[Q1 BJT] -- RES_COUPLE_RB --> Q2B[Q2 BJT]
        Q2B -- RES_COUPLE_RA --> Q1B
        SETRST[SET AND RESET TRIGGERS] --> Q1B
        SETRST --> Q2B
        OUTB[OUTPUT] --> WAVE_B[REMAINS HIGH OR LOW INDEFINITELY]
    end

    classDef mvClass fill:#E6F2FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outClass fill:#FFE6E6,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class Q1A,Q2A,Q1M,Q2M,Q1B,Q2B mvClass
    class WAVE_A,WAVE_M,WAVE_B outClass
```

### 4.2 Astable Multivibrator — Component-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VCC1[VCC PLUS 12V] --> RC1[RC1 COLLECTOR RESISTOR]
    VCC1 --> RC2[RC2 COLLECTOR RESISTOR]
    VCC1 --> RB1[RB1 BASE RESISTOR]
    VCC1 --> RB2[RB2 BASE RESISTOR]

    RC1 --> Q1C[Q1 COLLECTOR]
    RC2 --> Q2C[Q2 COLLECTOR]
    Q1C --> Q1E[Q1 EMITTER]
    Q2C --> Q2E[Q2 EMITTER]
    Q1E --> GND1[GROUND]
    Q2E --> GND1

    RB1 --> Q1B[Q1 BASE]
    RB2 --> Q2B[Q2 BASE]
    Q1C -. COUPLING CAP C1 .-> Q2B
    Q2C -. COUPLING CAP C2 .-> Q1B

    Q1C --> OUT_ASTABLE[OUTPUT WAVEFORM 1]
    Q2C --> OUT_INV[OUTPUT WAVEFORM 2 INVERTED]

    classDef psClass fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#CC6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef qClass fill:#E6FFE6,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cClass fill:#E6F2FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class VCC1,RC1,RC2,RB1,RB2,GND1 psClass
    class Q1C,Q1B,Q1E,Q2C,Q2B,Q2E qClass
    class Q1C,Q2C cClass
```

### 4.3 Monostable Multivibrator — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VCC_M[VCC PLUS 12V] --> RCM[RC COLLECTOR RESISTOR]
    VCC_M --> RB2M[RB2 BASE PULLUP RESISTOR]
    VCC_M --> R1M[R1 DC COUPLING RESISTOR]

    RCM --> Q1CM[Q1 COLLECTOR]
    R1M --> Q1BM[Q1 BASE]
    RB2M --> Q2BM[Q2 BASE]
    RCM --> Q2CM[Q2 COLLECTOR]

    Q1CM --> Q1EM[Q1 EMITTER]
    Q2CM --> Q2EM[Q2 EMITTER]
    Q1EM --> GNDM[GROUND]
    Q2EM --> GNDM

    Q1CM -. TIMING CAPACITOR C .-> Q2BM

    TRIG_M[NEGATIVE TRIGGER PULSE] --> Q1BM
    Q2CM --> OUT_MONO[OUTPUT PULSE WIDTH 0.693 RC]

    classDef pwrClass fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#CC6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef trClass fill:#FFE6F2,stroke:#990066,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef capClass fill:#E6F2FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outClass fill:#E6FFE6,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class VCC_M,RCM,RB2M,R1M,GNDM pwrClass
    class TRIG_M trClass
    class Q1CM capClass
    class OUT_MONO outClass
```

### 4.4 Bistable Multivibrator — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VCC_B[VCC PLUS 12V] --> RCB1[RC1]
    VCC_B --> RCB2[RC2]
    VCC_B --> RB1B[RB1]
    VCC_B --> RB2B[RB2]
    VBB_B[VBB MINUS 12V] --> RK1[RK1]
    VBB_B --> RK2[RK2]

    RCB1 --> Q1CB[Q1 COLLECTOR]
    RCB2 --> Q2CB[Q2 COLLECTOR]
    RB1B --> Q1BB[Q1 BASE]
    RB2B --> Q2BB[Q2 BASE]
    RK1 --> Q1BB
    RK2 --> Q2BB

    Q1CB --> Q1EB[Q1 EMITTER]
    Q2CB --> Q2EB[Q2 EMITTER]
    Q1EB --> GNDB[GROUND]
    Q2EB --> GNDB

    Q1CB -- RESISTIVE COUPLING R -- Q2BB
    Q2CB -- RESISTIVE COUPLING R -- Q1BB

    SETB[SET PULSE TO Q2 BASE] --> Q2BB
    RSTB[RESET PULSE TO Q1 BASE] --> Q1BB
    Q1CB --> QBIT_OUT[OUTPUT Q STATE]
    Q2CB --> QBAR_OUT[OUTPUT Q BAR STATE]

    classDef setClass fill:#FFE6E6,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outClass fill:#E6FFE6,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class SETB,RSTB setClass
    class QBIT_OUT,QBAR_OUT outClass
```

### 4.5 Expected SPICE Simulation Waveforms — Architecture View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ASTABLE_WAVES[Astable Output Waveforms]
        VCC_REF[VCC RAIL 12V] --> BASE_A[BASE LINE 12V]
        BASE_A --> Q1C_WAVE[Q1 COLLECTOR SQ WAVE 0 to 12V FREQ 1 KHZ]
        BASE_A --> Q2C_WAVE[Q2 COLLECTOR SQ WAVE 0 to 12V PHASE SHIFTED 180 DEG]
    end

    subgraph MONO_WAVES[Monostable Output Waveform]
        MONO_TIME[TIME AXIS] --> TRIG_PULSE[SHORT NEGATIVE TRIGGER PULSE]
        TRIG_PULSE --> OUT_PW[OUTPUT AT Q2 COLLECTOR RISES AT TRIGGER FALLS AFTER 0.693 RC]
    end

    subgraph BISTABLE_WAVES[Bistable Output Waveform]
        BIST_TIME[TIME AXIS] --> SET_ACT[STATE GOES HIGH ON SET PULSE]
        SET_ACT --> STAYS_HIGH[REMAINS HIGH INDEFINITELY]
        STAYS_HIGH --> RESET_ACT[STATE GOES LOW ON RESET PULSE]
        RESET_ACT --> STAYS_LOW[REMAINS LOW INDEFINITELY]
    end

    classDef waveClass fill:#E6F2FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class Q1C_WAVE,Q2C_WAVE,OUT_PW waveClass
```
