<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define a multivibrator. List the three types of multivibrators with one application of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **multivibrator** is a two-stage regenerative switching circuit using two active devices cross-coupled to produce square, rectangular, or pulse waveforms. The three types are:

1. **Astable Multivibrator** — No stable state; free-running oscillator. *Application:* Clock generator in digital systems, LED flasher circuits.
2. **Monostable Multivibrator** — One stable state, one quasi-stable state. *Application:* Pulse-stretching circuit, missing-pulse detector, switch debouncer.
3. **Bistable Multivibrator** — Two stable states. *Application:* Flip-flop, basic memory cell (1-bit SRAM), digital counter stage.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for listing types + 1 mark for applications.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*Why are capacitors used in the cross-coupling of an astable multivibrator instead of resistors? What role does the $\ln 2$ factor play in the time-period equation?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Resistors provide **DC coupling** which would prevent the transistors from switching OFF (the base would be permanently pulled HIGH, holding the transistor ON). Capacitors provide **AC coupling** with **DC blocking** — they allow sudden voltage changes (edges) to pass from one collector to the opposite base, forcing fast switching, while preventing the DC collector voltage from disturbing the bias of the other stage. **(1.5 marks)**

The factor $\ln 2 = 0.693$ appears because the capacitor charges from $0 \, V$ (or the negative voltage) to $V_{CC}$ exponentially, and the switching threshold of $0.7 \, V$ is reached at the instant when the capacitor has charged to **half** its final voltage (when $V_{CC} \gg 0.7 \, V$). Since $e^{-0.693} = 0.5$, the natural logarithm of 0.5 is $-0.693$, giving the time $T = 0.693 \, RC$. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting $V_{CE(sat)}$ and using $V_{CC}$ directly** in $I_C$ calculation — lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing up the formula** $0.693(R_1C_1 + R_2C_2)$ with the symmetric $1.386 RC$ — partial marks cut.
> 3. **Not justifying the design with a saturation check** — full design questions lose 2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention $\ln 2$ derivation** in monostable/astable theory — 1 mark deducted.
> 5. **Confusing "stable state" with "quasi-stable state"** in monostable — conceptual error, 1 mark off.
> 6. **Not specifying the trigger polarity** (negative) in monostable/bistable — 0.5 mark lost.
> 7. **Failing to compute the percentage error** between theoretical and simulated values in lab-record questions — 1 mark deducted.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Astable Multivibrator Design**

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an astable multivibrator using BJTs. Derive the expression for the time period of oscillation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Description (2 marks):**
An astable multivibrator consists of two identical NPN transistor stages Q1 and Q2. The collector of Q1 is coupled to the base of Q2 via capacitor C1, and the collector of Q2 is coupled to the base of Q1 via capacitor C2. Resistors R1 and R2 connect the bases of Q2 and Q1 respectively to +Vcc (this provides the pull-up to keep the OFF transistor's base high). Collector resistors $R_{C1}$ and $R_{C2}$ connect the collectors to +Vcc. The emitters are grounded.

**Operation (3 marks):**
Assume at $t=0$, Q1 is ON (saturated) and Q2 is OFF. The voltage at Q1's collector is $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2 \, V$, and at Q2's collector it is $+V_{CC}$ (≈ 12 V). The capacitor C1, which had been charged earlier, discharges through R1 toward $+V_{CC}$. As soon as the voltage at Q2's base reaches $+0.7 \, V$, Q2 turns ON. The drop in Q2's collector voltage is transmitted through C2 to Q1's base, cutting Q1 OFF. Now Q1 is OFF and Q2 is ON — the roles are reversed. The process is regenerative and repeats indefinitely, producing a continuous square wave at both collectors (180° out of phase).

**Derivation of Time Period (2 marks):**
The time for which Q2 remains OFF (and Q1 is ON) is:

$$T_1 = R_1 C_1 \ln 2 = 0.693 \, R_1 C_1$$

Similarly, $T_2 = 0.693 \, R_2 C_2$. Total period:

$$T = T_1 + T_2 = 0.693 \, (R_1 C_1 + R_2 C_2)$$

For the symmetric case ($R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$):

$$T = 1.386 \, RC \quad ; \quad f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{0.721}{RC}$$

---

**(b)** Design an astable multivibrator to generate a **1 kHz square wave** with 50% duty cycle using $V_{CC} = 12 \, V$, $R_C = 1.2 \, k\Omega$, and $\beta_{dc(min)} = 50$. Simulate the circuit in SPICE and list the steps to verify the frequency from the waveform. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Design (4 marks):**

Step 1: Period required.

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{1000} = 1 \, \text{ms}$$

Step 2: For 50% duty cycle, $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$.

$$T = 1.386 \, RC \quad \Longrightarrow \quad RC = \frac{10^{-3}}{1.386} = 0.7215 \times 10^{-3} \, s$$

Step 3: Choose $C = 0.1 \, \mu F = 10^{-7} \, F$.

$$R = \frac{0.7215 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-7}} = 7.215 \, k\Omega \quad \text{(use standard } 6.8 \, k\Omega \text{)}$$

Step 4: Saturation check.

$$I_{C(sat)} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}}{R_C} = \frac{12 - 0.2}{1200} = 9.83 \, \text{mA}$$

$$I_{B(min)} = \frac{I_{C(sat)}}{\beta_{dc(min)}} = \frac{9.83}{50} = 0.197 \, \text{mA}$$

$$I_{B(actual)} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R} = \frac{12 - 0.7}{6800} = 1.66 \, \text{mA}$$

Since $1.66 \, mA \gg 0.197 \, mA$, saturation is guaranteed (safety factor ≈ 8.4). ✓

Final design: $R_C = 1.2 \, k\Omega$, $R = 6.8 \, k\Omega$, $C = 0.1 \, \mu F$, $V_{CC} = 12 \, V$.

**Simulation Steps (3 marks):**
1. Open **LTspice** → New Schematic → Place two 2N2222A NPN transistors, capacitors (0.1 µF), resistors ($R_C$ = 1.2 kΩ, $R_B$ = 6.8 kΩ), and Vcc = 12 V.
2. Wire the circuit: Q1 collector → C1 → Q2 base; Q2 collector → C2 → Q1 base; R1 from Q2 base to Vcc; R2 from Q1 base to Vcc.
3. Place a **voltage probe** at Q1's collector (Vout1) and at Q2's collector (Vout2).
4. Set the simulation command: `.TRAN 0.01m 3m UIC` (10 µs step, 3 ms run time).
5. Click **Run** (▶) → View the output waveforms.
6. Use the **cursor** to measure the time between two consecutive falling edges of Vout1 → this is $T$. Compute $f = 1/T$.
7. Compare with theoretical $f = 1 \, kHz$ and report percentage error: $\% \text{error} = \frac{\vert f_{sim} - f_{theory} \vert}{f_{theory}} \times 100\%$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Monostable & Bistable (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Draw the circuit of a **collector-coupled monostable multivibrator** and explain its operation. Derive the expression for the pulse width of the output. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit (2 marks):**
A monostable MV uses a capacitor C coupling Q1's collector to Q2's base, and a resistor R1 coupling Q2's collector directly (DC) to Q1's base. Q1 is normally held OFF by a negative bias $V_{BB}$ (or simply by R2 to ground), and Q2 is normally held ON by R1 (pulled to +Vcc).

**Operation (3 marks):**
- **Stable State**: Q2 ON (saturated, $V_{C2} \approx 0.2 \, V$), Q1 OFF. Capacitor C is charged to $V_{CC} - V_{BE2} \approx V_{CC}$.
- **Trigger**: A negative pulse applied to Q1's base turns Q1 ON. Its collector drops to $V_{CE(sat)} = 0.2 \, V$. Since the voltage across a capacitor cannot change instantaneously, Q2's base is dragged to $0.2 - V_{CC}$ (a large negative voltage). Q2 is cut OFF, and its collector jumps up to $+V_{CC}$ — this is the **leading edge of the output pulse**.
- **Quasi-Stable State**: Capacitor C now charges through R1 toward $+V_{CC}$. The voltage at Q2's base rises exponentially. When $V_{B2} = +0.7 \, V$, Q2 turns ON. The drop in Q2's collector is fed to Q1's base, ensuring Q1 turns OFF — the circuit **snaps back** to the stable state. The output pulse ends.

**Derivation of Pulse Width (2 marks):**

The base voltage of Q2 during the quasi-stable state is:

$$V_{B2}(t) = V_{CC} - (2V_{CC} - 0.2) e^{-t/RC} \approx V_{CC}(1 - 2 e^{-t/RC})$$

Setting $V_{B2}(T) = 0.7 \, V$ and using $V_{CC} \gg 0.7$:

$$e^{-T/RC} = \frac{1}{2} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad T = RC \ln 2 = 0.693 \, RC$$

Hence the pulse width is $\boxed{PW = 0.693 \, RC}$.

---

**(b)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **bistable multivibrator**. Mention the triggering method and any two applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit (2 marks):**
A bistable MV (also called the **Eccles-Jordan trigger circuit**) consists of two identical cross-coupled transistor stages. The collector of Q1 is connected to the base of Q2 via resistor R, and the collector of Q2 is connected to the base of Q1 via resistor R. The bases are also returned to a negative supply $V_{BB}$ via $R_K$ resistors to ensure hard cutoff of the OFF transistor.

**Operation (3 marks):**
- **State 1 (Stable)**: Suppose Q1 is ON (saturated). Its collector is at $0.2 \, V$, which is insufficient to forward-bias Q2's base-emitter junction, so Q2 is OFF. Q2's collector is at $+V_{CC}$, which through the base resistor holds Q1 firmly in saturation. The circuit **latches** in this state.
- **State 2 (Stable)**: By symmetry, Q1 OFF, Q2 ON is also a stable state. The circuit retains whichever state it is in indefinitely, with **no timing capacitor** and **no time limit**.

**Triggering (1 mark):**
- A **negative pulse at the base of the ON transistor** cuts it OFF. The rise in its collector is fed to the base of the OFF transistor, turning it ON. The drop in its collector further cuts OFF the first transistor. Regenerative action flips the state in microseconds.
- A separate **SET pulse** sets Q = 1, and a **RESET pulse** sets Q = 0. This is the basic **SR flip-flop**.

**Applications (1 mark):**
1. 1-bit memory cell in SRAM and registers.
2. Basic building block of all digital flip-flops (D, JK, T flip-flops) and binary counters.
3. Used in latches, comparators, and Schmitt trigger circuits.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print this before exam!)**

- [ ] **Astable**: 0 stable states, 2 capacitors in cross-coupling, $T = 0.693(R_1C_1 + R_2C_2)$, symmetric $T = 1.386 RC$, $f = 0.721/RC$.
- [ ] **Monostable**: 1 stable + 1 quasi-stable, 1 capacitor, $PW = 0.693 RC$, needs negative trigger.
- [ ] **Bistable**: 2 stable states, 0 capacitors, needs SET/RESET trigger, used as flip-flop / memory.
- [ ] The **factor 0.693 = ln 2** arises because the capacitor charges from $0$ to $V_{CC}$ and switching happens at the **half-charge point** ($0.693$ time-constants).
- [ ] **Saturation condition**: $I_{B(actual)} \geq I_{C(sat)} / \beta_{dc(min)}$. Always check this in design problems.
- [ ] **VCE(sat) = 0.2 V** and **VBE(on) = 0.7 V** for silicon BJTs.
- [ ] In **simulation**:
  - Use `.TRAN` for transient waveform viewing.
  - Use **cursors** to measure period T and pulse width PW.
  - **Two outputs are 180° phase-shifted** in astable.
  - Compute **% error** = $\vert V_{sim} - V_{theory} \vert / V_{theory} \times 100\%$.
- [ ] **Coupling capacitor choice**: For astable, $C \geq 10 \cdot C_{input} + $ stray capacitance to ensure reliable switching.
- [ ] **Bistable requires a negative supply $-V_{BB}$** for hard cutoff of the OFF transistor — a key design difference from astable/monostable.
- [ ] KTU lab typically uses **2N2222A** or **BC107** as the BJT and $V_{CC} = +12 \, V$.
- [ ] Remember the **3 keywords** that define each MV: Astable = *Free-Running*, Monostable = *One-Shot*, Bistable = *Flip-Flop / Latch*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RC Coupled CE Amplifier Simulation - Frequency Response Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL407 - ANALOG CIRCUITS AND SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** RC Coupled CE Amplifier Simulation - Frequency Response Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **RC Coupled CE (Common Emitter) Amplifier:** A multi-stage voltage amplifier in which a **single-stage common-emitter BJT amplifier** is coupled to a signal source and a load through **resistor-capacitor (RC) networks** that block DC while passing AC signals. The "RC Coupled" terminology specifically refers to the use of coupling capacitors at the input and output terminals of the stage.

The **frequency response characteristics** of such an amplifier describe the variation of its voltage gain magnitude (and phase) as a function of the input signal frequency. In simulation platforms such as **SPICE / Multisim / LTspice**, this response is obtained by performing an **AC Analysis (small-signal sweep)** and plotting the output magnitude in decibels (dB) against frequency on a logarithmic scale.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 2):** The experiment aims to simulate an RC coupled CE amplifier using SPICE, plot its frequency response, and extract the **(i) Mid-band gain $A_{mid}$ in dB, (ii) Lower cutoff frequency $f_L$, (iii) Upper cutoff frequency $f_H$, and (iv) Bandwidth $BW = f_H - f_L$.**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hearing Range" Intuition

Imagine a **musical equalizer** on a stereo system. The bass knob controls low-frequency sounds, the treble controls high-frequency sounds, and the volume controls the mid-range. Your stereo doesn't reproduce *all* frequencies equally well — it has a "sweet spot" in the middle and rolls off at both extremes.

An RC coupled CE amplifier behaves **identically**:

- **Low frequencies** (bass region) → attenuated because coupling and bypass capacitors act like *open circuits* and refuse to let slow-changing signals through.
- **Mid frequencies** (sweet spot) → all capacitors act like *short circuits* and the amplifier delivers its **maximum, flat gain**.
- **High frequencies** (treble region) → attenuated because internal transistor capacitances (like $C_{bc}$) act like *short circuits*, shunting high-frequency signals to ground.

The "sweet spot" is the **mid-band region**, and the boundaries where the gain drops to **70.7%** (or **−3 dB**) of its mid-band value are called the **cutoff frequencies**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Boltzmann constant | $k$ | $1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ | J/K |
| Thermal voltage (at 300 K) | $V_T$ | **25.85 mV** | V |
| Room temperature | $T$ | **300 K** | K |
| Cutoff frequency threshold | $f_c$ | $0.707 \cdot A_{mid}$ | Hz |
| Decibel conversion | $A_{dB}$ | $20 \log_{10}\vert A_v \vert$ | dB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why −3 dB?** When the gain drops to $1/\sqrt{2} = 0.7071$ of its mid-band value, the power is halved. Since $\text{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(0.7071) = -3.0103$ dB, this boundary is universally called the **−3 dB point** or **half-power point**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Operational Regions of the Frequency Response

The frequency response of an RC coupled CE amplifier is divided into **three distinct regions**, each governed by different physical mechanisms.

### Region 1: Low-Frequency Region ($f < f_L$)

- Dominated by **external coupling and bypass capacitors**: $C_{in}$, $C_{out}$, and $C_E$.
- As frequency decreases, the **reactance** $X_C = 1/(2\pi f C)$ **increases**.
- These capacitors begin to **drop significant AC voltage**, reducing the voltage delivered to the load.
- Gain rolls off at **+20 dB/decade** as frequency decreases.

### Region 2: Mid-Frequency Region ($f_L \le f \le f_H$)

- All external capacitors ($C_{in}, C_{out}, C_E$) act as **ideal short circuits** (low reactance).
- All internal transistor capacitances ($C_{bc}$, $C_{ce}$) act as **ideal open circuits** (high reactance).
- The amplifier behaves as a **resistive, linear network** with maximum, constant gain $A_{mid}$.
- Phase shift is **−180°** (signal inversion inherent to common-emitter configuration).

### Region 3: High-Frequency Region ($f > f_H$)

- Dominated by **internal junction capacitances** of the BJT, primarily the **base-collector capacitance $C_{bc}$** (also called $C_\mu$).
- The **Miller effect** multiplies $C_{bc}$ by a factor of $(1 + g_m R_C)$ when reflected to the input.
- This creates a large **equivalent input capacitance** that shunts high-frequency signals.
- Gain rolls off at **−20 dB/decade** as frequency increases.

## 2.2 Why Each Component Matters

- **Input coupling capacitor $C_{in}$**: Blocks DC from the source biasing the base, but at low frequencies its reactance becomes large, attenuating the input signal.
- **Output coupling capacitor $C_{out}$**: Blocks DC collector voltage from reaching the load, but introduces a high-pass filter with the output resistance and load.
- **Emitter bypass capacitor $C_E$**: Short-circuits the unbypassed emitter resistor $R_E$ at signal frequencies, preventing degenerative (negative) feedback that would reduce gain. At low frequencies, it loses its bypass action.
- **Internal capacitances $C_{bc}$, $C_{ce}$**: Form low-pass RC networks with source and load resistances, limiting high-frequency response.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for RC coupled CE amplifier frequency response. Memorize these and you can solve 90% of KTU problems on this topic. Note: $\vert$ symbols are rendered as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transconductance | $g_m = I_C / V_T$ | $I_C$ in A, $V_T \approx 26$ mV at 300 K; $g_m$ in **A/V (S)** |
| 2 | Input resistance (h-parameter) | $R_{in} = h_{ie} \approx \beta \cdot r_e$ | $r_e = V_T / I_E$; $R_{in}$ in **Ω** |
| 3 | Mid-band voltage gain | $A_{mid} = -g_m R_C$ (no load) | Dimensionless, **negative** sign indicates phase inversion |
| 4 | Mid-band voltage gain (with load) | $A_{mid} = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$ | Accounts for AC load line |
| 5 | Input RC low-cutoff (coupling cap) | $f_{L1} = 1 / [2\pi (R_S + R_{in}) C_{in}]$ | $R_S$ = source resistance; in **Hz** |
| 6 | Output RC low-cutoff (coupling cap) | $f_{L2} = 1 / [2\pi (R_C + R_L) C_{out}]$ | $R_L$ = load resistance; in **Hz** |
| 7 | Emitter RC low-cutoff (bypass cap) | $f_{L3} = 1 / [2\pi (R_E \parallel R_{in}/\beta) C_E]$ | When unbypassed; in **Hz** |
| 8 | Overall lower cutoff | $f_L \approx 1.1 \sqrt{f_{L1}^2 + f_{L2}^2 + f_{L3}^2}$ | For close-by cutoffs; in **Hz** |
| 9 | Miller input capacitance | $C_{M} = C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_v \vert) \approx C_{bc} \cdot g_m R_C$ | Reflected to base; in **F** |
| 10 | Total input shunt capacitance | $C_{T} = C_{be} + C_{M} = C_{\pi} + C_\mu(1 + g_m R_C)$ | in **F** |
| 11 | Upper cutoff frequency | $f_H = 1 / (2\pi R_{th} C_T)$ | $R_{th} = R_S \parallel R_B \parallel R_{in}$; in **Hz** |
| 12 | Bandwidth | $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ (since $f_H \gg f_L$) | in **Hz** |
| 13 | Gain in dB | $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\vert A_v \vert$ | in **dB** |
| 14 | −3 dB condition | $20 \log_{10}(0.7071) = -3.0103$ dB | Universal cutoff threshold |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio amplifiers (20 Hz – 20 kHz)**: Must provide flat gain across the entire audible band; design must ensure $f_L < 20$ Hz and $f_H > 20$ kHz.
- **RF communication receivers**: The bandwidth $BW$ determines **channel selectivity** and **information capacity** (Shannon–Hartley theorem).
- **Oscilloscope front-end amplifiers**: Wide bandwidth required to faithfully reproduce fast transients.
- **Biomedical signal amplifiers (ECG, EEG)**: Lower cutoff $f_L$ determines the lowest frequency artifact that can be rejected (e.g., 0.05 Hz for ECG baseline drift removal).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Mid-Band Voltage Gain $A_{mid}$

Consider a single-stage CE amplifier with a fully bypassed emitter resistor. The small-signal hybrid-π model is valid in the mid-band.

**Step 1:** The small-signal collector current is controlled by the base-emitter voltage:

$$i_c = g_m \cdot v_{be}$$

**Step 2:** The output voltage develops across the parallel combination of $R_C$ and $R_L$:

$$v_{out} = -i_c \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L)$$

**Step 3:** The negative sign indicates **180° phase inversion** (collector voltage decreases as base voltage increases).

**Step 4:** The voltage gain is therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{mid} &= \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = \frac{-g_m \cdot v_{be} \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L)}{v_{be}} \\
A_{mid} &= -g_m \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** When the load $R_L$ is disconnected (open circuit), this reduces to:

$$A_{mid} = -g_m R_C$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Lower Cutoff Frequency $f_{L1}$ (Input Coupling Capacitor)

The input coupling capacitor $C_{in}$ forms a high-pass RC filter with the source resistance $R_S$ and the amplifier's input resistance $R_{in}$.

**Step 1:** Apply Thevenin's theorem looking from $C_{in}$'s perspective. The Thevenin resistance seen by $C_{in}$ is the series combination of $R_S$ and $R_{in}$ (since the base node sees $R_S$ in series with the source):

$$R_{th,L1} = R_S + R_{in}$$

**Step 2:** The cutoff frequency of a first-order high-pass RC filter is:

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R C}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting $R_{th,L1}$ and $C_{in}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{L1} &= \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot R_{th,L1} \cdot C_{in}} \\
f_{L1} &= \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S + R_{in}) C_{in}}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Lower Cutoff Frequency $f_{L3}$ (Emitter Bypass Capacitor)

The bypass capacitor $C_E$ is in parallel with $R_E$. When $C_E$ is *not* a perfect short, the emitter is not fully bypassed.

**Step 1:** The resistance seen by $C_E$ looking into the emitter is the parallel combination of $R_E$ and the resistance looking into the emitter from outside (which is approximately $R_S / h_{fe}$ when reflected through the BJT):

$$R_{th,L3} = R_E \parallel \frac{R_S}{h_{fe}}$$

**Step 2:** Applying the standard RC cutoff formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{L3} &= \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot R_{th,L3} \cdot C_E} \\
f_{L3} &= \frac{1}{2\pi \left( R_E \parallel \dfrac{R_S}{h_{fe}} \right) C_E}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Since $R_S / h_{fe}$ is typically much smaller than $R_E$, the parallel combination is dominated by $R_S / h_{fe}$:

$$f_{L3} \approx \frac{h_{fe}}{2\pi R_S C_E}$$

This shows that $f_{L3}$ is often the **dominant** (highest) lower cutoff frequency.

## 3.4 Overall Lower Cutoff Frequency $f_L$

Since the three cutoff poles are independent and contribute independently, the total response magnitude squared is the sum of contributions. When the three $f_{L}$ values are close to one another (within a factor of ~3), the empirical approximation is:

$$f_L \approx 1.1 \sqrt{f_{L1}^2 + f_{L2}^2 + f_{L3}^2}$$

When one pole is clearly dominant (e.g., $f_{L3} \gg f_{L1}, f_{L2}$):

$$f_L \approx f_{L3}$$

## 3.5 Derivation of the Miller Input Capacitance $C_M$

**Step 1:** The base-collector capacitance $C_{bc} = C_\mu$ is connected between input (base) and output (collector). The voltage across it is:

$$v_{bc} = v_b - v_c = v_{be} - v_{ce} = v_{be} - (-A_v v_{be}) = v_{be}(1 + A_v)$$

**Step 2:** The current through $C_\mu$ is:

$$i_{C_\mu} = C_\mu \frac{d v_{bc}}{dt} = C_\mu (1 + A_v) \frac{d v_{be}}{dt}$$

**Step 3:** The current drawn from the input source must supply this same current, so the effective input capacitance is:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{M} &= C_\mu (1 + A_v) \\
C_{M} &= C_{bc} (1 + \vert A_v \vert) \quad \text{(using magnitude since } A_v < 0\text{)} \\
C_{M} &\approx C_{bc} \cdot g_m R_C \quad \text{(since } A_v = -g_m R_C \text{ and } g_m R_C \gg 1\text{)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Physical Intuition:** A small capacitance (few pF) between input and output becomes a *large* equivalent capacitance at the input (tens to hundreds of pF) because the output voltage swings in the opposite direction, **doubling the effective voltage change** across the capacitor.

## 3.6 Derivation of the Upper Cutoff Frequency $f_H$

**Step 1:** The total capacitance shunting the input node is the sum of the base-emitter capacitance and the Miller-multiplied collector capacitance:

$$C_T = C_{be} + C_M = C_\pi + C_\mu (1 + g_m R_C)$$

**Step 2:** The Thevenin resistance seen at the base is the parallel combination of source resistance, biasing resistors, and amplifier input resistance:

$$R_{th,H} = R_S \parallel R_B \parallel R_{in}$$

**Step 3:** This forms a low-pass RC filter with cutoff:

$$\begin{aligned}
f_H &= \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th,H} C_T} \\
f_H &= \frac{1}{2\pi \left( R_S \parallel R_B \parallel R_{in} \right) \left[ C_\pi + C_\mu (1 + g_m R_C) \right]}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.7 Complete Frequency Response Equation

Combining all three regions into a single transfer function:

$$\vert A_v(f) \vert = \frac{A_{mid}}{\sqrt{1 + \left( \dfrac{f_L}{f} \right)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \left( \dfrac{f}{f_H} \right)^2}}$$

**In decibel form:**

$$\vert A_v(f) \vert_{dB} = A_{mid,dB} - 10 \log_{10}\!\left[ 1 + \left( \frac{f_L}{f} \right)^2 \right] - 10 \log_{10}\!\left[ 1 + \left( \frac{f}{f_H} \right)^2 \right]$$

## 3.8 Analytical Worked Example

**Problem:** A CE amplifier has $I_C = 1$ mA, $R_C = 4.7$ kΩ, $R_L = 10$ kΩ, $R_S = 600$ Ω, $R_E = 1$ kΩ, $C_{in} = 10$ μF, $C_{out} = 10$ μF, $C_E = 100$ μF, $C_\pi = 50$ pF, $C_\mu = 5$ pF, $h_{fe} = 100$, $R_B = 100$ kΩ.

**Step 1 — Calculate $g_m$:**

$$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{1 \text{ mA}}{26 \text{ mV}} = 0.03846 \text{ S} = 38.46 \text{ mA/V}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $R_{in} \approx h_{fe} \cdot r_e$:**

$$r_e = \frac{V_T}{I_E} \approx \frac{26 \text{ mV}}{1 \text{ mA}} = 26 \text{ Ω}$$

$$R_{in} = 100 \times 26 = 2600 \text{ Ω} = 2.6 \text{ kΩ}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate $A_{mid}$:**

$$A_{mid} = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L) = -0.03846 \times (4700 \parallel 10000)$$

$$R_C \parallel R_L = \frac{4700 \times 10000}{4700 + 10000} = \frac{47{,}000{,}000}{14{,}700} = 3197 \text{ Ω}$$

$$A_{mid} = -0.03846 \times 3197 = -122.96 \approx -123$$

In decibels: $A_{mid,dB} = 20 \log_{10}(123) = 41.8$ dB

**Step 4 — Calculate $f_{L1}$:**

$$f_{L1} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S + R_{in}) C_{in}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (600 + 2600)(10 \times 10^{-6})}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{L1} &= \frac{1}{2\pi \times 3200 \times 10^{-5}} \\
f_{L1} &= \frac{1}{0.2011} = 4.97 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate $f_{L2}$:**

$$f_{L2} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_C + R_L) C_{out}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (4700 + 10000)(10 \times 10^{-6})}$$

$$f_{L2} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 14700 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{1}{0.9236} = 1.08 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 6 — Calculate $f_{L3}$:**

$$R_{th,L3} = R_E \parallel \frac{R_S}{h_{fe}} = 1000 \parallel \frac{600}{100} = 1000 \parallel 6 \approx 5.96 \text{ Ω}$$

$$f_{L3} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 5.96 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1}{0.003745} = 267 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 7 — Dominant lower cutoff frequency:**

Since $f_{L3} = 267$ Hz is the largest, it dominates:

$$f_L \approx 267 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 8 — Calculate $f_H$ (Miller effect):**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_M &= C_\mu (1 + g_m R_C) = 5 \text{ pF} \times (1 + 0.03846 \times 4700) \\
C_M &= 5 \text{ pF} \times (1 + 180.76) = 5 \text{ pF} \times 181.76 = 908.8 \text{ pF}
\end{aligned}$$

$$C_T = C_\pi + C_M = 50 + 908.8 = 958.8 \text{ pF}$$

$$R_{th,H} = R_S \parallel R_B \parallel R_{in} = 600 \parallel 100000 \parallel 2600$$

$$R_{th,H} \approx 600 \parallel 2600 = \frac{600 \times 2600}{600 + 2600} = 487.5 \text{ Ω}$$

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 487.5 \times 958.8 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{1}{2.937 \times 10^{-6}} = 340.5 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 9 — Bandwidth:**

$$BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H = 340.5 \text{ kHz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The Miller effect has *multiplied* a 5 pF capacitance into nearly 1 nF at the input, drastically reducing $f_H$. This is why the upper cutoff frequency is the **primary bandwidth limitation** in BJT amplifiers.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1, Module 2]**

**Q1. Define the term "−3 dB cutoff frequency" in the context of an RC coupled amplifier. Why is it called the "half-power point"?**

**Model Answer:**
The −3 dB cutoff frequency is the frequency at which the voltage gain of the amplifier drops to **0.7071 times** (or $1/\sqrt{2}$) of its maximum mid-band value, corresponding to **−3.0103 dB** on a logarithmic scale. It is called the half-power point because the power delivered to the load at this frequency is **half** (50%) of the power delivered at mid-band frequencies. This is because $P \propto V^2$, and $(1/\sqrt{2})^2 = 1/2$. The lower cutoff $f_L$ and upper cutoff $f_H$ are both defined at this threshold.

**Keywords for 3 marks:** 0.7071, −3.0103 dB, half-power, $1/\sqrt{2}$, $f_L$ and $f_H$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2, Module 2]**

**Q2. What is the Miller effect? How does it affect the upper cutoff frequency of a CE amplifier?**

**Model Answer:**
The Miller effect refers to the **apparent multiplication of a capacitance** connected between the input and output of an inverting amplifier when viewed from the input terminals. For a CE amplifier with gain $A_v = -g_m R_C$, the base-collector capacitance $C_{bc}$ appears at the input as:

$$C_M = C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_v \vert) \approx C_{bc} \cdot g_m R_C$$

This **drastically increases** the total input shunt capacitance (often by a factor of 100 or more), forming a low-pass filter with the source resistance. As a result, the **upper cutoff frequency $f_H$ is significantly reduced**, limiting the high-frequency response of the amplifier. For example, a 5 pF $C_{bc}$ can appear as nearly 1 nF at the input.

**Keywords for 3 marks:** $C_{bc}$, inverting amplifier, multiplication, $1 + \vert A_v \vert$, reduces $f_H$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, ESE Pattern]**

### Question A (14 Marks) — **OR** — Question B (14 Marks)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**For an RC coupled CE amplifier, derive expressions for the (a) lower cutoff frequency due to the emitter bypass capacitor, and (b) upper cutoff frequency considering the Miller effect. Hence, explain how the bandwidth is determined.**

#### Part (a) — Lower Cutoff due to Emitter Bypass Capacitor (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Identify the components. The emitter bypass capacitor $C_E$ is in parallel with the emitter resistor $R_E$. At signal frequencies, $C_E$ should ideally short-circuit $R_E$ to maximize gain. At low frequencies, its impedance becomes significant.

**Step 2:** Use Thevenin's theorem. The resistance seen by $C_E$ is the parallel combination of $R_E$ (looking outward) and the resistance looking into the emitter of the BJT.

**Step 3:** The resistance looking into the emitter (with the base driven by source $R_S$) is approximately $R_S / h_{fe}$:

$$R_{th} = R_E \parallel \frac{R_S}{h_{fe}}$$

**Step 4:** The cutoff frequency of the resulting high-pass RC network is:

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{L3} &= \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} C_E} \\
f_{L3} &= \frac{1}{2\pi \left( R_E \parallel \dfrac{R_S}{h_{fe}} \right) C_E}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Since typically $R_S / h_{fe} \ll R_E$, the parallel combination simplifies to:

$$f_{L3} \approx \frac{h_{fe}}{2\pi R_S C_E}$$

This is often the **dominant** (highest) lower cutoff frequency because $R_S / h_{fe}$ is small.

#### Part (b) — Upper Cutoff with Miller Effect (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Identify the internal capacitances of the BJT: $C_\pi = C_{be}$ (base-emitter) and $C_\mu = C_{bc}$ (base-collector). Of these, $C_\mu$ is the most troublesome because of the Miller effect.

**Step 2:** Apply the Miller theorem. The capacitance $C_\mu$ between input (base) and output (collector) appears at the input as:

$$C_M = C_\mu (1 + \vert A_v \vert) = C_\mu (1 + g_m R_C)$$

**Step 3:** The total input capacitance is:

$$C_T = C_\pi + C_M = C_\pi + C_\mu(1 + g_m R_C)$$

**Step 4:** The Thevenin resistance seen at the base node is:

$$R_{th} = R_S \parallel R_B \parallel R_{in}$$

**Step 5:** This forms a low-pass RC filter, and the upper cutoff frequency is:

$$\begin{aligned}
f_H &= \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} C_T} \\
f_H &= \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S \parallel R_B \parallel R_{in}) [C_\pi + C_\mu(1 + g_m R_C)]}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6:** Bandwidth determination. The overall lower cutoff $f_L$ is the highest among $f_{L1}, f_{L2}, f_{L3}$, and the overall upper cutoff is $f_H$. The bandwidth is:

$$BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H \quad (\text{since } f_H \gg f_L)$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**A CE amplifier has the following parameters: $g_m = 40$ mA/V, $h_{fe} = 100$, $r_e = 25$ Ω, $R_C = 4.7$ kΩ, $R_L = 10$ kΩ, $R_S = 600$ Ω, $R_B = 100$ kΩ, $C_{in} = C_{out} = 10$ μF, $C_E = 100$ μF, $C_\pi = 50$ pF, $C_\mu = 5$ pF.**

**Calculate: (a) the mid-band voltage gain in dB, and (b) the lower and upper cutoff frequencies and the bandwidth of the amplifier.**

#### Part (a) — Mid-Band Voltage Gain (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Compute the AC load resistance:

$$R_{ac} = R_C \parallel R_L = \frac{4700 \times 10000}{4700 + 10000} = 3197 \text{ Ω}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the mid-band voltage gain:

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{mid} &= -g_m \cdot R_{ac} \\
A_{mid} &= -0.040 \times 3197 = -127.88
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to decibels:

$$A_{mid,dB} = 20 \log_{10}(127.88) = 20 \times 2.1069 = 42.14 \text{ dB}$$

#### Part (b) — Cutoff Frequencies and Bandwidth (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Input resistance:**

$$R_{in} = h_{fe} \cdot r_e = 100 \times 25 = 2500 \text{ Ω}$$

**Step 2 — Lower cutoff $f_{L1}$ (input coupling):**

$$f_{L1} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S + R_{in}) C_{in}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (600 + 2500)(10 \times 10^{-6})} = 5.13 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3 — Lower cutoff $f_{L3}$ (emitter bypass):**

$$R_{th,L3} = \frac{R_E}{1} \parallel \frac{R_S}{h_{fe}} = 1000 \parallel 6 = 5.96 \text{ Ω}$$

$$f_{L3} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 5.96 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}} = 267 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Dominant $f_L$:** $f_L \approx f_{L3} = 267$ Hz (since $f_{L3} \gg f_{L1}, f_{L2}$)

**Step 5 — Miller capacitance:**

$$C_M = C_\mu (1 + g_m R_C) = 5 \text{ pF} \times (1 + 0.040 \times 4700) = 5 \times 189 = 945 \text{ pF}$$

**Step 6 — Total input capacitance:**

$$C_T = C_\pi + C_M = 50 + 945 = 995 \text{ pF}$$

**Step 7 — Thevenin resistance at high frequency:**

$$R_{th,H} = R_S \parallel R_B \parallel R_{in} = 600 \parallel 100000 \parallel 2500 \approx 483 \text{ Ω}$$

**Step 8 — Upper cutoff:**

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 483 \times 995 \times 10^{-12}} = 331 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 9 — Bandwidth:**

$$BW = f_H - f_L = 331{,}000 - 267 \approx 330.7 \text{ kHz}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the magnitude bars in Miller formula:** Writing $C_M = C_\mu(1 + A_v)$ instead of $C_\mu(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$. Since $A_v$ is negative in a CE amplifier, forgetting the absolute value gives a wrong (smaller) answer. **−1 to −2 marks penalty.**
> 2. **Wrong $R_E$ approximation for bypass cutoff:** Using $R_E$ alone instead of $R_E \parallel (R_S/h_{fe})$. This gives a $f_{L3}$ value that is **100× too small**, completely missing the dominant pole. **−2 marks.**
> 3. **Confusing dB formulas:** Writing $A_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\vert A_v \vert$ (power gain formula) instead of $20 \log_{10}\vert A_v \vert$ (voltage gain formula). **−1 mark.**
> 4. **Missing units in final answer:** Failing to write Hz, kHz, dB explicitly. **−0.5 mark per answer.**
> 5. **Not identifying the dominant pole:** Writing all three $f_L$ values without commenting on which one dominates $f_L$. Examiners specifically look for this analytical step. **−1 to −2 marks.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — RC Coupled CE Amplifier Frequency Response**

- ☐ **Three regions** of frequency response: **Low ($f < f_L$)**, **Mid ($f_L \le f \le f_H$)**, **High ($f > f_H$)**.
- ☐ **Mid-band gain** is set by transconductance and AC load: $A_{mid} = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$.
- ☐ **Phase inversion** of 180° is inherent to CE configuration.
- ☐ **Lower cutoff** is governed by **external** capacitors: $C_{in}$, $C_{out}$, $C_E$.
- ☐ **Upper cutoff** is governed by **internal** transistor capacitances: $C_\pi$, $C_\mu$.
- ☐ **Miller effect** multiplies $C_\mu$ by factor $(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ when reflected to input.
- ☐ **Dominant lower cutoff** is almost always the emitter bypass pole $f_{L3}$.
- ☐ **−3 dB point** corresponds to voltage gain $= A_{mid} / \sqrt{2} = 0.7071 \cdot A_{mid}$.
- ☐ **Decibel conversion** for voltage gain: $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\vert A_v \vert$.
- ☐ **Bandwidth** $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ for $f_H \gg f_L$.
- ☐ **Roll-off slopes**: +20 dB/decade below $f_L$, −20 dB/decade above $f_H$.
- ☐ **SPICE directive**: `.ac dec 100 1 100Meg` for Bode plot simulation.
- ☐ **$g_m = I_C / V_T$** with $V_T = 26$ mV at room temperature (300 K).
- ☐ **Trade-off**: Higher gain (larger $R_C$) reduces bandwidth due to increased Miller effect.
- ☐ **Cascode and emitter-follower** configurations are used to break the gain-bandwidth product limit.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE EXPERIMENT 2: CASCADE AMPLIFIER SIMULATION — GAIN & FREQUENCY RESPONSE

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Cascade Amplifier** is a **multi-stage amplifier** formed by connecting the output of one amplifier stage to the input of the next stage, such that the overall gain is the product of the individual stage gains. In the context of this experiment, a **two-stage RC-coupled Common Emitter (CE) BJT amplifier** is simulated using SPICE-based tools (Multisim / LTspice / PSpice), and its **mid-band voltage gain**, **lower cutoff frequency ($f_L$)**, **upper cutoff frequency ($f_H$)**, and **bandwidth (BW)** are measured via **AC frequency-response analysis**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment specifically tests the student's ability to *plot the frequency response*, *measure the 3-dB bandwidth*, and *verify the gain-bandwidth product* for a two-stage RC-coupled amplifier using virtual instruments (Bode plotter / AC sweep).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket Relay" Intuition

Imagine two workers standing in a line trying to empty a flooded room using buckets:
- **Worker 1 (Stage 1)** scoops water and pours it into a small intermediate tank.
- **Worker 2 (Stage 2)** then takes water from the intermediate tank and empties it further away.

Each worker has limited reach (gain) and a maximum speed at which they can work (bandwidth). When they work **in cascade**, the total reach becomes the **product** of their individual reaches, and the total speed becomes a *combined, slightly reduced* speed because the slower worker dictates the pace. This is exactly how a cascade amplifier behaves — the **overall gain is multiplied**, but the **bandwidth shrinks** slightly compared to a single stage.

### 1.3 Key Physical & Standard Metrics

- **Mid-band Gain ($A_{v,\text{mid}}$):** Voltage gain in the flat region of the response (typically the **1 kHz** reference).
- **Lower Cutoff Frequency ($f_L$):** Frequency at which gain drops to **0.707** ($A_v / \sqrt{2}$) of mid-band value → **−3 dB point**.
- **Upper Cutoff Frequency ($f_H$):** Frequency at which gain drops to **0.707** of mid-band value → **−3 dB point**.
- **Bandwidth (BW):** $BW = f_H - f_L$.
- **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW):** $GBW = \vert A_{v,\text{mid}} \vert \times BW$ (a **constant** for a given amplifier topology).

> [!NOTE]
> **Cascading Rule of Thumb:** Cascading two identical amplifier stages increases the mid-band gain by a factor of $A_{v1} \times A_{v2}$, but the bandwidth becomes **narrower** because $f_L$ rises and $f_H$ falls relative to a single stage.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block-Level Operational Theory

A two-stage RC-coupled cascade amplifier consists of:

1. **Stage 1:** Common Emitter amplifier with bias network ($R_1, R_2, R_E, R_C$) and emitter bypass capacitor $C_E$.
2. **Coupling Network:** Capacitor $C_{C1}$ between Stage-1 collector and Stage-2 base — blocks DC, passes AC.
3. **Stage 2:** Identical CE topology receiving signal from $C_{C1}$.
4. **Load:** Output taken through coupling capacitor $C_{C2}$ across load $R_L$.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

- **DC Isolation:** The coupling capacitors $C_{C1}, C_{C2}$ block the DC bias of one stage from disturbing the next stage's Q-point.
- **AC Signal Flow:** The amplified AC signal at the collector of Stage 1 is fed to the base of Stage 2.
- **Cumulative Gain:** Total voltage gain in mid-band is the **multiplicative product** of the two individual stage gains.
- **Frequency-Dependent Components:** At low frequencies, coupling and bypass capacitors introduce high impedance → gain falls. At high frequencies, internal transistor capacitances ($C_{bc}, C_{be}$) and Miller effect dominate → gain falls.

### 2.3 Why Cascading is Used

| Reason | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|
| **Higher Gain** | Single CE stage typically gives $A_v \approx 50$–$200$. Cascade gives $A_v \approx 2500$–$40000$ (in dB: $20 \log_{10} A_v$). |
| **Improved Input Impedance** | First stage can be designed for high $Z_{in}$ (e.g., using Darlington or buffer). |
| **Improved Output Drive** | Final stage can be designed for low $Z_{out}$. |
| **Better Selectivity** | Narrower bandwidth sharpens tuned responses. |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The vertical pipe `\vert` is used instead of `\mid` or `|` in the table to prevent markdown table breakage.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Overall Mid-band Voltage Gain | $A_{v,\text{total}} = A_{v1} \times A_{v2}$ | Dimensionless (ratio); in dB: $A_{v(\text{dB})} = A_{v1(\text{dB})} + A_{v2(\text{dB})}$ |
| 2 | Single Stage Gain (CE, bypassed $R_E$) | $A_v = -g_m \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L)$ | $g_m = I_C / V_T$; $V_T \approx 26\,\text{mV}$ at 300 K |
| 3 | Lower Cutoff (n identical stages) | $f_L(\text{cascade}) = \dfrac{f_{L1}}{\sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}}$ | $n$ = number of stages |
| 4 | Upper Cutoff (n identical stages) | $f_H(\text{cascade}) = f_{H1} \cdot \sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}$ | Hz |
| 5 | For $n=2$ (special case) | $f_L(\text{cas}) = 1.554 \cdot f_{L1}$ ; $f_H(\text{cas}) = 0.644 \cdot f_{H1}$ | Numerical constants |
| 6 | Bandwidth | $BW = f_H - f_L$ | Hz |
| 7 | Gain-Bandwidth Product | $GBW = \vert A_{v,\text{mid}} \vert \times BW$ | Constant for the topology |
| 8 | 3 dB Condition | $\vert A_v \vert = A_{v,\text{mid}} / \sqrt{2}$ | Equivalent to $0.707 \cdot A_{v,\text{mid}}$ |
| 9 | Lower Cutoff due to $C_C$ | $f_L = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_S + R_{in}) C_C}$ | $R_S$ = source resistance |
| 10 | Upper Cutoff (Miller) | $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{\text{eq}} C_{\text{in,total}}}$ | $C_{M} = C_{bc}(1 + g_m R_C')$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio Preamplifiers:** Hi-Fi systems use 2- or 3-stage cascades to achieve 60–80 dB gain before the power stage.
- **RF Front-Ends:** Communication receivers chain tuned RF amplifiers (cascade of resonant stages) to amplify weak antenna signals.
- **Operational Amplifier Internals:** The op-amp IC (e.g., $\mu$A741, LM358) is essentially a 2- or 3-stage cascade of differential and CE gain stages on a single silicon die.
- **Instrumentation:** ECG / EEG amplifiers use cascade stages to amplify micro-volt biosignals to usable levels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Overall Mid-band Gain

Let the small-signal voltage gain of Stage 1 be $A_{v1}$ and that of Stage 2 be $A_{v2}$.

**Step 1 — Output of Stage 1:**
$$V_{o1} = A_{v1} \cdot V_{in}$$

**Step 2 — This output drives Stage 2 (coupling capacitor acts as short circuit in mid-band):**
$$V_{in2} = V_{o1} = A_{v1} \cdot V_{in}$$

**Step 3 — Output of Stage 2:**
$$V_{o2} = A_{v2} \cdot V_{in2} = A_{v2} \cdot (A_{v1} \cdot V_{in})$$

**Step 4 — Total Gain Definition:**
$$A_{v,\text{total}} = \frac{V_{o2}}{V_{in}}$$

**Step 5 — Substituting:**
$$A_{v,\text{total}} = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2}$$

This explains why **dB gains add** while **linear gains multiply**.

## 3.2 Derivation of Cascade Cutoff Frequencies (Identical Stages)

For $n$ identical non-interacting stages, each with transfer function:
$$A_{v,i}(f) = \frac{A_{v,\text{mid}}}{1 + j(f_{L,i}/f) - j(f/f_{H,i})}$$

The overall response is the product:
$$A_{v,\text{tot}}(f) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_{v,i}(f)$$

**Lower 3-dB condition** — magnitude drops by $\sqrt{2}$:
$$\left(1 + \left(\frac{f_L}{f}\right)^2\right)^{n/2} = \sqrt{2}$$

Solving for $f_L$:
$$1 + \left(\frac{f_L}{f_{L,\text{stage}}}\right)^2 = 2^{1/n}$$
$$\left(\frac{f_L}{f_{L,\text{stage}}}\right)^2 = 2^{1/n} - 1$$
$$f_L(\text{cascade}) = f_{L,\text{stage}} \cdot \sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}^{-1} = \frac{f_{L,\text{stage}}}{\sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}}$$

**Upper 3-dB condition** — analogous derivation gives:
$$f_H(\text{cascade}) = f_{H,\text{stage}} \cdot \sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}$$

**For $n = 2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2^{1/2} - 1 &= \sqrt{2} - 1 \approx 1.4142 - 1 = 0.4142 \\
\sqrt{0.4142} &\approx 0.6436 \\
\frac{1}{0.6436} &\approx 1.5536
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:
$$f_L(\text{cascade}) = 1.554 \cdot f_{L,\text{stage}}$$
$$f_H(\text{cascade}) = 0.644 \cdot f_{H,\text{stage}}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Board-Exam Style)

**Given:** A two-stage RC-coupled amplifier with each stage having:
- $A_{v1} = A_{v2} = 50$
- $f_{L,\text{stage}} = 100\,\text{Hz}$
- $f_{H,\text{stage}} = 100\,\text{kHz}$

**Find:** Overall gain (dB), $f_L$, $f_H$, BW, GBW of the cascade.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Overall mid-band gain (linear):**
$$A_{v,\text{total}} = 50 \times 50 = 2500$$

**Step 2 — Convert to dB:**
$$A_{v,\text{total(dB)}} = 20 \log_{10}(2500)$$
$$\log_{10}(2500) = \log_{10}(2.5 \times 10^3) = 3 + \log_{10}(2.5) = 3 + 0.3979 = 3.3979$$
$$A_{v,\text{total(dB)}} = 20 \times 3.3979 = 67.96\,\text{dB} \approx 68\,\text{dB}$$

**Step 3 — Cascade lower cutoff:**
$$f_L(\text{cas}) = 1.554 \times 100 = 155.4\,\text{Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Cascade upper cutoff:**
$$f_H(\text{cas}) = 0.644 \times 100{,}000 = 64{,}400\,\text{Hz} = 64.4\,\text{kHz}$$

**Step 5 — Bandwidth:**
$$BW = 64{,}400 - 155.4 \approx 64{,}244.6\,\text{Hz} \approx 64.24\,\text{kHz}$$

**Step 6 — Gain-Bandwidth Product:**
$$GBW = 2500 \times 64{,}244.6 \approx 1.606 \times 10^8\,\text{Hz} \approx 160.6\,\text{MHz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification by Addition of dB:** $A_{v1(\text{dB})} = 20\log_{10}(50) = 33.98\,\text{dB}$. Adding: $33.98 + 33.98 = 67.96\,\text{dB}$ ✓ — matches Step 2.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Frequency-Band Behaviour

| Frequency Band | Dominant Reactance | Behaviour of $C_C, C_E$ | Behaviour of $C_{bc}, C_{be}$ | Gain Trend |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $f < f_L$ (Low) | $X_C$ of coupling caps is **high** | Acts as **blocker** (low signal passes) | Negligible | **Falls** at −20 dB/decade |
| $f_L < f < f_H$ (Mid) | All caps ideal shorts | $C_C, C_E \to$ short | $C_{bc}, C_{be} \to$ open | **Flat** (constant $A_v$) |
| $f > f_H$ (High) | $X_C$ of internal caps is **low** | Short | Miller-multiplied $C_{bc}$ shorts input | **Falls** at −20 dB/decade per stage |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of Two-Stage RC-Coupled Cascade

    q1A --> c2A[Coupling Cap CC2]

    bias2A --> q2A[Stage 2 BJT CE]

## 4.4 Component-Level Reference Schematic (Text Description)

For exam purposes, the schematic is typically drawn as:

- **Stage 1:** NPN BJT (e.g., **BC547** or **2N3904**) with:
  - $R_1 = 33\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ (voltage divider bias)
  - $R_C = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_E = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$
  - $C_E = 47\,\mu\text{F}$ (emitter bypass), $C_{C1} = 10\,\mu\text{F}$
- **Inter-stage coupling:** $C_{C2} = 10\,\mu\text{F}$ between Stage-1 collector and Stage-2 base.
- **Stage 2:** Identical component values as Stage 1.
- **Output coupling:** $C_{C3} = 10\,\mu\text{F}$, $R_L = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$.
- **Supply:** $V_{CC} = +12\,\text{V DC}$, input sine $V_{in} = 10\,\text{mV}$ peak, **1 kHz**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define a cascade amplifier. Why is the overall gain expressed in decibels, and what is the advantage of expressing it this way?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A cascade amplifier is a multi-stage amplifier where the output of one stage feeds the input of the next. The overall voltage gain is:
$$A_{v,\text{total}} = A_{v1} \times A_{v2} \times \ldots \times A_{vn}$$
Expressed in dB:
$$A_{v(\text{dB})} = 20\log_{10}\left(\vert A_v \vert\right)$$
Since dB conversion involves a logarithm, multiplication of linear gains becomes **addition of dB gains**, simplifying cascade calculations. For example, two stages each of gain 50 give $33.98\,\text{dB} + 33.98\,\text{dB} = 67.96\,\text{dB}$ rather than the multiplicative $50 \times 50 = 2500$.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by the −3 dB bandwidth of an amplifier? How does the bandwidth change when two identical amplifier stages are cascaded?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The −3 dB bandwidth is the range of frequencies between the lower cutoff $f_L$ and upper cutoff $f_H$ where the amplifier gain remains within 3 dB (i.e., $0.707 \times A_{v,\text{mid}}$) of the mid-band value. For two identical cascaded stages:
$$f_L(\text{cas}) = 1.554 \cdot f_{L,\text{stage}}, \quad f_H(\text{cas}) = 0.644 \cdot f_{H,\text{stage}}$$
Hence the **bandwidth narrows** compared to a single stage because $f_L$ increases and $f_H$ decreases. The gain-bandwidth product, however, **remains approximately constant**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **two-stage RC-coupled cascade amplifier**. Discuss the role of coupling and bypass capacitors. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A two-stage amplifier has identical stages, each with $A_{v1} = A_{v2} = 60$, $f_{L1} = f_{L2} = 80\,\text{Hz}$, and $f_{H1} = f_{H2} = 200\,\text{kHz}$. Calculate the **overall mid-band gain in dB**, the **new lower and upper cutoff frequencies**, the **bandwidth**, and the **gain-bandwidth product**. **\[7 marks\]**

#### Model Solution to (a):

A two-stage RC-coupled amplifier uses **resistor-capacitor coupling** between two Common Emitter (CE) BJT stages. The block diagram shows:

- **Input** $V_{in}$ → coupling capacitor $C_{C1}$ → base of $Q_1$ (Stage 1).
- Stage 1 amplifies; its output (collector of $Q_1$) is coupled via $C_{C2}$ to the base of $Q_2$ (Stage 2).
- Stage 2 output is coupled via $C_{C3}$ to the load $R_L$.

**Role of capacitors:**
- **Coupling capacitors ($C_{C1}, C_{C2}, C_{C3}$):** Block DC bias of one stage from the next, allowing only AC signal to pass. They must have low reactance ($X_C \ll R$) in the mid-band.
- **Emitter bypass capacitor ($C_E$):** Provides a low-impedance path for AC emitter current to ground, preventing AC negative feedback and preserving the high gain of the CE stage. At low frequencies, $C_E$ fails to bypass $R_E$ fully, causing gain to fall — this is the primary cause of the lower cutoff $f_L$.

**Working:** In the mid-band, all capacitors behave as ideal shorts. The signal is amplified by $A_{v1}$ in Stage 1, then by $A_{v2}$ in Stage 2. The total gain $A_{v,\text{total}} = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2}$.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Overall mid-band gain (linear):**
$$A_{v,\text{total}} = 60 \times 60 = 3600$$

**Step 2 — Convert to dB:**
$$A_{v,\text{total(dB)}} = 20\log_{10}(3600) = 20 \times 3.5563 = 71.13\,\text{dB}$$

**Step 3 — Cascade lower cutoff:**
$$f_L(\text{cas}) = 1.554 \times 80 = 124.32\,\text{Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Cascade upper cutoff:**
$$f_H(\text{cas}) = 0.644 \times 200{,}000 = 128{,}800\,\text{Hz} = 128.8\,\text{kHz}$$

**Step 5 — Bandwidth:**
$$BW = 128{,}800 - 124.32 \approx 128{,}675.7\,\text{Hz} \approx 128.68\,\text{kHz}$$

**Step 6 — Gain-Bandwidth Product:**
$$GBW = 3600 \times 128{,}675.7 \approx 4.63 \times 10^8\,\text{Hz} \approx 463\,\text{MHz}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often forget that the **constant 1.554** and **0.644** are only valid for **two identical stages** ($n=2$). For three stages, the constants change to **1.923** and **0.510**. Using the wrong constant for the wrong $n$ is the most common mark-losing error in this problem. Also remember to express the GBW in **Hz or MHz**, not in dB.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **frequency response** of a single-stage CE amplifier. Sketch and label the **Bode plot** showing the three regions (low, mid, high). **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A two-stage RC-coupled cascade amplifier uses identical transistors with $g_m = 50\,\text{mA/V}$, $R_C = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_L = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$, and $C_C = 10\,\mu\text{F}$. The source resistance is $R_S = 600\,\Omega$ and input resistance $R_{in} = 1.2\,\text{k}\Omega$. Calculate (i) the **mid-band gain of each stage**, (ii) the **lower cutoff frequency** of each stage, and (iii) the **cascade lower cutoff** assuming the two stages are identical. **\[7 marks\]**

#### Model Solution to (a):

The frequency response of a single-stage CE amplifier is divided into three regions:

1. **Low-frequency region ($f < f_L$):** Coupling capacitor $C_C$ and emitter bypass capacitor $C_E$ introduce significant reactance. The gain rolls off at **−20 dB/decade** as frequency decreases.
2. **Mid-band region ($f_L < f < f_H$):** All capacitors behave as ideal shorts. The gain is **flat** at the maximum value $A_{v,\text{mid}} = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$.
3. **High-frequency region ($f > f_H$):** Internal transistor capacitances $C_{be}$ and Miller-multiplied $C_{bc}$ dominate. The gain rolls off at **−20 dB/decade** as frequency increases.

The Bode plot is a graph of $\vert A_v \vert$ in dB (y-axis, log scale) versus frequency $f$ in Hz (x-axis, log scale). The −3 dB points at $f_L$ and $f_H$ bound the flat mid-band region, and the gain is constant within this band.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** $g_m = 50\,\text{mA/V} = 0.05\,\text{S}$, $R_C = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_L = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C_C = 10\,\mu\text{F}$, $R_S = 600\,\Omega$, $R_{in} = 1.2\,\text{k}\Omega$.

**Step (i) — Mid-band gain of each stage:**

Effective AC load:
$$R_C \parallel R_L = \frac{4.7 \times 4.7}{4.7 + 4.7} = \frac{22.09}{9.4} = 2.35\,\text{k}\Omega$$

Single stage gain magnitude:
$$\vert A_{v,\text{stage}} \vert = g_m \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L) = 0.05 \times 2350 = 117.5$$

**Step (ii) — Lower cutoff frequency of each stage:**

$$f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S + R_{in}) C_C}$$

$$R_S + R_{in} = 600 + 1200 = 1800\,\Omega = 1.8\,\text{k}\Omega$$

$$f_L = \frac{1}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 1800 \times 10 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$f_L = \frac{1}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 1800 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{1}{0.1131} \approx 8.84\,\text{Hz}$$

**Step (iii) — Cascade lower cutoff:**

$$f_L(\text{cas}) = 1.554 \times 8.84 = 13.74\,\text{Hz}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** A common mistake is using $R_C$ alone (forgetting to take the parallel combination with $R_L$) when computing the mid-band gain. Another frequent error is forgetting to **add $R_S$ and $R_{in}$** in series when computing the lower cutoff — they form a voltage divider with the coupling capacitor. Always convert $\mu\text{F}$ to $\text{F}$ by multiplying by $10^{-6}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cascade amplifier** = multiple stages in series; **overall gain = product of stage gains**; in dB, gains **add**.
- The **−3 dB bandwidth** is defined at points where $\vert A_v \vert = 0.707 A_{v,\text{mid}}$.
- For **two identical stages**, cascade cutoffs are $f_L(\text{cas}) = 1.554 \cdot f_{L1}$ and $f_H(\text{cas}) = 0.644 \cdot f_{H1}$.
- For **$n$ identical stages** (general): $f_L = f_{L1} / \sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}$ and $f_H = f_{H1} \cdot \sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}$.
- **Bandwidth decreases** with cascading, but **gain increases** proportionally — the **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)** remains (approximately) constant.
- **Coupling capacitors** $C_C$ cause the **lower cutoff** $f_L$; **internal transistor capacitances** (especially Miller-multiplied $C_{bc}$) cause the **upper cutoff** $f_H$.
- **Emitter bypass capacitor** $C_E$ is critical for mid-band gain; loss of bypassing at low $f$ is a major contributor to $f_L$.
- **Single-stage CE gain:** $A_v = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$ with $g_m = I_C / V_T$ and $V_T \approx 26\,\text{mV}$ at 300 K.
- **Simulation tools** for KTU lab: Multisim, LTspice, PSpice, Proteus — AC sweep with decade sweep from **10 Hz to 100 MHz** is typical.
- In simulation, **observe the Bode plot** at the **−3 dB** drop from the mid-band flat region to read $f_L$ and $f_H$.
- For dB conversion: $A_{v(\text{dB})} = 20 \log_{10}(\vert A_v \vert)$. Always state units explicitly.
- **Negative sign** of $A_v$ in CE indicates a **180° phase inversion** between input and output — mention this in answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MOSFET Amplifier (CS - Frequency Response Characteristics) Simulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL407 - ANALOG CIRCUITS AND SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** MOSFET Amplifier (CS - Frequency Response Characteristics) Simulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Common Source (CS) MOSFET Amplifier** is a small-signal voltage amplifier configuration in which the input signal is applied between the **Gate** and **Source** terminals, while the amplified output is taken between the **Drain** and **Source** terminals. The Source terminal is therefore *common* to both the input port and the output port — hence the name "Common Source."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Standard):**
> The CS amplifier exhibits a **180° phase inversion** between input and output, provides moderate **voltage gain ($A_v$)**, high **input impedance ($Z_{in}$)**, and moderate **output impedance ($Z_{out}$)**. The **frequency response** describes how the gain magnitude $\vert A_v(f) \vert$ and phase $\angle A_v(f)$ vary as a function of the input signal frequency $f$, typically characterized by a **midband gain ($A_{mid}$)**, **lower cutoff frequency ($f_L$)**, **upper cutoff frequency ($f_H$)**, and **bandwidth ($BW = f_H - f_L$)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Water Tap Amplifier"**
> Imagine a sophisticated water tap. A small turn of the handle (input voltage at the gate) controls a huge flow of water (drain current). The MOSFET works exactly like this: a tiny voltage variation at the gate causes a large current variation at the drain. The **drain resistor ($R_D$)** then converts this large current swing into a large voltage swing at the output.
>
> - **Coupling capacitor ($C_{in}$, $C_{out}$):** Acts like a *one-way valve* — it lets the AC signal pass into the amplifier but blocks any DC bias from disturbing the source or the load. Think of it as a frequency-dependent "door."
> - **Bypass capacitor ($C_S$):** Acts like a *shortcut* for the AC signal around the source resistor $R_S$, so that the AC signal doesn't waste energy on $R_S$, but the DC bias is still maintained by $R_S$.
> - **Frequency response:** Just as a hi-fi speaker cannot reproduce very low bass notes (too slow for the woofer) or very high treble (beyond tweeter range), an amplifier has a "sweet spot" of frequencies (midband) where it works best.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield Highlights)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (CS Amp) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Midband Voltage Gain | $A_{mid}$ | $-g_m (R_D \Vert r_o)$ | V/V (dimensionless) |
| Transconductance | $g_m$ | $1$ to $10$ | mS (milli-Siemens) |
| Input Resistance | $R_{in}$ | $\approx R_1 \Vert R_2$ | $\Omega$ (very high, $\sim$M$\Omega$) |
| Output Resistance | $R_{out}$ | $R_D \Vert r_o$ | $\sim$k$\Omega$ |
| Lower Cutoff Frequency | $f_L$ | $1$ to $100$ | Hz |
| Upper Cutoff Frequency | $f_H$ | $100$k to $10$M | Hz |
| Bandwidth | $BW$ | $f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ | Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bandwidth is the "usable range" of the amplifier.** Outside this range, the gain drops below $0.707 \cdot A_{mid}$ (the **$-3$ dB point**).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Three Frequency Regions

The frequency response of any CS amplifier is divided into three distinct regions. The transition between them is governed by the reactive elements (capacitors) in the circuit.

### 2.1.1 Midband Region ( $f_L \ll f \ll f_H$ )

- All coupling and bypass capacitors behave as **short circuits** ( $X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C} \to 0$ ).
- All internal parasitic (Miller) capacitances behave as **open circuits** ( $X_C \to \infty$ ).
- The circuit is **purely resistive**, and the gain is constant and maximum.
- The small-signal model uses only $g_m$, $R_D$, $r_o$, and the bias network.

### 2.1.2 Low-Frequency Region ( $f < f_L$ )

- Coupling capacitors ($C_{in}$, $C_{out}$) and bypass capacitor ($C_S$) begin to dominate.
- Their increasing reactance ($X_C$ rises as $f$ drops) attenuates the signal.
- **Three high-pass RC networks** are formed, each contributing a pole:
  - **Input coupling pole:** $f_{L1} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S + R_{in}) C_{in}}$
  - **Output coupling pole:** $f_{L2} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_{out} + R_L) C_{out}}$
  - **Source bypass pole:** $f_{L3} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S \Vert \frac{1}{g_m}) C_S}$
- The **dominant pole** is the largest of these three, and the overall $f_L$ is approximately equal to the sum:
  
  $$f_L \approx f_{L1} + f_{L2} + f_{L3}$$

### 2.1.3 High-Frequency Region ( $f > f_H$ )

- Coupling/bypass capacitors now look like short circuits (no effect).
- **Parasitic capacitances** $C_{gs}$ (gate-source) and $C_{gd}$ (gate-drain, the **Miller capacitance**) dominate.
- The **Miller effect** multiplies $C_{gd}$ by $(1 - A_v)$, creating a large effective input capacitance:

$$C_{M} = C_{gd} (1 - A_v)$$

- This forms a low-pass filter with the Thevenin resistance seen at the gate, producing:

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{sig}' (C_{gs} + C_M)}$$

- As $f$ rises past $f_H$, $\vert A_v \vert$ rolls off at **$-20$ dB/decade**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the gain rolls off at high frequencies?**
> At high $f$, the internal transistor capacitances act like short circuits, *bypassing* the high-gain amplifying action. The MOSFET can no longer "follow" the rapidly changing input.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind the CS Topology

- The CS configuration is the **workhorse of analog IC design** because it provides both **voltage gain** and **current gain**, making it ideal for the first stage of multistage amplifiers.
- The **180° phase inversion** is useful in feedback oscillator design.
- The **high input impedance** ensures that the amplifier does not load the previous stage.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_D = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS})$ | **DC drain current** in saturation (with channel-length modulation $\lambda$) |
| 2 | $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th}) = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} I_D}$ | **Transconductance** — converts $v_{gs}$ to $i_d$ |
| 3 | $r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{V_A}{I_D}$ | **Output resistance of MOSFET** (Early voltage $V_A$) |
| 4 | $A_{mid} = -g_m (R_D \Vert r_o \Vert R_L)$ | **Midband voltage gain** (negative sign ⇒ 180° inversion) |
| 5 | $R_{in} = R_1 \Vert R_2$ | **Input resistance** set by bias divider |
| 6 | $R_{out} = R_D \Vert r_o$ | **Output resistance** looking into drain |
| 7 | $C_M = C_{gd} (1 + \vert A_{mid} \vert)$ | **Miller capacitance** at the input (using magnitude) |
| 8 | $f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} (C_{gs} + C_M)}$ | **Upper cutoff frequency** |
| 9 | $f_L \approx f_{L,in} + f_{L,out} + f_{L,source}$ | **Approximate lower cutoff** (sum of poles) |
| 10 | $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ | **Bandwidth** |
| 11 | $\text{Gain (dB)} = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$ | **Decibel conversion** |
| 12 | $f_{3dB}$: frequency where $\vert A_v \vert = \frac{A_{mid}}{\sqrt{2}}$ | **$-3$ dB cutoff** definition |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Board Tip:** When asked for "frequency response," examiners expect a **Bode plot sketch** with labelled $f_L$, $f_H$, midband gain in dB, and slopes of $\pm 20$ dB/decade outside the band. Always mention the **$-3$ dB point**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Midband Voltage Gain $A_{mid}$

We start with the small-signal equivalent of the CS amplifier. In midband, all capacitors are open or short, leaving only controlled sources and resistors.

**Step 1 — Replace the MOSFET with its small-signal model.**

The MOSFET becomes a voltage-controlled current source $g_m v_{gs}$ between drain and source, with output resistance $r_o$ in parallel.

**Step 2 — Identify the load seen at the drain.**

Looking from the drain node, three resistances appear in parallel:
- $R_D$ (drain resistor)
- $r_o$ (intrinsic MOSFET output resistance)
- $R_L$ (external load, coupled through $C_{out}$ which is a short at midband)

Define the effective AC load:

$$R_{L}' = R_D \Vert r_o \Vert R_L = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{R_D} + \frac{1}{r_o} + \frac{1}{R_L}}$$

**Step 3 — Write KCL at the drain node.**

The output voltage $v_{out}$ is the voltage developed across $R_L'$ by the current source $g_m v_{gs}$ flowing *out of* the drain (drain current decreases when $v_{gs}$ increases, hence the negative sign):

$$v_{out} = -g_m v_{gs} \cdot R_{L}'$$

**Step 4 — Recognize that $v_{gs} = v_{in}$ (since the gate draws no current and $R_S$ is bypassed).**

$$v_{gs} = v_{in}$$

**Step 5 — Form the voltage gain ratio.**

$$A_{mid} = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = \frac{-g_m v_{gs} R_{L}'}{v_{gs}}$$

$$\boxed{A_{mid} = -g_m (R_D \Vert r_o \Vert R_L)}$$

> The **negative sign** indicates the **180° phase inversion** characteristic of the CS topology.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Transconductance $g_m$ from the DC Bias Point

The MOSFET in saturation obeys the square-law:

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} k_n \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$$

where $k_n = \mu_n C_{ox}$ is the process transconductance parameter (units: $\mu$A/V²).

**Step 1 — Take the partial derivative of $I_D$ with respect to $V_{GS}$ at the Q-point (small-signal definition of $g_m$):**

$$g_m = \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}}\bigg\vert_{Q} = \frac{1}{2} k_n \frac{W}{L} \cdot 2 (V_{GSQ} - V_{th})$$

$$g_m = k_n \frac{W}{L} (V_{GSQ} - V_{th})$$

**Step 2 — Express in terms of the DC drain current $I_{DQ}$.**

Substitute $V_{GSQ} - V_{th} = \sqrt{\frac{2 I_{DQ}}{k_n (W/L)}}$:

$$g_m = k_n \frac{W}{L} \sqrt{\frac{2 I_{DQ}}{k_n (W/L)}}$$

$$\boxed{g_m = \sqrt{2 k_n \frac{W}{L} I_{DQ}}}$$

This is the form most often quoted in KTU problems because the Q-point current $I_{DQ}$ is usually given or computed first.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Miller Capacitance and Upper Cutoff Frequency

The **Miller effect** is the single most important high-frequency phenomenon in CS amplifiers. It explains why CS stages lose gain at high frequencies much faster than common-gate or source-follower stages.

**Step 1 — Identify the parasitic capacitances.**

In the small-signal high-frequency model, two internal capacitances appear:
- $C_{gs}$: between gate and source (input capacitance)
- $C_{gd}$: between gate and drain (feedback / Miller capacitance)

**Step 2 — Recognize that $C_{gd}$ is "floated" between input and output.**

The current through $C_{gd}$ depends on the voltage across it: $v_{gd} = v_{gs} - v_{ds} = v_{in} - v_{out}$.

Since $v_{out} = A_v \cdot v_{in}$ and $A_v$ is large and negative:

$$v_{gd} = v_{in} (1 - A_v)$$

**Step 3 — Replace $C_{gd}$ with an equivalent capacitance at the input node.**

The current drawn from the input by $C_{gd}$ is:

$$i_{C_{gd}} = C_{gd} \frac{d v_{gd}}{dt} = C_{gd} (1 - A_v) \frac{d v_{in}}{dt}$$

This is mathematically equivalent to a capacitance $C_M$ at the input node where:

$$\boxed{C_M = C_{gd} (1 - A_v) = C_{gd} (1 + \vert A_v \vert)}$$

(Using magnitude since $A_v$ is negative; $1 - A_v$ is large and positive.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** Because the output is inverted and amplified, the small input voltage $v_{gs}$ appears across $C_{gd}$ multiplied by $(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$, so the charging current is much larger. The capacitor effectively "looks" $(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ times bigger at the input.

**Step 4 — Compute the upper cutoff frequency.**

The total capacitance at the gate input is now $C_{gs} + C_M$, and the Thevenin resistance seen at the gate is the source resistance $R_{sig}$ in parallel with $R_1 \Vert R_2$:

$$R_{th} = R_{sig} \Vert R_1 \Vert R_2$$

The high-frequency pole is:

$$\boxed{f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} (C_{gs} + C_M)} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} \left[ C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_{mid} \vert) \right]}}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

> **Problem:** A CS amplifier uses an NMOS with $k_n (W/L) = 2$ mA/V², $V_{th} = 1$ V, biased at $I_{DQ} = 1$ mA. The drain resistor $R_D = 4.7$ k$\Omega$, load $R_L = 10$ k$\Omega$, source bypass perfect. Assume $r_o = \infty$, $C_{gs} = 5$ pF, $C_{gd} = 1$ pF, and $R_{sig} = 1$ k$\Omega$. Compute $g_m$, $A_{mid}$ (in dB), and $f_H$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $g_m$:**

$$g_m = \sqrt{2 \cdot k_n (W/L) \cdot I_{DQ}} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 2 \text{ mA/V}^2 \cdot 1 \text{ mA}}$$

$$g_m = \sqrt{4 \text{ mA}^2/\text{V}^2} = 2 \text{ mA/V} = 2 \text{ mS}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $A_{mid}$ (in V/V, then dB):**

$$R_L' = R_D \Vert R_L = \frac{4.7 \text{k} \cdot 10 \text{k}}{4.7 \text{k} + 10 \text{k}} = \frac{47}{14.7} \approx 3.197 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$A_{mid} = -g_m R_L' = -(2 \text{ mS}) \cdot (3.197 \text{ k}\Omega) = -6.394 \text{ V/V}$$

$$A_{mid}(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(6.394) = 20 \cdot 0.806 = 16.12 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Miller capacitance:**

$$C_M = C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_{mid} \vert) = 1 \text{ pF} \cdot (1 + 6.394) = 7.394 \text{ pF}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the total input capacitance:**

$$C_{in,total} = C_{gs} + C_M = 5 \text{ pF} + 7.394 \text{ pF} = 12.394 \text{ pF}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $R_{th}$ (assuming $R_1 \Vert R_2 \gg R_{sig}$, so $R_{th} \approx R_{sig} = 1$ k$\Omega$):**

$$R_{th} \approx 1 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 6 — Compute $f_H$:**

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 1000 \cdot 12.394 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{1}{7.788 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$\boxed{f_H \approx 12.84 \text{ MHz}}$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation:** The amplifier has a midband gain of **16.12 dB** and a bandwidth extending up to **12.84 MHz**. The bandwidth is dominated by the Miller-multiplied $C_{gd}$, illustrating why CS stages are *not* used at very high frequencies.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Element | Low-Frequency Effect | High-Frequency Effect |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{in}$ | Forms HPF with $R_S + R_{in}$ | — |
| $C_{out}$ | Forms HPF with $R_{out} + R_L$ | — |
| $C_S$ | Forms HPF with $R_S \Vert (1/g_m)$ | — |
| $C_{gs}$ | — | Adds to input capacitance |
| $C_{gd}$ | — | **Miller-multiplied** ⇒ dominates $f_H$ |
| $r_o$ | — | Slightly reduces $\vert A_v \vert$ |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Common Source MOSFET Amplifier — Circuit Schematic

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** of the CS amplifier, showing signal path, biasing, and the critical reactive elements. (Since physical transistor symbols cannot be rendered in Mermaid safely, a block-level flow is provided that captures all the electrical relationships.)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[VSig AC Source] -->|via Rsig| B(Cin Coupling Capacitor)
    B --> C[GATE Node]
    C --> D[R1 Pull-up to VDD]
    C --> E[R2 Pull-down to GND]
    C --> F[MOSFET Gate]
    F --> G[Cgs Parasitic]
    G --> H[SOURCE Node]
    H --> I[CS Bypass Capacitor to GND]
    H --> J[RS Source Resistor to GND]
    F --> K[Cgd Parasitic Miller]
    K --> L[DRAIN Node]
    L --> M[RD to VDD]
    L --> N[gm*vgs Controlled Current Source]
    N --> H
    L --> O(Cout Coupling Capacitor)
    O --> P[RL Load Resistor to GND]
    P --> Q[Vout Output Node]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **vertical column** of the MOSFET (Gate → Drain) carries the **signal flow**.
- **$C_{in}$** and **$C_{out}$** are AC-coupling "doors" (block DC, pass AC).
- **$C_S$** is the bypass that grounds the source for AC while keeping $R_S$ effective for DC bias.
- **$C_{gs}$** and **$C_{gd}$** are intrinsic to the device and cannot be removed — they set the **high-frequency limit**.

---

## 4.2 Bode Plot — Frequency Response (Gain vs. Frequency)

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[Low Freq Region: Gain rises at +20 dB/decade] --> M1
    M1[Midband: Flat Gain A_mid in dB] --> H1
    H1[High Freq Region: Gain falls at -20 dB/decade]
    M1 --> M2[fL = Lower Cutoff at -3dB below A_mid]
    M1 --> M3[fH = Upper Cutoff at -3dB below A_mid]
```

**Standard Bode Plot Sketch (to be drawn by student in exam):**

```
   |A_v| (dB)
        |
  A_mid |-------------------------____________________
   (dB) |                        /                    \
        |                       /                      \
        |                      /                        \
  A_mid-|---------------------/<----- -3 dB points ----->\
   3dB  |                    / f_L                 f_H     \
        |                   /                                \
        |                  /                                  \
        |_________________/____________________________________\____
        0                f_L                              f_H   f (Hz, log scale)
                 (+20 dB/dec)              (-20 dB/dec)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Bode Plot Must-Haves:**
> 1. **X-axis** = logarithmic frequency (Hz).
> 2. **Y-axis** = $\vert A_v \vert$ in **dB**.
> 3. **Midband line** drawn as **horizontal**, labelled with $A_{mid}$ (dB).
> 4. **$-3$ dB points** clearly marked at $f_L$ and $f_H$.
> 5. **Slopes** of $\pm 20$ dB/decade labelled on both roll-off regions.

---

## 4.3 Small-Signal High-Frequency Equivalent Circuit

```mermaid
graph TD
    InNode[v_sig via R_sig] --> RthNode[R_th = Rsig in parallel with R1 parallel R2]
    RthNode --> Gnode[Gate Node]
    Gnode --> Cgs[(Cgs)]
    Cgs --> Snode[Source = AC Ground]
    Gnode --> Cgd[(Cgd Miller)]
    Cgd --> Dnode[Drain Node]
    Dnode --> gmSrc[(gm * vgs Current Source)]
    gmSrc --> Snode
    Dnode --> RoNode[ro in parallel with RD]
    RoNode --> Snode
    Dnode --> VoutNode[v_out]
```

This is the **exact equivalent circuit** used in hand calculations of $f_H$. The $C_{gd}$ between gate and drain is the source of the Miller multiplication.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "Miller Effect" in a CS MOSFET amplifier. Why is it critical at high frequencies? `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Miller effect refers to the **apparent multiplication of a feedback capacitance** $C_{gd}$ when reflected to the input of an inverting amplifier. For a CS stage with midband gain $A_v$ (negative), the effective input capacitance becomes:

$$C_M = C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$$

This **drastically increases the input capacitance**, lowering the upper cutoff frequency $f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th}(C_{gs} + C_M)}$. It is critical because it is the **dominant high-frequency limitation** in CS amplifiers — even a tiny $C_{gd}$ of 1 pF can become 10 pF or more at the input, severely limiting $f_H$. **(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for significance.)**

---

### Question 2: What is the "$-3$ dB Bandwidth" of an amplifier? How is it related to $f_L$ and $f_H$? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **$-3$ dB bandwidth (BW)** is the range of frequencies over which the amplifier's gain magnitude is at least $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ (or 70.7%) of its midband value. It corresponds to the **half-power point**, where output power drops to half the midband power.

It is defined as:

$$BW = f_H - f_L$$

For most CS amplifiers where $f_H \gg f_L$, $BW \approx f_H$. The frequencies $f_L$ and $f_H$ are called the **lower and upper $-3$ dB cutoff frequencies** (or corner frequencies), at which the gain has dropped by exactly 3 dB from the midband level. **(3 marks.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1 `[KTU Model Exam Paper – 2024]`

**Q.A.(a)** Design the DC bias of a CS amplifier using an NMOS transistor with $V_{th} = 1.5$ V, $k_n (W/L) = 1$ mA/V², $V_{DD} = 12$ V. Choose $I_{DQ} = 1$ mA and $V_{DSQ} = 6$ V. Compute $R_D$, $R_S$, and the small-signal $g_m$. Assume $R_{sig} = 1$ k$\Omega$, $C_{gs} = 4$ pF, $C_{gd} = 1.2$ pF, $R_L = 5$ k$\Omega$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $g_m$ from the Q-point:**

$$g_m = \sqrt{2 \cdot k_n (W/L) \cdot I_{DQ}} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 1 \text{ mA/V}^2 \cdot 1 \text{ mA}} = \sqrt{2} \text{ mA/V} \approx 1.414 \text{ mS}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $V_{GSQ}$:**

From $I_D = \frac{1}{2} k_n (W/L) (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$:

$$1 = 0.5 \cdot 1 \cdot (V_{GS} - 1.5)^2 \Rightarrow (V_{GS} - 1.5)^2 = 2 \Rightarrow V_{GS} = 1.5 + \sqrt{2} \approx 2.914 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $R_D$ from the KVL on the drain-source loop:**

$$V_{DD} = I_{DQ} R_D + V_{DSQ} + I_{DQ} R_S$$

Choosing $R_S = 1$ k$\Omega$ (typical, provides DC stability):

$$R_D = \frac{V_{DD} - V_{DSQ} - I_{DQ} R_S}{I_{DQ}} = \frac{12 - 6 - 1 \cdot 1}{1 \text{ mA}} = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Compute midband gain (assuming $r_o = \infty$):**

$$R_L' = R_D \Vert R_L = \frac{5 \cdot 5}{5 + 5} = 2.5 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$A_{mid} = -g_m R_L' = -(1.414 \text{ mS})(2.5 \text{ k}\Omega) = -3.535 \text{ V/V}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Miller capacitance:**

$$C_M = C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_{mid} \vert) = 1.2 \text{ pF} \cdot (1 + 3.535) = 5.44 \text{ pF}$$

**Step 6 — Compute $f_H$ (assuming $R_1 \Vert R_2 \gg R_{sig}$):**

$$R_{th} \approx R_{sig} = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$C_{in,total} = C_{gs} + C_M = 4 + 5.44 = 9.44 \text{ pF}$$
