$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 1000 \cdot 9.44 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{1}{5.93 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 16.86 \text{ MHz}$$

**Final Answers:** $R_D = 5$ k$\Omega$, $R_S = 1$ k$\Omega$, $g_m \approx 1.414$ mS, $A_{mid} = -3.54$ V/V, $f_H \approx 16.86$ MHz. **(7 marks: 2 for bias, 2 for $g_m$ and $A_{mid}$, 3 for $f_H$.)**

---

**Q.A.(b)** With the design in (a), sketch the Bode magnitude plot of $\vert A_v(f) \vert$ in dB. Identify the midband gain, $f_L$ (assume $f_L \approx 50$ Hz from coupling caps), and $f_H$. Mark all $-3$ dB points. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert $A_{mid}$ to dB:**

$$A_{mid}(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(3.535) = 20 \cdot 0.5485 = 10.97 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the $-3$ dB points:**

- At $f_L = 50$ Hz: gain $= 10.97 - 3 = 7.97$ dB.
- At $f_H \approx 16.86$ MHz: gain $= 7.97$ dB.

**Step 3 — Sketch the Bode plot:**

```
   |A_v| (dB)
        |
  10.97 |==========================________________________
   dB   |                         /                        \
        |                        /                          \
        |                       /                            \
   7.97 |----------------------/<------- -3 dB ----------->---\
   dB   |                     /   50 Hz          16.86 MHz     \
        |                    /                                    \
        |                   /                                      \
        |__________________/________________________________________\____
        0.1 Hz           50 Hz                          16.86 MHz   f
                   (+20 dB/dec)             (-20 dB/dec)
```

**Step 4 — Compute the bandwidth:**

$$BW = f_H - f_L = 16.86 \text{ MHz} - 50 \text{ Hz} \approx 16.86 \text{ MHz}$$

**Final answer:** $A_{mid} \approx 10.97$ dB, $f_L = 50$ Hz, $f_H \approx 16.86$ MHz, $BW \approx 16.86$ MHz. The plot shows the classic band-pass response with $+20$ dB/decade rise below $f_L$ and $-20$ dB/decade fall above $f_H$. **(7 marks: 1 for dB conversion, 2 for $-3$ dB computation, 4 for plot with correct labels and slopes.)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**Q.B.(a)** For a CS amplifier, derive the expression for the **midband voltage gain** $A_{mid} = -g_m (R_D \Vert r_o)$ starting from the small-signal model. State clearly any assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
- Midband frequency: $C_{in}$, $C_{out}$, $C_S$ are short circuits; $C_{gs}$, $C_{gd}$ are open circuits.
- Source bypass capacitor is ideal, so $R_S$ does not appear in the AC model.
- $R_1 \Vert R_2 \gg R_{sig}$, so input signal reaches the gate unattenuated.
- Small-signal operation: $v_{gs} \ll 2(V_{GS} - V_{th})$.

**Step 1 — Draw the small-signal model:** Replace MOSFET with $g_m v_{gs}$ current source in parallel with $r_o$. Source is AC ground. Drain sees $R_D \Vert r_o$ to ground.

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at the drain node:**

Current entering the node from the current source: $-g_m v_{gs}$ (sign convention).
Current leaving through $R_D \Vert r_o$ to ground: $\frac{v_{out}}{R_D \Vert r_o}$.

Setting sum to zero:

$$-g_m v_{gs} = \frac{v_{out}}{R_D \Vert r_o}$$

**Step 3 — Note that $v_{gs} = v_{in}$ (no gate current, source is grounded for AC):**

$$v_{out} = -g_m v_{in} (R_D \Vert r_o)$$

**Step 4 — Form the gain ratio:**

$$\boxed{A_{mid} = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -g_m (R_D \Vert r_o)}$$

The negative sign represents the **180° phase inversion**. **(7 marks: 2 for assumptions, 3 for derivation, 1 for result, 1 for physical interpretation.)**

---

**Q.B.(b)** A CS amplifier has $g_m = 5$ mS, $R_D = 2$ k$\Omega$, $R_L = 4$ k$\Omega$, $r_o = 20$ k$\Omega$, $C_{gs} = 5$ pF, $C_{gd} = 2$ pF, $R_{sig} = 1$ k$\Omega$. Calculate the midband gain in dB and the upper cutoff frequency $f_H$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the effective AC load:**

$$R_L' = R_D \Vert r_o \Vert R_L$$

First, $R_D \Vert R_L = \frac{2 \cdot 4}{2 + 4} = \frac{8}{6} = 1.333 \text{ k}\Omega$

Then, $R_L' = 1.333 \text{k} \Vert 20 \text{k} = \frac{1.333 \cdot 20}{1.333 + 20} = \frac{26.66}{21.33} = 1.25 \text{ k}\Omega$

**Step 2 — Compute the midband gain (V/V and dB):**

$$A_{mid} = -g_m R_L' = -(5 \text{ mS})(1.25 \text{ k}\Omega) = -6.25 \text{ V/V}$$

$$A_{mid}(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(6.25) = 20 \cdot 0.796 = 15.92 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Miller capacitance:**

$$C_M = C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_{mid} \vert) = 2 \text{ pF} \cdot (1 + 6.25) = 2 \text{ pF} \cdot 7.25 = 14.5 \text{ pF}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the total input capacitance:**

$$C_{in,total} = C_{gs} + C_M = 5 + 14.5 = 19.5 \text{ pF}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $R_{th}$ (assume $R_1 \Vert R_2$ is very large):**

$$R_{th} \approx R_{sig} = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 6 — Compute $f_H$:**

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 1000 \cdot 19.5 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{1}{1.225 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$\boxed{f_H \approx 8.16 \text{ MHz}}$$

**Final Answer:** $A_{mid} \approx 15.92$ dB, $f_H \approx 8.16$ MHz. The Miller multiplication has enlarged the 2 pF $C_{gd}$ to 14.5 pF, dominating the high-frequency response. **(7 marks: 2 for $A_{mid}$ computation, 2 for Miller calc, 3 for $f_H$ computation.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Sign error in $A_{mid}$:** The CS gain is **negative** (180° inversion). Writing $+g_m R_L'$ loses a mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the magnitude in Miller:** $C_M = C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$, **not** $C_{gd}(1 - A_v)$. Many students confuse the sign.
> 3. **Using $R_D$ alone instead of $R_D \Vert R_L$:** The external load must be included in the AC gain calculation, but **not** in the DC bias calculation.
> 4. **Bode plot missing the $-3$ dB point label:** Always mark both $f_L$ and $f_H$ with explicit $-3$ dB indication.
> 5. **Forgetting dB conversion:** KTU expects gains expressed in dB for frequency response questions.
> 6. **Confusing $C_{in}$ (coupling) with $C_{gs}$ (parasitic):** Coupling caps set $f_L$, parasitic caps set $f_H$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **CS Amplifier** = input at Gate, output at Drain, Source is **common** (AC ground via $C_S$).
- ✅ **Midband gain:** $A_{mid} = -g_m (R_D \Vert r_o \Vert R_L)$ — **always negative** (180° inversion).
- ✅ **Transconductance:** $g_m = \sqrt{2 k_n (W/L) I_{DQ}}$ — depends on the Q-point current.
- ✅ **Three low-frequency poles:** input coupling, output coupling, and source bypass — $f_L$ ≈ sum of these.
- ✅ **Miller effect:** $C_M = C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_{mid} \vert)$ — the dominant high-frequency pole mechanism.
- ✅ **Upper cutoff:** $f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th}(C_{gs} + C_M)}$ — falls as gain rises (Gain-Bandwidth trade-off).
- ✅ **Bandwidth:** $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ for wideband amplifiers.
- ✅ **Bode plot must show:** midband flat region, $+20$ dB/decade low-freq roll-on, $-20$ dB/decade high-freq roll-off, $-3$ dB points at $f_L$ and $f_H$.
- ✅ **Phase response:** midband = 180°, transitions to 90° at very low $f$ and 270° at very high $f$ (extra $-90$° from $C_{gd}$).
- ✅ **SPICE simulation tip:** Use `.ac dec 10 1 100Meg` for frequency response sweep, `.op` for Q-point, `.tran` for transient waveform.
- ✅ **Decibel formula:** $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$; the $-3$ dB point corresponds to $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707$ of midband gain.
- ✅ **Key engineering trade-off:** Increasing $R_D$ raises $A_{mid}$ but lowers $f_H$ (worsens bandwidth) — a classic Gain-BandWidth Product (GBW) limit.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feedback Amplifiers (Current Series, Voltage Series) Simulation - Gain and Frequency Response
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL407 - ANALOG CIRCUITS AND SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Feedback Amplifiers (Current Series, Voltage Series) Simulation - Gain and Frequency Response

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **PCAEL407 - Analog Circuits and Simulation Lab (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, Module Experiment 2 focuses on the **simulation of feedback amplifiers** and the verification of their **gain and frequency response** characteristics. A **Feedback Amplifier** is a multi-port network formed by cascading an **Basic Amplifier (A)** with a **Feedback Network (β)** such that a fraction of the output signal is returned to the input, modifying the overall transfer behavior.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus classification, feedback topologies are categorized into four canonical types based on the **sampling method** (voltage or current at the output) and the **mixing method** (series or shunt at the input). The two topologies covered in this experiment are:

- **Voltage Series Feedback (V-SFB)** — Voltage sampling, Series mixing. Acts as a **Voltage-Controlled Voltage Source (VCVS)**.
- **Current Series Feedback (I-SFB)** — Current sampling, Series mixing. Acts as a **Current-Controlled Voltage Source (CCVS)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCAEL407, Module 2):** The student is expected to **simulate the feedback amplifier circuit, plot the gain-versus-frequency (Bode magnitude) response, and extract the bandwidth, midband gain, and the 3 dB cutoff frequencies** using tools like LTspice, Multisim, or PSpice. Tabulation of theoretical versus simulated values is a mandatory part of the record.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **feedback amplifier** like a **smart cruise-control system in a car**:

- **Without feedback (Open Loop):** You press the accelerator based on rough guess. The car's speed is unpredictable — too fast uphill, too slow downhill.
- **With feedback (Closed Loop):** A sensor continuously measures the actual speed, compares it to your setpoint (the **error signal**), and adjusts the accelerator automatically. The result is **stable, predictable, and accurate speed**, regardless of road conditions.

In electronics, the **sensor is the feedback network (β)**, the **setpoint is the input signal (V<sub>in</sub>)**, and the **error signal (V<sub>ε</sub> = V<sub>in</sub> − V<sub>fb</sub>)** drives the amplifier. The amount of feedback is measured by the **loop gain (Aβ)**, which is the key parameter determining stability, gain accuracy, bandwidth, and distortion reduction.

| Real-World Analogy | Electronic Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Steering wheel input | Input signal V<sub>in</sub> |
| Car's actual direction | Output signal V<sub>out</sub> or I<sub>out</sub> |
| Wind / disturbance | Noise, temperature drift, component tolerances |
| Driver's correction | Feedback signal V<sub>fb</sub> or I<sub>fb</sub> |
| Cruise control algorithm | Feedback network β |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Loop Gain (Aβ):** The product of the open-loop gain (A) of the basic amplifier and the feedback factor (β) of the feedback network. When Aβ ≫ 1, the closed-loop gain becomes approximately **1/β**, which is set by passive components and hence extremely stable.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **3 dB Bandwidth (f<sub>H</sub>):** The frequency at which the closed-loop gain falls to **0.707** of its midband value, equivalent to a **−3 dB** drop in the logarithmic magnitude.
- **Midband Gain (A<sub>M</sub>):** The flat, constant gain in the frequency range where the amplifier behaves as an ideal voltage or current amplifier (no reactive effects).
- **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW):** For a single-pole amplifier, GBW = A<sub>M</sub> × f<sub>H</sub> = constant. Feedback increases f<sub>H</sub> by the factor (1 + Aβ) while decreasing A<sub>M</sub> by the same factor, preserving GBW.
- **Noise Figure Reduction:** The signal-to-noise ratio improves by the factor (1 + Aβ) for noise generated within the feedback loop.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** Negative feedback **trades gain for performance**. By sacrificing some gain, we obtain benefits such as increased bandwidth, reduced distortion, stabilized gain, and modified input/output impedances. This is the fundamental design philosophy behind every operational amplifier.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Canonical Feedback Topologies

The four canonical feedback topologies are derived from a **2×2 combinatorial matrix** of sampling methods (voltage or current) and mixing methods (series or shunt):

| Sampling → / Mixing ↓ | **Voltage Sampling** | **Current Sampling** |
|---|---|---|
| **Series Mixing** | Voltage-Series (V-SFB) — **VCVS** | Current-Series (I-SFB) — **CCVS** |
| **Shunt Mixing** | Voltage-Shunt (V-PFB) — **VCCS** | Current-Shunt (I-PFB) — **CCCS** |

Where:
- **VCVS** = Voltage-Controlled Voltage Source → Input is V, Output is V → R<sub>i</sub> ↑, R<sub>o</sub> ↓
- **CCVS** = Current-Controlled Voltage Source → Input is I, Output is V → R<sub>i</sub> ↑, R<sub>o</sub> ↑
- **VCCS** = Voltage-Controlled Current Source → Input is V, Output is I → R<sub>i</sub> ↓, R<sub>o</sub> ↑
- **CCCS** = Current-Controlled Current Source → Input is I, Output is I → R<sub>i</sub> ↓, R<sub>o</sub> ↓

### 2.2 Generalized Closed-Loop Transfer Function

For any negative feedback amplifier:

$$A_{CL} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$

Where:
- **A** = Open-loop gain of the basic amplifier (without feedback)
- **β** = Reverse transmission factor of the feedback network
- **Aβ** = Loop gain (return ratio)
- **(1 + Aβ)** = Desensitivity factor (Return Difference)

**Core operational principles:**

- If **Aβ ≫ 1** (deep negative feedback), then **A<sub>CL</sub> ≈ 1/β**, becoming essentially independent of A.
- The closed-loop gain is **reduced by (1 + Aβ)**.
- The **bandwidth is extended by (1 + Aβ)**.
- The **nonlinear distortion is reduced by (1 + Aβ)**.
- The **sensitivity to temperature, supply, and component variations is reduced by (1 + Aβ)**.

### 2.3 Effect of Feedback on Input and Output Impedances

**Why does feedback modify impedances?** Because the feedback signal subtracts from the input, the effective driving-point impedance changes. Series mixing increases input impedance (because the source sees the error voltage, not the full input), while shunt mixing decreases it. Similarly, voltage sampling decreases output impedance, while current sampling increases it.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table — Essential Formulas for Feedback Amplifier Analysis (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

| # | Parameter | Voltage-Series Feedback (V-SFB) | Current-Series Feedback (I-SFB) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sampling Quantity | Output Voltage V<sub>o</sub> | Output Current I<sub>o</sub> |
| 2 | Mixing Quantity | Feedback Voltage V<sub>f</sub> in series with V<sub>s</sub> | Feedback Voltage V<sub>f</sub> in series with V<sub>s</sub> |
| 3 | Controlled Source Type | VCVS | CCVS |
| 4 | Input Signal | V<sub>s</sub> (voltage) | I<sub>s</sub> (current) |
| 5 | Output Signal | V<sub>o</sub> (voltage) | V<sub>o</sub> (voltage) |
| 6 | Basic Amplifier Type | Transconductance from V to V (voltage amp) | Transresistance (current-to-voltage) |
| 7 | Feedback Factor β | V<sub>f</sub>/V<sub>o</sub> (dimensionless) | V<sub>f</sub>/I<sub>o</sub> (resistance, Ω) |
| 8 | Closed-Loop Gain A<sub>f</sub> | A<sub>v</sub> / (1 + A<sub>v</sub>β) | A<sub>v</sub> / (1 + A<sub>v</sub>β) ≈ 1/β |
| 9 | Input Resistance R<sub>if</sub> | R<sub>i</sub>(1 + Aβ) — **increases** | R<sub>i</sub>(1 + Aβ) — **increases** |
| 10 | Output Resistance R<sub>of</sub> | R<sub>o</sub> / (1 + Aβ) — **decreases** | R<sub>o</sub>(1 + Aβ) — **increases** |
| 11 | Bandwidth f<sub>Hf</sub> | f<sub>H</sub>(1 + Aβ) — **increases** | f<sub>H</sub>(1 + Aβ) — **increases** |
| 12 | Midband Gain A<sub>Mf</sub> | A<sub>M</sub> / (1 + Aβ) — **decreases** | A<sub>M</sub> / (1 + Aβ) — **decreases** |
| 13 | GBW Product | A<sub>M</sub>·f<sub>H</sub> = A<sub>Mf</sub>·f<sub>Hf</sub> (constant) | A<sub>M</sub>·f<sub>H</sub> = A<sub>Mf</sub>·f<sub>Hf</sub> (constant) |
| 14 | Voltage Gain A<sub>vf</sub> | A<sub>v</sub> / (1 + A<sub>v</sub>β) | (A<sub>i</sub>·R<sub>L</sub>) / (1 + Aβ) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** V-SFB is the workhorse topology for **op-amp non-inverting amplifier** design. I-SFB is the classic topology for the **emitter-degenerated BJT/FET amplifier**, where the unbypassed emitter resistor R<sub>E</sub> provides the feedback voltage V<sub>f</sub> = I<sub>o</sub>·R<sub>E</sub>.

### 2.5 Frequency Response Analysis

A practical single-stage BJT amplifier has a **low-frequency pole** introduced by coupling/bypass capacitors and a **high-frequency pole** due to internal transistor capacitances (C<sub>π</sub>, C<sub>μ</sub>). The open-loop voltage gain with one dominant high-frequency pole at f<sub>H</sub> is:

$$A_v(s) = \frac{A_M}{1 + s/(2\pi f_H)}$$

With negative feedback, the closed-loop transfer function becomes:

$$A_{vf}(s) = \frac{A_M/(1+A_M\beta)}{1 + s/[2\pi f_H(1+A_M\beta)]}$$

The new upper cutoff frequency is **f<sub>Hf</sub> = f<sub>H</sub>(1 + Aβ)**, and the new midband gain is **A<sub>Mf</sub> = A<sub>M</sub>/(1 + Aβ)**. The product remains invariant: **A<sub>M</sub>·f<sub>H</sub> = A<sub>Mf</sub>·f<sub>Hf</sub>**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Generic Closed-Loop Gain Equation

**Statement:** A basic amplifier with open-loop gain A is cascaded with a feedback network β that samples the output and feeds back a signal to the input, where it is subtracted from the source.

**Step 1 — Define the open-loop gain of the basic amplifier (without feedback):**

The output V<sub>o</sub> is related to the input V<sub>ε</sub> (the error signal at the amplifier input) by:

$$V_o = A \cdot V_\varepsilon$$

**Step 2 — Define the feedback signal sampled from the output:**

The feedback network samples the output and produces a feedback signal V<sub>f</sub>:

$$V_f = \beta \cdot V_o$$

**Step 3 — Apply KVL/KCL at the input node to express the error signal:**

For **series mixing**, the input source V<sub>s</sub> drives the series combination of the source resistance and the feedback voltage opposes the input. The error voltage is the difference between the source and the feedback:

$$V_\varepsilon = V_s - V_f$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Step 2 into Step 3:**

$$V_\varepsilon = V_s - \beta \cdot V_o$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Step 4 into Step 1:**

$$V_o = A \cdot (V_s - \beta \cdot V_o)$$

**Step 6 — Expand and collect terms containing V<sub>o</sub>:**

$$V_o = A \cdot V_s - A \cdot \beta \cdot V_o$$

$$V_o + A \cdot \beta \cdot V_o = A \cdot V_s$$

$$V_o (1 + A\beta) = A \cdot V_s$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the closed-loop gain A<sub>f</sub> = V<sub>o</sub>/V<sub>s</sub>:**

$$A_f = \frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$

This is the **fundamental closed-loop gain equation** of any negative feedback amplifier.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Input Resistance with Series Feedback

**Statement:** In series mixing, the feedback voltage opposes the source voltage, reducing the net current drawn from the source. This effectively raises the input resistance as seen by the source.

**Step 1 — Without feedback, the input resistance is:**

$$R_i = \frac{V_\varepsilon}{I_i}$$

**Step 2 — With series feedback, the source sees V<sub>s</sub>, not V<sub>ε</sub>:**

$$R_{if} = \frac{V_s}{I_i}$$

**Step 3 — Express V<sub>s</sub> in terms of V<sub>ε</sub> using the loop equation:**

$$V_s = V_\varepsilon + V_f = V_\varepsilon + \beta \cdot V_o = V_\varepsilon + \beta \cdot A \cdot V_\varepsilon = V_\varepsilon(1 + A\beta)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into R<sub>if</sub>:**

$$R_{if} = \frac{V_\varepsilon(1 + A\beta)}{I_i} = (1 + A\beta) \cdot \frac{V_\varepsilon}{I_i} = R_i(1 + A\beta)$$

**Conclusion:** Series feedback **increases the input resistance by the factor (1 + Aβ)**.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Output Resistance — Voltage Sampling Case

**Statement:** For voltage sampling, the feedback samples V<sub>o</sub> and feeds back a voltage. To find R<sub>of</sub>, we set V<sub>s</sub> = 0, apply a test voltage V<sub>t</sub> at the output, and measure I<sub>t</sub>.

**Step 1 — With V<sub>s</sub> = 0, the input error signal becomes:**

$$V_\varepsilon = 0 - V_f = -\beta \cdot V_t$$

**Step 2 — The amplifier produces an output that opposes V<sub>t</sub>:**

The dependent source of the basic amplifier (with output resistance R<sub>o</sub>) gives:

$$V_{source} = A \cdot V_\varepsilon = -A\beta \cdot V_t$$

**Step 3 — Apply KCL at the output node with test source V<sub>t</sub>:**

$$I_t = \frac{V_t - V_{source}}{R_o} = \frac{V_t - (-A\beta V_t)}{R_o} = \frac{V_t(1 + A\beta)}{R_o}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for R<sub>of</sub>:**

$$R_{of} = \frac{V_t}{I_t} = \frac{R_o}{1 + A\beta}$$

**Conclusion:** Voltage sampling **decreases the output resistance by the factor (1 + Aβ)**.

### 3.4 Derivation of the Frequency Response with Feedback

**Statement:** A single-pole open-loop amplifier has the transfer function A(s). When feedback is applied, the closed-loop transfer function A<sub>f</sub>(s) is found by substituting A(s) into the feedback equation.

**Step 1 — Define the open-loop transfer function with one dominant pole at f<sub>H</sub>:**

$$A(s) = \frac{A_M}{1 + s/\omega_H} = \frac{A_M}{1 + s/(2\pi f_H)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the closed-loop equation A<sub>f</sub> = A/(1 + Aβ):**

$$A_f(s) = \frac{A_M/(1 + s/\omega_H)}{1 + [A_M/(1 + s/\omega_H)]\beta}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply numerator and denominator by (1 + s/ω<sub>H</sub>):**

$$A_f(s) = \frac{A_M}{1 + s/\omega_H + A_M \beta} = \frac{A_M}{1 + A_M\beta + s/\omega_H}$$

**Step 4 — Factor out (1 + A<sub>M</sub>β) from the denominator:**

$$A_f(s) = \frac{A_M/(1 + A_M\beta)}{1 + \dfrac{s}{\omega_H(1 + A_M\beta)}}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the new midband gain and new pole frequency:**

$$A_{Mf} = \frac{A_M}{1 + A_M\beta}$$

$$\omega_{Hf} = \omega_H(1 + A_M\beta)$$

**Step 6 — Verify the gain-bandwidth product invariance:**

$$A_{Mf} \cdot \omega_{Hf} = \frac{A_M}{1+A_M\beta} \cdot \omega_H(1+A_M\beta) = A_M \cdot \omega_H$$

The **GBW product is constant** — feedback redistributes gain into bandwidth.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Emitter-Degenerated BJT (I-SFB)

**Problem:** A common-emitter BJT amplifier with R<sub>C</sub> = 4.7 kΩ, R<sub>E</sub> = 1.2 kΩ, r<sub>π</sub> = 2 kΩ, β<sub>ac</sub> = 100, and an unbypassed emitter resistor R<sub>E</sub>. Find the voltage gain with and without feedback, and the new upper cutoff frequency given f<sub>H</sub> (open loop) = 2 MHz.

**Step 1 — Without feedback (R<sub>E</sub> bypassed), the CE voltage gain is:**

$$A_v = -g_m R_C = -\frac{\beta_{ac}}{r_\pi} \cdot R_C = -\frac{100}{2000} \cdot 4700 = -235$$

**Step 2 — With I-SFB (R<sub>E</sub> unbypassed), the small-signal voltage gain becomes:**

$$A_{vf} = \frac{-g_m R_C}{1 + g_m R_E} = \frac{A_v}{1 + g_m R_E}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the loop gain factor:**

$$g_m R_E = \frac{100}{2000} \cdot 1200 = 60$$

$$1 + g_m R_E = 1 + 60 = 61$$

**Step 4 — Compute the closed-loop gain:**

$$A_{vf} = \frac{-235}{61} \approx -3.85$$

**Step 5 — Compute the new bandwidth:**

$$f_{Hf} = f_H(1 + g_m R_E) = 2 \text{ MHz} \times 61 = 122 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 6 — Verify the GBW invariance:**

$$A_v \cdot f_H = 235 \times 2 \text{ MHz} = 470 \text{ MHz}$$

$$A_{vf} \cdot f_{Hf} = 3.85 \times 122 \text{ MHz} \approx 470 \text{ MHz} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The gain dropped from 235 to 3.85 (a factor of 61), but the bandwidth expanded from 2 MHz to 122 MHz (also a factor of 61). The transistor's intrinsic gain-bandwidth product is preserved, demonstrating the fundamental **gain-bandwidth trade-off** in negative feedback design.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Negative Feedback Amplifier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Source["Input Source Vs"] --> SumNode["Summing Junction\nSubtract Vfb from Vs"]
    SumNode --> BasicAmp["Basic Amplifier\nOpen Loop Gain A"]
    BasicAmp --> OutputNode["Output Port\nVo or Io"]
    OutputNode --> Load["Load RL"]
    OutputNode --> FeedbackNet["Feedback Network\nTransfer Function Beta"]
    FeedbackNet --> FeedbackSig["Feedback Signal\nVfb or Ifb"]
    FeedbackSig --> SumNode
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NodeA["Vs Source"] --> NodeB["Sum Sum Negation"]
    NodeB --> NodeC["Error Signal Veps"]
    NodeC --> NodeD["Amplifier A Block"]
    NodeD --> NodeE["Output Vo"]
    NodeE --> NodeF["To Load RL"]
    NodeE --> NodeG["Beta Network Sampling"]
    NodeG --> NodeH["Feedback Vfb"]
    NodeH --> NodeB
```

### 4.3 Comparison Flow — Four Canonical Topologies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TopStart["Feedback Topology Selection"] --> MixQ{"Mixing Method?"}
    MixQ --> MixSeries["Series Mixing at Input"]
    MixQ --> MixShunt["Shunt Mixing at Input"]
    MixSeries --> SampleQ1{"Sampling Method at Output?"}
    MixShunt --> SampleQ2{"Sampling Method at Output?"}
    SampleQ1 --> VSFBSol["Voltage Series VCVS\nRi increases, Ro decreases"]
    SampleQ1 --> ISFBSol["Current Series CCVS\nRi increases, Ro increases"]
    SampleQ2 --> VPFBSol["Voltage Shunt VCCS\nRi decreases, Ro decreases"]
    SampleQ2 --> IPFBSol["Current Shunt CCCS\nRi decreases, Ro increases"]
```

### 4.4 Frequency Response Comparison (Conceptual Bode Plot)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FreqAxis["Frequency Axis log f"] --> LowF["Low Frequency Region\nGain rises with 20 dB per decade"]
    LowF --> MidF["Midband Region\nFlat gain A_M or A_Mf"]
    MidF --> HighF["High Frequency Region\nGain rolls off at minus 20 dB per decade"]
    HighF --> FbPoint["f_H Open Loop Pole"]
    HighF --> FbPointNew["f_Hf Closed Loop Pole\nf_Hf greater than f_H"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Lab Note:** When simulating the frequency response in LTspice or Multisim, perform an **AC Analysis** with decade sweep from 10 Hz to 10× the expected f<sub>Hf</sub>. Use **probe cursors** to read the gain at 1 kHz (midband) and the −3 dB point to extract f<sub>Hf</sub>. Tabulate the simulated values against the theoretical values in your lab record.

### 4.5 Simulation Circuit Topology — Voltage Series Feedback (Non-Inverting Op-Amp)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VinSrc["Input Source Vs AC 1V"] --> R1Node["Resistor R1 to Ground"]
    VinSrc --> PosInput["Opamp Non Inverting Input"]
    Rfeedback["Feedback Resistor Rf"] --> NegInput["Opamp Inverting Input"]
    R1Node --> NegInput
    OpampCore["Opamp Aol Gain Block"] --> VoutNode["Output Vo"]
    Rfeedback --> VoutNode
```

### 4.6 Simulation Circuit Topology — Current Series Feedback (Emitter-Degenerated CE)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VsSrc["AC Source Vin"] --> CinNode["Coupling Capacitor Cin"]
    CinNode --> BaseNode["BJT Base"]
    BaseNode --> Rbias1["Bias Resistor R1 to VCC"]
    BaseNode --> Rbias2["Bias Resistor R2 to Ground"]
    EmitterN["BJT Emitter"] --> REun["Unbypassed Resistor RE"]
    REun --> GroundN["Ground"]
    CollectorN["BJT Collector"] --> RcNode["Collector Resistor RC"]
    RcNode --> VccNode["VCC Supply"]
    CollectorN --> CoutNode["Coupling Capacitor Cout"]
    CoutNode --> VoutLoad["Output Vo to Load"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define negative feedback in amplifiers. Mention any two effects of negative feedback on amplifier performance.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Negative feedback is the process by which a fraction of the output signal is returned to the input in **opposition** (180° out of phase) to the original input signal, thereby reducing the net input to the amplifier.

**Effects (2 marks — any two):**
1. **Gain is reduced** by a factor of (1 + Aβ).
2. **Bandwidth is increased** by a factor of (1 + Aβ), while the gain-bandwidth product remains constant.
3. **Input impedance** is increased (for series mixing) or decreased (for shunt mixing) by (1 + Aβ).
4. **Output impedance** is decreased (for voltage sampling) or increased (for current sampling) by (1 + Aβ).
5. **Nonlinear distortion** is reduced by the factor (1 + Aβ).
6. **Stability** of gain against temperature, supply, and component variation is improved.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sample Paper]**
*Distinguish between voltage-series and current-series feedback amplifiers based on the controlled source type and impedance characteristics.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Voltage-Series Feedback | Current-Series Feedback |
|---|---|---|
| Controlled Source Type | VCVS (Voltage-Controlled Voltage Source) | CCVS (Current-Controlled Voltage Source) |
| Sampling Quantity | Output voltage V<sub>o</sub> | Output current I<sub>o</sub> |
| Mixing Method | Series at input | Series at input |
| Effect on R<sub>i</sub> | Increases by (1 + Aβ) | Increases by (1 + Aβ) |
| Effect on R<sub>o</sub> | Decreases by (1 + Aβ) | Increases by (1 + Aβ) |
| Typical Application | Non-inverting op-amp, emitter follower | Emitter-degenerated CE amplifier |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse the controlled-source classification. A **VCVS** has both input and output as **voltages** (no current → current conversion), while a **CCVS** has a current input and voltage output. Memorize the four combinations: VCVS, VCCS, CCVS, CCCS — they appear frequently in 3-mark questions and module tests.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**A common-emitter BJT amplifier has the following parameters: R<sub>C</sub> = 2.2 kΩ, R<sub>E</sub> (unbypassed) = 470 Ω, r<sub>π</sub> = 1.5 kΩ, β<sub>ac</sub> = 120, and an open-loop upper cutoff frequency f<sub>H</sub> = 1.5 MHz. The load resistance R<sub>L</sub> = 4.7 kΩ.**

**(a) Identify the type of feedback and derive the closed-loop voltage gain with feedback. Compare the input and output impedances with and without feedback. (7 marks)**

**(b) Determine the new bandwidth with feedback and verify the gain-bandwidth product invariance. Sketch the Bode magnitude plot qualitatively. (7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify the feedback topology. Since the emitter resistor R<sub>E</sub> is unbypassed, the output current I<sub>o</sub> (collector current I<sub>C</sub>) flows through R<sub>E</sub>, producing a feedback voltage V<sub>f</sub> = I<sub>o</sub>·R<sub>E</sub>. This voltage is in **series** with the input V<sub>in</sub>. The topology is therefore **Current-Series Feedback (I-SFB)**, classified as a **CCVS**.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the transconductance and open-loop gain.

$$g_m = \frac{\beta_{ac}}{r_\pi} = \frac{120}{1500} = 0.08 \text{ S}$$

$$A_v = -g_m \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L) = -0.08 \times \frac{2200 \times 4700}{2200 + 4700} = -0.08 \times 1499.5 \approx -120$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the closed-loop gain.

$$1 + g_m R_E = 1 + 0.08 \times 470 = 1 + 37.6 = 38.6$$

$$A_{vf} = \frac{A_v}{1 + g_m R_E} = \frac{-120}{38.6} \approx -3.11$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Compare impedances.

- Input resistance without feedback: R<sub>i</sub> = r<sub>π</sub> = 1.5 kΩ
- Input resistance with feedback: R<sub>if</sub> = R<sub>i</sub>(1 + g<sub>m</sub>R<sub>E</sub>) = 1500 × 38.6 = **57.9 kΩ** (increases)
- Output resistance without feedback: R<sub>o</sub> ≈ R<sub>C</sub> = 2.2 kΩ
- Output resistance with feedback: R<sub>of</sub> = R<sub>o</sub>(1 + g<sub>m</sub>R<sub>E</sub>) = 2200 × 38.6 = **84.92 kΩ** (increases)

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute the new upper cutoff frequency.

$$f_{Hf} = f_H (1 + g_m R_E) = 1.5 \text{ MHz} \times 38.6 = 57.9 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Verify the GBW product.

$$A_v \times f_H = 120 \times 1.5 \text{ MHz} = 180 \text{ MHz}$$

$$A_{vf} \times f_{Hf} = 3.11 \times 57.9 \text{ MHz} \approx 180 \text{ MHz} \quad \checkmark$$

The GBW product is **invariant** at 180 MHz.

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Qualitative Bode magnitude plot description.

| Region | Open-Loop Gain (dB) | Closed-Loop Gain (dB) |
|---|---|---|
| Low frequency (f < f<sub>L</sub>) | Rises at +20 dB/dec | Rises at +20 dB/dec |
| Midband | Flat at 20 log(120) = **41.6 dB** | Flat at 20 log(3.11) = **9.85 dB** |
| High frequency | Rolls off at −20 dB/dec starting at f<sub>H</sub> = 1.5 MHz | Rolls off at −20 dB/dec starting at f<sub>Hf</sub> = 57.9 MHz |

The two curves have the same shape but are vertically shifted by **−31.75 dB** (factor of 38.6 in linear scale), and the closed-loop pole is pushed **38.6× higher** in frequency.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]**

**A non-inverting operational amplifier has the following configuration: R<sub>1</sub> = 1 kΩ, R<sub>f</sub> = 10 kΩ, open-loop gain A = 2 × 10<sup>5</sup>, and open-loop upper cutoff frequency f<sub>H</sub> = 10 Hz.**

**(a) Identify the type of feedback, derive the closed-loop voltage gain using the feedback equation, and compute the feedback factor β. (7 marks)**

**(b) Calculate the closed-loop bandwidth and the gain-bandwidth product. If the input source resistance is R<sub>s</sub> = 600 Ω and the op-amp open-loop input resistance is R<sub>i</sub> = 2 MΩ, compute the input resistance with feedback. (7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify the topology. The non-inverting op-amp samples the **output voltage** and mixes the **feedback voltage** in **series** with the input. This is **Voltage-Series Feedback (V-SFB)**, a **VCVS** topology.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the feedback factor β using the voltage divider at the inverting input.

$$\beta = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f} = \frac{1000}{1000 + 10000} = \frac{1}{11} \approx 0.0909$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the loop gain Aβ.

$$A\beta = 2 \times 10^5 \times \frac{1}{11} = 18181.8$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Compute the closed-loop gain.

$$A_{vf} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{1 + 18181.8} = \frac{200000}{18182.8} \approx 11.0$$

This matches the ideal non-inverting gain: **1 + R<sub>f</sub>/R<sub>1</sub> = 1 + 10 = 11**.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute the closed-loop bandwidth.

$$f_{Hf} = f_H (1 + A\beta) = 10 \text{ Hz} \times 18182.8 = 181828 \text{ Hz} \approx 181.83 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Verify the GBW product.

$$A \times f_H = 2 \times 10^5 \times 10 = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz} = 2 \text{ MHz}$$

$$A_{vf} \times f_{Hf} = 11 \times 181828 = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz} = 2 \text{ MHz} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Compute the input resistance with series feedback.

$$R_{if} = R_i (1 + A\beta) = 2 \times 10^6 \times 18182.8 = 3.636 \times 10^{10} \text{ Ω} \approx 36.36 \text{ GΩ}$$

This is why op-amp non-inverting configurations present an extremely high input impedance to the source, making them ideal for **buffer amplifier** and **instrumentation** applications.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes:**
> 1. **Sign error in feedback equation:** Students often write A<sub>f</sub> = A(1 + Aβ) instead of A/(1 + Aβ). The feedback **reduces** the gain — it never increases it for negative feedback.
> 2. **Confusing sampling vs. mixing:** A common error is to mix up the impedance-modification rules. Memorize: **Series mixing → R<sub>i</sub> increases; Shunt mixing → R<sub>i</sub> decreases; Voltage sampling → R<sub>o</sub> decreases; Current sampling → R<sub>o</sub> increases.**
> 3. **Wrong controlled-source acronym:** Mixing up VCVS with VCCS costs 1–2 marks. Recall: the **first letter** is the input quantity, the **second letter** is the output quantity. VCVS = Voltage in, Voltage out. CCVS = Current in, Voltage out.
> 4. **Not stating the unit of β:** For V-SFB, β is dimensionless. For I-SFB, β has units of resistance (ohms). For V-PFB, β has units of conductance (siemens). For I-PFB, β is dimensionless. Failing to specify the unit loses 1 mark.
> 5. **GBW verification step missing:** Examiners love to see the explicit verification that A·f<sub>H</sub> = A<sub>f</sub>·f<sub>Hf</sub>. Skipping this loses 1–2 marks in 14-mark questions.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU PCAEL407 Module 2**

- ✅ **Negative feedback** returns a fraction of the output to the input in **opposition** to the source, reducing gain but improving performance.
- ✅ **Closed-loop gain equation:** **A<sub>f</sub> = A / (1 + Aβ)** — applicable to all four canonical topologies.
- ✅ **Loop gain (Aβ)** is the single most important parameter — it controls the amount of trade-off between gain and other performance metrics.
- ✅ **Desensitivity factor (1 + Aβ)** appears in **every** feedback-induced improvement (gain stability, bandwidth extension, distortion reduction, impedance modification).
- ✅ **Four canonical topologies:** V-SFB (VCVS), I-SFB (CCVS), V-PFB (VCCS), I-PFB (CCCS) — classified by sampling and mixing methods.
- ✅ **Series mixing increases R<sub>i</sub> by (1 + Aβ).** Shunt mixing decreases R<sub>i</sub> by (1 + Aβ).
- ✅ **Voltage sampling decreases R<sub>o</sub> by (1 + Aβ).** Current sampling increases R<sub>o</sub> by (1 + Aβ).
- ✅ **Gain-bandwidth product is invariant:** A<sub>M</sub>·f<sub>H</sub> = A<sub>Mf</sub>·f<sub>Hf</sub> = constant.
- ✅ **For I-SFB (emitter degeneration):** A<sub>vf</sub> ≈ −(R<sub>C</sub> ∥ R<sub>L</sub>) / R<sub>E</sub> for large g<sub>m</sub>R<sub>E</sub>.
- ✅ **For V-SFB (non-inverting op-amp):** A<sub>vf</sub> = 1 + R<sub>f</sub>/R<sub>1</sub>; the β factor is R<sub>1</sub>/(R<sub>1</sub> + R<sub>f</sub>).
- ✅ **Simulation steps in LTspice/Multisim:** Build the circuit → AC analysis with decade sweep 10 Hz to 100 MHz → Plot V<sub>o</sub>/V<sub>in</sub> in dB → Use cursor to read midband gain (1 kHz) and −3 dB point → Tabulate against theory.
- ✅ **Three-port feedback model:** Treat the feedback network as a two-port with forward transmission (ideally zero), reverse transmission β, and loading effects R<sub>11</sub>, R<sub>22</sub> that must be absorbed into the basic amplifier.
- ✅ **Nyquist stability criterion** must be satisfied: |Aβ| < 1 when the phase shift reaches −180°, to prevent oscillations.
- ✅ **Phase margin** of 45°–60° is the engineering standard for closed-loop stability in feedback amplifier design.
- ✅ **Lab record must include:** Circuit schematic, component values, simulation parameters, Bode plot screenshot, table of theoretical vs. simulated midband gain and bandwidth, and percentage error calculation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Low Frequency Oscillators Simulation - RC Phase Shift and Wien Bridge
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL407 - ANALOG CIRCUITS AND SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Low Frequency Oscillators Simulation - RC Phase Shift and Wien Bridge

# Module Experiment 2 — Low Frequency Oscillators Simulation

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Low Frequency Oscillator?

A **Low Frequency Oscillator (LFO)** is an electronic oscillator circuit that generates sinusoidal (or sometimes non-sinusoidal) waveforms in the audio frequency range, typically from **0.1 Hz to 100 kHz**. In the context of the KTU 2024 lab syllabus, the two most important LF oscillator topologies studied are:

1. **RC Phase Shift Oscillator** (using a BJT/FET inverting amplifier + 3-stage RC ladder)
2. **Wien Bridge Oscillator** (using an Op-Amp non-inverting amplifier + frequency-selective Wien network)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Barkhausen Criterion (The Fundamental Oscillation Theorem):**
> For any sinusoidal oscillator to sustain oscillations, two conditions must be simultaneously met:
> 1. **Loop Gain Magnitude Condition:** $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$
> 2. **Loop Phase Condition:** $\angle A\beta = 0° \text{ (or } 360°\text{)}$
> Where $A$ is the amplifier gain and $\beta$ is the feedback network transfer function.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Swinging Pendulum on a Spring"

Imagine pushing a child on a swing. If you push **in the same direction** of motion, **at exactly the right moment**, the swing will rise higher and higher. But if you push at the wrong time (wrong phase), the swing will eventually stop.

An electronic oscillator works on the **exact same principle**:
- The **amplifier** supplies the "push" (energy).
- The **RC feedback network** decides **when** to give the push (phase shift + frequency selection).
- If the energy added per cycle equals the energy lost, the oscillation is **sustained**.

The two oscillators in this experiment simply use **different networks to time the push correctly**.

| Oscillator | Topology Type | Frequency Range | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| RC Phase Shift | Inverting (CE BJT) | 1 Hz – 100 kHz | Audio tone generators, function generators |
| Wien Bridge | Non-Inverting (Op-Amp) | 1 Hz – 1 MHz | Laboratory sine-wave sources, audio test equipment |

> [!NOTE]
> Both oscillators covered here use **only resistors and capacitors** — no inductors — which makes them ideal for **simulation (SPICE/Multisim)** and **IC fabrication**. This is why they are highlighted in KTU's Analog Lab syllabus.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 RC Phase Shift Oscillator — Working Principle

The RC phase shift oscillator uses a **single-stage CE BJT amplifier** (which provides **180° phase inversion**) cascaded with a **three-section RC ladder network**. Each RC section is designed to provide a **maximum phase shift of 60°**, and three sections together produce the required **180° phase shift** to satisfy the Barkhausen criterion.

**Total phase around loop** = 180° (from CE amplifier) + 180° (from RC network) = **360° = 0°** ✓

#### Step-by-Step Operational Logic:

- **Step 1:** A small noise voltage is amplified by the BJT in CE mode, producing an inverted output (180° phase shift).
- **Step 2:** This output is fed to the 3-stage RC feedback ladder.
- **Step 3:** The ladder shifts the phase progressively: 60° → 120° → 180°.
- **Step 4:** The 180°-shifted feedback is applied to the input, reinforcing the original noise.
- **Step 5:** If $|A\beta| \geq 1$, oscillations build up and stabilize at the frequency where the phase shift is exactly 180°.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Constraint for RC Phase Shift Oscillator:**
> For sustained oscillations, the amplifier gain must satisfy $\vert A_v \vert \geq 29$ (the "magic number 29"). Below this gain, oscillations die out.

### 2.2 Wien Bridge Oscillator — Working Principle

The Wien bridge oscillator uses an **Op-Amp in non-inverting configuration** (which provides **0° phase shift**) combined with a **Wien bridge network** — a frequency-selective lead-lag network.

**Total phase around loop** = 0° (from Op-Amp) + 0° (from Wien network at resonance) = **0°** ✓

The Wien network is a series-parallel combination of **R-C**:
- **Series arm:** $Z_s = R_1 + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_1}$
- **Parallel arm:** $Z_p = R_2 \parallel \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_2}$

For the symmetric case ($R_1 = R_2 = R$, $C_1 = C_2 = C$):
- **At resonance frequency** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$, the network behaves as a **purely resistive voltage divider** with ratio exactly **1/3**.
- The Op-Amp must therefore provide a gain of exactly **3** to satisfy $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$.

#### Why "Bridge"?

The Wien bridge is derived from the **Wheatstone bridge** principle, where the bridge becomes **balanced** at one specific frequency, allowing maximum positive feedback.

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, a gain slightly greater than 3 is used initially so oscillations build up. Automatic gain control (AGC) using diodes, thermistors, or JFETs stabilizes the amplitude.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Parameter | RC Phase Shift Oscillator | Wien Bridge Oscillator |
|---|---|---|
| Amplifier Used | CE BJT (inverting) | Op-Amp non-inverting |
| Phase shift by Amplifier | 180° | 0° |
| Phase shift by Feedback Network | 180° (3 × 60°) | 0° (at $f_0$) |
| Frequency of Oscillation | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ |
| Feedback Factor $\beta$ at $f_0$ | $\dfrac{1}{29}$ | $\dfrac{1}{3}$ |
| Minimum Gain Required | $\vert A_v \vert \geq 29$ | $A_v \geq 3$ |
| Loop Gain at $f_0$ | $A\beta = 1$ | $A\beta = 1$ |
| Typical Frequency Range | 1 Hz – 100 kHz | 1 Hz – 1 MHz |
| Waveform Purity | Moderate (distortion ~5%) | High (distortion <0.1% with AGC) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Component Values (KTU Lab Default):** $R = 1\text{ k}\Omega$ to $10\text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.01\text{ }\mu\text{F}$ to $1\text{ }\mu\text{F}$ are commonly used. Always use **non-polarized (ceramic/MKT) capacitors** in the feedback path.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio Test Equipment:** Function generators, audio sine-wave sources.
- **Musical Instruments:** Electronic organs and synthesizers for tone generation.
- **Communication Systems:** Carrier generation in low-frequency transmitters.
- **Instrumentation:** Carrier for chopper-stabilized amplifiers, lock-in detectors.
- **Biomedical:** ECG/EEG calibration, pacemaker testing (sub-Hz LFOs).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: RC Phase Shift Oscillator Frequency

Consider a 3-stage RC ladder with identical components $R$ and $C$. The feedback factor $\beta$ is given by the transfer function from the output node back to the input.

$$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{V_f}{V_o}$$

For three identical RC sections, applying standard nodal analysis (or cascading three first-order high-pass filters), we obtain:

$$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 - 5(\omega RC)^2 + j\left[6(\omega RC) - (\omega RC)^3\right]}$$

**Step 1: Apply the Barkhausen Phase Condition.**

For oscillation, the imaginary part of the denominator must be **zero** (so that $\beta$ is purely real).

$$6(\omega RC) - (\omega RC)^3 = 0$$

**Step 2: Solve for $\omega RC$.**

Let $x = \omega RC$. Then $6x - x^3 = 0 \Rightarrow x(6 - x^2) = 0$.

Since $x \neq 0$:

$$x^2 = 6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = \sqrt{6}$$

Therefore:

$$\omega RC = \sqrt{6} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC\sqrt{6}}$$

**Step 3: Convert to frequency.**

$$f_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical KTU result** for the RC phase shift oscillator with identical R and C values in all three sections.

**Step 4: Determine the feedback factor magnitude at $f_0$.**

Substituting $\omega RC = \sqrt{6}$ into the real part:

$$\beta = \frac{1}{1 - 5(\sqrt{6})^2} = \frac{1}{1 - 30} = \frac{1}{-29} = -\frac{1}{29}$$

The negative sign indicates the required 180° phase inversion (matches the CE amplifier's inversion). The magnitude is:

$$\vert \beta \vert = \frac{1}{29}$$

**Step 5: Apply the Barkhausen Magnitude Condition.**

$$\vert A_v \vert \geq \frac{1}{\vert \beta \vert} = 29$$

$$\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}} \quad ; \quad \vert A_v \vert_{min} = 29}$$

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Wien Bridge Oscillator Frequency

The Wien network feedback factor is:

$$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{Z_p}{Z_s + Z_p}$$

where $Z_s = R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$ and $Z_p = \dfrac{R \cdot \frac{1}{j\omega C}}{R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}} = \dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}$

**Step 1: Substitute into the $\beta$ expression.**

$$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{\dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}}{R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C} + \dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}}$$

**Step 2: Multiply numerator and denominator by $j\omega C(1 + j\omega RC)$.**

$$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega RC}{j\omega RC(1 + j\omega RC) + (1 + j\omega RC) + j\omega RC}$$

**Step 3: Simplify the denominator.**

$$\text{Denom} = j\omega RC + (j\omega RC)^2 + 1 + j\omega RC + j\omega RC$$

$$\text{Denom} = 1 + 3j\omega RC + (j\omega RC)^2 = 1 + 3j\omega RC - (\omega RC)^2$$

**Step 4: Write the standard form.**

$$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 - (\omega RC)^2 + j\,3\omega RC}$$

**Step 5: Apply the Barkhausen Phase Condition (Imaginary part of denominator = 0... actually the imaginary part of $\beta$ must be 0).**

The denominator must be purely real, meaning the imaginary part of $1 - (\omega RC)^2 + j3\omega RC$ must equal zero... but wait, this is the denominator. For $\beta$ to be purely real, the imaginary part of the denominator must vanish OR be a multiple of the numerator's imaginary part.

Cross-multiplying:

$$\beta = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 - (\omega RC)^2 + j3\omega RC} \cdot \frac{[1 - (\omega RC)^2] - j3\omega RC}{[1 - (\omega RC)^2] - j3\omega RC}$$

Real part of numerator: $j\omega RC \cdot [1 - (\omega RC)^2] = j\omega RC[1-(\omega RC)^2]$
Imaginary part contribution: $j\omega RC \cdot (-j3\omega RC) = 3(\omega RC)^2$

$$\beta = \frac{3(\omega RC)^2 + j\omega RC[1 - (\omega RC)^2]}{[1 - (\omega RC)^2]^2 + 9(\omega RC)^2}$$

**Step 6: For $\beta$ to be real, the imaginary part of the numerator must be zero.**

$$\omega RC[1 - (\omega RC)^2] = 0$$

Since $\omega RC \neq 0$:

$$(\omega RC)^2 = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_0 RC = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

**Step 7: Substitute back to find $\beta$ at resonance.**

$$\beta(f_0) = \frac{3(\omega_0 RC)^2}{[1 - (\omega_0 RC)^2]^2 + 9(\omega_0 RC)^2} = \frac{3(1)^2}{0 + 9(1)} = \frac{3}{9} = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Step 8: Apply the magnitude condition.**

$$A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} \geq \frac{1}{\beta} = 3$$

For non-inverting Op-Amp gain: $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$, so we need:

$$\frac{R_f}{R_1} \geq 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_f \geq 2 R_1$$

$$\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} \quad ; \quad \beta = \frac{1}{3} \quad ; \quad A_{v,min} = 3}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Lab Simulation Style)

**Problem:** Design an RC phase shift oscillator using a BJT (CE mode) with $R = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.01\text{ }\mu\text{F}$ in each section. Determine the frequency of oscillation and the required minimum gain.

**Solution:**

Given: $R = 10 \times 10^3\text{ }\Omega = 10^4\text{ }\Omega$, $C = 0.01 \times 10^{-6}\text{ F} = 10^{-8}\text{ F}$

**Step 1: Calculate the time constant.**

$$RC = 10^4 \times 10^{-8} = 10^{-4}\text{ s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate $\omega_0$.**

$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC\sqrt{6}} = \frac{1}{10^{-4} \times 2.449} = \frac{1}{2.449 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\omega_0 = 4082.5\text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3: Calculate $f_0$.**

$$f_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{4082.5}{6.2832} = 649.7\text{ Hz} \approx 650\text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4: Minimum gain requirement.**

$$\vert A_v \vert_{min} = 29$$

> [!TIP]
> In SPICE simulation, you can verify this by placing a **TRAN analysis** with a small initial condition (e.g., `IC=1mV` on a node) and observing the output waveform grow and stabilize at ~650 Hz.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Wien Bridge

**Problem:** Design a Wien bridge oscillator using an Op-Amp (741) with $R = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.01\text{ }\mu\text{F}$. Find $f_0$ and required feedback resistors.

**Solution:**

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^4 \times 10^{-8}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$f_0 = 1591.5\text{ Hz} \approx 1.59\text{ kHz}$$

For $A_v = 3$: $1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} = 3 \Rightarrow R_f = 2 R_1$

Choose $R_1 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, then $R_f = 20\text{ k}\Omega$.

> [!TIP]
> For amplitude stability in simulation, replace $R_f$ with a **non-linear element** (e.g., two back-to-back Zener diodes or a JFET as voltage-controlled resistor) so that gain drops to exactly 3 once amplitude stabilizes.

---

### 3.5 Comparative Analysis Matrix (KTU Board Favorite Table)

| Feature | RC Phase Shift Oscillator | Wien Bridge Oscillator |
|---|---|---|
| Active Device | BJT / FET | Operational Amplifier |
| Configuration | Inverting (CE) | Non-Inverting |
| Number of RC Sections | 3 | 2 (one series, one parallel) |
| Total Phase Shift (Feedback) | 180° | 0° at $f_0$ |
| Frequency Formula | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ |
| Gain Required | $\geq 29$ | $\geq 3$ |
| Frequency Stability | Moderate | Very High |
| Waveform Purity | 3–5% THD | <0.1% THD (with AGC) |
| Amplitude Stabilization | Difficult | Easy (AGC/AGC-less) |
| Suitability for IC Design | Low | High |
| KTU Typical Frequency | 500 Hz – 5 kHz | 1 kHz – 10 kHz |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — RC Phase Shift Oscillator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    supplyNode[DC Supply Vcc]:::supply --> biasNet[Bias Network]
    biasNet --> bjtStage[BJT CE Amplifier]
    bjtStage -- 180 degree phase inversion --> rcLadder[Three Stage RC Ladder Network]
    rcLadder -- 180 degree progressive phase shift --> inputNode[Input Node]
    inputNode --> bjtStage
    bjtStage --> outputNode[Sinusoidal Output]
    outputNode -.feedback sample.-> rcLadder

    classDef supply fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#ff8c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef active fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef passive fill:#e0ffe0,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Architecture Explanation:**
- The **DC supply** energizes the BJT bias network.
- The **BJT CE stage** provides voltage gain and 180° phase inversion.
- The **three RC sections** form the frequency-selective feedback path.
- The signal circulates: BJT → RC ladder → back to BJT input, sustaining oscillation.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Wien Bridge Oscillator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    vcc[Vcc Positive Rail] --> opampA[Op Amp U1 741]
    vneg[Vee Negative Rail] --> opampA
    noninvertInput[Non Inverting Input Node] --> opampA
    opampA --> outputNodeA[Sinusoidal Output Vout]
    outputNodeA --> wienSeries[Series Arm R1 C1]
    outputNodeA --> wienParallel[Parallel Arm R2 C2]
    wienParallel --> feedbackNode[Feedback Node Vfb]
    wienSeries --> feedbackNode
    feedbackNode --> noninvertInput
    outputNodeA --> feedbackResistor[Feedback Resistor Rf]
    feedbackResistor --> invertingInputNode[Inverting Input Node]
    groundR[Resistor R1 to Ground] --> invertingInputNode
    invertingInputNode --> opampA

    classDef supply fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#ff8c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef active fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef passive fill:#e0ffe0,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Architecture Explanation:**
- The **Op-Amp** is in non-inverting configuration (gain = $1 + R_f/R_1$).
- The **Wien network** (series $R_1C_1$ + parallel $R_2C_2$) connects output to non-inverting input.
- At resonance $f_0$, $\beta = 1/3$ and gain = 3 → loop gain = 1 → stable oscillation.

### 4.3 Complete Signal Flow & Phase Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RCPhaseShiftOsc["RC PHASE SHIFT OSCILLATOR"]
        direction LR
        nodeStart[Input 0 deg] --> nodeInv[CE Amplifier Output 180 deg]
        nodeInv --> nodeR1[R1 C1 Section 240 deg]
        nodeR1 --> nodeR2[R2 C2 Section 300 deg]
        nodeR2 --> nodeR3[R3 C3 Section 360 deg equals 0 deg]
        nodeR3 --> nodeStart
    end

    subgraph WienBridgeOsc["WIEN BRIDGE OSCILLATOR"]
        direction LR
        nodeWStart[Input 0 deg] --> nodeWOp[Op Amp Output 0 deg]
        nodeWOp --> nodeWSeries[Series RC 45 deg lag]
        nodeWSeries --> nodeWParallel[Parallel RC 45 deg lead]
        nodeWParallel --> nodeWResonance[Resonance 0 deg]
        nodeWResonance --> nodeWStart
    end

    classDef phase fill:#fffacd,stroke:#daa520,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class nodeStart,nodeInv,nodeR1,nodeR2,nodeR3,nodeWStart,nodeWOp,nodeWSeries,nodeWParallel,nodeWResonance phase
```

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram visualizes how each oscillator satisfies the **Barkhausen phase criterion** at the operating frequency.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the Barkhausen criterion for sustained oscillations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Barkhausen criterion states that for sustained sinusoidal oscillations, the loop gain of the feedback amplifier must satisfy two simultaneous conditions:
1. The **magnitude** of the loop gain must equal unity: $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$
2. The **total phase shift** around the loop must be zero (or an integer multiple of 360°): $\angle A\beta = 0° \text{ or } n \cdot 360°$

If $\vert A\beta \vert < 1$, oscillations die out (damped); if $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$, oscillations build up exponentially until non-linearities limit them.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Why does the RC phase shift oscillator use three RC sections and not two?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A common-emitter BJT amplifier provides a phase inversion of **180°**. To satisfy the Barkhausen phase criterion, the feedback network must supply an **additional 180°** of phase shift. A single RC section can provide at most **90°** of phase shift (asymptotically, at infinite frequency), and two sections can only reach ~180° in the unrealistic limit. **Three RC sections are needed** because each section can realistically provide a maximum of about **60°** at the oscillation frequency, summing to exactly **180°** at $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$.

---

### PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — RC Phase Shift Oscillator**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation of an RC phase shift oscillator using a BJT in CE configuration with identical components $R$ and $C$ in each section. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the oscillator in (a), if $R = 4.7\text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.01\text{ }\mu\text{F}$, calculate (i) the frequency of oscillation, and (ii) the minimum gain required. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

The RC ladder has three sections. The feedback factor is derived by nodal analysis:

$$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 - 5(\omega RC)^2 + j\left[6(\omega RC) - (\omega RC)^3\right]}$$

**Apply Barkhausen phase condition:** Imaginary part of denominator must be zero:

$$6(\omega RC) - (\omega RC)^3 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (\omega RC)^2 = 6$$

$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC\sqrt{6}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}}$$

**Apply Barkhausen magnitude condition:** Substitute $(\omega RC)^2 = 6$ into the real part:

$$\beta = \frac{1}{1 - 5 \times 6} = -\frac{1}{29}$$

Therefore, $\vert A\beta \vert \geq 1 \Rightarrow \vert A \vert \geq 29$.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $R = 4.7\text{ k}\Omega = 4.7 \times 10^3\text{ }\Omega$; $C = 0.01\text{ }\mu\text{F} = 10^{-8}\text{ F}$

**(i) Frequency:**

$$RC = 4.7 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-8} = 4.7 \times 10^{-5}\text{ s}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 4.7 \times 10^{-5} \times \sqrt{6}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 4.7 \times 10^{-5} \times 2.449}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{7.227 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1383.7\text{ Hz} \approx 1.38\text{ kHz}$$

**(ii) Minimum gain:**

$$\boxed{\vert A_v \vert_{min} = 29}$$

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Wien Bridge Oscillator**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation of a Wien bridge oscillator using an Op-Amp with $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$. Show that the feedback factor at resonance is **1/3** and the amplifier gain required is **3**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a Wien bridge oscillator with $R = 1.5\text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.1\text{ }\mu\text{F}$, determine the frequency of oscillation. If the Op-Amp uses feedback resistors $R_1 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_f = 20\text{ k}\Omega$, verify whether the circuit will sustain oscillations. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

The Wien network feedback factor is:

$$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 - (\omega RC)^2 + j\,3\omega RC}$$

For $\beta$ to be real, the imaginary part of the numerator must vanish:

$$\omega RC[1 - (\omega RC)^2] = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_0 RC = 1$$

$$\boxed{\omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC} \quad ; \quad f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$

At resonance, $\beta = \dfrac{3(\omega RC)^2}{[1-(\omega RC)^2]^2 + 9(\omega RC)^2} = \dfrac{3}{0 + 9} = \dfrac{1}{3}$

For oscillation: $A_v \geq \dfrac{1}{\beta} = 3$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $R = 1.5\text{ k}\Omega = 1.5 \times 10^3\text{ }\Omega$; $C = 0.1\text{ }\mu\text{F} = 10^{-7}\text{ F}$

$$RC = 1.5 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-7} = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}\text{ s}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1.5 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{1}{9.4248 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1061\text{ Hz} \approx 1.06\text{ kHz}$$

**Gain verification:**

$$A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 1 + \frac{20\text{ k}\Omega}{10\text{ k}\Omega} = 1 + 2 = 3$$

Since $A_v = 3$ equals the required minimum, $\vert A\beta \vert = 3 \times \frac{1}{3} = 1$ ✓

**The circuit will sustain oscillations at approximately 1.06 kHz.** However, for stable amplitude, a gain **slightly above 3** (e.g., $A_v = 3.1$) is preferred, with amplitude stabilization via AGC.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{6}$ factor** in RC phase shift frequency — students often write $f = 1/(2\pi RC)$, which is wrong; that's the Wien bridge formula.
> 2. **Confusing inverting/non-inverting configurations:** RC phase shift uses CE (inverting, 180°), Wien bridge uses Op-Amp non-inverting (0°). Mixing up phase relationships destroys the derivation.
> 3. **Writing the gain as "≥ 3" or "≥ 29"** without justification — always derive it from the magnitude condition.
> 4. **Incorrect unit conversions:** $\mu\text{F} = 10^{-6}\text{ F}$, $\text{nF} = 10^{-9}\text{ F}$, $\text{k}\Omega = 10^3\text{ }\Omega$. Misplacing decimal points loses easy marks.
> 5. **Skipping the imaginary part = 0 step** — KTU expects the explicit phase condition derivation before solving for $\omega_0$.
> 6. **Not mentioning Barkhausen criterion explicitly** in derivations — always anchor your derivation to this theorem.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Barkhausen Criterion:** Both magnitude ($\vert A\beta \vert = 1$) AND phase ($\angle A\beta = 0°$) must be satisfied.
- [ ] **RC Phase Shift Oscillator:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$, $\beta = -\dfrac{1}{29}$, $\vert A_{min} \vert = 29$.
- [ ] **Wien Bridge Oscillator:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$, $\beta = \dfrac{1}{3}$, $A_{min} = 3$.
- [ ] **Phase shifts:** RC phase shift → 180° (3 × 60°); Wien bridge → 0° at resonance.
- [ ] **Amplifier types:** BJT CE (inverting) for RC phase shift; Op-Amp non-inverting for Wien bridge.
- [ ] **Feedback factor signs:** RC phase shift has NEGATIVE $\beta$ (inverting); Wien bridge has POSITIVE $\beta$ (non-inverting).
- [ ] **Frequency tuning:** Both oscillators can be tuned by varying $R$ (using ganged potentiometers) or $C$ (using switched capacitor banks).
- [ ] **Amplitude stabilization:** Wien bridge uses AGC (diodes, thermistors, JFETs) for low-distortion sine wave; RC phase shift is harder to stabilize.
- [ ] **SPICE simulation tip:** Use `IC` (initial condition) on a capacitor to kick-start oscillations; otherwise the simulation may show zero output.
- [ ] **KTU Viva favorites:** Difference between damped/undamped oscillations, role of $\sqrt{6}$, why three sections, why gain 29/3.
- [ ] **Conversion factor to memorize:** $\sqrt{6} \approx 2.449$; $2\pi \approx 6.2832$.
- [ ] **Typical lab frequencies:** RC phase shift ~1 kHz; Wien bridge ~1.5 kHz (with standard $R = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $C = 0.01\text{ }\mu\text{F}$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Amplifiers (Transformerless) Simulation - Class B and Class AB
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL407 - ANALOG CIRCUITS AND SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Power Amplifiers (Transformerless) Simulation - Class B and Class AB

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transformerless Power Amplifier (TLPA):** A power amplifier topology that achieves impedance matching and DC isolation **without using an output transformer**, instead relying on complementary transistor pairs (NPN–PNP), direct-coupled emitter followers, or quasi-complementary Darlington configurations.

**Class B Power Amplifier:** A push-pull amplifier in which each transistor in the output pair conducts for **exactly 180°** of the input cycle (conduction angle = π radians). One transistor amplifies the positive half-cycle, the complementary transistor amplifies the negative half-cycle. The quiescent (DC) bias current is essentially **zero (I_CQ ≈ 0)**.

**Class AB Power Amplifier:** A push-pull amplifier in which each output transistor is biased with a small forward voltage (≈ V_BE) so that the quiescent current is small but non-zero. The conduction angle lies **between 180° and 360°** (typically **200° to 220°**). This small bias eliminates the dead-zone at the zero-crossing of the input signal.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition: The Two-Person Rowing Boat**
>
> Imagine a long boat being rowed across a river:
> - **Class A** = One rower rows constantly, even when no passenger is on board. The rower works 360° of every cycle. It works fine, but **wastes enormous energy** (efficiency only 25%).
> - **Class B** = Two rowers take turns. Rower-1 rows only when the right oar is needed (positive half-cycle), Rower-2 only when the left oar is needed (negative half-cycle). They never both row, and never row when idle. **Highly efficient (78.6%)**, but there is a brief moment during the hand-off when **no one is rowing** — the boat momentarily drifts → this is **crossover distortion**.
> - **Class AB** = Same two rowers, but each pulls the oar with a tiny resting tension so that during the hand-off, **both are slightly engaged**. The transition is smooth → **no drift, no dead-zone**, and efficiency is still reasonably high (~ 50–70 %).

In an electronic sense, the "oars" are the emitter currents of the NPN and PNP transistors, and the "passenger" is the AC load (loudspeaker). A transformer is avoided because modern matched complementary transistors (or Darlington pairs) can directly drive a low-impedance speaker.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value / Range |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum theoretical efficiency of Class B | η_max | **π/4 = 78.6 %** |
| Typical efficiency of Class AB | η_AB | **50 % – 70 %** |
| Quiescent current (Class B) | I_CQ | **≈ 0** |
| Quiescent current (Class AB) | I_CQ | **5 mA – 50 mA** |
| Conduction angle of Class B | θ_c | **180° (π rad)** |
| Conduction angle of Class AB | θ_c | **200° – 220°** |
| Diode bias string voltage | V_bias | **2 V_BE ≈ 1.2 V – 1.4 V** |
| Crossover distortion amplitude | V_cross | **≈ ± V_BE = ± 0.7 V** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Memory Hook:** Whenever a problem gives **V_CC**, **R_L**, and asks for maximum efficiency, the first number to write is always **η_max = π/4 = 0.785 = 78.6 %** for an ideal Class B push-pull stage. For Class AB, the efficiency expression is the **same form**, but the peak efficiency is slightly lower because of the standing bias current.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Circuit Operation – Class B Complementary Symmetry Push-Pull

The complementary symmetry push-pull stage uses one **NPN transistor (Q1)** and one **PNP transistor (Q2)** connected in emitter-follower configuration. The two emitters are tied together and feed the load R_L directly (no transformer). The bases of Q1 and Q2 are joined at the input node.

**Step-by-step operating logic:**

1. When **V_in > V_BE(on) ≈ +0.7 V** → Q1 (NPN) turns ON, Q2 (PNP) is OFF. The output follows V_in minus V_BE (≈ V_in − 0.7 V). Current flows **out of** the emitter of Q1 into R_L.
2. When **V_in < −V_BE(on) ≈ −0.7 V** → Q2 (PNP) turns ON, Q1 is OFF. The output follows V_in plus V_BE (≈ V_in + 0.7 V). Current flows **into** the emitter of Q2 from R_L.
3. When **−V_BE < V_in < +V_BE** (the **dead-zone** of width ≈ 1.4 V) → both transistors are OFF. The output stays near 0 V regardless of V_in → this is the source of **crossover distortion**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why we need Class AB:** The dead-zone of width 2 V_BE is exactly the **crossover distortion** that appears as a "kink" or "notch" near zero in the output waveform. By pre-biasing the bases with a small voltage V_bias = 2 V_BE (using diodes, V_BE multiplier, or resistor divider), we slide both transistors just past their cut-in voltage. The transfer characteristic becomes a **straight line through the origin**, and the dead-zone vanishes.

## 2.2 Circuit Operation – Class AB with Diode Biasing

The diode biasing network (D1, D2, and resistor R) provides a small, **temperature-stable** forward voltage between the bases of Q1 and Q2. Because the diodes are mounted on the same heatsink as the power transistors, their V_BE drops track the transistor V_BE's, keeping the quiescent current stable with temperature.

The current through the bias diodes is set by R:

$$I_{bias} = \frac{V_{CC} - 2V_{BE}}{R}$$

The resulting V_BE × 2 drop is approximately 1.2 – 1.4 V at room temperature, which is **just enough** to overcome the cut-in voltages of both Q1 and Q2, keeping them at the **edge of conduction** (Class AB operation, I_CQ ≈ 5–50 mA).

## 2.3 Why Transformerless?

In traditional Class B/AB amplifiers, an **output transformer** was used to:
- Block the DC component of the collector current (which would otherwise burn out a low-impedance speaker).
- Provide impedance matching between the high-impedance collector load and the low-impedance (4 Ω, 8 Ω) speaker.

The complementary symmetry topology eliminates the transformer by:
- Using **NPN + PNP** matched transistors whose emitters are at the same potential → output can be DC-coupled to the speaker (often through a large output capacitor in single-supply designs).
- For dual-supply (±V_CC) operation, the output is **directly coupled** to the load → no output capacitor, no transformer, no phase shift.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Transformerless Class AB amplifiers are the heart of every audio amplifier IC (LM386, TDA2030, LM3886, TPA3116). They are smaller, lighter, cheaper, and have wider bandwidth than transformer-coupled designs. The simulation in this experiment lets you visualize crossover distortion and confirm the diode-biasing fix.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Conduction angle of Class B | θ_c = π = 180° | rad / ° | Each device ON for half-cycle |
| 2 | Conduction angle of Class AB | 180° < θ_c < 360° | ° | Typically 200°–220° |
| 3 | Peak output voltage (ideal) | V_p = V_CC − V_CE(sat) | V | For ±V_CC dual supply |
| 4 | RMS output voltage | V_rms = V_p / √2 | V | Sinusoidal assumption |
| 5 | AC power delivered to load | P_AC = V_p² / (2 R_L) | W | Also = V_rms² / R_L |
| 6 | DC power drawn from supply | P_DC = (2/π) · V_p · V_CC / R_L | W | Average over full cycle |
| 7 | Maximum theoretical efficiency | **η_max = π/4 = 78.6 %** | – | Occurs at V_p = V_CC |
| 8 | Power dissipated in transistors | P_D = P_DC − P_AC | W | Equal split: P_Q1 = P_Q2 = P_D/2 |
| 9 | Maximum power dissipation (Class B) | P_D(max) = 2 V_CC² / (π² R_L) | W | Occurs at V_p = 2 V_CC / π |
| 10 | Quiescent bias voltage (Class AB) | V_bias ≈ 2 V_BE | V | ≈ 1.2 V – 1.4 V (silicon) |
| 11 | Bias-diode current | I_bias = (V_CC − 2 V_BE)/R | A | Sets quiescent operating point |
| 12 | Crossover distortion magnitude | V_cross ≈ ± V_BE | V | Dead-zone width = 2 V_BE |
| 13 | THD with crossover distortion | THD > 1 % (Class B) | % | Drops to < 0.1 % in Class AB |
| 14 | General efficiency expression | η = (π/4) · (V_p / V_CC) | – | Holds for B and AB |

> [!WARNING]
> **Mark-loss alert:** In KTU exams, students frequently confuse the **AC power** delivered to the load with the **DC power** drawn from the supply. Remember: **P_AC** is what the *load* sees; **P_DC** is what the *supply* delivers. The difference (P_DC − P_AC) is heat dissipated in the transistors. This distinction appears in nearly every 14-mark derivation problem.

## 2.5 Sub-categories of Transformerless Push-Pull

| Topology | Description | Advantage | Disadviation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Complementary Symmetry** | One NPN + one PNP matched pair | Simple, fewest components | Hard to find matched high-power PNP |
| **Quasi-Complementary** | NPN + NPN (or PNP + PNP) using Darlington / Sziklai pairs | Both transistors can be NPN (cheaper) | Slightly more complex, asymmetric V_BE |
| **Output-capacitor (OTL) coupled** | Single V_CC supply, large C_out blocks DC to speaker | Uses only one supply | Large C_out (1000 µF+) needed, low-freq limit |
| **Direct-coupled (OCL)** | Dual ±V_CC supply, speaker directly between emitters and GND | No C_out, full audio bandwidth | Needs matched transistors and dual supply |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strict Exhaustive-Derivation Mandate:** Every algebraic step is shown below. No transitions are skipped, and every conversion is annotated with a plain-English explanation.

## 3.1 Derivation of Maximum Efficiency of Class B Push-Pull Amplifier

### Step 1 — Define the output voltage waveform

The output voltage across the load R_L for a sinusoidal input is:

$$v_o(t) = V_p \sin(\omega t)$$

where V_p is the peak output voltage (limited by V_CC − V_CE(sat) on each side). This is a standard sinusoidal assumption used in all KTU derivations.

### Step 2 — Compute the AC power delivered to the load

The instantaneous power dissipated in R_L is:

$$p_o(t) = \frac{v_o^2(t)}{R_L} = \frac{V_p^2 \sin^2(\omega t)}{R_L}$$

The average (AC) power over one full cycle is:

$$P_{AC} = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T p_o(t)\,dt = \frac{V_p^2}{R_L}\cdot\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T \sin^2(\omega t)\,dt$$

Using the trigonometric identity $\sin^2(\omega t) = \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2}$:

$$P_{AC} = \frac{V_p^2}{R_L}\cdot\frac{1}{2} = \frac{V_p^2}{2 R_L}$$

**Plain-English explanation:** The $\cos(2\omega t)$ term averages to zero over a full cycle, leaving only the constant $\frac{1}{2}$ factor. This gives the familiar $V_{rms}^2 / R_L$ result because $V_{rms} = V_p/\sqrt{2}$.

### Step 3 — Compute the DC current drawn from each supply

Each supply (V_CC and −V_CC) delivers current to the load only during the half-cycle when its associated transistor is ON. For the positive half-cycle, Q1 (NPN) sources current i_C1(t) into the load:

$$i_{C1}(t) = \frac{V_p \sin(\omega t)}{R_L}, \quad \text{for } 0 \leq \omega t \leq \pi$$

For the negative half-cycle, Q2 (PNP) sources current from the −V_CC rail:

$$i_{C2}(t) = \frac{V_p \sin(\omega t)}{R_L}, \quad \text{for } \pi \leq \omega t \leq 2\pi$$

The current from each supply is therefore a **half-wave-rectified sine wave** of peak value V_p / R_L.

### Step 4 — Average the half-sine current to get the DC supply current

The average current from the **positive** V_CC supply over a full period is:

$$I_{DC(+)} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_0^{\pi} \frac{V_p \sin(\omega t)}{R_L}\,d(\omega t)$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$I_{DC(+)} = \frac{V_p}{2\pi R_L}\bigl[-\cos(\omega t)\bigr]_0^{\pi} = \frac{V_p}{2\pi R_L}\bigl[-\cos(\pi) + \cos(0)\bigr]$$

$$I_{DC(+)} = \frac{V_p}{2\pi R_L}\bigl[1 + 1\bigr] = \frac{V_p}{\pi R_L}$$

By symmetry, the **negative** −V_CC supply contributes an identical DC current:

$$I_{DC(-)} = \frac{V_p}{\pi R_L}$$

**Plain-English explanation:** The average of a half-sine wave over a full period is (peak)/(π). The factor of 1/2 in (V_p)/(2πR_L) comes from averaging over the full 2π period, while the integration only covers π (one half-cycle).

### Step 5 — Compute the total DC power drawn from both supplies

The total DC power is the sum of the power drawn from each supply:

$$P_{DC} = V_{CC}\cdot I_{DC(+)} + V_{CC}\cdot I_{DC(-)} = 2 V_{CC}\cdot \frac{V_p}{\pi R_L} = \frac{2 V_{CC} V_p}{\pi R_L}$$

**Plain-English explanation:** Both supplies deliver the same average current, and each supply voltage has magnitude V_CC, so we add the two contributions.

### Step 6 — Form the efficiency expression

Efficiency is defined as the ratio of AC power delivered to the load versus the DC power drawn from the supplies:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{AC}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{\dfrac{V_p^2}{2 R_L}}{\dfrac{2 V_{CC} V_p}{\pi R_L}}$$

Simplify by cancelling one factor of V_p / R_L:

$$\eta = \frac{V_p}{2 R_L} \cdot \frac{\pi R_L}{2 V_{CC}} = \frac{\pi V_p}{4 V_{CC}}$$

### Step 7 — Apply the maximum-output condition V_p = V_CC

The largest possible peak output voltage (ignoring V_CE(sat)) is V_CC. Substituting V_p = V_CC:

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{\pi V_{CC}}{4 V_{CC}} = \frac{\pi}{4} = 0.785 = 78.6\,\%$$

**Plain-English explanation:** The amplifier reaches its highest efficiency when the output swing is as large as possible. At that limit, the ratio (V_p / V_CC) = 1, and the formula collapses to the famous π/4 number. Any smaller input signal gives proportionally lower efficiency.

> [!TIP]
> **Final consolidated result:**
> $$\boxed{\;\eta_{max} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} = 78.6\,\% \quad \text{(ideal Class B push-pull)}\;}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Power Dissipation in Transistors

### Step 1 — Express total transistor dissipation

The total power dissipated in the two transistors is the DC power drawn from the supply minus the AC power delivered to the load:

$$P_D = P_{DC} - P_{AC} = \frac{2 V_{CC} V_p}{\pi R_L} - \frac{V_p^2}{2 R_L}$$

**Plain-English explanation:** Whatever energy is supplied and not delivered to the load must be converted to heat in the transistors.

### Step 2 — Find the V_p that maximizes P_D

Differentiate P_D with respect to V_p and set the derivative to zero:

$$\frac{dP_D}{dV_p} = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi R_L} - \frac{2 V_p}{2 R_L} = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi R_L} - \frac{V_p}{R_L} = 0$$

Solving for V_p:

$$\frac{V_p}{R_L} = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi R_L} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad V_{p,\,P_D(max)} = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi}$$

**Plain-English explanation:** The transistor dissipation is maximum at a moderate output level — not at the peak. This is the worst-case thermal condition the heatsink must handle.

### Step 3 — Substitute V_p = 2 V_CC / π into the P_D expression

$$P_{D(max)} = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi R_L} \cdot \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi} - \frac{1}{2 R_L}\left(\frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi}\right)^2$$

$$P_{D(max)} = \frac{4 V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L} - \frac{4 V_{CC}^2}{2 \pi^2 R_L} = \frac{4 V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L} - \frac{2 V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L} = \frac{2 V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L}$$
