Each transistor shares this dissipation equally (due to the symmetry of the push-pull stage), so:

$$\boxed{\;P_{Q1(max)} = P_{Q2(max)} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L}\;}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Crossover Distortion Magnitude (Class B)

### Step 1 — Identify the dead-zone

In Class B, the transfer characteristic is a piecewise-linear function. The output follows the input only when |V_in| > V_BE. Therefore, for the input range −V_BE ≤ V_in ≤ +V_BE, the output remains at 0 V (since both transistors are OFF).

### Step 2 — Quantify the width of the dead-zone

The dead-zone has a total width of:

$$\Delta V_{in} = 2 V_{BE} \approx 1.4 \text{ V (silicon)}$$

**Plain-English explanation:** Because each transistor needs ≈ 0.7 V to start conducting, the combined stage "ignores" the central 1.4 V of the input signal. For low-amplitude inputs (e.g., 50 mV), the output is essentially zero — the amplifier is **dead** for small signals.

### Step 3 — Effect on the output waveform

A small-signal sine input of amplitude A < V_BE will produce **zero output** in Class B. For larger inputs, the output waveform has a flat region (a "notch") near each zero crossing. In the frequency domain, this notch introduces strong **odd-order harmonics** (3rd, 5th, 7th) and elevates **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** to several percent — unacceptable for hi-fi audio.

### Step 4 — Class AB fix

Adding a bias voltage V_bias ≈ 2 V_BE between the bases of Q1 and Q2 shifts the transfer characteristic vertically so that the dead-zone disappears. The new quiescent operating point is at the **edge of conduction** (V_CE ≈ V_CC / 2 for each transistor), and the transfer curve becomes a **single straight line** through the origin. Crossover distortion → essentially zero.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Class B vs Class AB

| Parameter | Class B | Class AB |
|---|---|---|
| Quiescent current I_CQ | **0** | **5 mA – 50 mA** |
| Conduction angle | **180°** | **200° – 220°** |
| Maximum efficiency | **78.6 %** | **50 % – 70 %** |
| Crossover distortion | **Severe (notch near 0 V)** | **Negligible (eliminated by V_bias)** |
| THD | **> 1 %** (often 5–10 %) | **< 0.1 %** (with proper bias) |
| Power dissipation at zero signal | **0** | **Small but non-zero (2 V_CC I_CQ)** |
| Biasing complexity | **Trivial (no bias needed)** | **Diode string or V_BE multiplier** |
| Thermal stability | **Good (no I_CQ drift issue)** | **Requires bias tracking with T** |
| Use case | **Low-power, low-fidelity** | **Audio amplifiers, hi-fi, IC output stages** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of Class B Complementary Symmetry Push-Pull (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["V in AC Source"] --> nodeB["Bias Network V bias approx 0 V for Class B"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Base of Q1 NPN Upper"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Base of Q2 PNP Lower"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Q1 Emitter Follower Active on Positive Half Cycle"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["Q2 Emitter Follower Active on Negative Half Cycle"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["Common Emitter Node V out"]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH["R L Speaker Load 4 to 8 ohm"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Ground Node"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ["Output Coupling Capacitor Optional in OTL"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Direct to Speaker"]
    nodeE --> nodeL["V CC Positive Rail"]
    nodeF --> nodeM["V EE Negative Rail or Ground"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The arrows trace the signal path from the AC source through the bias network into the bases of the complementary pair. Both transistors are wired as **emitter followers**, so their emitters are tied together at the output node. The speaker load sits between the output node and ground (or, in single-supply OTL designs, between the output node and ground via a large coupling capacitor).

## 4.2 Block Diagram of Class AB with Diode-Bias Network

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["V in AC Source"] --> nodeB["Input Coupling Capacitor C in"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Base Node of Q1 and Q2"]
    nodeL["V CC Positive Rail"] --> nodeD["Bias Resistor R bias"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Diode D1 Anode"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Diode D2 Cathode"]
    nodeF --> nodeM["V EE Negative Rail or Ground"]
    nodeE --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeG["Base of Q1 NPN Driver"]
    nodeC --> nodeH["Base of Q2 PNP Driver"]
    nodeG --> nodeI["Q1 Emitter Output Upper"]
    nodeH --> nodeJ["Q2 Emitter Output Lower"]
    nodeI --> nodeK["Common Output Node V out"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeL --> nodeI
    nodeM --> nodeJ
    nodeK --> nodeN["R L Speaker Load"]
    nodeN --> nodeO["Ground"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bias-network function:** The resistor R_bias and the two series diodes (D1, D2) form a **diode bias string**. The forward drop across D1 and D2 is approximately 2 × 0.7 V = 1.4 V, which is exactly the bias needed to keep both Q1 and Q2 just at the edge of conduction. Mounting D1 and D2 on the same heatsink as Q1 and Q2 makes V_D track V_BE, providing excellent **thermal stabilization** of the quiescent current.

## 4.3 Signal-Flow and Waveform Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Vin Small Sinusoid"] --> nodeB["Class B Stage"]
    nodeB --> nodeC{"Output"}
    nodeC --> nodeD["V out with Notch at Zero Crossing"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["V out Smooth Linear Through Origin"]

    nodeA2["Vin Small Sinusoid"] --> nodeB2["Class AB Stage"]
    nodeB2 --> nodeE
```

**Reading the diagram:** The left branch shows that a small-amplitude input passing through a Class B stage produces a notched output (crossover distortion). The right branch shows that the same input through a properly biased Class AB stage produces a clean, linear output — this is the **primary visual difference** you will observe in the SPICE transient analysis.

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Power Flow in a Push-Pull Stage

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["V CC Supply"] -->|"P DC"| nodeB["Two Half Wave Rectified Currents from Q1 and Q2"]
    nodeB -->|"P AC"| nodeC["R L Load Speaker"]
    nodeB -->|"P D Heat"| nodeD["Transistor Pair Heatsink"]

    nodeE["Signal Source V in"] -->|"Controls switching of Q1 and Q2"| nodeB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** The DC supply feeds current into the transistors; the transistors act as **valves** that direct the current into the load (useful work) or into the heatsink (wasted heat). The efficiency of the amplifier is the ratio (P_AC) / (P_DC). A good Class AB design minimizes P_D by ensuring V_p is as large as possible.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Pattern Reference (KTU 2024 Scheme ESE / Model Exam):**
> - Part A: Short-answer questions, **3 marks each** (typically 2 from the experiment).
> - Part B: Long-answer questions, **14 marks each** (typically one full design + analysis problem per experiment).
> - Total marks per experiment module: 10–14 marks.

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**Define a Class B power amplifier. What is its maximum theoretical efficiency?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Class B power amplifier is a push-pull configuration in which each transistor conducts for exactly **180°** of the input cycle. The quiescent DC bias current is **I_CQ ≈ 0**, so each device amplifies one half of the input waveform. The maximum theoretical efficiency of an ideal Class B push-pull amplifier is:

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{\pi}{4} = 78.6\,\%$$

(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for stating 180° conduction, 1 mark for the efficiency value with the formula.)

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**What is crossover distortion? How is it eliminated in a Class AB amplifier?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Crossover distortion is the **nonlinearity** that appears in the output of a Class B push-pull amplifier near the zero-crossing of the input signal. It arises because both transistors are OFF in the input range −V_BE ≤ V_in ≤ +V_BE (a dead-zone of ≈ 1.4 V), producing a notch in the output waveform. **(1.5 marks)**

It is eliminated in a Class AB amplifier by applying a small forward bias of **V_bias ≈ 2 V_BE (≈ 1.2 – 1.4 V)** between the bases of the two output transistors, so that both are kept at the **edge of conduction** at the quiescent point. The dead-zone vanishes, and the transfer characteristic becomes a single straight line through the origin. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 2 Carried Over]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **transformerless complementary symmetry Class B push-pull amplifier**. Derive the expression for its maximum efficiency.

**(b) [7 marks]** A Class B push-pull amplifier operates from ±15 V supplies and drives an 8 Ω load. Calculate:
1. The maximum AC power delivered to the load.
2. The DC power drawn from the supplies at maximum output.
3. The maximum power dissipation in each transistor.
4. The efficiency at V_p = 10 V (peak).

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Circuit diagram (textual representation):**

> V_CC (+15 V) → Collector of Q1 (NPN, e.g., 2N3055)
> Emitter of Q1 ─┐
>                ├── Common output node V_out → R_L (8 Ω) → Ground (0 V)
> Emitter of Q2 ─┘
> Collector of Q2 (PNP, e.g., MJ2955) → V_EE (−15 V)
> Base of Q1 ─┐
>            ├── Joined at input node V_in
> Base of Q2 ─┘

**Operation:** When V_in > +0.7 V, Q1 conducts and sources current into R_L; when V_in < −0.7 V, Q2 conducts and sinks current from R_L. In the dead-zone (−0.7 V < V_in < +0.7 V), both transistors are OFF.

**Derivation of maximum efficiency:** *(Refer to Section 3.1, Steps 1–7 for the full derivation.)*

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{\pi}{4} = 78.6\,\%$$

(Marks distribution: 2 marks for diagram, 2 marks for operating description, 3 marks for derivation.)

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

Given: V_CC = 15 V, R_L = 8 Ω. Assume V_CE(sat) ≈ 0 (ideal).

**(1) Maximum AC power delivered to the load:**

At maximum output, V_p = V_CC = 15 V.

$$P_{AC(max)} = \frac{V_p^2}{2 R_L} = \frac{(15)^2}{2 \times 8} = \frac{225}{16} = 14.06 \text{ W}$$

**(2) DC power drawn from supplies at maximum output:**

$$P_{DC} = \frac{2 V_{CC} V_p}{\pi R_L} = \frac{2 \times 15 \times 15}{\pi \times 8} = \frac{450}{25.133} = 17.90 \text{ W}$$

**(3) Maximum power dissipation in each transistor:**

The maximum dissipation occurs at V_p = 2 V_CC / π = 2 × 15 / π = 9.55 V.

$$P_{D(max)} = \frac{2 V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L} = \frac{2 \times 225}{\pi^2 \times 8} = \frac{450}{78.957} = 5.70 \text{ W (total)}$$

Per transistor:

$$P_{Q1(max)} = P_{Q2(max)} = \frac{P_{D(max)}}{2} = 2.85 \text{ W}$$

**(4) Efficiency at V_p = 10 V:**

$$\eta = \frac{\pi V_p}{4 V_{CC}} = \frac{\pi \times 10}{4 \times 15} = \frac{31.416}{60} = 0.5236 = 52.4\,\%$$

**Verification:** At V_p = 10 V, $P_{AC} = \frac{100}{16} = 6.25$ W, $P_{DC} = \frac{2 \times 15 \times 10}{\pi \times 8} = 11.94$ W, so $\eta = 6.25 / 11.94 = 52.3\,\%$ ✓ (rounding error).

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2024, Module 2 Alternative]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the operation of a **Class AB complementary symmetry power amplifier** with a diode-biasing network. Show how crossover distortion is eliminated. Sketch the input–output transfer characteristic with and without the bias network.

**(b) [7 marks]** In a Class AB amplifier with ±20 V supplies, the quiescent current in each output transistor is 20 mA. The amplifier drives a 4 Ω load. Estimate:
1. The standing DC power dissipation with no input signal.
2. The maximum AC output power (assume V_p = V_CC).
3. The total DC power drawn from the supplies at maximum output.
4. The conversion efficiency at maximum output.

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Circuit description:** A diode-bias string consisting of R_bias, D1, and D2 is connected between +V_CC and −V_EE. The forward drop across D1 + D2 ≈ 1.4 V appears between the bases of Q1 (NPN) and Q2 (PNP). This pre-bias keeps both transistors at the edge of conduction (I_CQ ≈ 5–50 mA), eliminating the dead-zone.

**Transfer characteristic:**

| Input voltage V_in | Output voltage V_out (Class B) | Output voltage V_out (Class AB) |
|---|---|---|
| V_in > +V_BE | V_out = V_in − V_BE (linear) | V_out = V_in − V_BE (linear) |
| −V_BE < V_in < +V_BE | V_out = 0 (DEAD-ZONE) | V_out = V_in (linear through origin) |
| V_in < −V_BE | V_out = V_in + V_BE (linear) | V_out = V_in + V_BE (linear) |

The Class B curve has a **horizontal step at V_out = 0** for |V_in| < V_BE. The Class AB curve is a **single straight line** through the origin.

**Key advantage:** The Class AB transfer curve has no notch, so small-signal inputs are amplified faithfully. THD drops from several percent (Class B) to well under 0.1 % (Class AB).

(Marks distribution: 2 marks for circuit description, 2 marks for transfer characteristic sketch, 3 marks for explanation of how dead-zone is eliminated.)

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

Given: V_CC = 20 V, R_L = 4 Ω, I_CQ = 20 mA (per transistor).

**(1) Standing DC power dissipation with no input signal:**

With no input, each transistor conducts I_CQ. The total quiescent dissipation is:

$$P_{D(quiescent)} = 2 \times V_{CC} \times I_{CQ} = 2 \times 20 \times 0.020 = 0.80 \text{ W}$$

**(2) Maximum AC output power:**

At V_p = V_CC = 20 V:

$$P_{AC(max)} = \frac{V_p^2}{2 R_L} = \frac{(20)^2}{2 \times 4} = \frac{400}{8} = 50 \text{ W}$$

**(3) Total DC power drawn from supplies at maximum output:**

$$P_{DC} = \frac{2 V_{CC} V_p}{\pi R_L} = \frac{2 \times 20 \times 20}{\pi \times 4} = \frac{800}{12.566} = 63.66 \text{ W}$$

**(4) Conversion efficiency at maximum output:**

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{P_{AC}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{50}{63.66} = 0.7854 = 78.5\,\% \approx \frac{\pi}{4}$$

(Note: The small quiescent bias current has negligible effect on the maximum efficiency because it is much smaller than the signal-driven currents at high output levels.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Board Exams (Power Amplifier Questions):**
>
> 1. **Confusing V_p with V_CC:** Students often substitute V_CC into formulas that ask for V_p (or vice-versa). Always read the question carefully — *maximum* output means V_p = V_CC; *general* output means V_p is a variable.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the DC power:** The total DC power is drawn from **two** supplies (±V_CC). Many students compute the current from only one supply and forget to double it. The full formula is $P_{DC} = \frac{2 V_{CC} V_p}{\pi R_L}$, not $\frac{V_{CC} V_p}{\pi R_L}$.
> 3. **Miscounting the conduction angle:** Class B = **180° per device**, **360° total** for the pair. Class AB = **200°–220° per device**. Do NOT write 360° per device.
> 4. **Stating efficiency without the formula:** Examiners expect the symbolic derivation $\eta = \pi V_p / (4 V_{CC})$ before the numerical substitution. Writing only the final number 78.6 % without justification will cost 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Ignoring crossover distortion in Class B:** Even if the numerical part of the question is about Class AB, you should explicitly mention that Class B *suffers* from crossover distortion. Examiners love this "compare and contrast" angle.
> 6. **Drawing transformer-coupled diagrams in a transformerless question:** The question explicitly says "transformerless" — any diagram showing an output transformer is **wrong** and will attract a 1–2 mark penalty.
> 7. **Sign error in the bias-voltage formula:** $V_{bias} = 2 V_{BE} \approx +1.4 \text{ V}$ (NOT −1.4 V) measured from base of Q2 to base of Q1. The polarity is critical.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam!**

- ✅ **Class B:** Conduction angle **180°**, I_CQ ≈ 0, max efficiency **π/4 = 78.6 %**, suffers from **crossover distortion** (±V_BE dead-zone).
- ✅ **Class AB:** Conduction angle **200°–220°**, I_CQ ≈ 5–50 mA, efficiency **50 % – 70 %**, **crossover distortion eliminated** by V_bias ≈ 2 V_BE.
- ✅ **Transformerless design** = complementary symmetry (NPN + PNP) or quasi-complementary (Darlington / Sziklai pairs), no output transformer.
- ✅ **Maximum AC power:** $P_{AC(max)} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L}$ — occurs at V_p = V_CC.
- ✅ **Maximum DC power:** $P_{DC} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC} V_p}{\pi R_L}$ — remember the **factor of 2** for dual supply.
- ✅ **Maximum transistor dissipation:** $P_{D(max)} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L}$ — **NOT** at full output, but at $V_p = 2V_{CC}/\pi \approx 0.637 V_{CC}$.
- ✅ **Crossover distortion magnitude:** ±V_BE ≈ ±0.7 V per device → total dead-zone ≈ 1.4 V.
- ✅ **Biasing network in Class AB:** Diode string, V_BE multiplier, or resistor divider; diodes mounted on heatsink for thermal tracking.
- ✅ **Efficiency formula (general):** $\eta = \dfrac{\pi V_p}{4 V_{CC}}$ — maximum 78.6 % when V_p = V_CC.
- ✅ **Simulation checklist (LTspice / Multisim):** Run **DC operating point** to confirm I_CQ; run **transient analysis** to view input vs output (look for notch in Class B, clean sine in Class AB); run **Fourier analysis** to measure THD.
- ✅ **Common load values in problems:** **4 Ω, 8 Ω** (audio speakers).
- ✅ **Conduction angle formula reminder:** θ_c = 360° × (fraction of cycle the device conducts) = 180° for Class B.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line memory anchor for the night before the exam:**
> *"Class B = 180° per device, π/4 efficient, but distorted at the zero-crossing. Class AB = 220° per device, biased just past cut-in, smooth and almost as efficient. Transformerless = use complementary pairs and skip the iron."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Series Voltage Regulator Using BJT Simulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL407 - ANALOG CIRCUITS AND SIMULATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Series Voltage Regulator Using BJT Simulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Series Voltage Regulator** is a type of linear voltage regulator in which the controlling active element (a **Bipolar Junction Transistor**, BJT) is placed in **series** between the unregulated DC input source and the load. The pass transistor operates in the **active (linear) region** and behaves as a variable resistor whose dynamic resistance is continuously adjusted by a feedback control loop to maintain a constant DC output voltage despite variations in **input voltage (line variations)** or **load current (load variations)**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme lab context, the Series Voltage Regulator is realized using a discrete BJT (typically **2N2222 / SL100 / BD139** in hardware, or simulated using **SPICE models in Multisim / LTspice / OrCAD-PSpice**).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):** *Design and simulate a series voltage regulator using BJT to study line regulation, load regulation, and ripple rejection.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Variable Dam" Model

Imagine a water reservoir feeding a city through a pipe. The water level in the city (analogous to **Vout**) must remain constant even when:
- The reservoir level fluctuates due to rain/drought (analogous to **line variation ΔVin**).
- The number of open taps in the city changes (analogous to **load variation ΔIL**).

A **dam gate operator** (the **BJT pass transistor**) continuously opens or closes the gate:
- If the city water level drops → gate opens more → more water flows → level restored.
- If the city water level rises → gate closes → less water flows → level restored.

The dam operator's actions are dictated by a **float sensor and comparator** (analogous to the **Zener reference + sampling feedback network + error amplifier**). This is the essence of **closed-loop negative feedback** in a series regulator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Series"?** The control element (BJT) is connected **in series with the load**, not in parallel (which would be a *shunt regulator*). Series regulators are preferred over shunt regulators for **higher load currents** and **better efficiency** because the BJT does not need to divert excess current to ground.

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU High-Yield Parameters)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Ideal Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Line Regulation** | LR | % / V (or mV/V) | 0 (constant) |
| **Load Regulation** | LDR | % (or mV/mA) | 0 (constant) |
| **Ripple Rejection Ratio** | PSRR / RR | dB | > 40 dB |
| **Output Impedance** | Zout | Ω (milliohms) | 0 |
| **Temperature Coefficient** | TC | mV/°C | 0 |
| **Efficiency** | η | % | ≤ Vout/Vin |
| **Dropout Voltage** | Vdo | V | Minimum Vin − Vout |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture of a Series Voltage Regulator

A practical series regulator is built from **four functional blocks** (KTU board examiners expect this block-level understanding):

1. **Pass Element (BJT, series):** The main current-handling transistor (Q1) operating in the active region. It drops the excess voltage `(Vin − Vout)` and modulates its collector-emitter resistance.
2. **Reference Source (Zener Diode Dz):** Provides a temperature-stable reference voltage `Vz`. Often a Zener operated in reverse breakdown.
3. **Sampling Network (Resistor Divider R1, R2):** Senses a fraction of Vout and feeds it back to the error amplifier. Defines the **feedback factor** `β = R2 / (R1 + R2)`.
4. **Error Amplifier (BJT Q2 or Op-Amp):** Compares the sampled feedback voltage with Vz and generates the base drive for the pass transistor Q1 to correct any deviation.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

- **Step 1 (Sampling):** A fraction `β·Vout` is sampled by the divider R1–R2.
- **Step 2 (Comparison):** This sampled voltage is compared with the Zener reference `Vz`.
- **Step 3 (Error Generation):** Any difference `ε = Vz − β·Vout` is amplified by the error amplifier.
- **Step 4 (Correction):** The amplified error adjusts the base current of Q1.
  - If Vout **rises** → `β·Vout > Vz` → base drive of Q1 **decreases** → VCE(Q1) **increases** → Vout **falls back**.
  - If Vout **falls** → `β·Vout < Vz` → base drive of Q1 **increases** → VCE(Q1) **decreases** → Vout **rises back**.
- **Step 5 (Steady State):** In equilibrium, `β·Vout = Vz`, giving the closed-loop output:

$$V_{out} = V_z \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2} = \frac{V_z}{\beta}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Memorize these formulas verbatim. Question banks in the 2024 Scheme frequently ask derivations and numerical substitutions directly from this set.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Output Voltage (with feedback)** | $$V_{out} = V_z \cdot \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2}$$ | Closed-loop regulated output |
| 2 | **Output Voltage (simple Zener-BJT, no feedback)** | $$V_{out} = V_z + V_{BE}$$ | Q1 emitter follows Zener + VBE |
| 3 | **Feedback Factor** | $$\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$ | Sampling ratio |
| 4 | **Zener Current** | $$I_z = \dfrac{V_{in} - V_z}{R_S}$$ | Must lie between `Iz(min)` and `Iz(max)` |
| 5 | **Load Current (simple type)** | $$I_L \approx \dfrac{V_{out} - V_z}{R_E}$$ | Through emitter resistor RE |
| 6 | **Line Regulation** | $$LR = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} \times 100\ \%$$ | In % or mV/V |
| 7 | **Load Regulation** | $$LDR = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\ \%$$ | NL = No-Load, FL = Full-Load |
| 8 | **Ripple Rejection Ratio** | $$RR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{ripple(in)}}{V_{ripple(out)}}\right) \text{ dB}$$ | Typically 40–60 dB |
| 9 | **Output Impedance** | $$Z_{out} = \dfrac{r_e}{\beta \cdot A_{v(error)}}$$ | Very small (milliohms) |
| 10 | **Pass Transistor Dissipation** | $$P_D = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I_L$$ | Critical for heatsink design |
| 11 | **Dropout Condition** | $$V_{in(min)} = V_{out} + V_{CE(sat)} \approx V_{out} + 0.2\text{ V}$$ | Below this, regulation fails |
| 12 | **Efficiency** | $$\eta = \dfrac{V_{out} \cdot I_L}{V_{in} \cdot I_{in}} \times 100\ \%$$ | Upper-bounded by `Vout/Vin` |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mobile phone chargers & adapters** use series regulator ICs (e.g., **LM7805, LM7812**) built on this exact topology.
- **Automotive ECUs** require stable supply despite alternator voltage swings (12 V battery → 14.5 V during charging).
- **Bench DC power supplies** use series pass transistors (often **2N3055 / TIP3055**) with op-amp feedback.
- **PCB-level point-of-load regulation** in digital and analog mixed-signal boards.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Output Voltage with Feedback Divider

**Given:** A Zener reference `Vz` and a feedback divider `R1` (top, between Vout and feedback node) and `R2` (bottom, between feedback node and ground). An error amplifier drives the base of pass transistor Q1.

**Goal:** Derive the regulated output `Vout`.

**Step 1:** Apply the **voltage divider rule** to R1–R2. The voltage at the feedback node (call it `Vf`) is:

$$V_f = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 2:** In steady state, the error amplifier forces the feedback voltage to equal the Zener reference (this is the **virtual short** principle for the op-amp, or **balance condition** for a BJT differential pair):

$$V_f = V_z$$

**Step 3:** Equate the two expressions for `Vf`:

$$V_z = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for `Vout`:

$$V_{out} = V_z \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2}$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange using the feedback factor `β`:

$$\beta = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; V_{out} = \frac{V_z}{\beta}$$

> **Physical Meaning:** By changing the ratio `R1/R2`, any desired `Vout` greater than `Vz` can be obtained. This is exactly how adjustable regulators like **LM317** work.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Line Regulation (Closed-Form Expression)

**Given:** A change in input voltage `ΔVin` causes a small change `ΔVout` at the output. The closed-loop gain of the regulator (from Vin to Vout) is `Av(cl)`, while the open-loop gain of the error amplifier is `A`.

**Step 1:** In the **open loop** (no feedback), a change `ΔVin` would propagate to the output as `ΔVin` (since the pass transistor passes the input through, minus a small drop). The open-loop gain from input to output is approximately unity.

**Step 2:** With **negative feedback of factor β**, the closed-loop gain from Vin to Vout becomes:

$$A_{v(cl)} = \frac{A_{v(ol)}}{1 + A_{v(ol)} \cdot \beta} \approx \frac{1}{\beta} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{1}{A_{v(ol)} \cdot \beta}}$$

**Step 3:** For very high open-loop gain `Avol · β >> 1`, this simplifies to:

$$\frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} \approx \frac{1}{A_{ol} \cdot \beta}$$

**Step 4:** Therefore, the line regulation in mV/V is:

$$LR = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} \approx \frac{1}{A_{ol} \cdot \beta}$$

> **Conclusion:** Higher loop gain and tighter feedback factor → better (smaller) line regulation. This is why IC regulators use **op-amps with gain > 10⁵**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Load Regulation from Output Impedance

**Step 1:** The closed-loop output impedance of any feedback amplifier is:

$$Z_{out(cl)} = \frac{Z_{out(ol)}}{1 + A_{ol} \cdot \beta}$$

**Step 2:** A load current change `ΔIL` produces an output voltage change:

$$\Delta V_{out} = -Z_{out(cl)} \cdot \Delta I_L$$

**Step 3:** Substituting the closed-loop `Zout`:

$$\Delta V_{out} = -\frac{Z_{out(ol)}}{1 + A_{ol} \cdot \beta} \cdot \Delta I_L$$

**Step 4:** Load regulation (per unit current) is therefore:

$$LDR = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta I_L} = -\frac{Z_{out(ol)}}{1 + A_{ol} \cdot \beta}$$

> **Conclusion:** Load regulation improves (smaller magnitude) as `Aol · β` increases. For a discrete BJT error stage `Aol ≈ 100` to `1000`, while for an op-amp error stage `Aol > 10⁵`, giving near-ideal regulation.

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Lab Typical)

**Problem:** Design a BJT series regulator to deliver `Vout = +9 V` at `IL = 100 mA` from a `Vin = 15 V` supply. Use a Zener diode with `Vz = 5.6 V`, `Iz(min) = 5 mA`, `Iz(max) = 30 mA`, and a feedback divider with `R1 = 2.2 kΩ` and `R2 = 3.9 kΩ`.

**Step 1: Verify Output Voltage.**

$$V_{out} = 5.6 \times \frac{2.2 + 3.9}{3.9} = 5.6 \times \frac{6.1}{3.9} \approx 8.76 \text{ V}$$

Acceptable; close to 9 V. To get exactly 9 V, choose `R1 = 2.7 kΩ`:

$$V_{out} = 5.6 \times \frac{2.7 + 3.9}{3.9} = 5.6 \times 1.692 \approx 9.47 \text{ V}$$

Use `R1 = 2.4 kΩ`:

$$V_{out} = 5.6 \times \frac{2.4 + 3.9}{3.9} = 5.6 \times 1.615 \approx 9.05 \text{ V} \;\;✓$$

**Step 2: Zener Series Resistor RS.**

$$I_z = \frac{V_{in} - V_z}{R_S} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R_S = \frac{15 - 5.6}{15 \text{ mA}} = \frac{9.4}{0.015} \approx 627 \text{ Ω}$$

Use standard `RS = 620 Ω`.

**Step 3: Power Dissipation of Pass Transistor.**

$$P_D = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I_L = (15 - 9) \times 0.1 = 0.6 \text{ W}$$

A **BD139** (rated ~1.25 W with small heatsink) is sufficient.

**Step 4: Line Regulation Test.**

Simulate with `Vin = 15 V` → `Vout = 9.05 V`. Change `Vin` to `18 V` → record `Vout = 9.10 V`. Then:

$$LR = \frac{9.10 - 9.05}{18 - 15} = \frac{0.05}{3} \approx 16.7 \text{ mV/V} = 0.0167 \ \%/\text{V}$$

**Step 5: Load Regulation Test.**

With `Vin = 15 V`, `IL = 0` (no load) → `Vout = 9.08 V (NL)`. With `IL = 100 mA` (full load) → `Vout = 9.05 V (FL)`. Then:

$$LDR = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100 \ \% = \frac{9.08 - 9.05}{9.05} \times 100 \ \% \approx 0.33 \ \%$$

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define a series voltage regulator. Why is it preferred over a shunt regulator for high-current applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **series voltage regulator** is a linear voltage regulator in which a **controlling active element (BJT)** is connected **in series** between the unregulated input and the load, continuously adjusting its voltage drop to maintain a constant output voltage.

**Preferred over shunt regulator because:**
1. In a **shunt regulator**, excess current is **diverted (wasted) to ground**, leading to poor efficiency at high load currents. In a **series regulator**, the pass transistor drops only the *excess* voltage, dissipating less power.
2. Series regulators have **lower output impedance** and **better load regulation** due to inherent series-element feedback.
3. Series regulators can deliver **higher load currents** without overheating the pass element, since the same current flows through both regulator and load (no current diversion).

*(1 mark definition + 2 marks for comparison)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Differentiate between line regulation and load regulation. Mention their ideal values.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Line Regulation | Load Regulation |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Ability to maintain constant Vout despite changes in input line voltage Vin | Ability to maintain constant Vout despite changes in load current IL |
| **Formula** | `LR = (ΔVout / ΔVin) × 100 %` | `LDR = (VNL − VFL) / VFL × 100 %` |
| **Cause** | Source / supply variations | Load impedance variations |
| **Ideal Value** | 0 (zero) | 0 (zero) |

*(2 marks for definitions + formulas, 1 mark for ideal values and cause distinction)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates an **internal choice** between two full questions. Both options are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Design-Oriented*

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Design a BJT series voltage regulator using a Zener reference and a feedback divider to deliver `Vout = +12 V` at `IL = 150 mA` from a `Vin = 18 V` supply. The Zener is rated `Vz = 5.6 V`, `Pz(max) = 0.5 W`. The pass transistor is **BD139** with `β(dc) = 40` and `VBE = 0.7 V`. Compute `R1`, `R2`, `RS`, and the pass-transistor power dissipation. Verify dropout margin.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Choose Zener Current.**
Maximum allowed: `Iz(max) = Pz(max) / Vz = 0.5 / 5.6 ≈ 89 mA`. Choose safe `Iz = 20 mA`.

**Step 2: Compute Zener Series Resistor RS.**

$$R_S = \frac{V_{in} - V_z}{I_z} = \frac{18 - 5.6}{20 \text{ mA}} = \frac{12.4}{0.020} = 620 \text{ Ω}$$

**Step 3: Choose R2 and Compute R1 from Output Voltage Formula.**

Choose standard `R2 = 1 kΩ`. Then:

$$R_1 = R_2 \cdot \left(\frac{V_{out}}{V_z} - 1\right) = 1\text{k} \cdot \left(\frac{12}{5.6} - 1\right) = 1\text{k} \cdot 1.143 = 1.14 \text{ kΩ}$$

Use standard `R1 = 1.2 kΩ` (gives `Vout ≈ 12.32 V`, acceptable tolerance).

**Step 4: Pass Transistor Power Dissipation.**

$$P_D = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I_L = (18 - 12) \times 0.15 = 0.9 \text{ W}$$

A small clip-on heatsink is required (BD139 in TO-126 package dissipates ~1.25 W free-air, marginal — heatsink strongly recommended).

**Step 5: Dropout Margin Check.**

$$V_{in(min)} = V_{out} + V_{CE(sat)} = 12 + 0.2 = 12.2 \text{ V}$$

Since `Vin = 18 V > 12.2 V`, dropout margin is `5.8 V` — safe. ✔

**Step 6: Base Current and Drive Capability.**

$$I_B = \frac{I_L}{\beta} = \frac{150 \text{ mA}}{40} = 3.75 \text{ mA}$$

The error amplifier (Q2) must source at least 3.75 mA into the base of Q1. ✔

---

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** For the regulator designed in (a), simulate in SPICE with `Vin` swept from **14 V to 20 V** and report:
(i) Output voltage variation
(ii) Line regulation in mV/V
(iii) Plot the regulation curve.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: SPICE Setup.**
Use a DC sweep: `.DC Vin 14 20 0.5`. Probe node Vout.

**Step 2: Simulated Reading (typical values, BD139 + 5.6 V Zener + R1 = 1.2 kΩ, R2 = 1 kΩ):**

| Vin (V) | Vout (V) | ΔVout (mV) |
|---|---|---|
| 14.0 | 12.18 | — |
| 15.0 | 12.20 | +20 |
| 16.0 | 12.22 | +20 |
| 17.0 | 12.23 | +10 |
| 18.0 | 12.24 | +10 |
| 19.0 | 12.25 | +10 |
| 20.0 | 12.25 | 0 |

**Step 3: Line Regulation.**

$$LR = \frac{V_{out}(20V) - V_{out}(14V)}{20 - 14} = \frac{12.25 - 12.18}{6} = \frac{0.07}{6} \approx 11.67 \text{ mV/V}$$

In percentage: `LR ≈ 0.097 %/V` (excellent for a discrete design).

**Step 4: Plot Generation.**
Use `.PROBE` or the waveform viewer in Multisim/LTspice. X-axis: Vin (V), Y-axis: Vout (V). The curve is **nearly flat**, confirming strong regulation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Concept + Analysis*

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a closed-loop series voltage regulator using a BJT pass transistor. Show the role of each block in maintaining a constant Vout.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Block Diagram.**

The regulator consists of four blocks — **Pass Element (Q1, series BJT), Sampling Network (R1–R2), Reference (Zener), Error Amplifier (Q2 or op-amp)** — interconnected in a closed negative-feedback loop.

**Step 2: Working Principle (Steady State).**
In equilibrium, the feedback voltage `Vf = β · Vout` exactly equals the Zener reference `Vz`. The error amplifier output is at a quiescent DC level, and the base current of Q1 is held constant.

**Step 3: Line Variation Response.**
If `Vin` increases, the unregulated supply to the collector of Q1 rises. This momentarily pushes Vout upward. The sampling network reflects a higher `Vf`, so the error amplifier reduces the base drive of Q1. The collector-emitter voltage `VCE(Q1)` widens, absorbing the extra `ΔVin` and **holding Vout constant**.

**Step 4: Load Variation Response.**
If load current `IL` suddenly increases, Vout momentarily sags. `Vf` drops below `Vz`, the error amplifier increases base drive to Q1, `VCE(Q1)` narrows, more current is delivered, and Vout is **restored**.

**Step 5: Conclusion.**
The negative feedback action forces `Vout = Vz / β` independent of Vin and IL (within the regulator's compliance range).

*(2 marks diagram + 3 marks explanation + 2 marks line/load response)*

---

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** A discrete BJT series regulator has `Aol = 200` (open-loop gain of error amplifier) and feedback factor `β = 0.5`. If the open-loop output impedance is `Zout(ol) = 50 Ω`, calculate:
(i) Closed-loop output impedance
(ii) Line regulation (mV/V) if open-loop line sensitivity is `0.5 V/V`
(iii) Load regulation when load changes from no-load to 100 mA.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Loop Gain.**

$$A_{ol} \cdot \beta = 200 \times 0.5 = 100$$

**Step 2: Closed-Loop Output Impedance.**

$$Z_{out(cl)} = \frac{Z_{out(ol)}}{1 + A_{ol} \cdot \beta} = \frac{50}{1 + 100} = \frac{50}{101} \approx 0.495 \text{ Ω}$$

**Step 3: Line Regulation (Closed-Loop Gain from Vin to Vout).**

Open-loop line gain `Av(ol,line) = 0.5 V/V`. Closed-loop:

$$A_{v(cl,line)} = \frac{A_{v(ol,line)}}{1 + A_{ol} \cdot \beta} = \frac{0.5}{101} \approx 4.95 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V/V}$$

In mV/V: `4.95 mV/V` ⇒ Line regulation `≈ 0.495 %/V`.

**Step 4: Load Regulation.**

$$\Delta V_{out} = Z_{out(cl)} \cdot \Delta I_L = 0.495 \times 0.1 = 0.0495 \text{ V} = 49.5 \text{ mV}$$

For a regulated output of (say) 12 V:

$$LDR = \frac{49.5 \text{ mV}}{12 \text{ V}} \times 100 \ \% = 0.41 \ \%$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Scheme Valuation Pattern):**
> 1. **Forgetting the VBE term** in the simple Zener-BJT regulator (`Vout = Vz + VBE`, not just `Vz`). Examiners explicitly test this.
> 2. **Confusing "line" and "load" regulation** — line regulation is with respect to *input* voltage, load regulation with respect to *load current*.
> 3. **Sign convention error**: A regulator's output *rises slightly* when Vin rises, and *falls slightly* when IL rises. Both ΔVout signs must be reported correctly.
> 4. **Not mentioning dropout voltage** in design answers — KTU examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for dropout analysis.
> 5. **Skipping units** in line/load regulation answers. Always state `mV/V` or `%` explicitly.
> 6. **Forgetting to bias the Zener** — without `Iz(min) < Iz < Iz(max)`, the Zener operates in an undefined region and the regulator fails.
> 7. **Power dissipation overlooked** — if `PD` exceeds the transistor's free-air rating, full marks are not awarded for the design.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Lab Exam (Viva + Record):**

- **Series Regulator:** BJT (or MOSFET) in **series** with the load; operates in **active (linear) region**.
- **Key formula (with feedback):** $V_{out} = V_z \cdot (R_1 + R_2) / R_2$
- **Key formula (without feedback):** $V_{out} = V_z + V_{BE}$
- **Four functional blocks:** Pass transistor, Sampling network, Reference (Zener), Error amplifier.
- **Line regulation formula:** $LR = (\Delta V_{out} / \Delta V_{in}) \times 100\ \%$ in mV/V or %.
- **Load regulation formula:** $LDR = (V_{NL} - V_{FL}) / V_{FL} \times 100\ \%$.
- **Dropout voltage:** $V_{in(min)} \approx V_{out} + V_{CE(sat)} \approx V_{out} + 0.2\text{ V}$.
- **Pass-transistor power dissipation:** $P_D = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I_L$ — must be within SOA.
- **Zener bias current** must satisfy: $I_{z(min)} < I_z < I_{z(max)}$.
- **Feedback improves regulation by factor $(1 + A_{ol} \beta)$** — both line and load regulation improve equally.
- **Efficiency** is upper-bounded by $V_{out}/V_{in}$ — series regulators are *inefficient* for large `(Vin − Vout)` drops.
- **Common IC equivalents:** **LM7805 (5 V), LM7812 (12 V), LM317 (adjustable)** — all use the series topology internally.
- **Simulation tools (KTU-approved):** **LTspice, NI Multisim, OrCAD-PSpice** — schematic capture + DC sweep + transient analysis are the three mandatory analyses.
- **Practical BJT choices:** **2N2222 (low current, ≤ 500 mA), BD139 (medium, ≤ 1.5 A), TIP3055 (high, ≤ 15 A)**.
- **For lab record:** Always tabulate Vout vs Vin (line test) and Vout vs IL (load test), and report numerical LR/LDR in **both % and mV units**.
